We used two different sets of X-ray images of the lungs in addition to two different sets of CT scans and the classification is done using a variety of networks that have been pre-trained such as VGG (16, 19), Densenet (121), Resnet (50, 50 V2, 101 V2), Mobile net (V2), Xception Inception (V3, Resnet V2), Efficient net (B0) and Nasnet (Large). On the X-ray and CT image datasets, Resnet and VGG architecture have shown the ability to properly differentiate COVID-19 from normal images, with an average accuracy of 95 to 98 percent respectively. Our acquired results on the classification datasets are competitive and superior to previously reported findings in the literature.

</details>


### [26] [Do Vision--Language Models Understand 3D Scenes or Just Catalogue Objects?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20448)
*Animesh Maheshwari,Divyansh Sahu,Nishit Verma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLMs excel at rearrangement planning (53-97%) but severely struggle with occlusion (6-45%) and reflection (<7%) understanding; the bottleneck is the visual-token merger that loses spatial information recoverable in earlier encoder layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether vision-language models that can name objects also actually understand the 3D spatial layout of those objects in a scene.

Method: A 3,034-sample human-curated benchmark with three spatial tasks (depth-ordered occlusion, optical-geometry via reflections, volumetric rearrangement planning), tested on six VLMs with 18,204 human-annotated responses and white-box analysis on Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking to localize failure.

Result: VLMs perform well on rearrangement planning (53-97%) but poorly on occlusion (6-45%) and reflections (<7%). The failure is localized to the visual-token merger where spatial information becomes inaccessible after token compression.

Conclusion: VLMs show a sharp dissociation between action-oriented spatial reasoning and perceptual spatial understanding, with the visual-token merger being a critical bottleneck for 3D layout representation.

Abstract: Vision--language models reliably name objects in a scene, but do they represent the 3D layout those objects inhabit? We introduce a 3,034-sample human-curated benchmark targeting three components of spatial understanding: depth-ordered occlusion (probed via three independent counterfactual operationalisations), optical-geometry inference over visible reflections, and volumetric rearrangement planning. Six frontier and open-weight VLMs, scored by trained annotators on 18,204 responses with no LLM-as-judge, reveal a sharp dissociation: models that plan rearrangements over visible layouts at 53--97% accuracy and rarely violate collision constraints fall to 6--45% on occlusion and below 7% on reflections. An embodied-reasoning model reproduces the same profile. White-box analysis on Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking localises the failure to the visual-token merger: spatial information recoverable throughout the vision encoder becomes inaccessible after token compression and only stabilises again when clean post-merger activations are patched into the language decoder.

</details>


### [27] [ELEMENT: Multi-Modal Retinal Vessel Segmentation Based on a Coupled Region Growing and Machine Learning Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20458)
*Erick O. Rodrigues,Aura Conci,Panos Liatsis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ELEMENT is a multi-modal retinal vessel segmentation framework combining feature extraction, machine learning classification, and connectivity-based region growing that outperforms 25 of 26 state-of-the-art methods (including deep learning approaches) across multiple datasets (DRIVE, STARE, CHASE-DB, VAMPIRE FA, IOSTAR SLO, RC-SLO) with accuracies exceeding 97%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual or interactive retinal vessel segmentation is time-consuming and prone to human errors, yet vessel structure analysis is critical for diagnosing ocular diseases like age-related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, and glaucoma across multiple imaging modalities.

Method: ELEMENT (vEsseL sEgmentation using Machine lEarning and coNnecTivity) integrates feature extraction (capturing grey-level and vessel connectivity properties) with pixel-based classification using region growing and machine learning, seamlessly propagating connectivity information through the classification phase.

Result: Achieved 97.40% accuracy on DRIVE (beating 25/26 SOTA methods), 98.27% on STARE, 97.78% on CHASE-DB, 98.34% on VAMPIRE FA, 98.04% on IOSTAR SLO, and 98.35% on RC-SLO, outperforming all competitors including six deep learning approaches.

Conclusion: ELEMENT provides a robust multi-modal framework that reduces segmentation inconsistencies, speeds up throughput, and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art vessel segmentation algorithms across fundus, SLO, and FA imaging modalities.

Abstract: Vascular structures in the retina contain important information for the detection and analysis of ocular diseases, including age-related macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy and glaucoma. Commonly used modalities in diagnosis of these diseases are fundus photography, scanning laser ophthalmoscope (SLO) and fluorescein angiography (FA). Typically, retinal vessel segmentation is carried out either manually or interactively, which makes it time consuming and prone to human errors. In this research, we propose a new multi-modal framework for vessel segmentation called ELEMENT (vEsseL sEgmentation using Machine lEarning and coNnecTivity). This framework consists of feature extraction and pixel-based classification using region growing and machine learning. The proposed features capture complementary evidence based on grey level and vessel connectivity properties. The latter information is seamlessly propagated through the pixels at the classification phase. ELEMENT reduces inconsistencies and speeds up the segmentation throughput. We analyze and compare the performance of the proposed approach against state-of-the-art vessel segmentation algorithms in three major groups of experiments, for each of the ocular modalities. Our method produced higher overall performance, with an overall accuracy of 97.40%, compared to 25 of the 26 state-of-the-art approaches, including six works based on deep learning, evaluated on the widely known DRIVE fundus image dataset. In the case of the STARE, CHASE-DB, VAMPIRE FA, IOSTAR SLO and RC-SLO datasets, the proposed framework outperformed all of the state-of-the-art methods with accuracies of 98.27%, 97.78%, 98.34%, 98.04% and 98.35%, respectively.

</details>


### [28] [Pixel Wised Lesion Prediction on COVID-19 CT Imagery: A Comparative Analysis of Automated Image Segmentation Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20459)
*Sarmad Khan,Arslan Shaukat,Umer Asgher,Basim Azam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates 4 deep learning architectures (Unet, PSPNet, Linknet, FPN) combined with 6 pre-trained backbones for COVID-19 lesion segmentation in CT images, achieving up to 98% F1-Score for binary and 75-77% for multi-class segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field of deep learning for medical image segmentation lacks standardized methodology and uses diverse datasets, hindering reliability. The authors aim to comprehensively evaluate contemporary frameworks to establish a reference point for COVID-19 lesion segmentation and broader imaging scenarios.

Method: Four deep learning architectures (Unet, PSPNet, Linknet, FPN) are integrated with six pre-trained encoders (VGG19, DenseNet121, Inception ResNet V2, MobileNet V2, SeresNet 101, EfficientNet B0) to create diverse testing architectures. Experiments are conducted on three COVID-19 CT segmentation datasets for both binary and multi-class segmentation tasks.

Result: Deep learning architectures achieve precise and efficient segmentation. Maximum F1-Score of 98% for binary class segmentation; multi-class segmentation yields 75% and 77% F1-Scores across two datasets.

Conclusion: The evaluation demonstrates that combining various deep learning architectures with pre-trained backbones produces reliable segmentation results, enhancing AI-driven diagnostic processes for pandemic diseases.

Abstract: In recent years, there has been a notable increase in the level of attention that is given to algorithms based on deep learning in the context of medical image segmentation. Nevertheless, the reliability of the field has been hindered due to the absence of a standardized methodology for performance analysis and the utilization of different datasets in previous research. The primary objective of the research is to comprehensively evaluate contemporary segmentation frameworks combined with state-of-the-art pre-trained backbones in order to accurately predict COVID-19 lesions in CT images. Moreover, this evaluation can serve as a point of reference for the segmentation of images in various other imaging scenarios. In order to accomplish this, we integrate four distinct deep learning architectures, namely Unet, PSPNet, Linknet, and FPN, with six pre-trained encoders, including VGG 19, DenseNet 121, Inception ResNet V2, MobileNet V2, SeresNet 101, and EfficientNet B0. This approach enables the development of diverse testing architectures. In the context of image segmentation, our research encompassed both binary and multi-class experimentation. The findings derived from our analysis of three distinct COVID-19 CT segmentation datasets indicate that deep learning architectures yield precise and efficient segmentation outcomes. Significantly, a maximum F1-Score of 98% was attained for binary class segmentation, while multi-class segmentation yielded F1-Scores of 75% and 77% across two separate datasets. The utilization of artificial intelligence and deep learning enhances the diagnostic process for pandemic diseases across multiple dimensions.

</details>


### [29] [Understanding Model Behavior in Monocular Polyp Sizing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20461)
*Xinqi Xiong,Andrea Dunn Beltran,Junmyeong Choi,Sarah K. McGill,Marc Niethammer,Roni Sengupta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper audits binary polyp size classification (≤5mm vs >5mm) using multiple datasets, models, and modalities, finding that current models rely on behavioral cues rather than true metric scales. Providing perfect scale information offers limited gains due to segmentation errors under distribution shift, identifying metric scale and mask robustness as two independent bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate polyp size stratification is critical for surveillance decisions (lesions >5mm need closer follow-up), but monocular colonoscopy lacks reliable metric reference, motivating a systematic audit of current sizing approaches.

Method: Binary polyp size classification evaluated across public multi-center datasets, multiple model families, and patient-stratified cross-validation. Tested various input modalities (RGB, depth, photometry). Used oracle scale ladders, shortcut partitions, and mask substitution to quantify improvement potential and identify bottlenecks.

Result: Model performance is moderately consistent across architectures but relies on examination behavior cues rather than true metric scales. Current depth estimation and global calibration offer limited gains. Segmentation errors under distribution shift eliminate most potential improvement, with oracle scale under predicted masks recovering only baseline performance.

Conclusion: Metric scale and mask robustness are two independent bottlenecks for polyp sizing. The paper provides reusable evaluation tools (oracle scale ladders, shortcut partitions, mask substitution) for auditing future polyp sizing pipelines.

Abstract: Accurate polyp size stratification guides surveillance decisions, with lesions larger than 5 mm typically requiring closer follow-up. However, monocular colonoscopy lacks a reliable metric reference. We present a diagnostic audit of binary polyp size classification (<=5 mm vs. >5 mm) across multiple public multi-center datasets, model families, and patient-stratified cross-validation. Across architectures and input modalities, including RGB appearance, relative depth, and photometry, model performance is moderately consistent, suggesting reliance on cues correlated with examination behavior rather than true metric scales. By providing ground-truth scale at varying granularities, we quantify the potential improvement from perfect scale information and show that current depth estimation and global calibration offer limited gains. We further demonstrate that segmentation errors under distribution shift eliminate most of this potential, with oracle scale under predicted masks recovering only baseline performance. These results highlight metric scale and mask robustness as two independent bottlenecks and provide reusable evaluation tools such as oracle scale ladders, shortcut partitions, and mask substitution for auditing future polyp sizing pipelines. Our code is publicly accessible at https://github.com/anaxqx/polyp-sizing-audit.

</details>


### [30] [HalluCXR: Benchmarking and Mitigating Hallucinations in Medical Vision-Language Models for Chest Radiograph Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20469)
*Haoyu Wang,Zitong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HalluCXR evaluates 6 VLMs on 15,408 medical image interpretations, finding 61.9-82.3% of outputs contain hallucinations with up to 80.2% clinically dangerous errors. Key patterns: normal X-rays attract severe hallucinations, common findings are over-fabricated while rare ones are under-detected, and response length predicts hallucination risk (AUC=0.908).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs hallucinate clinically plausible but incorrect findings in medical images, posing direct patient safety risks. Existing benchmarks lack systematic evaluation of hallucination patterns across diverse VLMs and query types.

Method: Evaluated 6 architecturally diverse VLMs on 856 MIMIC-CXR chest radiographs with 3 query types (15,408 total evaluations). Developed an 8-category hallucination taxonomy with clinical severity ratings and a two-layer detection pipeline validated against 250 human annotations (auto-detection F1=0.959; LLM judge F1=0.907).

Result: 61.9-82.3% of outputs contain hallucinations; clinically dangerous errors in up to 80.2%. Normal radiographs attract most severe hallucinations. Response length predicts hallucination risk (AUC=0.908). Six-model ensemble reduces fabrication by 84.8% but increases omission; three-model subset achieves comparable performance at half cost.

Conclusion: Hallucination auditing, verbosity-based risk monitoring, and ensemble-based safety layers are prerequisites for clinical deployment of VLMs in medical image interpretation.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used for medical image interpretation, yet they frequently hallucinate, generating clinically plausible but factually incorrect findings that pose direct patient safety risks. We introduce HalluCXR, a benchmark evaluating six architecturally diverse VLMs across 856 stratified MIMIC-CXR chest radiographs and three query types, yielding 15,408 model evaluations. An eight-category hallucination taxonomy with clinical severity ratings and a two-layer detection pipeline are validated against 250 human annotations (auto-detection F1=0.959; LLM judge F1=0.907). We find that 61.9--82.3% of outputs contain hallucinations, with clinically dangerous errors in up to 80.2%. Three key patterns emerge: normal radiographs paradoxically attract the most severe hallucinations, common findings are systematically over-fabricated while rare findings go under-detected, and response length alone predicts hallucination risk (AUC up to 0.908). A six-model ensemble reduces fabrication by up to 84.8% at the cost of increased omission; a three-model subset retains comparable performance at half the cost. These results establish that hallucination auditing, verbosity-based risk monitoring, and ensemble-based safety layers are prerequisites for clinical deployment.

</details>


### [31] [EPC-3D-Diff: Equivariant Physics Consistent Conditional 3D Latent Diffusion for CBCT to CT Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20470)
*Alzahra Altalib,Chunhui Li,Haytham Al Ewaidat,Khaled Alawneh,Ahmad Qendel,Alessandro Perelli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EPC-3D-Diff is a conditional 3D latent diffusion framework for CBCT-to-CT synthesis that introduces a projection-domain equivariance loss leveraging acquisition physics, achieving substantial improvements in PSNR (+7.4 dB phantom, +1.8 dB clinical), SSIM, and HU accuracy over state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CBCT suffers from scatter, noise, and reconstruction artifacts that degrade HU accuracy, limiting its quantitative reliability for radiotherapy patient setup. Existing synthesis methods lack physics-consistent constraints.

Method: The method uses a conditional 3D latent diffusion model with a projection-domain equivariance loss: in-plane rotation of a volume corresponds to angular shift in its projections. The model forward-projects rotated synthesized CT volumes and matches them to angle-shifted projections of target CT. A lightweight 3D autoencoder compresses the volume into a compact latent space for efficient diffusion training.

Result: Validated on phantom (+7.4 dB PSNR improvement) and clinical data (+1.8 dB PSNR), with improved SSIM and HU accuracy within tissue boundaries, outperforming diffusion baselines and CycleGAN.

Conclusion: EPC-3D-Diff improves robustness and physics consistency for HU-aware CBCT-to-CT synthesis, supporting downstream radiotherapy workflows.

Abstract: Cone-beam CT (CBCT) is routinely acquired during radiotherapy for patient setup, but its quantitative reliability is degraded by scatter, noise, and reconstruction artifacts, limiting Hounsfield Unit (HU) accuracy. We propose EPC-3D-Diff, a novel conditional 3D latent diffusion framework for volumetric CBCT to CT synthesis that introduces a projection domain equivariance loss derived from acquisition physics. Unlike common image domain equivariance, we exploit the fact that an in plane rotation of the volume corresponds to an angular shift in its projections. During training, we enforce this relationship by forward projecting rotated synthesized CT volumes and matching them to appropriately angle shifted projections of the paired target CT, yielding a physics consistent equivariance constraint integrated into the diffusion objective. To capture full 3D context efficiently, conditional diffusion is performed in a compact latent space learnt by a lightweight 3D autoencoder, preserving axial depth while downsampling in plane resolution for stable training. We validate on a paired head CBCT/CT phantom dataset, including repeat scans, and paired clinical data using patient wise splits, and perform single and mixed domain training, ablations, and comparisons with diffusion and CycleGAN. EPC-3D-Diff generalizes well and achieved substantial improvements, +7.4 dB (phantom) and +1.8 dB (clinical data) in PSNR compared to state of the art methods, alongside improved SSIM and HU accuracy, within tissue boundaries. Overall, EPC-3D-Diff improves robustness and physics consistency, supporting HU aware synthesis for downstream radiotherapy workflows.

</details>


### [32] [Goodbye Drift: Anchored Tree Sampling for Long-Horizon Video-to-Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20476)
*Matthew Bendel,Stephen W. Bailey,Mithilesh Vaidya,Sumukh Badam,Xingzhe He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ATS is a training-free inference-time scheduler that replaces left-to-right video generation with sparse-to-dense anchor-bounded tree sampling, reducing drift and enabling stable 40+ minute video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon video generation suffers from drift (quality degradation over time) and continuity issues (object permanence, transient content inconsistency). Existing autoregressive distillation methods attack both problems simultaneously, but the authors choose to focus on drift directly.

Method: Anchored Tree Sampling (ATS): a training-free inference-time scheduler using sparse-to-dense, anchor-bounded imputation organized as a tree. A root call produces sparse anchors over the full horizon, recursive refinement generates intermediate anchors, and final leaf spans are synthesized between neighboring anchors. Reduces critical path from K sequential steps to L+1 tree-hierarchical steps.

Result: ATS outperforms two autoregressive baselines on Wan 2.1 + VACE across five conditioning modalities (inpainting, outpainting, edge, pose, depth) in overall quality and drift prevention. Demonstrates stable ≥40-minute generation on LTX-2.3 across the same modalities.

Conclusion: ATS effectively addresses drift in long-horizon video generation. The paper proposes future work to extend ATS to arbitrarily long T2V generation, dynamic-camera, and multi-shot regimes.

Abstract: Long-horizon video generation suffers from two intertwined issues. First, there is drift, where video quality degrades over time. Second, there are continuity issues which manifest as object permanence issues, or improperly rendering transient content (e.g., an object that appears in non-consecutive frames changing color/style). Recent work has focused on autoregressive distillation techniques that attack both problems simultaneously. We instead choose to focus on drift directly and introduce \textbf{Anchored Tree Sampling (ATS)}: a training-free inference-time scheduler that replaces left-to-right rollout with sparse-to-dense, anchor-bounded imputation organized as a tree. A root call produces sparse anchors over the full horizon, recursive refinement generates intermediate anchors, and final leaf spans are synthesized between neighboring anchors. This reduces the critical path from $K$ sequential rollout steps to $L+1$ tree-hierarchical steps and converts horizon-compounding drift into anchor-bounded drift. We focus on V2V generation in the \emph{static-camera} regime, where sparse anchors over the horizon are well approximated by the dense conditioning signal, and the base model can produce them without retraining. We evaluate ATS against two contemporary autoregressive baselines on Wan $2.1$ $+$ VACE, across five conditioning modalities (inpainting, outpainting, edge, pose, depth). We show that ATS outperforms both competitors in overall quality, as well as in drift prevention. We additionally demonstrate stable $\geq 40$-minute generation on LTX-$2.3$ across the same five modalities. We conclude by proposing a path forward to extend ATS to arbitrarily long T2V generation, as well as the dynamic-camera and multi-shot regimes.

</details>


### [33] [Oracle Supervision Transfers for Hyperparameter Prediction in Model-Based Image Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20479)
*Jianmin Liao,Lixin Shen,Yuesheng Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyperDn is a configuration-conditioned hyperparameter predictor that transfers oracle supervision from source denoiser configurations (e.g., TV/TGV) to target configurations (e.g., DiffPIR), achieving near-oracle performance with very few target labels (e.g., 2 labels → 30.23 dB, within 0.90 dB of oracle).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter prediction for image denoisers (from classical TV/TGV to diffusion models like DiffPIR) is a bottleneck because each new configuration requires its own oracle-labeled training set via costly hierarchical grid search against clean ground truth, making scaling impractical.

Method: HyperDn is a single configuration-conditioned predictor that pools oracle supervision across source configurations and predicts heterogeneous hyperparameters for new denoiser-noise configurations, enabling transfer from source to target with few or zero target oracle labels.

Result: With only 2 target oracle labels, HyperDn reaches 30.23 dB (within 0.90 dB of oracle), outperforming a 64-label per-configuration predictor using 1/32 of the labels. It also achieves near-oracle PSNR on unseen noise mixtures and cross-resolution transfer (96×96 → 512×768).

Conclusion: Oracle supervision for hyperparameter prediction can be effectively transferred from source to target configurations, substantially reducing the need to rebuild expensive oracle labels for each new denoising configuration.

Abstract: Hyperparameter prediction is a critical practical bottleneck for model-based image denoisers, ranging from classical TV/TGV variational solvers to modern diffusion-based models such as DiffPIR. While existing learned predictors can achieve near-oracle performance, this approach scales poorly: each new configuration conventionally requires its own oracle-labeled training set, and each label requires a hierarchical grid search evaluated against clean ground truth. We therefore ask whether oracle supervision collected on source configurations can transfer to target configurations with few or no target oracle labels. We propose HyperDn, a single configuration-conditioned predictor that pools oracle supervision across source configurations and predicts heterogeneous hyperparameters for new denoiser--noise configurations. In a cross-paradigm experiment, HyperDn transfers from relatively cheap TV/TGV variational sources to more expensive diffusion-based DiffPIR. With only $2$ target oracle labels, it reaches $30.23$\,dB, within $0.90$\,dB of the oracle, and outperforms the $64$-label per-configuration predictor trained from scratch, using $1/32$ as many target labels as that baseline point. Without any target oracle labels, HyperDn also reaches near-oracle PSNR on two unseen mixtures of seen noise types and on transfer from relatively cheap $96\times 96$ source images to $512\times 768$ targets. Together, these results show that expensive oracle supervision for hyperparameter prediction can be transferred from source to new target configurations, reducing the need to rebuild oracle labels for each new denoising configuration.

</details>


### [34] [A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Efficient Prompt Selection in Microscopy Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20495)
*Abhiram Kandiyana,Ankur Mali,Lawrence O. Hall,Peter R. Mouton,Dmitry Goldgof*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an active learning framework for selecting which unlabeled microscopy images should be prioritized for expert verification when building prompt sets for vision-language models, achieving high classification accuracy with minimal expert annotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the expensive, labor-intensive expert annotation required for deep-learning microscopy image classification by addressing two practical gaps: (1) which unlabeled images to prioritize for verification, and (2) how many verified exemplars are needed to reach a performance target.

Method: Formulates prompt-set construction as a target-driven active learning problem with three complementary selection criteria under strict low-resource constraints with small unlabeled pools. Uses vision-language models with human-in-the-loop verification and editing of captions.

Result: Achieves 100% test accuracy with as few as 20 annotated images on average, substantially outperforming random selection in reaching target performance with fewer expert-verified images.

Conclusion: The human-in-the-loop framework demonstrates a human-centered use of generative AI in biomedical image analysis, reducing annotation cost while keeping experts actively involved in verifying and refining model output.

Abstract: Deep-learning pipelines for microscopy image classification often require expensive, labor- and time-intensive expert annotation to produce high-quality ground truth for training. Recent work has shown that prompt tuning of vision-language models (VLMs) can reduce manual annotation by constructing a small prompt set of expert-verified image-caption exemplars that is reused as few-shot context to classify all remaining images at inference time. To further reduce effort, the VLM can draft captions for candidate exemplars, which experts then verify and lightly edit instead of writing text de novo. However, two practical questions remain unaddressed: (1) which unlabeled images should be prioritized for verification, and (2) how many verified exemplars are needed to reach a performance target. In this work, we address these questions by formulating prompt-set construction as a target-driven active learning problem that prioritizes which images to annotate. We study three complementary selection criteria under strict low-resource constraints with small unlabeled pools. Experiments show that our methods reach the target performance with substantially fewer expert-verified images than random selection, achieving 100% test accuracy with as few as 20 annotated images on average. More broadly, our human-in-the-loop framework demonstrates a human-centered use of generative AI in biomedical image analysis, where experts remain actively involved in verifying and refining model output while significantly reducing annotation cost. Code and data will be publicly available.

</details>


### [35] [ShadeBench: A Benchmark Dataset for Building Shade Simulation in Sustainable Society](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20510)
*Longchao Da,Mithun Shivakoti,Xiangrui Liu,T Pranav Kutralingam,Yezhou Yang,Hua Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ShadeBench is a new multimodal dataset and benchmark for analyzing urban shade patterns from buildings, supporting tasks like shade generation, segmentation, and 3D reconstruction to aid heat-resilient urban planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban heat exposure is worsening due to the urban heat island effect, and fine-grained shade patterns from buildings strongly affect pedestrian thermal comfort, but large-scale datasets and systematic evaluation frameworks for urban shade analysis are lacking.

Method: ShadeBench provides geographically diverse urban scenes with temporally varying simulated shade maps, textual descriptions, satellite imagery, building skeleton representations, and 3D building meshes, along with standardized evaluation protocols and baselines for downstream tasks.

Result: A comprehensive multimodal benchmark enabling scalable and fine-grained shade analysis across shade generation, shade segmentation, and 3D building reconstruction tasks.

Conclusion: ShadeBench provides a foundation for data-driven urban climate research and supports future heat-resilient urban planning and decision-making.

Abstract: Urban heat exposure is becoming an increasingly critical challenge due to the intensifying urban heat island effect. Fine-grained shade patterns, especially those induced by urban buildings, strongly influence pedestrians' thermal exposure and outdoor activity planning. However, accurately modeling and analyzing urban shade at scale remains difficult because of the lack of large-scale datasets and systematic evaluation frameworks. To address this challenge, we present ShadeBench, a comprehensive dataset and benchmark for urban shade understanding. ShadeBench contains geographically diverse urban scenes with temporally varying simulated shade maps and textual descriptions, together with aligned satellite imagery, building skeleton representations, and 3D building meshes. Built upon this multimodal dataset, ShadeBench supports a range of downstream tasks, including shade generation, shade segmentation, and 3D building reconstruction. We further establish standardized evaluation protocols and baseline methods for these tasks. By enabling scalable and fine-grained shade analysis, ShadeBench provides a foundation for data-driven urban climate research and supports future studies in heat-resilient urban planning and decision-making. The code and dataset are publicly available at https://darl-genai.github.io/shadebench/.

</details>


### [36] [HADS-Net:A Hybrid Attention-Augmented Dual-Stream Network with Physics-Informed Augmentation for Breast Ultrasound Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20536)
*Chinedu Emmanuel Mbonu,Blessing Nwamaka Iduh,Joseph Ikechukwu Odo,Doris Chinedu Asogwa*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HADS-Net is a dual-stream deep learning network for breast ultrasound classification that combines physics-informed augmented texture features (EfficientNet-B3) with Sobel edge boundary features via cross-attention fusion, achieving 96.58% accuracy with no malignant cases misclassified as normal on the BUSI dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Breast ultrasound classification is challenged by speckle noise, acoustic shadowing, and inter-class visual ambiguity. Existing single-stream methods use generic augmentation ignoring ultrasound physics, and none dedicate a stream to lesion boundary features—the most diagnostically significant visual cue.

Method: HADS-Net uses two parallel streams: Stream 1 applies physics-informed augmentation (speckle noise, acoustic shadowing, gain variation) and extracts texture features via pretrained EfficientNet-B3 projected to 512D; Stream 2 extracts Sobel edge maps processed by a lightweight CNN projected to 512D. A cross-attention fusion module allows texture to query boundary features, and an MLP with adaptive class-weighted focal loss performs classification.

Result: On BUSI, HADS-Net achieves 96.58% accuracy, macro ROC-AUC of 0.9978, macro F1 of 0.9654, and per-class F1-scores of 0.970, 0.951, 0.976 for benign, malignant, and normal. No malignant lesion misclassified as normal.

Conclusion: Modality-specific physics-informed augmentation combined with cross-modal attention fusion is an effective strategy for ultrasound-based breast cancer diagnosis.

Abstract: Accurate classification of breast ultrasound images into benign, malignant, and normal categories is a critical clinical task complicated by speckle noise, acoustic shadowing, and inter-class visual ambiguity. Existing deep learning methods rely on single-stream architectures with generic augmentation that ignores ultrasound acquisition physics, and no prior method dedicates a stream to the lesion boundary features identified as the most diagnostically significant visual cue. We propose HADS-Net, a Hybrid Attention-Augmented Dual-Stream Network exploiting global texture and local boundary cues through two parallel pathways. Stream 1 applies physics-informed augmentation simulating speckle noise, acoustic shadowing, and gain variation before extracting features via pretrained EfficientNet-B3 projected to 512 dimensions. Stream 2 extracts Sobel edge maps processed by a lightweight CNN projected to the same 512-dimensional space. A cross-attention fusion module allows the texture stream to selectively query boundary features, producing a jointly optimised representation classified by an MLP trained with adaptive class-weighted focal loss. Five-fold stratified cross-validation with cosine annealing over 50 epochs is used, with the globally best checkpoint selected by lowest validation loss evaluated on a held-out test set. On the BUSI dataset, HADS-Net achieves 96.58% accuracy, macro ROC-AUC of 0.9978, macro F1 of 0.9654, and per-class F1-scores of 0.970, 0.951, and 0.976 for benign, malignant, and normal. No malignant lesion is misclassified as normal. These results confirm that modality-specific augmentation with cross-modal attention fusion is an effective strategy for ultrasound-based breast cancer diagnosis.

</details>


### [37] [Continual Segmentation under Joint Nonstationarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20538)
*Prashant Pandey,Himanshu Kumar,Devineni Sri Venkatraya Chowdary,Brejesh Lall*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper addresses continual semantic segmentation under joint nonstationarity (simultaneous changes in classes, domains, and labels), proposing gradient-adaptive stabilization for stability-plasticity tradeoff and prototype anchored semi-supervision for leveraging unlabeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continual learning research typically studies class shifts, domain shifts, and label shifts in isolation, but real-world structured prediction systems face these challenges jointly. The authors aim to fill this gap by formalizing and addressing coupled nonstationarity in continual segmentation.

Method: (1) Gradient-adaptive stabilization: parameter-wise regularization via gradient-scaled stochastic perturbations. (2) Prototype anchored supervision: semi-supervised learning with pseudo-label validation using joint confidence and prototype consistency.

Result: Consistent improvements over prior methods across class-incremental, domain-incremental, and few-shot regimes. The work also exposes fundamental failure modes of existing continual segmentation approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed mechanisms enable effective continual segmentation under joint nonstationarity, providing insights for learning robust dense predictors in dynamic environments.

Abstract: Evolving data streams induce joint nonstationarity in continual semantic segmentation, where semantic classes, input distributions, and supervision availability change simultaneously over time. This setting reflects practical structured prediction systems, yet remains largely unexplored in prior continual learning work, which typically studies these factors in isolation. We formalize continual segmentation under coupled class, domain, and label shifts and investigate learning in heterogeneous dense prediction environments with limited annotations and abundant unlabeled data. To address instability and overfitting arising from few-shot supervision under distribution drift, we introduce gradient-adaptive stabilization, a parameter-wise regularization mechanism implemented via gradient-scaled stochastic perturbations that promotes a principled stability-plasticity tradeoff. We further leverage unlabeled data through semi-supervised learning and introduce prototype anchored supervision that validates pseudo-labels via joint confidence and prototype consistency. Together, these mechanisms enable learning under joint nonstationarity in continual segmentation. Extensive empirical evaluation across class-incremental, domain-incremental, and few-shot regimes demonstrates consistent improvements over prior methods in heterogeneous structured prediction settings. Our results expose fundamental failure modes of existing continual segmentation approaches and provide insight into learning robust dense predictors in dynamically evolving environments.

</details>


### [38] [Uncertainty-Guided Conservative Propagation for Structured Inference in Vessel Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20543)
*Huan Huang,Michele Esposito,Chen Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UGCP is a plug-in module for vessel segmentation that refines predictions through iterative logit-space updates guided by uncertainty, improving structural consistency and reducing vessel disconnections across 2D/3D tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep models for vessel segmentation rely on single-pass prediction, which cannot refine uncertain or disconnected regions during inference.

Method: UGCP performs iterative logit-space updates guided by predictive uncertainty, with structure-aware modulation and source-based stabilization, as a differentiable plug-in module.

Result: Consistent improvements in Dice, centerline Dice, and 95th percentile Hausdorff distance on four datasets (retinal, coronary, cerebral vessels) with both CNN and Transformer backbones.

Conclusion: UGCP effectively reduces vessel disconnections and improves structural consistency with minimal added computation.

Abstract: Accurate vessel segmentation is essential for medical image analysis, yet remains challenging due to complex vascular patterns and imaging ambiguity. Most deep models rely on single-pass prediction, limiting their ability to refine uncertain or disconnected regions during inference. To address this limitation, we propose Uncertainty-Guided Conservative Propagation (UGCP), a general plug-in module for vessel segmentation. Instead of directly using a one-shot output as the final prediction, UGCP performs a small number of logit-space update steps to refine the segmentation through local predictions interaction. Predictive uncertainty guides reliable regions to support ambiguous regions, while structure-aware modulation and source-based stabilization reduce unreliable propagation and excessive drift. The module is differentiable and can be trained end-to-end with different segmentation networks. We evaluate UGCP on four public vessel segmentation datasets covering 2D and 3D tasks, including retinal vessel, coronary artery, and cerebral vessel segmentation. Experiments with convolutional neural network-based and Transformer-based backbones show consistent improvements in Dice similarity coefficient, centerline Dice, and 95th percentile Hausdorff distance. Further analysis demonstrates that UGCP reduces vessel disconnections and improves structural consistency with limited additional computation. The code will be made available at https://github.com/chenzhao2023/UGC_PR.

</details>


### [39] [MAPS: A Synthetic Dataset for Probing Vision Models in a Controlled 3D Scene Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20549)
*Santiago Galella,Pamela Osuna-Vargas,Maren Wehrheim,Martina G. Vilas,Gemma Roig,Matthias Kaschube*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAPS is a scalable instrument using 2,618 photorealistic 3D meshes and a Blender pipeline to attribute vision model behavior to scene parameters (background, camera, lighting). Evaluating 20 models, it finds camera distance and elevation are universal failure axes, and modern CNNs/transformers cluster together distinct from older architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard benchmarks reveal little about which scene properties drive vision model predictions; existing robustness benchmarks manipulate global 2D properties, rely on entangled real-world variation, or cover limited 3D objects and scene parameters.

Method: MAPS comprises 2,618 curated photorealistic 3D meshes validated across 560 ImageNet classes, with a Blender-based rendering pipeline for on-demand image generation under continuous variation of nine independent scene factors (background, camera, lighting). Regression-based sensitivity analysis quantifies model reliance on these factors.

Result: Camera distance and elevation universally dominate recognition failure across all architectures regardless of ImageNet accuracy. Modern CNNs and transformers cluster together, distinct from older architectures, in terms of sensitivity profiles.

Conclusion: Fine-grained architectural design choices, rather than the coarse CNN-versus-transformer distinction, are the stronger determinant of sensitivity profiles. MAPS provides a scalable, controlled tool for attribution of model behavior to scene parameters.

Abstract: Modern vision models achieve strong performance on standard benchmarks, yet their aggregate accuracy reveals little about which scene properties drive their predictions. Existing robustness benchmarks provide important stress tests, but typically manipulate global 2D image properties, rely on entangled real-world variation, or cover only a limited set of 3D objects and scene parameters. We introduce MAPS (Manifolds of Artificial Parametric Scenes), a scalable instrument for controlled attribution of vision model behavior to scene parameters. MAPS comprises 2,618 curated photorealistic 3D meshes validated for recognizability across 560 ImageNet classes and provides a Blender-based rendering pipeline for on-demand image generation under continuous variation of nine independent scene-factors spanning background, camera, and lighting, extensible to other factors. To showcase its applicability, we use MAPS to evaluate 20 convolutional and transformer-based models by quantifying their reliance on these scene factors through regression-based sensitivity analysis. We find a near-universal failure axis across all tested architectures: camera distance and elevation consistently dominate recognition failure regardless of ImageNet accuracy. However, the full sensitivity structure reveals that modern CNNs and transformers cluster together, distinct from older architectures, suggesting that fine-grained architectural design choices, rather than the coarse CNN-versus-transformer distinction, are the stronger determinant of sensitivity profiles.

</details>


### [40] [Faster or Stronger: Towards Flexible Visual Place Recognition via Weighted Aggregation and Token Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20551)
*Zichao Zeng,June Moh Goo,Junwei Zheng,Weijia Fan,Jiaming Zhang,Rainer Stiefelhagen,Jan Boehm*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes two complementary contributions for Visual Place Recognition: WeiAD (weighted cluster aggregation for better global descriptors) and WeiToP (a VPR-oriented token pruning framework using self-distillation to reduce ViT feature extraction cost).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VPR aggregation methods uniformly pool patch tokens into clusters, ignoring that clusters encode distinct patterns and contribute unequally. Additionally, prior latency reduction work focuses on descriptor compression, overlooking the high feature extraction cost of ViT backbones.

Method: WeiAD assigns learnable weights to clusters during aggregation. WeiToP introduces a VPR-oriented token pruning framework where aggregation-induced token importance supervises a lightweight pruning module via self-distillation, attached to an early transformer layer for inference-time pruning.

Result: WeiToP outperforms existing token pruning methods adapted from general vision tasks. The framework enables flexible, on-demand accuracy-efficiency trade-off control without additional training.

Conclusion: The joint training of WeiAD and WeiToP produces more discriminative global descriptors while reducing feature extraction cost, achieving a better accuracy-efficiency balance for large-scale and edge-device VPR deployments.

Abstract: Visual Place Recognition (VPR) aims to match a query image to reference images of the same place in a large-scale database. Recent state-of-the-art methods employ Vision Transformers (ViTs) as backbone foundation models to extract patch-level features that are robust to viewpoint, illumination, and seasonal variations, which are then aggregated into a compact global descriptor for retrieval. Most existing aggregation methods uniformly pool patch tokens into learned clusters, despite the fact that different clusters often encode distinct spatial or semantic patterns and contribute unequally to VPR performance. To address this limitation, we propose Weighted Aggregated Descriptor (WeiAD), which assigns weights to clusters during aggregation, producing more discriminative global representations. Beyond accuracy, retrieval latency is a critical concern for large-scale deployments and resource-constrained edge devices. Prior work mainly reduces latency by compressing global descriptors, while overlooking the cost of feature extraction, an issue exacerbated by ViT-based backbones. We therefore introduce WeiToP, a VPR-oriented token pruning framework that reduces feature extraction cost via self-distillation, where aggregation-induced token importance supervises a lightweight pruning module attached to an early transformer layer, enabling inference-time token pruning. After a single joint training phase, WeiToP enables plug-and-play token pruning at inference time, allowing flexible and on-demand control over the accuracy-efficiency trade-off without additional training. Moreover, WeiToP outperforms existing token pruning methods adapted from general vision tasks.

</details>


### [41] [End-to-End Unmixing with Material Prompts for Hyperspectral Object Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20569)
*Xu Han,Mohammad Aminul Islam,Lei Wang,Zekun Long,Guanmanyi Fu,Wangshu Cai,Kuldip K. Paliwal,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes E2EMPT, an end-to-end material-aware hyperspectral tracking framework that jointly optimizes material decomposition and target localization via a weighted target-oriented unmixing loss, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hyperspectral trackers either adapt pretrained RGB trackers ignoring material information, or use external spectral unmixing pipelines decoupled from the tracking objective, limiting optimization of material representations for localization.

Method: (1) Material Representation Decomposition Module for deep learning-based spectral unmixing with adaptive frequency decomposition; (2) Dual-branch wavelet-enhanced material prompt module that learns low- and high-frequency material prompts via spatial-material interactions in the frequency domain; (3) Weighted target-oriented unmixing loss coupling material decomposition and target localization.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on standard hyperspectral tracking benchmarks, with validated effectiveness of the end-to-end material-aware framework.

Conclusion: The proposed model-agnostic framework seamlessly generalizes to different unmixing backbones and achieves superior tracking performance by explicitly integrating material information with localization objectives in an end-to-end manner.

Abstract: Hyperspectral imagery encodes rich material properties that can improve tracking robustness under appearance ambiguity, illumination change, and background clutter. However, due to the limited availability of hyperspectral video data, many existing methods adapt pretrained RGB trackers via spatial or channel fusion strategies, largely neglecting the intrinsic material information in hyperspectral imagery. Moreover, the few material-aware approaches typically rely on external spectral unmixing pipelines that are decoupled from the tracking objective, limiting effective optimization of material representations for target localization. To address these limitations, we formulate hyperspectral object tracking as a joint optimization problem of material decomposition and target localization, coupling the two tasks via a weighted target-oriented unmixing loss that explicitly aligns material representations with localization accuracy. Specifically, we propose a material representation decomposition module for deep learning-based spectral unmixing with adaptive frequency decomposition. Building on the decomposed material representations, we further introduce a dual-branch wavelet-enhanced material prompt module that learns low- and high-frequency material prompts through efficient spatial-material interactions in the frequency domain. The framework is model-agnostic and can be seamlessly generalized to different unmixing backbones. Extensive experiments on standard hyperspectral tracking benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and validate the effectiveness of the proposed end-to-end material-aware tracking framework. Code is available at https://github.com/han030927/E2EMPT.

</details>


### [42] [$Δ$ynamics: Language-Based Representation for Inferring Rigid-Body Dynamics From Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20576)
*Chia-Hsiang Kao,Cong Phuoc Huynh,Chien-Yi Wang,Noranart Vesdapunt,Stefan Stojanov,Bharath Hariharan,Oleksandr Obiednikov,Ning Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ΔYNAMICS is a vision-language framework that uses language as a unified representation for inferring rigid-body physics from monocular videos, achieving significant improvements over baseline VLMs on CLEVRER and demonstrating real-world transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches for inferring rigid-body physical states from monocular videos are limited by assumptions about specific physical systems, object types, and camera poses, preventing generalization to complex real-world settings.

Method: ΔYNAMICS uses language as a unified representation for rigid-body dynamics, generates scene configurations in structured text for physics simulation, integrates natural language motion reasoning, and uses optical flow as a semantic-agnostic input. It also employs test-time sampling and evolutionary search to improve performance.

Result: On CLEVRER, ΔYNAMICS achieves 0.30 segmentation IoU (7x improvement over leading VLMs). Test-time sampling and evolutionary search boost performance by 27% and 120% respectively. Strong transfer is demonstrated on 235 real-world rigid-body videos.

Conclusion: Language-driven physics inference shows strong potential for bridging perception and simulation, with good generalization from synthetic to real-world settings.

Abstract: Inferring rigid-body physical states and properties from monocular videos is a fundamental step toward physics-based perception and simulation. Existing approaches assume specific underlying physical systems, object types, and camera poses, making them unable to generalize to complex real-world settings. We introduce $Δ$YNAMICS, a vision-language framework that uses language as a unified representation of rigid-body dynamics. Instead of directly predicting parameters, $Δ$YNAMICS generates scene configurations in a structured text format for physics simulation. We enhance the model's generalization by integrating natural language motion reasoning and leveraging optical flow as a semantic-agnostic input. On the CLEVRER dataset, $Δ$YNAMICS achieves a segmentation IoU of 0.30, a 7x improvement over leading VLMs (InternVL3-8B, Qwen2.5-VL-7B and Claude-4-Sonnet). Additionally, test-time sampling and evolutionary search further boost performance by 27% and 120% in segmentation IoU, respectively. Finally, we demonstrate strong transfer to a new dataset of 235 real-world rigid-body videos, highlighting the potential of language-driven physics inference for bridging perception and simulation.

</details>


### [43] [QwenSafe: Multimodal Content Rating Description Identification via Preference-Aligned VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20584)
*Dishanika Denipitiyage,Aruna Seneviratne,Suranga Seneviratne*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QwenSafe is a Vision-Language Model that automatically identifies Apple-defined content rating descriptors by jointly reasoning over app metadata and screenshots, using a synthetic data pipeline (metadata2CRD) and DPO fine-tuning. It outperforms baselines including Qwen3-VL, LLaVA-1.6, and Gemini-2.5-Flash in binary CRD classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile app marketplaces require standardized content rating descriptors to inform users about sensitive/restricted content, but ensuring accuracy and consistency of these disclosures remains challenging due to the multimodal nature of app content (text + visuals).

Method: The authors propose QwenSafe, a VLM based on Qwen3-VL-8B, fine-tuned with supervised fine-tuning and Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). They introduce metadata2CRD, a data-construction pipeline that synthesizes descriptor-aligned question-answer pairs from app descriptions, screenshots, and formal descriptor definitions.

Result: QwenSafe consistently outperforms state-of-the-art VLMs (Qwen3-VL, LLaVA-1.6, Gemini-2.5-Flash) on 12 Apple-defined CRDs, achieving improvements in positive-class recall of 111.8%, 36.1%, and 2.1%, respectively.

Conclusion: Descriptor-aware multimodal alignment substantially improves automated content classification, and vision-language models show strong potential for supporting scalable and consistent content rating in mobile app marketplaces.

Abstract: Mobile app marketplaces require developers to disclose standardized content rating descriptors (CRDs) to inform users about potentially sensitive or restricted content. Ensuring the accuracy and consistency of these disclosures remains challenging due to the multimodal nature of app content, which spans textual descriptions and visual interfaces. In this paper, we present QwenSafe, a Vision-Language Model (VLM) designed to automatically identify the presence of Apple-defined CRDs by jointly reasoning over app metadata and screenshots. To enable scalable training for this task, we introduce metadata2CRD, a data-construction pipeline that synthesizes descriptor-aligned question-answer pairs by combining app descriptions, screenshots, and formal descriptor definitions. We adapt Qwen3-VL-8B using supervised fine-tuning followed by Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to align model predictions with descriptor-specific evidence and explanations across visual and textual modalities. We evaluate QwenSafe on 12 Apple-defined content rating descriptors and compare it against state-of-the-art vision-language models, including Qwen3-VL, LLaVA-1.6, and Gemini-2.5-Flash. QwenSafe consistently outperforms all baselines in binary CRD classification, achieving improvements in positive-class recall of 111.8%, 36.1%, and 2.1%, respectively. Our results demonstrate that descriptor-aware multimodal alignment substantially improves automated content classification and highlights the potential of vision-language models to support scalable and consistent content rating in mobile app marketplaces.

</details>


### [44] [Head-Aware Key-Value Compression for Efficient Autoregressive Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20600)
*Guotao Liang,Baoquan Zhang,Zhiyuan Wen,Yunming Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HeadKV is a head-aware KV cache compression framework for autoregressive image generation that allocates different cache budgets to attention heads based on their attention patterns (local vs. global), using early-stage head type identification to guide compression without additional training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KV cache compression methods for AR visual generation allocate a fixed budget to each attention head, overlooking the heterogeneity among attention heads with different attention patterns (local vs. global), leading to suboptimal memory allocation.

Method: HeadKV: (1) Identifies head types (locality-biased vs. broader attention) early in generation since heads exhibit consistent attention patterns across token positions; (2) Assigns smaller cache budgets to local heads and larger budgets to global heads; (3) Uses Stratified Token Eviction to preserve long-range information. Requires no additional training or dataset-level statistics.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate HeadKV's effectiveness across multiple autoregressive image generation models, achieving high-quality generation while significantly reducing memory usage and improving throughput.

Conclusion: HeadKV provides a training-free, adaptive KV cache compression method for AR image generation by recognizing and exploiting the heterogeneity of attention heads, achieving better memory-performance trade-offs than fixed-budget approaches.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) visual generation has achieved remarkable performance but suffers from high memory usage and low throughput, as it requires caching previously generated visual tokens. Recent research has shown that retaining only a few lines of cache tokens can maintain high-quality images while significantly reducing memory usage and improving throughput. However, these methods allocate a fixed budget to each attention head, overlooking the heterogeneity among attention heads, leading to suboptimal memory allocation. In this paper, we observe that attention heads across different layers exhibit diverse attention patterns, where some heads focus on local neighborhoods while others capture broader contextual dependencies. Based on this insight, we propose a novel head-aware key-value (KV) cache compression framework for autoregressive image generation, called HeadKV, which assigns smaller budgets to locality-biased heads and larger budgets to heads with broader attention. A key challenge lies in identifying the type of each attention head to guide cache compression. We further observe that, within the same layer, each head exhibits consistent attention patterns across token positions, \emph{i.e.}, a head's behavior for early tokens remains consistent with that for later tokens. This insight suggests that head types can be identified during the early stage and reused for KV compression throughout generation. Its advantage is that it requires no additional training or dataset-level statistics and generalizes seamlessly across different inputs. Moreover, we design a Stratified Token Eviction strategy to effectively preserve long-range information. Extensive experiments demonstrate its effectiveness across multiple autoregressive image generation models.

</details>


### [45] [Mind Your Margin and Boundary: Are Your Distilled Datasets Truly Robust?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20606)
*Muquan Li,Yingyi Ma,Yihong Huang,Hang Gou,Ke Qin,Ming Li,Yuan-Fang Li,Tao He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: C$^2$R is a robust dataset distillation framework that uses attack-aware curriculum learning and contrastive robustness to achieve better clean-robust accuracy trade-offs, outperforming prior robust DD methods by 2.8% on average across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robust dataset distillation methods suffer from poor accuracy-robustness trade-offs because they treat all adversarial perturbations uniformly (ignoring that robust risk is dominated by near-zero margins) and fail to explicitly increase inter-class separation at decision boundaries where attacks concentrate.

Method: C$^2$R derives a perturbation score from robust-margin theory to approximate each sample's robust hinge, enabling an attack-aware curriculum that prioritizes smallest-margin adversaries. It also introduces a class-balanced contrastive robustness loss that enforces adversarial invariance while widening boundary separation across classes.

Result: Experiments on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and multiple ImageNet-1K subsets under six attacks show C$^2$R achieves the best robust accuracy, outperforming prior robust DD methods by 2.8% on average.

Conclusion: C$^2$R effectively addresses the accuracy-robustness trade-off in robust dataset distillation through curriculum learning and contrastive objectives, setting a new state-of-the-art.

Abstract: Dataset distillation (DD) compresses a large training set into a small synthetic set for efficient training, but most DD methods optimize only clean accuracy and leave robustness uncontrolled. Recent robust DD methods improve robustness, yet they often suffer from a poor accuracy-robustness trade-off because they (i) treat all adversarially perturbed examples uniformly, despite robust risk being dominated by near-zero robust margins, and (ii) do not explicitly increase inter-class separation in the decision boundary where attacks concentrate. We present Contrastive Curriculum for Robust Dataset Distillation (C$^2$R), a framework that couples an attack-aware curriculum with a contrastive robustness objective. From a robust-margin perspective, we derive a perturbation score that approximates each sample's robust hinge, enabling a curriculum that prioritizes the smallest-margin adversaries that most directly drive robust error. In parallel, a class-balanced contrastive robustness loss enforces adversarial invariance while explicitly widening boundary separation across classes. Experiments on CIFAR-10/100, Tiny-ImageNet, and multiple ImageNet-1K subsets under six attacks show that C$^2$R achieves the best robust accuracy, outperforming prior robust DD by $2.8$% on average.

</details>


### [46] [Beyond Routing: Characterising Expert Tuning and Representation in Vision Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20610)
*Gene Tangtartharakul,Katherine R. Storrs*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper analyzes Mixture-of-Experts vision models and finds that expert specialization extends beyond simple category routing, revealing broader tuning to continuous visual and semantic dimensions through neuroscience-inspired analysis methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing interpretations of MoE models focus primarily on which categories are routed to which experts, but this does not reveal what experts actually encode internally. The authors aim to provide deeper, feature-level understanding of expert specialization.

Method: The authors train sparsely-gated convolutional MoE models with contrastive learning on natural images, then apply tools from visual neuroscience: measuring per-expert category separability, identifying most exciting inputs, analyzing tuning via semantic dimensions from THINGS dataset, and assessing stability across independent initializations using representational similarity analysis.

Result: An animate-inanimate distinction dominates expert partitioning and is stable across models. Experts show broader tuning to continuous visual and semantic dimensions beyond category boundaries, with similar category-separability despite distinct feature tuning.

Conclusion: Expert specialization in vision MoEs extends well beyond category routing and is better understood by probing fine-grained expert-level tuning and representational structure rather than relying solely on gating-level analyses.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models are often interpreted by analysing which categories are routed to which experts. However, routing alone does not reveal what each expert actually encodes. We train sparsely-gated convolutional MoE models with a contrastive objective on natural images and characterise expert specialisation using tools from visual neuroscience. Extending from gating-level to expert-level analyses, we measure per-expert category separability, and per-expert tuning using the most exciting inputs. Extending from category-level to feature-level explanations, we interpret tuning via semantic dimensions derived from a dataset of human behavioural judgements (THINGS). Finally, we use tuning and representational similarity analysis to assess the stability of expertise-allocation across independent initialisations. We find that an animate-inanimate distinction dominates expert partitioning, apparent from gating through to expert readout, and is stable across independently trained models. Although routing statistics suggest relatively sparse, categorical preferences, expert analyses reveal broader tuning to continuous visual and semantic dimensions that extend beyond category boundaries. Experts exhibit similar category-separability to one another, despite distinct feature tuning, demonstrating the explanatory benefits of moving beyond category-level analyses. Together, these results show that expert specialisation in vision MoEs extends well beyond category routing and is better understood by probing fine-grained expert-level tuning and representational structure.

</details>


### [47] [Accelerating Video Inverse Problem Solvers with Autoregressive Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20624)
*Taesung Kwon,Jonghyun Park,Hyungjin Chung,Jong Chul Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AVIS proposes an autoregressive video diffusion framework that reduces initial latency from 114s to 4s and increases throughput to 1.18 FPS for zero-shot video inverse problems, with an AVIS Flash variant reaching 5.91 FPS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion models for zero-shot video inverse problems suffer from high initial latency due to holistic video restoration and low throughput caused by multiple VAE passes for measurement consistency enforcement, hindering real-time deployment.

Method: AVIS leverages autoregressive video diffusion models for streaming restoration, initializes reverse diffusion with a measurement-consistent estimate to reduce sampling steps, and introduces AVIS Flash which enforces measurement consistency only on the first chunk for further acceleration.

Result: AVIS reduces initial latency from 114s to 4s, increases throughput from 0.71 to 1.18 FPS; AVIS Flash achieves 5.91 FPS on a single RTX 4090 GPU while maintaining competitive performance.

Conclusion: The autoregressive approach effectively addresses latency and throughput bottlenecks in video inverse problems, with AVIS Flash offering a favorable efficiency-performance trade-off toward real-time deployment.

Abstract: Diffusion models provide powerful priors for zero-shot video inverse problems, but their real-time deployment is hindered by two inefficiencies: high initial latency caused by holistic video restoration, and low throughput resulting from multiple VAE passes to enforce measurement consistency in pixel space. To overcome these limitations, we propose Autoregressive Video Inverse problem Solver (AVIS). The AVIS framework leverages autoregressive video diffusion models to restore videos in a streaming manner, naturally eliminating latency bottlenecks. Specifically, AVIS initializes reverse diffusion with a measurement-consistent estimate, reducing the required sampling steps. Compared to leading non-autoregressive solvers, AVIS drastically reduces initial latency from 114s to 4s and increases throughput from 0.71 to 1.18 FPS while achieving superior restoration quality. We further introduce a highly accelerated variant, dubbed AVIS Flash, that enforces measurement consistency solely on the first chunk. AVIS Flash substantially boosts throughput to 5.91 FPS on a single RTX 4090 GPU while maintaining competitive performance and achieving a favorable efficiency-performance trade-off, paving the way toward real-time deployment.

</details>


### [48] [Pareto-Enhanced Portrait Generation: Vision-Aligned Text Supervision for Alignment, Realism, and Aesthetics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20640)
*Yunlong Wang,Jinjin Shi,Wenbin Gao,Xuran Xu,Runyu Shi,Ying Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a feature supervision paradigm for Multimodal Diffusion Transformers that uses cross-modal alignment and multi-granularity aesthetic signals to simultaneously improve text-image alignment, photorealism, and human-perceived aesthetics in portrait generation, overcoming the trilemma faced by traditional Supervised Fine-Tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models face a trilemma in human portrait generation where text-image alignment, photorealism, and human-perceived aesthetics inhibit one another. Traditional SFT methods improve photorealism but cause overfitting, degrade pre-trained priors, and harm alignment or aesthetics.

Method: A feature supervision paradigm for MM-DiT that: (1) uses a lightweight cross-modal alignment mechanism to extract multi-granularity vision-aligned text representations from SigLIP 2 and applies supervision to the image branch during training with zero extra inference cost; (2) mines implicit multi-granularity aesthetic signals from pre-trained vision foundation models.

Result: The method pushes the Pareto frontier on MM-DiTs, achieving synergistic improvements across all three dimensions (text-image alignment, photorealism, human-perceived aesthetics) simultaneously.

Conclusion: The proposed feature supervision paradigm effectively breaks the trilemma in portrait generation by avoiding SFT-induced degradation while injecting vision-aligned text guidance and aesthetic optimization, achieving holistic performance gains.

Abstract: Text-to-image diffusion models often face a severe trilemma in human portrait generation: text-image alignment, photorealism, and human-perceived aesthetics inherently inhibit one another. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) is an effective method for enhancing the photorealism of image generation. However, it often leads to overfitting to the training dataset, corrupts pre-trained image priors, and degrades alignment or aesthetics. To break this bottleneck, we propose a feature supervision paradigm for Multimodal Diffusion Transformers (MM-DiT). Specifically, we introduce a lightweight cross-modal alignment mechanism that implicitly extracts multi-granularity vision-aligned text representations from SigLIP 2 and applies supervision to the image branch of MM-DiT during the training stage, with zero extra inference overhead. Our method injects vision-aligned text guidance while preserving the base model's original generalization, avoiding degradation caused by SFT. Furthermore, our method directly mines implicit multi-granularity aesthetic signals from pre-trained vision foundation models to optimize human-perceived aesthetics. Extensive experiments on MM-DiTs show that our method pushes the Pareto frontier and achieves synergistic improvements across text-image alignment, photorealism, and human-perceived aesthetics.

</details>


### [49] [Seeing Through Fog: Towards Fog-Invariant Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20645)
*Enqi Liu,Liyuan Pan,Zhi Gao,Lingzhi Li,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces FogAct (the first benchmark dataset for foggy action recognition) and FogNet (a two-stream CLIP model that learns fog-invariant semantic information from degraded videos), achieving competitive performance against SOTA approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing action recognition methods assume favorable weather and high-quality video inputs, but foggy conditions degrade visibility and contrast, making semantic cue extraction difficult. There is a lack of benchmark datasets and models designed specifically for foggy action recognition.

Method: The authors propose FogAct, a paired clean-foggy video dataset collected with a stereo camera system (10 scenes, 55 actions, ~10,000 clips). They also propose FogNet, a two-stream CLIP model that learns fog-invariant representations by leveraging guidance from clean videos to capture shared structural and motion cues between clean and foggy videos.

Result: Extensive experiments on FogAct and three other popular datasets show competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

Conclusion: FogNet effectively mitigates fog-induced degradation for action recognition by discovering fog-invariant semantic information, and FogAct serves as a valuable benchmark for future research in this domain.

Abstract: Foggy conditions are commonly encountered in real-world applications; however, existing action recognition approaches typically assume favorable weather and high-quality video inputs. On foggy days, unpredictable visibility degradation and reduced contrast obstruct the extraction of semantic cues, posing significant challenges for current action recognition methods. In this paper, we mitigate the issues faced in action recognition under foggy conditions by employing two strategies. First, we present FogAct, the first benchmark dataset for foggy action recognition, consisting of paired clean and foggy videos captured with a stereo camera system. The dataset spans 10 scenes and 55 action categories, comprising nearly 10,000 video clips. Second, we propose FogNet, a two-stream CLIP model that discovers fog-invariant semantic information hidden behind the degraded videos. FogNet learns robust representations of foggy videos with guidance from clean videos, effectively capturing shared structural and motion cues between clean and foggy videos. Extensive experiments on FogAct and three other popular datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance compared with state-of-the-art (SOTA) approaches. Our FogAct and FogNet are given in our project page.

</details>


### [50] [Gaze into the Details: Locality-Sensitive Enhancement for OCTA Retinal Vessel Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20651)
*Tuopusen Huang,Ding Ma,Xiangqian Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LSENet improves U-Net for OCTA vessel segmentation by introducing three modules (PIE, MFF, CRD) that address low local contrast, achieving SOTA with fewer parameters on three public datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing U-Net based OCTA segmentation methods focus on holistic representation and fail to handle low local contrast, causing vessel discontinuities and detail loss.

Method: Three innovations on U-Net: (1) Patch Information Enhance (PIE) replaces skip connections with patch-wise attention for vessel continuity; (2) Multiscale Feature Fusion (MFF) feeds multi-scale visual features to PIE; (3) Connectivity Refinement Decoder (CRD) refines multi-level features with large-kernel convolution to reduce fragmentation.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on OCTA-500, ROSE-1, and ROSSA datasets with fewer parameters than prior methods.

Conclusion: LSENet effectively addresses the low local contrast problem in OCTA vessel segmentation and offers an efficient, high-performance alternative to existing U-Net based approaches.

Abstract: Existing deep learning frameworks for Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA) vessel segmentation are largely derived from the U-Net architecture, which serves as the foundation for most current designs. However, most of these methods focus only on holistic representation, struggling to address the problem of low local contrast unique to OCTA, which leads to vessel discontinuities and loss of detail. To address these problems, we propose LSENet, which builds upon the U-Net architecture by introducing three core innovative modules: To address vessel discontinuities, we introduce the Patch Information Enhance module (PIE), which replaces standard skip connections to execute patch-wise attention. To mitigate detail loss, the Multiscale Feature Fusion module (MFF) is proposed to feed the PIE module rich, multi-scale information by extracting visually interpretable features from both the original input and preceding layers. Finally, the Connectivity Refinement Decoder (CRD) is designed to refine features from all levels and utilize a large kernel in the final convolutional layer to reduce fragmentation. Experiments on three public datasets (OCTA-500, ROSE-1, and ROSSA) demonstrate that our proposed LSENet achieves state-of-the-art performance while requiring fewer parameters.

</details>


### [51] [RoPeSLR: 3D RoPE-driven Sparse-LowRank Attention for Efficient Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20659)
*Yuxi Liu,Zekun Zhang,Yixiang Cai,Renjia Deng,Yutong He,Kun Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RoPeSLR tackles the quadratic attention bottleneck in video Diffusion Transformers by proposing a 3D RoPE-guided sparse-low-rank attention framework. It decouples attention into sparse high-frequency spikes and low-rank background, achieving sub-quadratic complexity with near-lossless generation quality (up to 10x FLOPs reduction, 2.26x inference speedup).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) achieve high-quality video generation but suffer from O(L²) attention complexity, making long-sequence synthesis prohibitively expensive. Existing sparse-linear attention hybrids fail under extreme sparsity due to the "RoPE Dilemma"—standard linear attention cannot preserve the orthogonal relative-position structure of 3D RoPE, losing critical distance awareness.
