Method: Proposes RoPeSLR: (1) Empirically validates that DiT attention manifolds decouple into high-frequency semantic spike sets (O(L^{3/2}) sparsity) and extreme low-rank background continua (O(d_h log L)); (2) Replaces standard linear attention with head-wise low-rank parameterization + learnable 3D Absolute Positional Embedding injection to synthesize relative distance decay; (3) Guarantees sub-quadratic sparsity and sub-linear rank growth.

Result: At 90% sparsity: up to 10x FLOPs reduction on Wan2.1-1.3B, 2.26x end-to-end inference speedup on 100K+ token sequences (HunyuanVideo-13B), and under 1.3% average VBench degradation.

Conclusion: RoPeSLR effectively resolves the RoPE Dilemma in sparse-linear attention hybrids, enabling scalable ultra-long video generation with near-lossless fidelity.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have revolutionized high-fidelity video generation, yet their $\mathcal{O}(L^2)$ attention complexity poses a formidable bottleneck for long-sequence synthesis. While recent sparse-linear attention hybrids aim to mitigate this, their performance severely degrades at extreme sparsity due to the "RoPE Dilemma": standard linear attention fails to preserve the orthogonal relative-position structure of 3D Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE), neutralizing vital distance awareness. To address this, we propose \textbf{RoPeSLR}, a 3D RoPE-guided Sparse-LowRank attention framework. We establish that under empirically validated assumptions, the DiT attention manifold admits a decoupling into a high-frequency semantic spike set (bounded by $\mathcal{O}(L^{3/2})$ sparsity) and an extreme low-rank ($\mathcal{O}(d_h \log L)$) background continuum. Guided by this structural prior, RoPeSLR eschews standard linear attention for a head-wise low-rank parameterization equipped with a learnable 3D Absolute Positional Embedding (PE) injection, seamlessly synthesizing long-range relative distance decay. By guaranteeing sub-quadratic sparsity and sub-linear rank growth, RoPeSLR is exceptionally suited for scaling to ultra-long video inference. Extensive evaluations validate this scalable superiority: at 90\% sparsity, RoPeSLR achieves up to $10\times$ fewer FLOPs on Wan2.1-1.3B and delivers a $2.26\times$ end-to-end inference speedup on the ultra-long 100K+ token sequences of HunyuanVideo-13B, all while maintaining near-lossless generation fidelity (less than 1.3\% average VBench degradation).

</details>


### [52] [VSCD: Video-based Scene Change Detection in Unaligned Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20821)
*Jiae Yoon,Ue-Hwan Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces Video-based Scene Change Detection (VSCD), a new task for detecting pixel-wise changes between two unconstrained RGB videos of the same indoor space recorded at different times. The authors build a large-scale benchmark (1.1M+ frames) and propose a query-centric multi-reference model that achieves state-of-the-art results, validated on a real mobile robot for surveillance and incremental learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting environmental changes is essential for long-term robot autonomy, but existing change detection methods assume fixed viewpoints, mild misalignment, or few changed objects. The authors aim to tackle the more challenging and realistic scenario where both reference and query videos are captured under unconstrained camera motion and are not temporally synchronized.

Method: A query-centric multi-reference model that: (1) learns temporal matching implicitly from change-mask supervision, (2) aligns candidate reference features to the query via local patch correspondence, and (3) fuses per-candidate change features using frame-level and patch-level confidence before decoding a high-resolution mask per frame.

Result: The proposed approach achieves state-of-the-art performance against strong image- and video-based baselines on the new benchmark. The method is validated on a real mobile robot for two downstream applications: visual surveillance and object incremental learning.

Conclusion: Video-based Scene Change Detection is a feasible and practical task for long-term autonomy. The proposed model and large-scale benchmark advance change detection under unconstrained video conditions, with demonstrated real-world robotic deployment.

Abstract: Detecting what has changed in an environment is essential for long-term autonomy, yet most change detection settings assume fixed viewpoints, mild misalignment, or only a few changed objects. We introduce Video-based Scene Change Detection (VSCD), which predicts a pixel-wise change mask for each query frame, given a reference and a query RGB video of the same indoor space recorded at different times under unconstrained camera motion. The two videos are not temporally synchronized, and many object instances may appear or disappear. To study this setting, we build a large-scale benchmark with over 1.1 million frames annotated with pixel-accurate change masks, together with a real-world test set for evaluating transfer beyond simulation. We propose a query-centric multi-reference model that learns temporal matching implicitly from change-mask supervision, aligns candidate reference features to the query via local patch correspondence, and fuses per-candidate change features using frame-level and patch-level confidence before decoding a high-resolution mask once per frame. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance against strong image- and video-based baselines, and we validate its real-world impact by deploying it on a mobile robot for two downstream applications -- visual surveillance and object incremental learning.

</details>


### [53] [LER-YOLO: Reliability-Aware Expert Routing for Misaligned RGB-Infrared UAV Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20667)
*Liming Hou,Yueping Peng,Hexiang Hao,Ji Wang,Xuekai Zhang,Wei Tang,Zecong Ye,Xin Ying,Yubo He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LER-YOLO proposes a reliability-aware sparse mixture-of-experts framework for misaligned RGB-infrared UAV detection, achieving 89.7% AP50 on the MBU benchmark by using uncertainty-aware target alignment and reliability-guided expert fusion to suppress cross-modal mismatch artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting small UAVs from RGB-infrared pairs is challenging due to tiny target scale, cluttered backgrounds, and spatial misalignment between heterogeneous sensors. Existing bimodal detectors align or fuse features without assessing the reliability of local cross-sensor correspondence, allowing mismatch artifacts to propagate into the detection head.

Method: LER-YOLO introduces (1) an Uncertainty-Aware Target Alignment module that resamples visible features toward the infrared reference and estimates a spatial reliability map, and (2) a Reliability-Guided Sparse MoE Fusion module that adaptively selects k experts from RGB-dominant, infrared-dominant, and interactive fusion experts based on the reliability prior, enabling trustworthy cross-modal interaction while suppressing unreliable fusion.

Result: On the MBU benchmark under YOLOv5s-family protocol, LER-YOLO achieves 89.7±0.2% AP50 over three independent seeds, with a best result of 89.9%. Gains are attributed to reliability-guided expert routing rather than increased model capacity.

Conclusion: The reliability-aware sparse MoE approach effectively addresses the cross-modal misalignment problem in RGB-infrared UAV detection, with extensive ablations, parameter-matched comparisons, synthetic-shift evaluations, and complexity analysis validating the design choices.

Abstract: Detecting small unmanned aerial vehicles from RGB-infrared remote-sensing pairs remains challenging due to tiny target scale, cluttered backgrounds, and spatial misalignment between heterogeneous sensors. Existing bimodal detectors often align or fuse features without assessing the reliability of local cross-sensor correspondence, allowing mismatch artifacts to propagate into the detection head. To address this issue, we propose LER-YOLO, a reliability-aware sparse mixture-of-experts framework for misaligned RGB-infrared UAV detection. LER-YOLO first introduces an Uncertainty-Aware Target Alignment module that resamples visible features toward the infrared reference and estimates a spatial reliability map. This reliability prior is then used by a Reliability-Guided Sparse MoE Fusion module to adaptively select k experts from RGB-dominant, infrared-dominant, and interactive fusion experts, enabling trustworthy cross-modal interaction while suppressing unreliable fusion. Experiments on the public MBU benchmark under a YOLOv5s-family protocol show that LER-YOLO achieves 89.7+/-0.2% AP50 over three independent seeds, with a best result of 89.9%. Extensive ablations, parameter-matched comparisons, synthetic-shift evaluations, and complexity analysis demonstrate that the gains mainly come from reliability-guided expert routing rather than increased model capacity.

</details>


### [54] [LiteViLNet: Lightweight Vision-LiDAR Fusion Network for Efficient Road Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21007)
*Daojie Peng,Bingtao Wang,Fulong Ma,Liang Zhang,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LiteViLNet is a lightweight multi-modal network (RGB + LiDAR) for road segmentation that achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among CNN-based methods (96.36% MaxF) with only 14.04M parameters, running at 163.79 FPS on RTX 4060 Ti and 22.18 FPS on Jetson Orin NX, making it suitable for real-time embedded deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-modal road segmentation methods rely on heavy transformer-based encoders that are too computationally expensive for real-time deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

Method: (1) Dual-stream lightweight encoder with depth-wise separable convolutions for efficient feature extraction from RGB and LiDAR; (2) Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Module (MSFM) for cross-modal interaction; (3) Large-kernel-bridge module to capture long-range dependencies with linear complexity.

Result: 14.04M parameters; 96.36% MaxF (best among CNN-based methods); 163.79 FPS on RTX 4060 Ti; 22.18 FPS on Jetson Orin NX; competitive with larger transformer-based models.

Conclusion: LiteViLNet achieves an excellent balance between accuracy and efficiency, validating its potential for real-time embedded deployment in autonomous driving and intelligent robotics.

Abstract: Road segmentation is a fundamental perception task for autonomous driving and intelligent robotic systems, requiring both high accuracy and real-time inference, especially for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. Existing multi-modal road segmentation methods often rely on heavy transformer-based encoders to achieve state-of-the-art performance, but their enormous computational cost prohibits real-time deployment on embedded platforms. To address this dilemma, we propose \textbf{LiteViLNet}, a lightweight multi-modal network that fuses RGB texture information and LiDAR geometric information for efficient road segmentation. Specifically, we design a dual-stream lightweight encoder and depth-wise separable convolutions to extract hierarchical features from both modalities with minimal parameters. We further propose a Multi-Scale Feature Fusion Module (MSFM) to facilitate cross-modal interaction at different levels, and a large-kernel-bridge module to capture long-range dependencies with linear complexity. Extensive experiments on the KITTI Road dataset and real-world applications demonstrate that LiteViLNet achieves a promising balance between accuracy and efficiency. Notably, with only 14.04M parameters, our model attains a 96.36\% MaxF score, ranking the best among all CNN-based methods and being comparable to larger transformer-based models, and runs at 163.79 FPS in model-only inference on RTX 4060 Ti (22.18 FPS on Jetson Orin NX). It outperforms numerous heavy-weight methods in inference speed while maintaining highly competitive accuracy, fully validating the potential of LiteViLNet for real-time embedded deployment in autonomous driving and intelligent robotics.

</details>


### [55] [GSA-YOLO: A High-Efficiency Framework via Structured Sparsity and Adaptive Knowledge Distillation for Real-Time X-ray Security Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20669)
*Jiahao Kong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GSA-YOLO is a lightweight detection framework built on YOLOv8n that integrates Group Lasso, Sparse Structure Selection, and Adaptive Knowledge Distillation to improve X-ray security inspection, achieving 189.62 FPS and 0.531-0.679 mAP50:95 with 2.4%-1.8% accuracy gains over baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing X-ray detection models struggle to balance severe occlusion, complex clutter, and strict real-time speed requirements.

Method: GSA-YOLO combines three components: Group Lasso (GL) for robust feature extraction in the neck, Sparse Structure Selection (SSS) for model slimming in the detection head, and Adaptive Knowledge Distillation (Ada-KD) for accuracy recovery.

Result: Achieves 189.62 FPS, reduces computational cost from 8.7G to 8.0G, and improves mAP50:95 by 2.4% (0.531) on HiXray and 1.8% (0.679) on PIDray over baseline.

Conclusion: GSA-YOLO effectively balances accuracy and efficiency, making it a promising solution for practical X-ray security inspection.

Abstract: X-ray security inspection requires accurate real-time detection of prohibited items, but existing models often struggle to balance the challenges of severe occlusion, complex clutter, and strict speed requirements. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes GSA-YOLO, a novel lightweight framework built upon the YOLOv8n architecture, specifically engineered to enhance detection robustness and inference efficiency. GSA-YOLO strategically integrates structured sparsity and adaptive knowledge transfer through three core components: Group Lasso (GL) applied to the network neck for robust feature extraction; Sparse Structure Selection (SSS) applied to the detection head for significant model slimming; and an Adaptive Knowledge Distillation (Ada-KD) mechanism for comprehensive accuracy recovery. This integrated approach synergistically enhances feature representation while pruning redundant channels, maximizing model efficiency without sacrificing performance. Rigorous evaluations on the HiXray and PIDray datasets confirm GSA-YOLO's comprehensive capability, achieving a leading inference speed of 189.62 FPS, accompanied by a reduction in computational cost from 8.7G to 8.0G. Crucially, GSA-YOLO secures mAP50:95 results of 0.531 and 0.679 on HiXray and PIDray, demonstrating 2.4% and 1.8% improvements over the baseline, respectively. Compared to other models, GSA-YOLO exhibits enhanced accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency, making it a promising solution for practical X-ray security inspection.

</details>


### [56] [Grounding Driving VLA via Inverse Kinematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21061)
*Junsung Park,Hyunjung Shim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Driving VLAs often ignore visual tokens due to a structurally ill-posed task formulation. By redesigning the system as an inverse kinematics solver — predicting future visual states and using a dedicated inverse kinematics network — a 0.5B model matches or outperforms 7B-8B VLAs on trajectory planning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Driving VLAs predict trajectories while largely ignoring visual tokens. The authors trace this not to insufficient training but to a structurally ill-posed task formulation that encourages shortcuts through ego status and text commands alone.

Method: 1) A next visual state prediction objective requiring the LLM to predict the future visual scene, providing dense visual supervision. 2) A separate Inverse Kinematics Network (cross-attention-based conditional diffusion model) that takes only current and future visual states as input, suppressing reliance on ego status and textual shortcuts during trajectory decoding.

Result: A 0.5B-scale model reaches trajectory planning performance comparable to 7B-8B VLAs on NAVSIM-v2 and nuScenes benchmarks. The improvement stems from recovered ability to exploit visual features, especially in dynamic situations like turning.

Conclusion: Reformulating Driving VLA as an inverse kinematics problem — with future visual state prediction and a dedicated inverse kinematics network — effectively restores visual grounding and achieves strong planning performance at a fraction of the model scale.

Abstract: Existing Driving VLAs predict trajectories while largely ignoring their visual tokens -- a phenomenon we trace not to insufficient training but to a structurally ill-posed task formulation. We show that trajectory recovery, when viewed through the lens of inverse kinematics, requires both a current and a future visual state as boundary conditions; existing VLAs supply only the former, which encourages the model to shortcut through ego status and text commands alone. To address this, we re-design Driving VLA in the style of an inverse kinematics solver. First, a next visual state prediction objective that requires the LLM to predict the future visual scene provides dense visual supervision and suppresses shortcut paths. Second, a separate Inverse Kinematics Network (a cross-attention-based conditional diffusion model) that takes only the current and future visual states as input is designed to suppress reliance on ego status and textual shortcuts during trajectory decoding. With this simple prescription alone, our 0.5B-scale model recovers visual grounding and reaches trajectory planning performance comparable to 7B--8B VLAs more than an order of magnitude larger, on both the closed-loop NAVSIM-v2 and the nuScenes benchmarks. Extensive analysis further shows that this improvement stems from a recovered ability to exploit visual features, with the effect being most pronounced in dynamic driving situations such as turning.

</details>


### [57] [VISTAQA: Benchmarking Joint Visual Question Answering and Pixel-Level Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20676)
*Mozhgan Nasr Azadani,Yimu Wang,Yongpeng Zhu,Lihong Chen,Milan Ganai,Sean Sedwards,Marco Pavone,Krzysztof Czarnecki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VISTAQA is a new benchmark (1,157 samples) and GROVE metric for jointly evaluating both answer correctness and pixel-level visual grounding in multimodal LLMs, showing that even top models struggle to align answers with supporting visual evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLM evaluations assess either text correctness or pixel localization separately, failing to enforce the alignment between model predictions and visual evidence, which is critical for transparency and reliability in multimodal reasoning.

Method: The authors curate VISTAQA with 1,157 expert-annotated samples across 6 task types and 6 visual domains, requiring both correct answers and precise segmentation masks. They propose GROVE, a unified metric combining textual accuracy and grounding quality via per-sample geometric mean.

Result: Experiments on grounding-aware models and hybrid MLLM pipelines show that even the strongest systems achieve limited GROVE performance, revealing a significant gap between answer accuracy and visual evidence alignment.

Conclusion: Joint evaluation of reasoning and grounding remains a substantial challenge; current models cannot reliably align their answers with supporting visual evidence, highlighting the need for more tightly coupled multimodal reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Establishing a clear link between model predictions and the visual evidence that supports them is critical for transparency and reliability in multimodal reasoning, yet current multimodal large language model (MLLM) evaluations do not explicitly enforce this alignment. Existing benchmarks assess either textual answer correctness or pixel-level localization in isolation, leaving the coupling of reasoning and grounding an open challenge. We introduce VISTAQA, a comprehensive benchmark for joint evaluation of free-form answer correctness and pixel-level evidence grounding in visual question answering. VISTAQA comprises 1,157 expert-curated samples spanning six task types and six visual domains, ranging from direct perception to compositional and relational reasoning. VISTAQA requires models to not only answer correctly, but to also provide precise segmentation masks that support their answers. It also includes hallucination-aware examples where no valid visual evidence exists. To support this enhanced evaluation, we introduce GROVE, a unified evaluation metric that enforces joint correctness by combining textual accuracy and grounding quality via a per-sample geometric mean, ensuring neither dimension can compensate for deficiencies in the other. Comprehensive experiments across grounding-aware models and hybrid pipelines with general-purpose MLLMs reveal that even the strongest systems achieve limited performance under GROVE, highlighting a substantial gap between answer accuracy and visual evidence alignment.

</details>


### [58] [Comparative Analysis of Military Detection Using Drone Imagery Across Multiple Visual Spectrums](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21157)
*Sourov Roy Shuvo,Prajwal Panth,Rajesh Chowdhury,Sorup Chakraborty,Sudip Chakrabarty,Prasant Kumar Pattnaik*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper extends the KIIT-MiTA drone imagery dataset with four environmental variants (Gray Scale, Thermal Vision, Night Vision, Obscura Vision) and uses YOLOv11-small to detect military objects under diverse real-world conditions, aiming to improve drone-based detection reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing datasets like KIIT-MiTA lack coverage of real-world environmental conditions (low visibility, nighttime, thermal scenarios), limiting the robustness of military object detection models in practical drone operations.

Method: Four simulated datasets (Gray Scale, Thermal Vision, Night Vision, Obscura Vision) are created from KIIT-MiTA; YOLOv11-small is trained and evaluated on these variants to test detection under diverse conditions.

Result: The paper demonstrates how YOLOv11-small performs across different environmental conditions, though specific metrics (e.g., mAP, precision, recall) are not detailed in the abstract.

Conclusion: The enhanced dataset and YOLOv11-based detection system contribute to more reliable drone-based military operations in both defensive and offensive missions.

Abstract: In modern warfare, drones are becoming an essential part of intelligence gathering and carrying out precise attacks in different kinds of hostile environments. Their ability to operate in real-time and hostile environments from a safe distance makes them invaluable for surveillance and military operations. The KIIT-MiTA dataset is comprised of images of different military scenarios taken from drones, and these provide a foundation for detecting military objects, but it does not take into account the various types of real-world scenarios. With that in mind, to evaluate how the models are performing under varying conditions, four different types of datasets are created: Gray Scale, Thermal Vision, Night Vision, and Obscura Vision. These simulate the real-world environments such as low visibility, heat-based imagery, and nighttime conditions. The YOLOv11-small model is trained and used to detect objects across diverse settings. This research boosts the performance and reliability of drone-based operations by contributing to the development of advanced detection systems in both defensive and offensive missions.

</details>


### [59] [DarkShake-DVS: Event-based Human Action Recognition under Low-light andShaking Camera Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20680)
*Jiaqi Chen,Qinfu Xu,Liyuan Pan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EIS-HAR proposes an Event-IMU stabilized framework for human action recognition under low-light and 6-DoF motion conditions, introducing the DarkShake-DVS benchmark with 18,041 real-world clips.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current HAR methods fail in low-light environments with 6-DoF camera motion due to degraded visual quality and temporal incoherence. There is no existing benchmark that integrates low-light conditions, 6-DoF motion, and synchronized IMU data, nor effective motion compensation techniques.

Method: EIS-HAR consists of two modules: (1) an Event-IMU Stabilization (EIS) module that reduces motion blur using a non-linear warping function for motion-compensated input; (2) a HAR module with a four-stage hybrid architecture for spatiotemporal feature extraction. The DarkShake-DVS benchmark was created with 18,041 real-world clips.

Result: Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate consistent superiority of EIS-HAR over state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Event cameras paired with IMU data, combined with the proposed motion compensation and hybrid architecture, effectively address HAR under challenging low-light and unconstrained motion conditions.

Abstract: Human Action Recognition (HAR) is a fundamental computer vision task with diverse real-world applications. Practical deployments often involve low-light environments and unconstrained 6-DoF camera motion, conditions that degrade visual quality, disrupt temporal coherence, and compromise reliability of existing methods. Event cameras, with high low-light sensitivity and microsecond-level temporal resolution, paired with an inertial measurement unit (IMU), present a promising solution. However, current research faces two key challenges: absence of a benchmark integrating low-light conditions, 6-DoF motion, and synchronized IMU data; and lack of effective motion compensation techniques. To address these, we propose Event-IMU Stabilized HAR (EIS-HAR), with two modules. The first is an EIS module that reduces motion blur via a non-linear warping function to reconstruct a motion-compensated input. The second is a HAR module with a four-stage hybrid architecture to efficiently extract spatiotemporal features for accurate action recognition. To alleviate data scarcity, we introduce DarkShake-DVS, the first large-scale event-based HAR benchmark that includes 18,041 realworld clips captured in low light and intense 6-DoF motion, supplemented by synchronized IMU data. Extensive experiments on three datasets demonstrate consistent superiority of EIS-HAR over state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [60] [Hyper-V2X: Hypernetworks for Estimating Epistemic and Aleatoric Uncertainty in Cooperative Bird's-Eye-View Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21309)
*Abhishek Dinkar Jagtap,Sanath Tiptur Sadashivaiah,Andreas Festag*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Hyper-V2X introduces a hypernetwork-based framework for uncertainty quantification (both epistemic and aleatoric) in V2X cooperative perception, enabling stochastic BEV segmentation with minimal computational overhead and architecture-agnostic integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty quantification in V2X-based cooperative perception remains largely unexplored, despite advances in multi-agent fusion for autonomous driving safety.

Method: Proposes a Bayesian hypernetwork with partial weight generation and V2X context embedding module, conditioned on fused multi-agent features to generate weight distributions for stochastic BEV segmentation.

Result: On the OPV2V benchmark, Hyper-V2X provides accurate, well-calibrated uncertainty estimates and improves overall perception reliability with little computation overhead.

Conclusion: Hyper-V2X is an architecture-agnostic framework that can seamlessly integrate with modern cooperative backbones like CoBEVT, enabling efficient uncertainty estimation for safer autonomous driving.

Abstract: Cooperative perception enabled by Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication enhances autonomous driving safety by creating a unified environmental representation through shared sensory data. While recent works have advanced multi-agent fusion for improved perception, uncertainty quantification in such cooperative frameworks remains largely unexplored. This paper introduces Hyper-V2X, a hypernetwork-based framework for estimating both epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties in V2X-based perception. Specifically, we propose a partial weight generation scheme and V2X context embedding module that conditions a Bayesian hypernetwork on fused multi-agent features to generate weight distributions for stochastic Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) segmentation. Unlike existing deterministic BEV models, Hyper-V2X enables efficient uncertainty estimation with little computation overhead. Our approach is architecture-agnostic, and can be seamlessly integrating with modern cooperative backbones such as CoBEVT. Experiments on the OPV2V benchmark demonstrate that Hyper-V2X provides accurate, well-calibrated uncertainty estimates and improves overall perception reliability. Our code and benchmark are publicly available under an open-source license: https://github.com/abhishekjagtap1/Hyper-V2X

</details>


### [61] [IndusAgent: Reinforcing Open-Vocabulary Industrial Anomaly Detection with Agentic Tools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20682)
*Rongbin Tan,Fangfang Lin,Zhenlong Yuan,Min Qiu,Kejin Cui,Mengmeng Wang,Yi Wang,Zijian Song,Zhiyuan Wang,Jiyuan Wang,Yue Wang,Shuhan Song§,Huawei Cao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: IndusAgent is a tool-augmented agentic framework for open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection that uses a structured dataset (Indus-CoT), dynamic tools, and gated reinforcement learning to achieve state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs struggle in open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection due to domain-misaligned reasoning and hallucinated structural inferences.

Method: (1) Indus-CoT dataset with global visual observations, high-res local patches, and expert normalcy priors; (2) IndusAgent framework orchestrating external tools (dynamic region cropping, feature enhancement, prior retrieval); (3) Gated reinforcement learning optimizing classification, localization, reasoning, and tool usage efficiency.

Result: State-of-the-art zero-shot performance on MVTec-AD, VisA, MPDD, DTD, and SDD benchmarks.

Conclusion: IndusAgent demonstrates robust generalization and superior zero-shot anomaly detection by combining structured training data, external tool orchestration, and reinforcement learning.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown remarkable capability in bridging visual perception and textual reasoning, enabling zero-shot understanding across diverse industrial scenarios. However, their performance in open-vocabulary industrial anomaly detection (IAD) is often limited by domain-misaligned reasoning and hallucinated structural inferences. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{IndusAgent}, a tool-augmented agentic framework for open-vocabulary IAD. Specifically, we first construct \textbf{Indus-CoT}, a structured dataset that integrates global visual observations, high-resolution local patches, and expert normalcy priors, providing supervision for fine-tuning the model on rigorous industrial inspection trajectories. Building on this, IndusAgent dynamically orchestrates a set of external tools, including dynamic region cropping, high-frequency feature enhancement, and prior retrieval, thus enabling the agent to actively resolve visual ambiguities and disentangle subtle anomalies. Furthermore, we introduce a gated reinforcement learning objective that jointly optimizes anomaly classification, localization accuracy, anomaly type reasoning, and efficient tool usage, ensuring that tool invocation occurs only when beneficial. Extensive evaluations on five industrial anomaly benchmarks, including MVTec-AD, VisA, MPDD, DTD, and SDD, demonstrate that IndusAgent achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance among all existing methods, validating our robustness and generalization capacity.

</details>


### [62] [Closed Loop Dynamic Driving Data Mixture for Real-Synthetic Co-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21372)
*Hongzhi Ruan,Pei Liu,Weiliang Ma,Zhengning Li,Xueyang Zhang,Jun Ma,Dan Xu,Kun Zhan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AutoScale is a closed-loop data engine that optimizes training data mixtures for end-to-end autonomous driving by dynamically adjusting scene types and quantities using graph-based scene representations, cluster-wise gradient ascent for importance estimation, and guided retrieval, achieving better performance with fewer synthetic samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world driving data is expensive to annotate and scene-biased. While real-synthetic co-training is promising, naively incorporating all synthetic data is inefficient and causes distribution shifts. Optimizing data mixtures under practical training budgets is under-explored.

Method: Proposes AutoScale: a closed-loop data engine with (1) Graph Regularized AutoEncoder (Graph-RAE) for scene representation, (2) Cluster-aware Gradient Ascent (Cluster-GA) for cluster-wise importance estimation and reweighting, and (3) cluster-guided vector retrieval to select high-value samples.

Result: On NavSim, AutoScale outperforms vanilla co-training and cross-domain baselines, achieving better performance with fewer synthetic samples under constrained budgets.

Conclusion: Data mixture requires clear guidance in terms of scene types and quantities; the proposed dynamic optimization approach effectively improves training efficiency for end-to-end autonomous driving.

Abstract: Data scaling is fundamental to modern deep learning, and grows increasingly critical as autonomous driving shifts to end-to-end learning. Real-world driving data is expensive to annotate and scene-biased, making real-synthetic co-training with near-infinite synthetic data a promising direction. However, naively incorporating all available synthetic data is inefficient and leads to distribution shifts, and optimizing data mixture under practical training budgets remains a critical yet under-explored problem. In this sense, we claim that the mixture of training data requires clear guidance in terms of scene types and quantities. Particularly in this work, we conceptualize the data mixture approximately as a dynamic optimization process that iteratively adjusts the training data mixture to maximize model performance, guided by closed-loop evaluation feedback, and propose AutoScale, a fully automated closed-loop data engine unifying scene representation, data mixture optimization and retrieval, as well as model training and evaluation. Specifically, we propose Graph Regularized AutoEncoder (Graph-RAE) for driving scene representations, introduce Cluster-aware Gradient Ascent (Cluster-GA) for cluster-wise importance estimation and reweighting, and perform cluster-guided vector retrieval to select high-value samples. Experiments on NavSim demonstrate that AutoScale outperforms vanilla co-training and cross-domain baselines, achieving better performance with fewer synthetic samples under constrained budgets.

</details>


### [63] [Rethinking Cross-Layer Information Routing in Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20708)
*Chao Xu,Maohua Li,Qirui Li,Yixuan Xu,Yanke Zhou,Yunhe Li,Cuifeng Shen,Hanlin Tang,Kan Liu,Tao Lan,Lin Qu,Shao-Qun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper diagnoses three problems with residual connections in Diffusion Transformers (monotonic forward magnitude inflation, sharp backward gradient decay, and block-wise redundancy) and proposes DAR, a learnable, timestep-adaptive residual replacement that improves FID by 2.11 on ImageNet and achieves 8.75× training speedup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While nearly every design axis of Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) has been revisited, the residual stream — governing cross-layer information flow — remains inherited from the original Transformer without systematic analysis or improvement.

Method: The authors first diagnose three symptoms of traditional residual addition in DiTs. They then propose Diffusion-Adaptive Routing (DAR), a drop-in replacement that performs learnable, timestep-adaptive, non-incremental aggregation over sublayer output history.

Result: DAR improves SiT-XL/2 by 2.11 FID (7.56 vs 9.67), matches converged quality with 8.75× fewer iterations, achieves 2× training acceleration with REPA, and preserves high-frequency details during Distribution Matching Distillation fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Cross-layer information routing is an underexplored yet orthogonal design axis in diffusion modeling, operating separately from representation-alignment objectives like REPA and applicable to both pretraining and fine-tuning stages.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) have become a de facto backbone of modern visual generation, and nearly every major axis of their design -- tokenization, attention, conditioning, objectives, and latent autoencoders -- has been extensively revisited. The residual stream that governs how information accumulates across layers, however, has been directly inherited from the original Transformer. In this paper, we present a systematic empirical analysis of cross-layer information flow in DiTs, jointly along depth and denoising timestep, and identify three concrete symptoms of traditional residual addition, namely monotonic forward magnitude inflation, sharp backward gradient decay, and pronounced block-wise redundancy. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose Diffusion-Adaptive Routing (\textsc{DAR}), a drop-in residual replacement that performs \emph{learnable, timestep-adaptive, and non-incremental} aggregation over the history of sublayer outputs. Moreover, the proposed \textsc{DAR} is compatible with many modern Transformer enhancement methods, such as REPA. On ImageNet $256\times256$, \textsc{DAR} improves SiT-XL/2 by $2.11$ FID ($7.56$ vs.\ $9.67$) and matches the baseline's converged quality with $8.75\times$ fewer training iterations. Stacked on top of REPA, it yields a $2\times$ training acceleration in the early stage, suggesting cross-layer information routing as an underexplored design axis in diffusion modeling, one that operates orthogonally to existing representation-alignment objectives. Beyond pretraining, \textsc{DAR} can also be applied during the fine-tuning stage of large-scale T2I models and preserves high-frequency details during Distribution Matching Distillation.

</details>


### [64] [SAVER: Selective As-Needed Vision Evidence for Multimodal Information Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20713)
*Miaobo Hu,Shuhao Hu,Bokun Wang,Rui Chen,Xin Wang,Xiaobo Guo,Daren Zha,Jun Xiao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAVER proposes a selective vision-as-needed framework for multimodal information extraction in social media, using conformal prediction to decide when to consult images and a submodular selector to choose a compact, relevant subset, achieving better efficiency and accuracy than always-on multimodal baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal IE in social media is challenging because multiple attached images can be weakly related, redundant, or misleading relative to text; always-on multimodal fusion wastes computation and amplifies spurious visual cues.

Method: (1) Conformal Groundability Gate (CGG) for span-level visual groundability estimation (MNER) and pair-level activation (MRE), with Clopper-Pearson threshold calibration. (2) Submodular relevance-diversity selector for compact evidence subset. (3) Set Transformer for aggregation. (4) Energy-inspired joint scoring head combining text, visual evidence, consistency, and sparse routing.

Result: SAVER consistently improves F1 over text-only and always-on multimodal baselines, while reducing AURC, increasing activation coverage at fixed risk, and lowering FLOPs and P90 latency.

Conclusion: Selectively activating vision only when needed and choosing a compact, diverse image subset yields better performance and efficiency than always-on multimodal fusion for social media IE.

Abstract: Multimodal IE in social media is difficult because a post may attach multiple images that are weakly related, redundant, or even misleading with respect to the text. In this setting, always-on multimodal fusion wastes computation and can amplify spurious visual cues. The core challenge is to decide, for each candidate span or marked entity pair, whether vision should be consulted at all and, if so, which small subset of images provides trustworthy evidence.
  We propose SAVER, a selective vision-as-needed framework for multimodal named entity recognition and multimodal relation extraction. SAVER uses a Conformal Groundability Gate (CGG) to estimate span-level visual groundability in MNER, derive pair-level activation in MRE from the two marked entities, and calibrate the activation threshold on a held-out split via a conformal-style procedure with Clopper--Pearson upper bounds. When activated, a submodular relevance--diversity selector chooses a compact evidence subset across images, which is then aggregated by a Set Transformer. An energy-inspired joint scoring head combines text, optional visual evidence, text--image consistency, and sparse routing for entity typing or relation classification.
  Experiments show that SAVER consistently improves F1 over strong text-only and always-on multimodal baselines, while reducing AURC, increasing activation coverage at a fixed risk level, and lowering FLOPs and P90 latency.

</details>


### [65] [Holistic Reliability Propagation: Decoupling Annotation and Prediction for Robust Noisy-Label](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20725)
*Jingyang Mao,Ningkang Peng,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HRP disentangles label and pseudo-label reliabilities via bilevel meta-learning, using reliability-aware Mixup and beta-gated contrastive learning to improve noisy-label multimedia classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: External annotations and model predictions can fail for different reasons, but existing methods combine them into a single reliability weight, losing valuable disentangled information.

Method: Bilevel meta-learning produces two batch-normalized scalars per sample (alpha for given label, beta for pseudo-label). HRP routes them via reliability-aware Mixup (global gating on input branch) and beta-gated pseudo-label positives (contrastive branch).

Result: HRP improves average accuracy over strong baselines on synthetic and real-world benchmarks and remains competitive at the highest noise rates.

Conclusion: Disentangling label and pseudo-label reliabilities is more effective than using a single combined weight for learning with noisy labels in multimedia classification.

Abstract: Learning with noisy labels in multimedia classification often combines external annotations and model predictions into a single reliability weight, even though the two sources can fail for different reasons. We instead estimate disentangled reliabilities: bilevel meta-learning produces two batch-normalized scalars per sample, alpha for the given label and beta for the pseudo-label, without constraining them to sum to one. Holistic Reliability Propagation (HRP) then routes them to different objectives, using reliability-aware Mixup with global gating on the input branch and beta-gated pseudo-label positives on the contrastive branch. On synthetic and real-world benchmarks, HRP improves average accuracy over strong baselines and remains competitive at the highest noise rates.

</details>


### [66] [GAMR: Geometric-Aware Manifold Regularization with Virtual Outlier Synthesis for Learning with Noisy Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20727)
*Ningkang Peng,Jingyang Mao,Xiaoqian Peng,Peirong Ma,Xichen Yang,Weiguang Qu,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Geometry-aware Manifold Regularization Paradigm that actively reshapes feature space geometry by synthesizing virtual outlier samples to build energy barriers between data manifolds, enhancing robustness against noisy labels and achieving SOTA performance on benchmarks like CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods passively filter clean samples in noise-degraded feature spaces, struggling to distinguish challenging samples from noisy samples. The paper highlights for the first time the importance of actively reshaping feature space geometry for learning from noisy data.

Method: A Geometry-aware Manifold Regularization Paradigm that explicitly constructs energy barriers between data manifolds by actively synthesizing virtual outlier samples, imposing geometric constraints for intra-class compactness and inter-class separation.

Result: Surpasses current SOTA methods on multiple benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10), particularly under challenging asymmetric noise conditions. Also significantly enhances Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection capability.

Conclusion: The proposed regularization mechanism is universal, independent of noise pattern assumptions, and can be integrated as a standalone module into existing sample selection frameworks for improved robustness in diverse noisy environments and open-world deployment.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) experience significant performance degradation when processing noisy labels, primarily due to overfitting on mislabeled data. Current mainstream approaches attempt to mitigate this issue by passively filtering clean samples during training. However, simple sample filtering within feature spaces degraded by noise struggles to distinguish between challenging samples and noisy samples, creating a bottleneck for model performance. We highlight for the first time the fundamental importance of actively reshaping feature space geometry for learning from noisy data. We propose a novel Geometry-aware Manifold Regularization Paradigm whose core idea is to explicitly construct energy barriers between data manifolds by actively synthesizing virtual outlier samples. By imposing geometric constraints that promote intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, this approach enhances the discriminability between hard and noisy samples, leading to the learning of more robust representations. Our regularization mechanism exhibits high universality, with effectiveness independent of any prior assumptions about noise patterns. It can be integrated as a standalone mechanism into existing sample selection frameworks, providing stronger robustness against diverse noisy environments. Experiments demonstrate that our paradigm achieves performance surpassing current state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on multiple benchmarks, including CIFAR-10, with particularly pronounced advantages under more challenging asymmetric noise conditions. Furthermore, this paradigm significantly enhances the model's capability in Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection, ensuring superior reliability and safety for deployment in open-world scenarios.

</details>


### [67] [Early High-Frequency Injection for Geometry-Sensitive OOD Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20728)
*Chuanjie Cheng,Ningkang Peng,Chenxi Liu,Yifan He,Peirong Ma,Yanhui Gu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EIHF is an input-side intervention that exposes high-frequency evidence before the first convolution to improve post-hoc OOD detection without changing the training objective, achieving strong results on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 but with limitations on scene-centric shifts like Places.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-hoc OOD detectors rely on geometry encoded in representations. The paper questions this by analyzing band-wise MMD^2 across various training methods, finding that low-frequency bands yield weaker ID/OOD separation while high-frequency bands provide stronger separability.

Method: The paper proposes EIHF (Expose Input High-Frequency), an input-side intervention that amplifies high-frequency information before the first convolution layer, without modifying the training objective. It also uses a band-wise MMD^2 diagnostic across CE, SimCLR, SupCon, and PALM.

Result: EIHF reshapes class-conditional feature geometry and reduces ID/OOD Mahalanobis score overlap. It achieves gains on CIFAR-100 and the best average FPR95 with second-best average AUROC on ImageNet-100. A limitation is observed on the scene-centric Places shift.

Conclusion: High-frequency information plays a crucial role in improving geometry-sensitive OOD detection. EIHF is an effective, training-objective-agnostic intervention, though its effectiveness may vary across different data distribution shifts (e.g., scene-centric vs. object-centric).

Abstract: Post-hoc OOD detectors score logits or features after training, so their success depends on the geometry already encoded in the representation. We revisit this assumption through a band-wise MMD^2 analysis across CE, SimCLR, SupCon, and the OOD-oriented representation method PALM. In our diagnostic, low-frequency input bands induce weaker ID/OOD feature discrepancy, whereas higher-frequency bands tend to provide stronger separability. This observation motivates EIHF, an input-side intervention that exposes high-frequency evidence before the first convolution without changing the training objective. EIHF is strongest for geometry-sensitive OOD detection: under matched training and scoring settings, it reshapes class-conditional feature geometry and reduces ID/OOD Mahalanobis score overlap. Experiments on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet-100 show gains on CIFAR-100 and the best average FPR95 with second-best average AUROC on ImageNet-100, while also revealing a limitation on the scene-centric Places shift. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/EIHF.

</details>


### [68] [TASTE: A Designer-Annotated Multi-Dimensional Preference Dataset for AI-Generated Graphic Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20731)
*Haonan Zhu,Elad Hirsch,Alexandria Minetti,Allison Nulty,Purvanshi Mehta*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TASTE is a new dataset with 10 professional designers rating text-to-image model outputs across 9 graphic design criteria. The paper shows designer agreement is significant but moderate, existing auto-evaluation systems underperform, and a simple trained head can close half the gap to human-level evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-to-image evaluation relies on photo-style preference data with a single overall verdict, but graphic designers evaluate along distinct axes (typography, hierarchy, color, layout, brief fidelity). A single label collapses these dimensions, motivating the need for a fine-grained, design-specific evaluation dataset and benchmark.

Method: The authors curate TASTE with 10 designers rating outputs from 4 models on 9 criteria across 2 cohorts (1,600 ratings per criterion + hallucination flags). They propose a criterion-agnostic signal test (Kendall's tau, majority probability, Condorcet cycles), benchmark 9 auto-evaluation systems (VLMs 3B-33B + 3 T2I scorers), and train a small pairwise-difference head.

Result: Designer agreement on graphic design sits between food/movie preferences and photo-style image quality; all TASTE criteria reject random-rater null. No pre-trained system exceeds 0.55 macro agreement with 5-designer majority; VLM scaling trades position bias vs. content sensitivity without accuracy gain. A small trained head achieves 0.611, halving the gap to the 0.741 human ceiling.

Conclusion: Graphic design evaluation requires dedicated multi-criteria benchmarks like TASTE, as existing systems fail at fine-grained design assessment. A lightweight trained approach can meaningfully close the human-machine gap, suggesting that current models lack design-specific perceptual understanding rather than scale.

Abstract: Text-to-image models produce graphic design at production scale, but their supervision comes from photo-style preference data with a single overall verdict per comparison. Designers evaluate along several distinct axes, including typography, visual hierarchy, color harmony, layout, and brief fidelity, and a single label collapses them. We release TASTE (Typography, Aesthetics, Spatial, Tone, Etc.): ten professional designers ranked outputs from four current text-to-image models on nine criteria across two disjoint cohorts, yielding 1,600 ratings per criterion plus per-image hallucination flags on the holistic-preference cohorts. We pair the dataset with three contributions. First, a criterion-agnostic signal test framework, using Kendall's tau, majority probability, and Condorcet cycles against exact iid-uniform nulls at p = 4 and R = 5, places designer agreement on graphic design between food and movie preferences and photo-style image quality, with every TASTE criterion rejecting the random-rater null. Second, no pre-trained system in our benchmark, including six open-weight VLM judges from 3B to 33B parameters and three dedicated T2I scorers, HPSv2.1, PickScore-v1, and LAION-Aesthetic-V2, exceeds 0.55 macro agreement with the 5-designer majority; VLM judges trade off position bias against content sensitivity, so scaling moves along this frontier without improving accuracy. Third, a small pairwise-difference head trained on TASTE reaches 0.611, closing roughly half the gap to the 0.741 single-rater ceiling.

</details>


### [69] [Deep Attention Reweighting: Post-Hoc Attention-Based Feature Aggregation in CNNs for Disentangling Core and Spurious Features under Spurious Correlations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20732)
*Kin Whye Chew,Jingxian Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DFR struggles with spurious correlations due to GAP-induced feature entanglement. DAR replaces GAP with an attention-based aggregation module, trained jointly with the classifier head, to selectively suppress spurious features, outperforming DFR across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CNNs exploit spurious correlations, leading to poor generalization and fairness. DFR is limited because it operates on entangled features caused by Global Average Pooling (GAP), which collapses spatially distinct core and spurious features indiscriminately.

Method: Proposes Deep Attention Reweighting (DAR), a post-hoc attention-based aggregation module that replaces GAP and is retrained jointly with the classification head to compute adaptive spatial weighting, selectively suppressing spurious features before pooling.

Result: DAR consistently outperforms DFR across various datasets, metrics, and ablations, demonstrating effective mitigation of GAP-induced entanglement and reduced spurious reliance.

Conclusion: Attention-based aggregation (DAR) effectively addresses the entanglement problem caused by GAP, improving post-hoc debiasing performance over DFR.

Abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) often exploit spurious correlations in datasets, learning superficially predictive yet causally irrelevant features, leading to poor generalization and fairness issues. Deep Feature Reweighting (DFR) is a post-hoc technique that reduces a trained model's reliance on spurious correlations by retraining its classification head on a target dataset. However, we show that DFR is fundamentally constrained by operating on entangled features, limiting its ability to amplify the core features while simultaneously suppressing the spurious ones. We trace this entanglement to the ubiquitous Global Average Pooling (GAP) layer, which indiscriminately collapses spatially distinct core and spurious features into a single representation. To address this, we propose Deep Attention Reweighting (DAR), a post-hoc attention-based aggregation module that replaces GAP and is retrained jointly with the classification head. DAR computes an adaptive weighting of spatial locations across feature maps, enabling selective suppression of spurious features before the collapse into entangled features. Across various datasets, metrics, and ablations, DAR consistently outperforms DFR, demonstrating that our attention-based aggregation mitigates GAP-induced entanglement and reduces spurious reliance.

</details>


### [70] [Sketch2MinSurf: Vision-Language Guided Generation of Editable Minimal Surfaces from Hand-Drawn Sketches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20733)
*Wenda Wang,Anqi Liu,Junqi Yang,Lei He,Luying Wang,Jiachen Lu,Weixin Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sketch2MinSurf integrates vision-language guidance with minimal-surface theory to convert hand-drawn sketches into editable, topologically consistent 3D surfaces, achieving 0.844 topological similarity score on 100 sketches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative models (GANs, NeRFs, diffusion) struggle to produce editable, topologically consistent 3D manifolds from hand-drawn sketches, limiting their use in downstream design workflows.

Method: A hybrid vision-language and geometric optimization framework using spatial-topological encoding (node coordinates + real/virtual edge skeletons) and a reward-modulated S2MS-Loss that jointly constrains geometric reconstruction and topological coherence.

Result: Topological similarity score of 0.844 on 100 sketches, outperforming existing baselines; generates directly editable, non-manifold-artifact-free surfaces; demonstrated via a public art installation.

Conclusion: Sketch2MinSurf effectively bridges sketch-based design and functional 3D geometry generation, enabling human-intent-driven form creation with topological guarantees.

Abstract: Converting hand-drawn sketches into structured 3D geometries remains challenging due to the difficulty of representing non-Euclidean surfaces and maintaining topological consistency. Existing generative models such as GANs, NeRFs, and diffusion architectures often fail to produce editable manifolds directly usable in downstream design workflows. We present Sketch2MinSurf, a hybrid vision-language and geometric optimization framework that integrates vision-language guidance with minimal-surface theory to generate smooth and editable 3D surfaces from hand-drawn sketches. The core of our approach is a spatial-topological encoding that represents geometry as tuples of node coordinates and real/virtual edge skeletons, enabling stable topological control during generation. We further introduce the Sketch2MinSurf Structural Loss (S2MS-Loss), a reward-modulated objective that jointly constrains geometric reconstruction and topological coherence. On a test set of 100 sketches, Sketch2MinSurf achieves a topological similarity score of 0.844, outperforming existing sketch-to-shape baselines. The generated manifolds are directly editable and free from non-manifold artifacts. A public art installation at a university showcases the method's potential for human-intent-driven 3D form generation. The dataset and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Sketch2MinSurf/.

</details>


### [71] [Lowering the Barrier to IREX Participation: Open-Source Algorithms, Toolkit, and Benchmarking for Iris Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20735)
*Siamul Karim Khan,Patrick J. Flynn,Adam Czajka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents two new open-source iris recognition algorithms (TripletIris and ArcIris), along with IREX-compliant C++ implementations of existing methods (HDBIF, CRYPTS), and provides open-source models for iris segmentation and circle estimation. The methods are evaluated under official IREX X protocols and multiple academic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To conduct the first-ever assessment of open-source iris recognition solutions using official IREX testing protocols, and to lower the barrier for other teams to submit open-source methods to IREX evaluation by providing a model C++ submission.

Method: Two neural networks: (i) TripletIris — trained with Triplet loss and Batch-Hard Triplet mining, (ii) ArcIris — trained with ArcFace loss. Also provides IREX-compliant C++ implementations of HDBIF (human saliency-driven filtering) and CRYPTS (Fuchs' crypts detection). Includes open-source iris segmentation and circle estimation models.

Result: Methods (except CRYPTS due to timing issues) passed official IREX X evaluation and were tested on multiple academic benchmarks including Quality-Face/Iris Research Ensemble, Warsaw-Biobase Post-Mortem Iris, CASIA-Iris-Thousand-V4, CASIA-Iris-Lamp-V4, IIT Delhi, IIITD Contact Lens, NDIris3D, and ND Iris Quality Release 2.

Conclusion: The paper provides a comprehensive open-source iris recognition framework with IREX-compliant implementations, enabling reproducible research and lowering entry barriers for participation in standardized iris recognition evaluations.

Abstract: This paper proposes two new open-source iris recognition algorithms, providing both Python and IREX-compliant C++ implementations to be submitted to the official IREX X program. This work has two primary goals: (a) to conduct the first-ever assessment of open-source iris recognition solutions according to IREX testing protocols, and (b) to offer a model C++ submission that significantly facilitates the entry of other teams' open-source methods into the IREX evaluation. The new methods consist of two Neural Networks trained with: (i) Triplet loss with Batch-Hard Triplet mining (TripletIris), and (ii) ArcFace loss (ArcIris). The paper also provides open-source IREX-compliant C++ implementations of two existing methods: (a) an iris image filtering-based algorithm utilizing human saliency-driven kernels (HDBIF), and (b) a human-interpretable algorithm for detecting and comparing Fuchs' crypts (CRYPTS). Except for CRYPTS, which faced timing constraints during 1:N search, these methods have undergone the official IREX X evaluation and have also been assessed using several popular academic benchmarks: Quality-Face/Iris Research Ensemble, Warsaw-Biobase Post-Mortem Iris, CASIA-Iris-Thousand-V4, CASIA-Iris-Lamp-V4, IIT Delhi Iris Database, IIITD Contact Lens Iris Database, NDIris3D, and Notre Dame Variable Iris Image Quality Release 2. Finally, this paper also provides open-source models for iris segmentation and circle estimation that can be incorporated into any new iris recognition method.

</details>


### [72] [Resolving Long-Tail Ambiguity in Unsupervised 3D Point Cloud Segmentation with Language Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20737)
*Siqi Wei,Hongbin Xu,Feng Xiao,Tian Lan,Chun Li,Ming Li,Qiuxia Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LangTail introduces a language-guided hierarchical learning framework that uses balanced world knowledge from language models to mitigate long-tail class ambiguity in unsupervised 3D point cloud segmentation, achieving significant improvements over existing methods on multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unsupervised 3D segmentation methods rely on purely visual clustering, which causes long-tail ambiguity where minor classes get absorbed by dominant clusters, leading to severely imbalanced predictions.

Method: LangTail constructs entity-level semantic priors from language models and injects them into a hierarchical clustering framework via contrastive alignment, guiding multi-granularity semantic structure formation to protect minor classes from being absorbed by dominant clusters.

Result: Outperforms existing methods by +13.5, +12.9, and +8.9 mIoU on ScanNet-v2, S3DIS, and nuScenes, respectively.

Conclusion: Language priors are effective in improving representation of minority classes in 3D point clouds, and the code will be released.

Abstract: Existing approaches for unsupervised 3D point cloud segmentation predominantly rely on a purely visual similarity-based learning-by-clustering paradigm, which suffers from a fundamental limitation: long-tail ambiguity. In such a paradigm, features of minor classes are consistently absorbed by dominant clusters, leading to severely imbalanced predictions. To address this issue, we propose LangTail, a language-guided hierarchical learning framework that leverages the balanced world knowledge encoded in language models to mitigate long-tail ambiguity in unsupervised 3D segmentation. The key idea is to establish multi-level associations between language-derived semantic priors and visually underrepresented minor classes, thereby compensating for the biased attention of purely visual clustering toward dominant classes. Specifically, LangTail first constructs an entity-level semantic prior from language models, capturing balanced and fine-grained world knowledge across categories. These priors are injected into a hierarchical clustering framework via contrastive alignment. This guides multi-granularity semantic structure formation and prevents minor classes from being absorbed by dominant clusters, yielding more discriminative representations for underrepresented categories. Extensive experiments on ScanNet-v2, S3DIS, and nuScenes demonstrate that LangTail consistently outperforms existing methods by significant margins, \ie, +13.5, +12.9, and +8.9 mIoU, respectively. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of language priors in improving the representation of minority classes in 3D point clouds. The code will be released at: https://github.com/Whisky0129/langtail_official.

</details>


### [73] [STAR-IOD: Scale-decoupled Topology Alignment with Pseudo-label Refinement for Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20738)
*Yaoteng Zhang,Qing Zhou,Junyu Gao,Qi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STAR-IOD proposes a Subspace-decoupled Topology Distillation (STD) module and a Clustering-driven Pseudo-label Generator (CPG) to address intra-class scale variations and missing annotation issues in Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection, outperforming SOTA by 1.7-2.1% mAP on two new benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Remote sensing comes as continuous data streams, but traditional detectors suffer from catastrophic forgetting. Existing RS-IOD methods overlook intra-class scale variations and missing annotations for old classes, which degrades knowledge transfer and preservation.

Method: (1) Subspace-decoupled Topology Distillation (STD) — aligns inter-class topological relationships while reducing intra-class representation discrepancies from scale shifts. (2) Clustering-driven Pseudo-label Generator (CPG) — uses K-Means clustering to dynamically set class-specific thresholds, distinguishing true positives from background noise. (3) Two new benchmarks: DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD.

Result: Outperforms SOTA by 1.7% mAP on DIOR-IOD and 2.1% mAP on DOTA-IOD, effectively alleviating catastrophic forgetting on both base and novel classes.

Conclusion: STAR-IOD effectively tackles scale variation and missing annotation challenges in RS-IOD, with strong empirical results and publicly released code/datasets.

Abstract: Remote sensing imagery typically arrives in the form of continuous data streams. Traditional detectors often forget previously learned categories when learning new ones; therefore, research on Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection (RS-IOD) is of great significance. However, existing methods largely overlook the intra-class scale variations prevalent in remote sensing scenes, which undermines the effectiveness of knowledge transfer and old knowledge preservation. Moreover, RS-IOD also suffers from missing annotations, which cause the model to misclassify old-class instances as background. To address these challenges, we propose a novel framework, STAR-IOD. First, we introduce a Subspace-decoupled Topology Distillation (STD) module to transfer structural knowledge, explicitly aligning inter-class topological relationships and mitigating intra-class representation discrepancies induced by scale shifts. Furthermore, we introduce the Clustering-driven Pseudo-label Generator (CPG), a plug-and-play module that leverages K-Means clustering to dynamically identify class-specific thresholds, thereby guaranteeing an accurate distinction between true positive targets and background noise and alleviating the issue of missing annotations for old classes. We also constructed two Remote Sensing Incremental Object Detection datasets, DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD to facilitate research on RS-IOD. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches by 1.7% and 2.1% mAP on DIOR-IOD and DOTA-IOD, respectively, effectively alleviating catastrophic forgetting while preserving strong detection performance on both base and novel classes. The code and dataset are released at: https://github.com/zyt95579/STAR-IOD.

</details>


### [74] [Draw2Think: Harnessing Geometry Reasoning through Constraint Engine Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20743)
*Juncheng Hu,Jiawei Du,Xin Zhang,Joey Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Draw2Think is a framework that externalizes geometric reasoning by using a Propose-Draw-Verify loop with GeoGebra constraint engine, enabling verifiable, constraint-checked canvas interactions that achieve high construction fidelity and improved accuracy across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language models solve geometry problems but their intermediate reasoning remains latent and unverifiable, lacking exact, per-action geometric guarantees. Existing externalization methods based on pixels or one-shot scripts fail to provide constraint-checked verification.

Method: Draw2Think recasts geometric reasoning as agentic interaction with the GeoGebra constraint engine via a Propose-Draw-Verify loop, where hypotheses are externalized onto an executable canvas, exact geometric quantities are measured, and structured observations are fed back to guide subsequent reasoning.

Result: Achieves 95.9% predicate-level and 84.0% strict problem-level construction checks on GeoGoal, improves outcome accuracy by up to 4.1%/16.4% on planar/solid benchmarks, and attains 68.2%/90.5% strict/relaxed rendering scores on GenExam-math.

Conclusion: The proposed externalization approach makes two properties separately auditable: Construction Fidelity (canvas matches intended configuration) and Measurement Faithfulness (exact values from canvas constraints), providing verifiable geometric reasoning.

Abstract: Vision-language models solve geometry problems with rising accuracy, yet their intermediate states remain latent and unverifiable: a relation expressed in textual reasoning or drawing code carries no guarantee that a constraint-satisfying configuration realizes it. We observe that existing externalization methods based on rendered pixels or one-shot scripts fail to provide exact, per-action geometric guarantees. Enforcing geometric relations by algebraic definition closes this gap: the workspace becomes a constraint-checked evolving canvas. We present Draw2Think, a framework that recasts geometric reasoning from latent spatial inference into agentic interaction with the GeoGebra constraint engine. In a Propose-Draw-Verify loop, Draw2Think externalizes hypotheses onto an executable canvas, measures exact geometric quantities, and feeds structured observations back to the model, so subsequent reasoning proceeds from checked canvas state grounded by the shared workspace. This externalization makes two properties separately auditable: model-level Construction Fidelity (whether the canvas realizes the intended configuration) and engine-level Measurement Faithfulness (exact values and relations from canvas constraints). Across construction, outcome, and rendering evaluations, Draw2Think builds canvases that pass 95.9% predicate-level and 84.0% strict problem-level construction checks on GeoGoal, improves outcome accuracy by up to 4.1%/16.4% on planar/solid benchmarks, and attains 68.2%/90.5% strict/relaxed rendering scores on GenExam-math. Project page is available at https://draw2think.github.io/

</details>


### [75] [SpineContextResUNet: A Computationally Efficient Residual UNet for Spine CT Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20760)
*K S Nithurshen,Saurabh J. Shigwan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpineContextResUNet is a lightweight 3D CNN for spinal segmentation in CT scans that achieves ~88% Dice score on public benchmarks while being efficient enough to run on edge devices like Nvidia Jetson Orin Nano, unlike heavy Transformer-based models that suffer from GPU/memory constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art vertebral segmentation methods (Transformers, large ensembles) require substantial GPU resources, limiting clinical adoption in resource-constrained environments and edge devices.

Method: A 3D Residual U-Net with a lightweight Context Block using parallel multi-dilated convolutions to capture long-range dependencies without RNN latency or Self-Attention memory overhead; ~1.7M parameters.

Result: Dice scores of 88.17% (VerSe2020) and 88.13% (CTSpine1K); outperforms a bottlenecked SwinUNETR; runs on commodity hardware (Intel Core i5, 8GB RAM) where TotalSegmentator fails due to memory exhaustion.

Conclusion: SpineContextResUNet is a viable solution for point-of-care diagnostics and edge deployment, balancing accuracy with extreme computational efficiency.

Abstract: Automated segmentation of the vertebral column in Computed Tomography (CT) scans is a prerequisite for pathological assessment and surgical planning. However, state-of-the-art methods, particularly those based on Transformers or large-scale ensembles, demand substantial GPU resources, creating a barrier for clinical adoption in resource-constrained environments or on edge devices. To address this, we introduce SpineContextResUNet, a computationally efficient 3D Residual U-Net designed for rapid spinal localization. Our architecture integrates a lightweight Context Block that employs parallel multi-dilated convolutions to capture long-range anatomical dependencies without the high latency of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) or the memory overhead of Self-Attention mechanisms. Extensive validation on two public benchmarks, VerSe2020 and CTSpine1K, demonstrates that our model achieves a Dice score of 88.17% and 88.13% respectively. To evaluate performance under strict hardware constraints, we compared our model against a bottlenecked SwinUNETR scaled to match our ~1.7M hardware footprint. While the constrained Transformer suffers severe performance degradation due to a lack of spatial inductive biases in a limited-data regime, our CNN-based approach successfully maintains high accuracy. Crucially, heavy baselines like TotalSegmentator fail due to memory exhaustion on commodity hardware (Intel Core i5, 8GB RAM), our model performs robust inference, making it a viable solution for point-of-care diagnostics and deployment on edge platforms like the Nvidia Jetson Orin Nano.

</details>


### [76] [Diffuse to Detect: Bi-Level Sample Rebalancing with Pseudo-Label Diffusion for Point-Supervised Infrared Small-Target Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20766)
*Zhu Liu,Yuanhang Yao,Ping Qian,Zihang Chen,Risheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A physics-guided framework using heat diffusion principles to convert point labels into reliable pseudo-masks, combined with a bi-level dual-update system for joint optimization, achieving efficient infrared small target detection with 5× annotation speedup and comparable performance using only 30% training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Point supervision is scalable for infrared small target detection but suffers from unstable pseudo-label evolution in cluttered, low-contrast imagery and severe sample-distribution imbalance.

Method: Proposes (1) a physics-induced annotation strategy leveraging thermal radiation-heat diffusion consistency to expand point labels into pseudo-masks, and (2) a bi-level dual-update framework that jointly optimizes detector weights, sample weights, and diffusion parameters via a meta-classifier and differentiable diffusion module.

Result: Five-fold annotation acceleration, superior detection accuracy, and comparable performance with only 30% of the training data across multiple datasets.

Conclusion: The physics-informed, adaptive framework effectively addresses point supervision bottlenecks, proving both efficient and practical for infrared small target detection.

Abstract: Point supervision has become a scalable solution to address dense annotation for infrared small target detection, but its performance is limited by two coupled bottlenecks: unstable pseudo-label evolution in cluttered, low-contrast infrared imagery and severe sample-distribution imbalance. In this paper, we present a more adaptive and stable framework to address these issues. Leveraging the intrinsic consistency between thermal radiation patterns and heat diffusion, we propose a physics-induced annotation strategy that expands single-point labels into reliable pseudo-masks. To further enhance supervision and alleviate sample imbalance, we develop a bi-level dual-update framework that jointly optimizes detector weights, sample weights, and diffusion parameters. A meta-classifier dynamically predicts sample-wise loss weights, while a differentiable diffusion module refines pseudo-labels with detection feedback, enabling adaptive interaction between training and hyperparameter optimization. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate five-fold annotation acceleration, superior detection accuracy, and comparable performance with 30% of the training data, validating the efficiency and practicality of our approach. Our code is available at https://github.com/yuanhang-yao/diffuse-to-detect.

</details>


### [77] [VIHD: Visual Intervention-based Hallucination Detection for Medical Visual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20772)
*Jiayi Chen,Benteng Ma,Zehui Liao,Winston Chong,Yasmeen George,Jianfei Cai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes VIHD, a visual intervention-based method that masks visual tokens to detect hallucinations in medical MLLMs by calibrating semantic entropy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination detection methods rely on heuristic, context-agnostic external perturbations and overlook internal cross-modal dependencies between generated tokens and visual tokens during decoding.

Method: VIHD has three steps: (1) Visual Dependency Probing (VDP) to locate visually dominant decoder layers, (2) Visual Intervention Decoding (VID) via token masking to calibrate semantic distribution, and (3) Calibrated Semantic Entropy (CSE) quantification as a hallucination signal.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on three medical VQA benchmarks with two medical MLLMs.

Conclusion: Fine-grained visual dependency is crucial for effective hallucination detection in medical MLLMs.

Abstract: While medical Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promise in assisting diagnosis, they still frequently generate hallucinated responses that appear linguistically plausible but lack visual evidence. Such hallucinations pose risks to clinical decision-making and necessitate effective detection.
