Existing introspective detection methods primarily perform uncertainty estimation or logical verification by analyzing model responses conditioned on original or perturbed inputs. However, such external perturbations are often heuristic and context-agnostic, which overlooks the internal cross-modal dependency between generated tokens and related visual tokens during decoding. To address this issue, we propose VIHD, a Visual Intervention-based Hallucination Detection method that leverages targeted visual token masking to calibrate semantic entropy for more effective hallucination detection. VIHD locates visually dominant decoder layers via Visual Dependency Probing (VDP), executes Visual Intervention Decoding (VID) via token masking to calibrate the semantic distribution, and quantifies the resulting Calibrated Semantic Entropy (CSE) as a reliable hallucination signal. Extensive experiments on three medical VQA benchmarks with two medical MLLMs demonstrate that VIHD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, underscoring the importance of fine-grained visual dependency for hallucination detection. The code will be available at https://github.com/Jiayi-Chen-AU/VIHD

</details>


### [78] [AttriStory: Fine-grained Attribute Realization for Visual Storytelling with Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20777)
*Manogna Sreenivas,Rohit Kumar,Soma Biswas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AttriStory introduces a benchmark and a plug-and-play latent optimization module (AttriLoss) to ensure fine-grained attributes like color and texture are faithfully rendered in visual storytelling, complementing existing character consistency methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current diffusion models for visual storytelling maintain character consistency across scenes but lack systematic methods to ensure fine-grained attributes (e.g., clothing color, textures) are faithfully rendered in generated images.

Method: (1) Curated 200 multi-scene stories across 10 artistic styles with detailed attribute specifications. (2) Proposed AttriLoss, a plug-and-play latent optimization module operating during early denoising steps, maximizing cross-attention alignment for desired attribute-object pairs while suppressing spurious associations.

Result: Consistent improvements on incorporating AttriLoss across all baselines, demonstrating effective attribute realization in visual storytelling.

Conclusion: Attributes realization is established as a distinct, complementary dimension alongside character consistency, advancing fine-grained attribute-controlled story generation without requiring architectural modifications.

Abstract: Visual storytelling with diffusion models has made impressive strides in maintaining character consistency across narrative scenes. However, a critical gap remains: while these methods ensure a character remains consistent across scenes, they provide no systematic method to ensure if fine-grained attributes such as color and textures of clothing, accessories are faithfully rendered in the generated images. Towards this goal, we introduce AttriStory, a benchmark enabling attribute realization in visual storytelling. We curate 200 multi-scene stories across 10 distinct artistic styles using Large Language Model. Each scene is constructed with detailed attribute specifications to enable rich visual narratives. Further, to address attribute realization, we propose a plug-and-play latent optimization module that operates during early denoising steps, when the model establishes structural and semantic content. We achieve this through AttriLoss objective designed to maximize alignment between the cross-attention maps for desired attribute-object pairs while suppressing spurious associations, guiding models to localize attributes correctly. This approach operates orthogonally to existing consistency mechanisms, integrating seamlessly with current story generation pipelines without requiring architectural modifications. Our experiments demonstrate consistent improvements on incorporating AttriLoss across all baselines. This work positions attribute realization as a distinct, complementary dimension of visual storytelling, alongside character consistency, advancing the field toward fine-grained attribute-controlled story generation. Project-page:https://manogna-s.github.io/attristory/

</details>


### [79] [Findings of the Counter Turing Test: AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20787)
*Rajarshi Roy,Nasrin Imanpour,Ashhar Aziz,Shashwat Bajpai,Gurpreet Singh,Shwetangshu Biswas,Kapil Wanaskar,Parth Patwa,Subhankar Ghosh,Shreyas Dixit,Nilesh Ranjan Pal,Vipula Rawte,Ritvik Garimella,Amitava Das,Amit Sheth,Vasu Sharma,Aishwarya Naresh Reganti,Vinija Jain,Aman Chadha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents results from the Defactify 4.0 workshop's Counter Turing Test (CT2) for detecting AI-generated images, using the MS COCOAI dataset (50K synthetic + real images). While binary detection achieved F1 > 0.83, identifying the specific generative model proved much harder (F1 = 0.4986).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The rapid advancement of generative AI (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Midjourney) creates realistic synthetic visuals that pose risks of misinformation, disinformation, and bias, necessitating reliable detection methods.

Method: Organized the CT2 competition with two tasks: (1) binary classification (AI-generated vs. real), (2) identifying the specific generative model. Built the MS COCOAI dataset (50K synthetic images from multiple models + real MS COCO images). Participants used CNNs, Vision Transformers, frequency-based analysis, contrastive learning, and multimodal techniques.

Result: Binary detection achieved high accuracy (F1 > 0.83), but model identification was much harder (best F1 = 0.4986).

Conclusion: There is a need for improved model fingerprinting, adversarial robustness, and real-time detection mechanisms.

Abstract: The rapid advancements in generative AI technologies, such as Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, and Midjourney, have significantly transformed the creation of synthetic visual content. While these models enable innovation across industries, they also pose serious challenges, including misinformation, disinformation, and biased content generation. The increasing realism of AI-generated images makes their detection a pressing concern for researchers, policymakers, and industry stakeholders.
  In this paper, we present the findings of the Defactify 4.0 workshop, which introduced the Counter Turing Test (CT2) for AI-Generated Image Detection. The competition consisted of two key tasks: (1) binary classification of images as either AI-generated or real and (2) identification of the specific generative model responsible for an AI-generated image. To facilitate this, we developed the MS COCOAI dataset, consisting of 50,000 synthetic images from multiple generative models alongside real-world images from the MS COCO dataset.
  Participants employed diverse detection strategies, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs), Vision Transformers (ViTs), frequency-based analysis, contrastive learning, and multimodal techniques. The results demonstrated that while AI-generated images can be detected with high accuracy (F1-score > 0.83), identifying the exact model used remains significantly more challenging (highest F1-score: 0.4986). These findings highlight the need for improved model fingerprinting, adversarial robustness, and real-time detection mechanisms.

</details>


### [80] [What Semantics Survive the Connector? Diagnosing VLM-to-DiT Alignment in Video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20795)
*Hangyu Lin,Chao Wen,Chengming Xu,Jianxiong Gao,Jiangning Zhang,Xiaobin Hu,Yanwei Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper challenges the assumption that Vision-Language Model (VLM) alignment with diffusion transformers (DiTs) is lossless for video editing. It introduces TRACE-Edit (a diagnostic dataset based on controlled video composition) and a diagnostic protocol to show that fine-grained structural semantics degrade during VLM-to-DiT alignment, identifying this alignment as a major bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video editing models rely on prepending VLMs to DiTs under the assumption that a connector can seamlessly align multi-modal reasoning with the text embedding space. However, this alignment may degrade fine-grained structural variables, and existing end-to-end evaluations fail to isolate alignment failures from generation errors. There is also a lack of disentangled annotations in natural datasets.

Method: The authors propose a controlled data processing pipeline based on video composition to create TRACE-Edit, a diagnostic dataset focusing on relation-based editing. They then design a comprehensive diagnostic protocol to analyze two key designs: meta-query and connector in existing video editing models. Four representative model cases are systematically evaluated.

Result: Systematic evaluation of four representative model cases reveals that fine-grained structural semantics are severely degraded during VLM-to-DiT alignment. The assumption of lossless semantic transfer is overturned.

Conclusion: The VLM-to-DiT alignment is identified as a major bottleneck in instruction-based video editing. This work provides a new diagnostic foundation for future multi-modal alignment architectures.

Abstract: Flow matching based video generative models have been increasingly relying on prepended Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to handle complex, instruction-based video editing. The prevailing assumption underlying this paradigm is that a connector module can seamlessly align the VLM's rich multi-modal reasoning with the original text embedding space of DiTs. However, we hypothesize that this alignment acts as a severe semantic bottleneck, degrading fine-grained structural variables. Verifying this is challenging, as end-to-end evaluations conflate alignment failures with generation errors, and natural datasets lack disentangled annotations. To rigorously investigate this, we propose a controlled data processing pipeline based on video composition that results in TRACE-Edit, a diagnostic dataset focusing on relation-based editing. Leveraging this dataset, we propose a comprehensive diagnostic protocol to analyze two important designs of meta-query and connector in the existing video editing models. Systematic evaluation of four representative model cases reveals that fine-grained structural semantics can be severely degraded during alignment. Our findings overturn the assumption of lossless semantic transfer, identifying the VLM-to-DiT alignment as a major bottleneck and providing a new diagnostic foundation for future multi-modal alignment architectures.

</details>


### [81] [OlmoEarth v1.1: A more efficient family of OlmoEarth models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20804)
*Gabriel Tseng,Yawen Zhang,Favyen Bastani,Henry Herzog,Joseph Redmon,Hadrien Sablon,Piper Wolters,Patrick Alan Johnson,Christopher Wilhelm,Patrick Beukema*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper presents improvements to OlmoEarth that reduce training GPU hours by 1.7x and inference MACs by 2.9x on Sentinel-2 tasks while maintaining performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce compute costs for both training and inference of the OlmoEarth family while preserving model performance.

Method: A set of unspecified improvements to the OlmoEarth architecture/training pipeline that reduce GPU hours (1.7x) during training and MACs (2.9x) during inference.

Result: 1.7x reduction in training GPU hours for Base models; 2.9x reduction in MACs on Sentinel-2 tasks; maintained overall performance.

Conclusion: The proposed improvements successfully cut compute costs significantly without sacrificing model quality, and the code is publicly available.

Abstract: We present a set of improvements to the OlmoEarth family. These improvements allow us to cut compute costs during training ($1.7 \times$ reduction in GPU hours required to train our Base models) and inference ($2.9\times$ reductions in MACs on Sentinel-2 tasks), while maintaining the models' overall performance. All training code is available at github.com/allenai/olmoearth_pretrain.

</details>


### [82] [Decomposing Subject-Driven Image Generation via Intermediate Structural Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20807)
*Hanzhong Guo,Yizhou Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a two-stage framework for subject-driven text-to-image generation that preserves high-frequency details by first predicting a Canny edge map, then rendering the final image. It also introduces a 100k-pair text-aware dataset for improved text handling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing subject-driven text-to-image generation methods struggle to preserve high-frequency identity details (logos, patterns, text) because they operate directly in RGB space, leading to detail degradation under substantial edits.

Method: A two-stage framework that decouples structure from appearance: (1) predict a Canny edge map from the source image, (2) render the final image conditioned on both source appearance and predicted structure. Additionally, a fully automatic pipeline constructs a 100k-pair text-aware dataset with cross-view textual consistency.

Result: Clear gains over selected baselines, validated via GPT-4.1-based evaluation and a knowledge distillation study.

Conclusion: Intermediate structural prediction is an effective route for high-fidelity subject-driven generation, and the dataset/code will be publicly released.

Abstract: Subject-driven text-to-image generation still struggles to preserve high-frequency identity details such as logos, patterns, and text. Existing methods typically operate directly in RGB space, which often leads to detail degradation under substantial edits. We propose a two-stage framework that decouples structure from appearance by first predicting a Canny map and then rendering the final image conditioned on both the source appearance and the predicted structure. To improve text handling, we further introduce a fully automatic pipeline that constructs a 100k-pair text-aware dataset with cross-view textual consistency. Experiments, including GPT-4.1-based evaluation and a knowledge distillation study, show clear gains over selected baselines and suggest that intermediate structural prediction is an effective route for high-fidelity subject-driven generation. Our dataset and code will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [83] [Spatial Gram Alignment for Ultra-High-Resolution Image Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20808)
*Jinjin Zhang,Xiefan Guo,Di Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SGA resolves the learnability-fidelity conflict in ultra-high-resolution text-to-image synthesis by aligning spatial self-similarities (Gram matrices) of generative features with foundation model priors, instead of direct patch-wise distillation, preserving LDMs' native capacity while achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling representation alignment methods (distilling visual priors from SAM/DINO) to pre-trained LDMs at extreme resolutions causes a learnability-fidelity conflict: direct patch-wise feature distillation perturbs the pre-trained latent manifold, leading to generation degradation.

Method: Spatial Gram Alignment (SGA) aligns internal self-similarities (Gram matrices) of generative features with those of foundation priors, imposing a non-invasive spatial constraint that establishes macroscopic structural coherence while preserving native generative objectives for microscopic fidelity.

Result: State-of-the-art performance for ultra-high-resolution text-to-image synthesis, effectively reconciling global structural integrity with fine-grained visual details. The method integrates seamlessly across both intermediate diffusion features and VAE latents.

Conclusion: SGA effectively resolves the learnability-fidelity conflict by using spatial Gram alignment as a non-invasive constraint, enabling scalable representation alignment for ultra-high-resolution generation without degrading LDMs' original capabilities.

Abstract: Modern ultra-high-resolution image synthesis relies heavily on the robust generative capacity of large-scale pre-trained Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs). While recent representation alignment methods have proven effective by distilling visual priors from foundation models (e.g., SAM or DINO) into generative latent features, scaling these approaches to pre-trained LDMs at extreme resolutions exposes a critical learnability-fidelity conflict. Specifically, forcing direct patch-wise feature distillation inherently perturbs the pre-trained latent manifold, ultimately leading to generation degradation. To address this bottleneck, we propose Spatial Gram Alignment (SGA), a novel framework that explicitly leverages the representation priors of vision foundation models while preserving the native generative capacity of LDMs. Moving beyond restrictive direct alignment, SGA imposes a non-invasive spatial constraint by aligning the internal self-similarities of the generative features with those of the foundation priors. This spatial constraint effectively establishes macroscopic structural coherence, while the native generative objectives retain the microscopic pixel-level fidelity inherent to the original LDMs. Notably, this versatile strategy integrates seamlessly across both intermediate diffusion features and VAE latents within pre-trained LDMs. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SGA achieves state-of-the-art performance for ultra-high-resolution text-to-image synthesis, yielding an effective reconciliation between global structural integrity and fine-grained visual details. Code is available at https://github.com/zhang0jhon/SGA.

</details>


### [84] [OSGNet with MLLM Reranking @ Ego4D Episodic Memory Challenge 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20818)
*Yisen Feng,Leigang Qu,Haoyu Zhang,Qiaohui Chu,Meng Liu,Xuemeng Song,Weili Guan,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The authors won first place in both NLQ and GoalStep tracks of the Ego4D Episodic Memory Challenge at CVPR 2026 by using a reranking framework that combines OSGNet candidate segments with MLLM-based selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve temporal localization in long untrimmed egocentric videos by combining the efficiency of conventional pipelines with the reasoning capability of multimodal large language models.

Method: A two-stage reranking framework: (1) generate candidate segments using OSGNet, (2) use a multimodal large language model (MLLM) to rerank and select the best matching segment for the query.

Result: Achieved first place in both Natural Language Queries and GoalStep tracks of the Ego4D Episodic Memory Challenge at CVPR 2026.

Conclusion: Leveraging MLLM for reranking over conventional localization outputs significantly improves accuracy for egocentric video temporal grounding tasks.

Abstract: In this report, we present our champion solutions for the Natural Language Queries and GoalStep tracks of the Ego4D Episodic Memory Challenge at CVPR 2026. Both tracks require accurately localizing temporal segments from long untrimmed egocentric videos. To address these tasks, we propose a reranking-based framework that effectively leverages the strong video-language reasoning capability of multimodal large language model (MLLM) while preserving the efficiency and candidate recall of conventional localization pipelines. Specifically, we first obtain a set of candidate segments from existing localization model OSGNet, and then employ MLLM to select the segment that best matches the given query, thereby refining the final prediction. Ultimately, our method achieved first place in both the Natural Language Queries and GoalStep tracks. Our code can be found at https://github.com/iLearn-Lab/CVPR25-OSGNet.

</details>


### [85] [AIR: Amortized Image Reconstruction Framework for Self-Supervised Feed-Forward 2D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20820)
*Zhaojie Zeng,Yuesong Wang,Yawei Luo,Tao Guan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AIR is a self-supervised feed-forward framework that amortizes iterative 2D Gaussian fitting into a single network pass, eliminating per-image test-time optimization. It uses a stage-wise residual architecture with Stage Control and a Predict-Optimize-Distill training strategy, achieving better reconstruction quality than Gaussian-based baselines with 160-300ms encoding time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 2D Gaussian splatting methods require costly per-image iterative optimization or rely on handcrafted priors for primitive allocation, which limits efficiency and scalability.

Method: AIR uses: (1) a stage-wise residual architecture that progressively predicts Gaussian primitives from reconstruction residuals, (2) an explicit Stage Control mechanism activating new primitives only in under-reconstructed regions, (3) a Predict-Optimize-Distill training strategy stabilizing multi-stage prediction by distilling optimized Gaussian increments back into the predictor, and (4) an image-adaptive quantizer for compact storage.

Result: AIR achieves better reconstruction quality than representative Gaussian-based baselines on Kodak and DIV2K datasets while reducing encoding time to 160-300ms.

Conclusion: The proposed feed-forward framework successfully eliminates per-image test-time optimization for 2D Gaussian splatting, offering both efficiency and quality improvements.

Abstract: 2D Gaussian splatting provides an efficient explicit representation for image reconstruction, but existing methods still require costly per-image iterative optimization or rely on handcrafted priors for primitive allocation. We present AIR, a self-supervised feed-forward framework that amortizes iterative Gaussian fitting into a single network pass, eliminating per-image test-time optimization. AIR adopts a stage-wise residual architecture that progressively predicts additional Gaussian primitives from reconstruction residuals, together with an explicit Stage Control mechanism that activates new primitives only in under-reconstructed regions. A Predict--Optimize--Distill training strategy stabilizes multi-stage prediction by distilling short-horizon optimized Gaussian increments back into the predictor. The stabilized predictor is then jointly finetuned across stages and equipped with an image-adaptive quantizer for compact Gaussian storage. Experiments on Kodak and DIV2K show that AIR achieves better reconstruction quality than representative Gaussian-based baselines while reducing encoding time to 160--300\,ms. Code: https://github.com/whoiszzj/AIR.git

</details>


### [86] [TERDNet: Transformer Encoder-Recurrent Decoder Network for Scene Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20822)
*Jiae Yoon,Ue-Hwan Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TERDNet is a Transformer Encoder-Recurrent Decoder Network for Scene Change Detection that outperforms prior methods across four benchmarks by using multi-level feature extraction, correlation volume fusion, iterative recurrent refinement (3-gate-GRU), and a combined upsampler; it also demonstrates robustness to viewpoint misalignment and benefits from segmentation-based pretraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SCD models overlook varying feature importance across layers, use single-step decoders limiting refinement, and provide limited insight into encoder pretraining strategies.

Method: TERDNet consists of (1) a transformer-based encoder for multi-level representations, (2) a feature fusion module integrating correlation volumes, (3) a recurrent 3-gate-GRU decoder for iterative refinement, and (4) a combined convolution-interpolation upsampler for fine-grained resolution restoration.

Result: TERDNet consistently outperforms prior approaches on four public benchmarks with more accurate and detailed change masks; ablation studies confirm the benefit of segmentation-based pretraining and the fusion design; robustness tests show resilience under viewpoint misalignment.

Conclusion: TERDNet effectively addresses key limitations in SCD, achieves state-of-the-art results, and demonstrates strong potential for deployment in real-world robotic systems requiring reliable perception.

Abstract: In this work, we address the challenge of Scene Change Detection (SCD), where the goal is to identify variations between two images of the same location captured at different times. Existing SCD models often overlook the varying importance of features across layers, employ single-step decoders that confine refinement, and provide limited insight into encoder pretraining strategies. We propose TERDNet, a Transformer Encoder-Recurrent Decoder Network designed to overcome these limitations. TERDNet consists of a transformer-based encoder that extracts multi-level representations, a feature fusion module that integrates correlation volumes with these features, a recurrent 3-gate-GRU decoder that performs iterative refinement, and a combined convolution-interpolation upsampler that restores fine-grained resolution. Extensive experiments on four public benchmarks show that TERDNet consistently outperforms prior approaches and produces more accurate and detailed change masks. Ablation studies confirm the benefit of segmentation-based pretraining and the effectiveness of our fusion design. In addition, robustness tests under viewpoint misalignment confirm TERDNet's potential for deployment in real-world robotic systems, where reliable perception is critical. Our code is available at https://github.com/AutoCompSysLab/TERDNet.

</details>


### [87] [RelWitness: Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graph Generation with Visual-Geometric Relation Witnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20823)
*Minh Anh Nguyen,Quang Huy Tran,Bao Ngoc Le,Tuan Kiet Pham,Sui Yang Guang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RelWitness is a framework for open-vocabulary 3D scene graph generation from RGB-D sequences that uses "relation witnesses"—visual-geometric cues—to handle incomplete relation supervision, identifying unannotated valid relations and improving unseen-relation recognition while reducing hallucination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The key challenge is not just vocabulary expansion but supervision reliability: existing 3D scene graph datasets have incomplete relation annotations, where many valid object-pair relations are unannotated, making standard supervised learning treat missing labels as negatives incorrectly.

Method: RelWitness introduces relation witness records from RGB views, depth maps, 3D geometry, role-sensitive text, null views, and multi-view consistency. A visual-geometric witness verifier classifies unannotated candidates as verified missing positives, reliable negatives, or uncertain. A witness-guided positive-unlabeled objective handles incomplete annotations, plus witness-consistent decoding is used.

Result: Simulated experiments on 3DSSG/3RScan and ScanNet datasets show improved unseen-relation recognition, higher witness precision, lower hallucination, and reduced redundant relation phrases. (Results are planning values, not actual reproduced measurements.)

Conclusion: The proposed witness-guided approach effectively addresses incomplete relation supervision in 3D scene graph generation, enabling open-vocabulary relation recognition without assuming all unannotated pairs are negative.

Abstract: Open-vocabulary 3D scene graph generation seeks to describe object instances and their relations with flexible natural-language predicates. The central difficulty is not only vocabulary expansion, but supervision reliability: relation annotations in 3D scene graph datasets are selective, and many valid object-pair relations are unannotated. We propose RelWitness, a framework for open-vocabulary 3D scene graph generation from posed RGB-D sequences under incomplete relation supervision. The key concept is a relation witness: a concrete visual-geometric cue that makes a relation observable in the captured scene. Support relations require contact and vertical ordering; containment requires enclosure; proximity requires metric closeness; orientation requires facing direction; and stable relations should persist across views where both objects are visible. RelWitness constructs relation witness records from RGB views, depth maps, reconstructed 3D geometry, role-sensitive text, object-prior null views, and multi-view consistency. A visual-geometric witness verifier assigns unannotated relation candidates to verified missing positives, reliable negatives, or uncertain unlabeled cases. A witness-guided positive-unlabeled objective then learns from incomplete annotations without turning every missing label into a negative. We further introduce witness-consistent decoding and an RGB-D missing-relation audit protocol. Simulated manuscript-planning experiments on 3DSSG/3RScan and ScanNet-derived open-vocabulary splits show the intended behavior: improved unseen-relation recognition, higher witness precision, lower hallucination, and reduced redundant relation phrases. All numerical results are planning values and must be replaced by reproduced measurements before submission

</details>


### [88] [HyDAR-Pano3D: A Hybrid Disentangled Anatomical Recovery Framework for Panoramic-to-3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20827)
*Yaoyao Yue,Jérôme Schmid,Xiaoshuang Li,Eduardo Delamare,Jinman Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyDAR-Pano3D is a two-stage framework that reformulates 2D panoramic radiograph to 3D CBCT reconstruction as a disentangled anatomical recovery problem, using a dual-encoder network with SAM semantic priors and an Anatomical Restoration Network, achieving state-of-the-art results (25.76 dB PSNR, 85.70% SSIM, 83.83% Dice) across three large-scale datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learning-based methods for PR-to-CBCT reconstruction entangle learning of common anatomical structures and patient-specific variations, leading to over-smoothed reconstructions with blurred anatomical boundaries due to the ill-posed 2D-to-3D inverse problem.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) A dual-encoder network integrates radiographic features with SAM-derived semantic priors to reconstruct an arch-normalized canonical volume; (2) An Anatomical Restoration Network predicts a prior-constrained structured deformation field to map the canonical volume back to native space, restoring individual morphological variations.

Result: Outperforms baseline methods (p<0.05) with 25.76 dB PSNR, 85.70% SSIM, 83.83% overall anatomical Dice score on three large-scale datasets. Downstream segmentation achieves 82.4% Dice for whole teeth and 72.2% Dice for inferior alveolar canal.

Conclusion: The disentangled approach preserves clinically relevant structures, enabling robust anatomy-aware CBCT assessment when CBCT data is unavailable.

Abstract: Panoramic radiograph (PR) is fundamentally used in routine dental care, but it inherently provides only a two-dimensional (2D) projection of complex three-dimensional (3D) craniofacial anatomy. Most existing learning-based methods attempt to computationally recover this 3D information by directly regressing native cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT) volumes from PR. However, this direct mapping requires the model to simultaneously learn common anatomical structures and patient-specific morphological variations. This entangled formulation makes the ill-posed 2D-to-3D inverse problem highly ambiguous, often producing over-smoothed reconstructions with blurred anatomical boundaries. To address this, we propose HyDAR-Pano3D, a two-stage framework that reformulates PR-to-CBCT reconstruction as a disentangled anatomical recovery problem. In Stage 1, a dual-encoder network integrates radiographic features with SAM-derived semantic priors to reconstruct an arch-normalized canonical volume. In Stage 2, an Anatomical Restoration Network predicts a prior-constrained structured deformation field to map this canonical volume back to the native space, restoring individual morphological variations. Experiments on three large-scale datasets show that HyDAR-Pano3D significantly outperforms baseline methods ($p < 0.05$), achieving a 25.76 dB PSNR, 85.70\% SSIM, and an 83.83\% overall anatomical Dice score. The synthesized volumes successfully support downstream segmentation of whole teeth (82.4\% Dice) and the inferior alveolar canal (72.2\% Dice), demonstrating that our disentangled approach preserves clinically relevant structures to enable robust anatomy-aware assessment when CBCT data is unavailable.

</details>


### [89] [ArchSIBench: Benchmarking the Architectural Spatial Intelligence of Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20837)
*Qirui Shen,Wenda Wang,Jiachen Lu,Zilong Huang,Jin Bai,Lei He,Hongxuan Chen,Weixin Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces ArchSIBench, a benchmark for evaluating architectural spatial intelligence in Vision-Language Models across five core dimensions (perception, reasoning, navigation, transformation, configuration) with 17 subtasks and 3,000 QA pairs. Results show VLMs lag behind human experts, especially in spatial transformation and configuration reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM spatial benchmarks only assess basic spatial skills (e.g., orientation, distance) and neglect higher-level architectural spatial intelligence such as layout understanding, circulation patterns, and functional zoning, which are crucial for robot navigation, embodied interaction, and 3D scene understanding.

Method: ArchSIBench is built on perspectives from architecture, cognitive science, and psychology, covering five core dimensions (perception, reasoning, navigation, transformation, configuration) with 17 fine-grained subtasks. 3,000 QA pairs are manually annotated by experts with architectural backgrounds for evaluation.

Result: Most VLMs show significant differences from human baselines, with substantial variability across capability dimensions. Some state-of-the-art models approach untrained human evaluators, but a clear gap remains compared to architecture-trained humans, especially in spatial transformation and configuration reasoning.

Conclusion: ArchSIBench provides systematic resources and insights for measuring and advancing architectural spatial intelligence in VLMs, highlighting key areas where models still fall short of trained human performance.

Abstract: Architectural spatial intelligence, the ability to recognize and infer architectural space, is fundamental to tasks such as robot navigation, embodied interaction, and 3D scene understanding and generation. Although extensive research has evaluated the basic spatial skills of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) such as relative orientation, distance comparison, and object counting, these tasks cover only the most elementary levels of spatial cognition and largely overlook higher-level cognition of architectural space, including layout understanding, circulation patterns, and functional zoning. In this work, we present ArchSIBench, a Benchmark for Architectural Spatial Intelligence based on the perspectives from architecture, cognitive science, and psychology. ArchSIBench covers five core dimensions: perception, reasoning, navigation, transformation, and configuration, comprising 17 fine-grained subtasks. Through careful manual annotation by experts with architectural backgrounds, we construct 3,000 question-answer pairs to enable comprehensive evaluation of architectural spatial intelligence. Based on ArchSIBench, we evaluate various VLMs and find that the architectural spatial intelligence of most models shows significant differences from human baselines; additionally, models exhibit substantial variability across capability dimensions. Some state-of-the-art models can approach the level of human evaluators without architectural training. However, a clear gap remains compared to human evaluators with architectural training, particularly in spatial transformation and configuration reasoning. We believe that ArchSIBench will provide important insights and systematic resources for measuring and advancing the architectural spatial intelligence of VLMs. The dataset and code are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ArchSIBench/ArchSIBench.

</details>


### [90] [USV: Towards Understanding the User-generated Short-form Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20838)
*Haoyue Cheng,Su Xu,Liwei Jin,Wayne Wu,Chen Qian,Limin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces USV, a large-scale dataset (~224K user-generated short-form videos) for high-level semantic understanding, along with two tasks (topic recognition and video-text retrieval) and baseline methods (MMF-Net and VTCL).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video datasets focus on instance-level recognition and largely overlook user-generated short-form videos; the authors aim to fill this gap by providing a dataset and benchmarks for high-level semantic video understanding.

Method: Authors collected ~224K videos from UGC platforms via label queries, established two tasks (topic recognition and video-text retrieval), and proposed two baselines: MMF-Net (multi-modality fusion for topic recognition) and VTCL (video-text contrastive learning for retrieval).

Result: A comprehensive dataset and benchmark with baseline results for topic recognition and video-text retrieval on user-generated short-form videos.

Conclusion: The USV dataset and proposed baselines provide a foundation for future research on high-level semantic understanding of short-form user-generated videos.

Abstract: Several large-scale video datasets have been published these years and have advanced the area of video understanding. However, the newly emerged user-generated short-form videos have rarely been studied. This paper presents USV, the User-generated Short-form Video dataset for high-level semantic video understanding. The dataset contains around 224K videos collected from UGC platforms by label queries without extra manual verification and trimming. Although video understanding has achieved plausible improvement these years, most works focus on instance-level recognition, which is not sufficient for learning the representation of the high-level semantic information of videos. Therefore, we further establish two tasks: topic recognition and video-text retrieval on USV. We propose two unified and effective baseline methods Multi-Modality Fusion Network (MMF-Net) and Video-Text Contrastive Learning (VTCL), to tackle the topic recognition task and video-text retrieval respectively, and carry out comprehensive benchmarks to facilitate future research. Our project page is https://usvdataset.github.io.

</details>


### [91] [Activation-Free Backbones for Image Recognition: Polynomial Alternatives within MetaFormer-Style Vision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20839)
*Jeffrey Wang,Jonathan Gregory,Grigorios G. Chrysos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper shows that standard nonlinear activations (ReLU, GELU, softmax) are unnecessary in MetaFormer-style vision backbones, replacing them with polynomial functions built from Hadamard products across MLPs, convolutions, and attention. Their PolyNeXt models match or surpass activation-based counterparts on ImageNet, ADE20K, and robustness benchmarks while outperforming prior polynomial networks at lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the assumption that pointwise nonlinear activations (ReLU, GELU, softmax) are essential for vision backbones, and to demonstrate that simpler polynomial alternatives can achieve competitive or superior performance.

Method: Replace standard nonlinearities with Hadamard-product-based polynomial functions in three core primitives: MLPs, convolutions, and attention. These activation-free modules are integrated into the MetaFormer framework to construct PolyNeXt models.

Result: PolyNeXt models match or exceed activation-based counterparts across multiple scales on ImageNet classification, ADE20K semantic segmentation, and out-of-distribution robustness, while significantly outperforming prior polynomial networks with lower computational cost.

Conclusion: Polynomial variants of standard modules can beat complex custom architectures, suggesting that nonlinear activations are not strictly required for strong vision backbone performance.

Abstract: Modern vision backbones treat pointwise activations (e.g., ReLU, GELU) and exponential softmax as essential sources of nonlinearity, but we demonstrate they are not required within MetaFormer-style vision backbones. We design activation-free polynomial alternatives for three core primitives (MLPs, convolutions, and attention), where Hadamard products replace standard nonlinearities to yield polynomial functions of the input. These modules integrate seamlessly into existing architectures: instantiated within MetaFormer, a modular framework for vision backbones, our PolyNeXt models match or exceed activation-based counterparts across model scales on ImageNet classification, ADE20K semantic segmentation, and out-of-distribution robustness. We also substantially outperform prior polynomial networks at reduced computational cost, showing that polynomial variants of standard modules beat complex custom architectures.

</details>


### [92] [Map-Mono-Ego: Map-Grounded Global Human Pose Estimation from Monocular Egocentric Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20889)
*Hiroyuki Deguchi,Ryosuke Hori,Kotaro Amaya,Tsubasa Maruyama,Mitsunori Tada,Hideo Saito*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MapMonoEgo achieves globally consistent monocular egocentric human pose estimation by leveraging pre-scanned 3D point clouds, eliminating translational drift without specialized hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular egocentric pose estimation lacks absolute location tracking and suffers from scale ambiguity and translational drift, limiting long-term monitoring without multi-sensor hardware.

Method: Uses a pre-scanned 3D point cloud as environmental context to constrain monocular egocentric human pose estimation, achieving global consistency.

Result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the new AIST-Living dataset, proving practical utility.

Conclusion: MapMonoEgo enables practical, hardware-free global pose tracking for ubiquitous activity monitoring.

Abstract: Monocular egocentric human pose estimation is essential for ubiquitous activity monitoring. However, understanding the user's absolute location within the environment remains a challenge. Existing methods primarily focus on relative motion from an initial position, and tend not to account for the wearer's absolute location within an environment. Furthermore, inherent scale ambiguity in monocular vision leads to severe translational drift, limiting long-term tracking without specialized multi-sensor hardware. To address this, we propose MapMonoEgo, a novel framework achieving globally consistent human pose estimation solely from a monocular camera by leveraging a pre-scanned 3D point cloud. We also introduce AIST-Living dataset, a new dataset pairing egocentric video with ground-truth motion in a scanned environment. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art baseline, proving its utility for practical monitoring tasks without specialized hardware.

</details>


### [93] [HDMoE: A Hierarchical Decoupling-Fusion Mixture-of-Experts Framework for Multimodal Cancer Survival Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20891)
*Huayi Wang,Haochao Ying,Yuyang Xu,Qiyao Zheng,jun wang,Cheng Zhang,Ying Sun,Jian Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes HDMoE, a hierarchical decoupling-fusion framework with Mixture-of-Experts and Random Feature Reorganization modules, to improve multimodal survival prediction by reducing redundant information before decoupling and capturing fine-grained intra- and inter-modality feature interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal survival prediction methods using feature decoupling-fusion have two key limitations: (1) they fail to reduce redundant information in modality features before decoupling, harming decoupling and fusion quality; (2) they lack the ability to model fine-grained feature relationships and capture local information interactions between intra- and inter-modality features.

Method: HDMoE employs a two-level MoE architecture: Level-1 MoE uses shared and routed experts to remove redundancy and extract fine-grained specific features within each modality; Level-2 MoE enables fine-grained inter-modality feature decoupling. Two RFR modules follow each MoE level to finely fuse intra- and inter-modality features, capturing more fine-grained cross-modal relationships.

Result: Extensive experiments on a private Liver Cancer (LC) dataset and three TCGA public datasets confirm the effectiveness of the proposed HDMoE method for multimodal survival prediction.

Conclusion: The HDMoE framework effectively addresses redundancy and fine-grained interaction limitations in multimodal survival prediction, achieving superior performance through hierarchical decoupling-fusion with MoE and RFR modules.

Abstract: Multimodal survival prediction, a crucial yet challenging task, demands the integration of multimodal medical data (\eg Whole Slide Images (WSIs) and Genomic Profiles) to achieve accurate prognostic modeling. Given the inherent heterogeneity across modalities, the feature decoupling-fusion paradigm has emerged as a dominant approach. However, these methods have the following shortcomings: (1) fail to reduce the redundant information of modality features before decoupling, which negatively affects the feature decoupling and fusion effect;(2) lack the ability to model the fine-grained relationships of the features and capture the local information interactions between intra- and inter-modality features. To address these issues, we propose a \underline{H}ierarchical \underline{D}ecoupling-Fusion \underline{M}ixture-\underline{o}f-\underline{E}xperts (HDMoE) framework with two levels of MoE and \underline{R}andom \underline{F}eature \underline{R}eorganization (RFR) modules.In the first-level MoE, shared experts and routed experts are employed to remove redundant information and extract fine-grained specific features within each modality, while the second-level MoE facilitates fine-grained inter-modality feature decoupling. Besides, we design two RFR modules following each level of MoE to finely fuse intra- and inter-modality features, which can help the model capture more fine-grained relationships between modalities. Extensive experimental results on our private Liver Cancer (LC) and three TCGA public datasets confirm the effectiveness of our proposed method. Codes are available at https://github.com/ZJUMAI/HDMoE.

</details>


### [94] [FruitEnsemble: MLLM-Guided Arbitration for Heterogeneous ensemble in Fine-Grained Fruit Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20892)
*Enhui Yu,Junhui Li,Ruitong Lu,Jialu Li,Youshan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FruitEnsemble is a two-stage dynamic inference framework combining weighted ensemble of heterogeneous backbones with an MLLM expert arbitration mechanism (triggered at confidence < 0.6) for fine-grained fruit classification, achieving 70.49% accuracy on a new 306-category dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-grained fruit classification suffers from severe shortage of high-quality datasets and high visual similarity between classes, limiting real-world agricultural applications.

Method: (1) Constructed a 306-category dataset with 116,233 samples. (2) Stage 1: validation-calibrated weighted ensemble of heterogeneous backbones generates Top-3 candidate pool. (3) Stage 2: MLLM expert arbitration with CoT reasoning triggered when confidence < 0.6. (4) Hard sample-aware joint loss optimization.

Result: Achieves 70.49% classification accuracy, outperforming existing state-of-the-art models on the dataset.

Conclusion: FruitEnsemble provides an efficient, deployment-oriented solution for real-world agricultural visual sorting and quality inspection tasks.

Abstract: Fine-grained fruit classification is a critical yet challenging task in agricultural computer vision, primarily hindered by a severe shortage of high-quality datasets and the high visual similarity between classes. To address these challenges, we first constructed a comprehensive dataset comprising 306 fruit categories with 116,233 samples. Moreover, we propose FruitEnsemble, a practical two-stage dynamic inference framework designed to overcome the generalization limitations of static single-model architectures. In the first stage, FruitEnsemble employs a validation-calibrated weighted ensemble of heterogeneous backbones to generate a robust Top-3 candidate pool. To tackle difficult samples, we introduce an expert arbitration mechanism: when ensemble confidence falls below 0.6, a multimodal large language model (MLLM) is triggered to perform rigorous visual verification by integrating external botanical descriptions using Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. Furthermore, we optimized the training pipeline with a hard sample-aware joint loss. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FruitEnsemble achieves a classification accuracy of 70.49\% and outperforms existing state-of-the-art models. Our framework provides an efficient, deployment-oriented solution for real-world agricultural visual sorting and quality inspection tasks.

</details>


### [95] [VISTA: Technical Report for the Ego4D Short-Term Object Interaction Anticipation at EgoVis 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20901)
*Qiaohui Chu,Haoyu Zhang,Yisen Feng,Meng Liu,Weili Guan,Dongmei Jiang,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VISTA is a V-JEPA Integrated StillFast Temporal Anticipator that achieved first place in the Ego4D Short-Term Object Interaction Anticipation Challenge at EgoVis 2026 by combining object-centric spatial detection (Faster R-CNN) with frozen V-JEPA 2.1 temporal context for predicting future human-object interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To solve the Ego4D Short-Term Object Interaction Anticipation (STA) task, which requires predicting future human-object interactions (bounding box, noun, verb, time-to-contact, confidence) from egocentric video.

Method: A StillFast-style design combining: (1) a COCO-pretrained Faster R-CNN ResNet-50 FPN for spatial object proposals from the last high-resolution frame; (2) a frozen V-JEPA 2.1 temporal branch for clip-level egocentric context; (3) feature modulation and ROI-level context fusion to inject temporal info; (4) multi-head STA predictors for box refinement, noun/verb classification, time-to-contact regression, and confidence estimation; (5) ensemble of complementary predictions.

Result: First place in the EgoVis 2026 Ego4D STA Challenge on the official challenge server.

Conclusion: VISTA demonstrates the effectiveness of combining frozen large-scale video representations (V-JEPA 2.1) with object detection for short-term interaction anticipation in egocentric video.

Abstract: We propose VISTA, a V-JEPA Integrated StillFast Temporal Anticipator for the Ego4D Short-Term Object Interaction Anticipation (STA) Challenge at EgoVis 2026. Given an egocentric video timestamp, the task requires anticipating the next human-object interaction, including the future active object's bounding box, noun category, verb category, time-to-contact, and confidence score. VISTA follows a StillFast-style design that combines object-centric spatial detection with short-horizon temporal context. Specifically, a COCO-pretrained Faster R-CNN ResNet-50 FPN detector generates object proposals from the last observed high-resolution frame, while a frozen V-JEPA 2.1 temporal branch extracts clip-level egocentric context from the observed video. The temporal representation is injected into the detection pathway through feature modulation and ROI-level context fusion. The fused proposal features are then passed to multi-head STA predictors for box refinement, noun classification, verb classification, time-to-contact regression, and interaction confidence estimation. For the final submission, we further ensemble complementary predictions to improve robustness. Experimental results on the official challenge server show that VISTA achieves first place in the EgoVis 2026 Ego4D STA Challenge. Our code will be released at https://github.com/CorrineQiu/VISTA.

</details>


### [96] [JFAA: Technical Report for the EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Action Anticipation Challenge at EgoVis 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20904)
*Qiaohui Chu,Haoyu Zhang,Yisen Feng,Meng Liu,Weili Guan,Dongmei Jiang,Liqiang Nie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: JFAA is a JEPA-based method that achieves first place in the EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Action Anticipation Challenge by using a frozen V-JEPA encoder/predictor with a lightweight attentive probe and field-aware ensemble.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To effectively anticipate future actions (verbs, nouns, and actions) in egocentric kitchen videos by leveraging the representation learning and future prediction capabilities of the V-JEPA model.

Method: 1) Freezes V-JEPA 2.1 encoder and predictor to extract observed context features and near-future latent tokens. 2) Trains a lightweight attentive probe with separate task queries to predict verb, noun, and action logits. 3) Builds a field-aware ensemble over selected epoch-level predictions for improved robustness.

Result: Achieves first place in the EgoVis 2026 EPIC-KITCHENS-100 Action Anticipation Challenge on the official challenge server.

Conclusion: JFAA demonstrates that combining a frozen JEPA-based feature extractor with a simple attentive probe and field-aware ensemble yields state-of-the-art performance for action anticipation in egocentric videos.

Abstract: We propose JFAA, a JEPA-based Future Action Anticipation method for the EPIC-KITCHENS-100 (EK-100) Action Anticipation task. Inspired by the representation learning and future prediction ability of V-JEPA 2.1, JFAA uses a frozen encoder and predictor to extract observed context features and near-future latent tokens. A lightweight attentive probe is then trained to predict verb, noun, and action logits with separate task queries. To improve robustness, we further build a field-aware ensemble over selected epoch-level predictions, allowing each output field to benefit from its most reliable candidates. Experimental results on the official challenge server show that JFAA achieves first place in the EgoVis 2026 EK-100 Action Anticipation Challenge. Our code will be released at https://github.com/CorrineQiu/JFAA.

</details>


### [97] [SynCB: A Synergy Concept-Based Model with Dynamic Routing Between Concepts and Complementary Neural Branches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20908)
*Tores Julie,Sun Rémy,Sassatelli Lucile,Ancarani Elisa,Wu Hui-Yin,Precioso Frédéric*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes SynCB, a hybrid framework combining concept-based and neural network branches with a dynamic routing module that selects which branch to use per input, achieving better accuracy and human intervention responsiveness than prior models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concept-based models offer interpretability but lower accuracy than standard neural networks. Prior hybrid approaches improved accuracy but sacrificed human intervention capabilities. The authors aim to bridge this gap.

Method: SynCB combines a concept-based (CB) branch and a complementary neural branch with a trainable routing module that dynamically selects the branch per input. The branches share a common backbone. A test-time intervention policy and corresponding loss are introduced to improve human intervention responsiveness.

Result: SynCB achieves up to 3.9 percentage points higher task accuracy than full neural baselines and exceeds the strongest competitor in intervention performance by up to 6.43 percentage points across five datasets.

Conclusion: SynCB demonstrates that keeping CB and neural branches distinct with dynamic routing can achieve both high accuracy and strong human intervention responsiveness, outperforming existing hybrid models.

Abstract: Concept-based (CB) models provide interpretability and support test-time human intervention, while standard neural networks (NN) offer strong task performance but little transparency. Prior work has explored hybrid formulations that integrate concepts and additional representations to improve accuracy, often at the cost of human interventions. We introduce the \emph{Synergy Concept-Based Model (SynCB)} framework, that combines a CB branch with a complementary neural branch, and a trainable routing module that dynamically selects which branch to use for each input. Unlike prior models, which fuse residual and concept-based predictions, SynCB keeps the two branches distinct and coordinates them through the routing module. Moreover, both branches are learned jointly, allowing information sharing between the complementary neural branch and CB branches through their common backbone. To improve responsiveness to interventions, we further introduce a test-time intervention policy and a corresponding loss. Across five datasets and CB benchmarks, SynCB consistently achieves higher task accuracy while remaining more responsive to human interventions, surpassing the full neural baseline by up to 3.9 percentage points and exceeding the strongest competitor in intervention performance by up to 6.43 percentage points.

</details>


### [98] [FlowLong: Inference-time Long Video Generation via Manifold-constrained Tweedie Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20910)
*Jangho Park,Geon Yeong Park,Gihyun Kwon,Jong Chul Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a training-free, architecture-agnostic inference method for long video generation using overlapping sliding windows, Tweedie matching for manifold constraint and temporal consistency, and stochastic early-phase sampling to synchronize trajectories, achieving superior long-horizon video quality across multiple model types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long video generation methods suffer from quality degradation (bidirectional models) or drift errors and repetitive motion (autoregressive models), and are often tightly coupled to specific architectures.

Method: A sliding window approach with overlapping regions; Tweedie matching blends clean predictions from adjacent windows to enforce manifold constraint and temporal consistency; stochastic early-phase sampling injects noise in high-noise phases to synchronize trajectories, followed by deterministic ODE sampling for fine visual fidelity.

Result: Generates videos several times longer than native window length; outperforms training-free and autoregressive baselines in temporal consistency and visual quality; extends to audio-video joint generation and text-to-3DGS without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The proposed simple inference-time method effectively addresses long video generation challenges in an architecture-agnostic manner, achieving strong results across diverse generative models and modalities.

Abstract: Extending the generation horizon of video diffusion models to long sequences remains a long-standing and important challenge. Existing training-free approaches fall into two categories: extensions of bidirectional models, which are tightly coupled to specific architectures and suffer from quality degradation over long horizons, and autoregressive models, which accumulate drift errors due to exposure bias and tend to produce repetitive motion patterns. To address these issues, we propose a novel but simple inference-time approach for long video generation that is architecture-agnostic and requires no additional training. Our method generates long videos via overlapping sliding windows, where predicted clean samples from adjacent windows are blended via \emph{Tweedie matching} to enforce both \textbf{manifold constraint and temporal consistency} across overlap regions. \emph{Stochastic early-phase sampling} then synchronizes per-window trajectories by injecting fresh noise after each Tweedie matching correction in the high-noise phase, before transitioning to deterministic ODE sampling to preserve fine-grained visual fidelity. Applied to various video generation models, our method generates videos several times longer than the native window length while outperforming both training-free and autoregressive baselines in temporal consistency and visual quality, and further extends to audio-video joint generation and text-to-3DGS without any fine-tuning.

</details>


### [99] [RISE: Reliable Improvement in Self-Evolving Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20914)
*Chaoran Xu,Yingmao Miao,Pengfei Zhang,Hao Dou,Lei Sun,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RISE is a self-evolving framework for VLMs that uses fine-grained role alternation, a quality supervisor, and skill-aware dynamic balancing to enable reliable model improvement from unlabeled images, outperforming baselines across seven benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human-constructed supervision for post-training VLMs is costly and labor-intensive, especially for reasoning-intensive multimodal tasks. Current self-evolving methods face issues with coarse-grained role alternation, degrading question quality, and narrow question type distribution.

Method: RISE introduces three components: (1) fine-grained role alternation to shorten the feedback loop between questioner and solver; (2) a quality supervisor to ensure question validity and pseudo-label reliability; (3) skill-aware dynamic balancing to prevent mode collapse and maintain diverse skill coverage during evolution.

Result: Experiments on two VLM backbones across seven benchmarks show consistent improvement over base models, with broad and sustained gains.

Conclusion: RISE provides a more reliable and effective approach to self-evolution for VLMs, reducing reliance on costly human supervision while achieving strong performance gains.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved strong multimodal reasoning capabilities, but further improving them still relies heavily on large-scale human-constructed supervision for post-training. Such supervision is costly to obtain, especially for reasoning-intensive multimodal tasks where questions, answers, and feedback signals must be carefully designed. This motivates self-evolving learning, where a model improves itself through a dual-role closed loop: a questioner autonomously poses questions and a solver learns to solve them. However, we observe that current VLM self-evolving methods still face three major challenges: coarse-grained role alternation delays the interaction between question generation and solver adaptation; generated questions can progressively degrade in quality; and question types may collapse toward a narrow distribution. These issues limit the efficiency and reliability of self-evolution. Thus, we propose \textbf{RISE}, a reliable self-evolving framework for vision-language models. RISE is built on three complementary designs: fine-grained role alternation, which shortens the feedback loop between the questioner and the solver to improve efficiency; a quality supervisor, which improves question validity and pseudo-label reliability; and skill-aware dynamic balancing, which mitigates mode collapse and maintains broad skill coverage during evolution. Together, these components enable more reliable and effective self-evolution from unlabeled images. Experiments on two VLM backbones across seven benchmarks show that RISE consistently improves the base models, yielding broad and sustained gains. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/RISE.

</details>


### [100] [3D Reconstruction and Knowledge Distillation to Improve Multi-View Image Models to Explore Spike Volume Estimation in Wheat](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20940)
*Olivia Zumsteg,Jannis Widmer,Yann Bourdé,Norbert Kirchgessner,Andreas Hund,Lukas Roth,Paraskevi Nousi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hybrid 2D-3D knowledge distillation approach for wheat spike volume estimation, where a 3D point cloud network with multi-view images trains a purely image-based Transformer student model, achieving faster inference (1.4ms vs 160ms) with competitive accuracy (MAE ~640 mm³, correlation up to 0.82).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate wheat spike volume estimation is crucial for yield analysis and stress assessment, but existing methods are limited: LiDAR/ToF is sensitive to motion or outdoor conditions, 3D reconstruction is computationally expensive, and pure 2D image models lack explicit geometric information.

Method: (1) Train a rigid-invariant point cloud network with distance-based histogram features for pose-robust 3D representations. (2) Combine the 3D model with a multi-view image-based regulated Transformer (RT) in an ensemble. (3) Distill ensemble knowledge into a purely image-based student RT using feature-based or label-based distillation.

Result: Distilled RTs reduce MAE from 654.31 mm³ to 639.93 mm³ (feature-based) and 644.62 mm³ (label-based), increase correlation from 0.76 to 0.77 and 0.82, and reduce inference time from 160ms to 1.4ms per spike. Distillation also mitigates volume-dependent bias and reshapes latent representations toward geometry-awareness.

Conclusion: 3D-informed training of a 2D Transformer enables scalable, efficient, and accurate spike volume estimation for high-throughput field phenotyping.

Abstract: Accurate estimation of wheat spike volume is important for yield component analysis and stress resilience assessment, yet field-based measurement remains challenging. Active 3D sensing methods such as Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) or time-of-flight (ToF) are sensitive to plant motion or poorly suited to outdoor conditions, while 3D reconstructions are computationally expensive. Direct 2D image processing would offer computational advantages, but image-based models lack explicit geometric information. We therefore propose a hybrid 2D-3D approach with knowledge distillation during training while enabling efficient image-only inference. First, we train a rigid-invariant point cloud network using distance-based histogram features to obtain pose-robust geometric representations. We then combine the 3D model with a proposed multi-view image-based regulated Transformer (RT) in an ensemble architecture. Finally, we distill the ensemble knowledge into a purely image-based student model using either feature-based or label-based distillation. The two distilled RTs reduce the mean absolute error (MAE) from 654.31 mm$^3$ of the non-distilled RT to 639.93 mm$^3$ and 644.62 mm$^3$, and increase correlation from 0.76 to 0.77 and 0.82, respectively. At the same time, inference time is reduced from 160 ms to 1.4 ms per spike. Distillation further mitigates volume-dependent bias and reshapes the latent representation of the image model toward a geometry-aware shape. Our results demonstrate that 3D-informed training of a 2D Transformer allows for scalable and efficient spike volume estimation for high-throughput field phenotyping.

</details>


### [101] [PaintCopilot: Modeling Painting as Autonomous Artistic Continuation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20941)
*Yunge Wen,Yuancheng Shen,Paul Pu Liang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PaintCopilot is a co-creative AI painting assistant that predicts future brushstrokes from artistic dynamics (like LLMs continue text) rather than reconstructing a target image, supporting interactive workflows via three complementary models and differentiable brush representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural painting methods frame painting as pixel reconstruction toward a predefined reference image, which limits creative exploration. The authors aim to model painting as an open-ended artistic behavior that supports genuine co-creation between artists and AI.

Method: Three complementary models: (1) ViT-based Target Predictor infers artist intent from partial canvas; (2) Autoregressive Next Stroke Predictor generates temporally coherent strokes via flow matching; (3) VAE-based Region Sampler synthesizes localized stroke sequences. Built on three differentiable brush representations (Hard Round, Brush Tip, 2D Gaussian). Supports four workflows: Optimize History, Stroke Completion, Region Inpainting, Dynamic Brush.

Result: Case studies with professional artists demonstrate that PaintCopilot enables fluid co-creative workflows where artists and AI can continuously alternate control throughout the creative process.

Conclusion: Modeling painting as an autoregressive artistic behavior without target images enables more natural, open-ended human-AI co-creation in digital painting, analogous to LLM-style continuation of creative sequences.

Abstract: We present PaintCopilot, a co-creative neural painting assistant that models painting as an open-ended autoregressive artistic behavior conditioned on evolving canvas states and prior brushstroke history, without requiring a target image. Unlike existing neural painting methods that frame painting as pixel reconstruction toward a predefined reference, PaintCopilot predicts future strokes directly from learned artistic dynamics, analogous to how large language models continue text sequences from prior context.
  The framework proposes three complementary models: a ViT-based Target Predictor that infers artist intent from partial canvas observations, an autoregressive Next Stroke Predictor that generates temporally coherent brushstrokes via flow matching, and a VAE-based Region Sampler that synthesizes semantically localized stroke sequences on demand. Built on three differentiable brush representations (Hard Round, Brush Tip, and 2D Gaussian), the system supports four interactive workflows: Optimize History, Stroke Completion, Region Inpainting, and Dynamic Brush. Through case studies with professional artists, we demonstrate that PaintCopilot enables fluid co-creative painting workflows in which artists and AI continuously alternate control throughout the creative process.

</details>


### [102] [Bridging Structure and Language: Graph-Based Visual Reasoning for Autonomous Road Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20942)
*Lena Wild,Katie Z Luo,Marco Pavone*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces CRS, a graph-grounded framework that combines geometric road structure with open-vocabulary semantics for autonomous driving. Training small VLMs (2-4B parameters) on as few as 20-80 CRS-enriched scenes significantly improves structured road reasoning, outperforming large closed-source models. Analysis shows the main bottleneck is structured supervision, not model scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs lack geometric/relational grounding for precise road reasoning, while traditional modular systems (HD maps, topological graphs) are structurally precise but semantically rigid. The goal is to bridge this gap with a unified representation.

Method: CRS combines road geometry, topology, and semantics into a single graph-grounded representation. It generates diverse QA pairs via recursive graph queries with "grounding for free" traceability and chain-of-thought supervision. Small VLMs (2B/4B) are fine-tuned on 20-80 CRS-enriched scenes.

Result: State-of-the-art VLMs (including large closed-source models) struggle with structured road reasoning. Small models trained on few CRS scenes achieve stable gains. Failures shift from relational understanding (baseline) to attribute recognition (CRS-trained), indicating structured supervision as the key bottleneck.

Conclusion: Structured supervision via graph-grounded representations like CRS is more critical than model scale for road understanding. Future efforts should focus on enhancing structured reasoning supervision rather than scaling model parameters.

Abstract: Structured road understanding of lane geometry, topology, and traffic element relationships is foundational to safe autonomous driving. While vision-language models (VLMs) offer promising semantic flexibility, they lack the geometric and relational grounding required for precise road reasoning. Conversely, traditional modular systems, e.g., HD maps and topological road graphs, provide structural precision but remain semantically rigid. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Combined Road Substrate (CRS), a graph-grounded framework that makes geometric road structure and open-vocabulary semantics jointly executable in a single representation. CRS enables the automatic generation of compositionally complex and linguistically varied question-answer pairs via recursive graph queries, augmented with a "grounding for free" mechanism that ensures logical traceability to specific map elements, and procedurally extracted chain-of-thought supervision traces. We demonstrate that state-of-the-art VLMs - including large, closed-source models - struggle significantly with structured road reasoning, yet training a small 2- or 4-billion-parameter model with as few as 20 to 80 CRS-enriched scenes yields stable gains in compositional reasoning tasks of varying depth. Analysis of model behavior via verifiable reasoning traces reveals a systematic shift in failure modes: whereas baseline models fail at relational scene understanding, CRS-trained models reduce failures to attribute recognition, suggesting that the primary bottleneck in road understanding is not model scale, but the absence of structured supervision.

</details>


### [103] [Focus-then-Context: Subject-Centric Progressive Visual Token Reduction for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20950)
*Yulin Zhao,Yun Wang,Dehua Zheng,Borui jiang,Zheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPpruner is a vision token reduction method for VLMs that mimics human visual focus-then-context processing, achieving up to 2.53x speedup with minimal accuracy loss by iteratively identifying salient subjects and their contextual relationships.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision token reduction methods preserve only query-aligned subjects, failing to explore salient subjects and their contextual relationships, leading to suboptimal visual representation.

Method: A subject-centric progressive reduction paradigm with two modules: (1) Focus Identification Module that models visual saliency and semantic relevance to excavate a comprehensive visual subject spectrum; (2) Context-aware Structural Scanning Module that aggregates contextual cues from neighboring regions to restore global relational dependencies.

Result: Outperforms SOTA methods with up to 2.53x speedup (22.2% tokens retained) on Qwen2.5-VL and 67% FLOPs reduction on LLaVA with only 0.6% accuracy drop.

Conclusion: The Focus-then-Context paradigm effectively balances efficiency and performance, providing a biologically inspired solution for vision token reduction in VLMs.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face a bottleneck of prohibitive computational costs arising from massive visual token sequences during inference. Existing vision token reduction methods alleviate this burden, but they unintentionally preserve the isolated visual subject strictly aligned with the user's query, which fails to substantially explore salient subjects and their contextual relationships. In this paper, we propose SPpruner, a subject-centric progressive reduction paradigm that emulates the \textit{Focus-then-Context} mechanism of the human visual perception system. Specifically, we first construct a focus identification module to explicitly model the interplay between visual saliency and semantic relevance. Herein, it can excavate the comprehensive visual subject spectrum to ensure a high-fidelity representation of visual input. Subsequently, a context-aware structural scanning module is developed to aggregate contextual cues from neighboring regions. As such, it can effectively restore global relational dependencies to uphold the structural integrity of the preserved subjects. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our paradigm consistently outperforms SOTA methods, achieving up to 2.53 times speedup with only 22.2% of visual tokens retained in Qwen2.5-VL and a 67% FLOPs reduction on LLaVA with a negligible 0.6% accuracy drop.

</details>


### [104] [DrawMotion: Generating 3D Human Motions by Freehand Drawing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20955)
*Tao Wang,Lei Jin,Zhihua Wu,Qiaozhi He,Jiaming Chu,Yu Cheng,Junliang Xing,Jian Zhao,Shuicheng Yan,Li Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DrawMotion is a diffusion-based framework that generates 3D human motions from both text descriptions and hand-drawn stickman sketches, reducing user input time by ~46.7% while improving spatial and semantic control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users struggle to precisely convey intended motions through text alone; the paper aims to provide a more intuitive and efficient way to control motion generation using hand-drawn sketches as an additional condition.

Method: 1) Automatic hand-drawn stickman sketch generation algorithm; 2) Multi-Condition Module (MCM) for fusing text and drawing conditions in diffusion process; 3) Training-free guidance via classifier-guidance gradients in continuous feature space.

Result: The freehand drawing approach reduces user time by approximately 46.7% when generating motions aligned with user imagination, validated through quantitative experiments and user studies.

Conclusion: DrawMotion effectively combines text and hand-drawn conditions for fine-grained motion generation, offering a more intuitive and efficient user experience while maintaining motion fidelity.

Abstract: Text-to-motion generation, which translates textual descriptions into human motions, faces the challenge that users often struggle to precisely convey their intended motions through text alone. To address this issue, this paper introduces DrawMotion, an efficient diffusion-based framework designed for multi-condition scenarios. DrawMotion generates motions based on both a conventional text condition and a novel hand-drawing condition, which provide semantic and spatial control over the generated motions, respectively. Specifically, we tackle the fine-grained motion generation task from three perspectives: 1) freehand drawing condition. To accurately capture users' intended motions without requiring tedious textual input, we develop an algorithm to automatically generate hand-drawn stickman sketches across different dataset formats; 2) multi-condition fusion. We propose a Multi-Condition Module (MCM) that is integrated into the diffusion process, enabling the model to exploit all possible condition combinations while reducing computational complexity compared to conventional approaches; and 3) training-free guidance.
