To address this, we introduce the first automated and scalable data synthesis pipeline for intelligent editing, transforming diverse VQA data into complex and effective editing instructions with embedded questions and nested logic. This yields Uni-Edit-148k, pairing diverse reasoning-intensive instructions with high-quality edited images. Extensive experiments on BAGEL and Janus-Pro demonstrate that tuning solely on Uni-Edit achieves comprehensive enhancements across all three capabilities without any auxiliary operations.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [159] [Starlink Beacons for Passive LEO-Aided 9D Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20394)
*Nisanur Camuzcu,Tiep M. Hoang,Alireza Vahid*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents an LEO-aided hybrid navigation framework that fuses GPS, Starlink downlink beacons, and IMU using an EKF for 9D PNT estimation, demonstrating that Starlink Doppler-rate provides complementary PNT when GNSS is degraded or unavailable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNSS signals are easily blocked, interfered, or spoofed in contested environments (e.g., maritime settings), creating gaps in PNT availability. There is a practical need for an independent, ubiquitous aiding signal that can be passively tracked and fused with inertial sensing without dedicated infrastructure.

Method: The framework fuses GPS, Starlink downlink beacons, and an IMU in a 9D (position, velocity, attitude) PNT system using an extended Kalman filter (EKF). Doppler-rate is extracted from Starlink beacon tones with satellite ID association, benchmarked against OFDM-derived range observables, and integrated into inertial navigation. Evaluation uses Fisher-information predictions, Monte Carlo simulations, and hardware measurements.

Result: Starlink Doppler-rate provides meaningful complementary PNT information and can aid 9D estimation when GNSS is degraded or intermittently unavailable.

Conclusion: The proposed LEO-aided hybrid framework (GPS-Starlink-IMU) effectively sustains full navigation-state estimation in contested environments where GNSS is compromised, using passively tracked Starlink signals as an independent aiding source.

Abstract: Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) underpin positioning, navigation, and timing (PNT), yet their low-power signals are easily blocked or disrupted, leaving gaps in PNT availability in contested environments (e.g. maritime settings) where interference, spoofing, or denial can occur. A key practical need is an independent, ubiquitous aiding signal that can be tracked passively and fused with inertial sensing to sustain full navigation-state estimation without dedicated or cooperative infrastructure. This paper presents an end-to-end LEO-aided hybrid framework that fuses GPS, Starlink downlink beacons, and an inertial measurement unit (IMU) in a 9D (3D position, 3D velocity, and 3D attitude) PNT system using an extended Kalman filter (EKF). We (i) extract Doppler-rate from Starlink downlink beacon tones by associating measurements with satellite IDs, (ii) benchmark beacon Doppler-rate against OFDM-derived range observables under a common processing/estimation pipeline, and (iii) integrate the resulting observable into inertial navigation. We evaluate GPS/IMU, Starlink/IMU, and GPS-Starlink-IMU using Fisher-information predictions, Monte Carlo simulations, and hardware measurements. Results show that Starlink Doppler-rate provides meaningful complementary PNT information, and can aid 9D estimation when GNSS is degraded or intermittently unavailable.

</details>


### [160] [A New Approach for ARMA Pole Estimation Using Higher-Order Crossings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20483)
*Timothy I. Salsbury,Ashish Singhal*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a new method for estimating ARMA model poles using higher-order crossings, where crossing counts are transformed into pole estimates via the autocorrelation domain, requiring only crossing counts to be stored.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to develop a method for estimating ARMA poles that minimizes data storage requirements, using only crossing counts as features, which is valuable for control performance evaluation where pole locations correspond to characteristic equation roots.

Method: The proposed method estimates ARMA poles by transforming higher-order crossing counts into pole estimates through the autocorrelation domain.

Result: The method successfully estimates ARMA poles using only crossing counts as stored features, with the advantage of reduced data storage requirements.

Conclusion: Higher-order crossings can effectively estimate ARMA poles with minimal data storage, making the method practical for control loop performance evaluation.

Abstract: The paper describes a new method for estimating the poles of an ARMA model using higher-order crossings. The method involves transforming counts of crossing events into estimates of ARMA poles via the autocorrelation domain. An important advantage of the method is that the crossing counts are the only features that need to be stored from the original data. The poles of an ARMA model of a control loop correspond to the roots of the characteristic equation and are thus useful for evaluating control performance.

</details>


### [161] [AMAR: Lightweight Attention-Based Multi-User Activity Recognition from Wi-Fi CSI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20649)
*Amirhossein Mohammadi,Hina Tabassum*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: AMAR is a transformer-based framework for multi-user WiFi activity recognition that uses attention mechanisms and edge-cloud split architecture to simultaneously identify multiple human activities from composite CSI signals, nearly doubling accuracy over baselines while reducing bandwidth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing WiFi-based HAR focuses on single-user scenarios, but real-world deployments involve multiple concurrent users whose movements create overlapping CSI patterns that break conventional classification methods.

Method: AMAR formulates HAR as a set prediction problem using a transformer architecture with learnable query embeddings (specialized activity detectors). It employs an edge-cloud split: lightweight CNNs on edge devices extract features, then residual vector quantization compresses data, and the cloud performs attention-based set matching for final prediction.

Result: AMAR nearly doubles the rate of perfectly predicting all concurrent activities compared to baselines, achieves 53.4% F1-score (vs 45.6% best baseline), reduces occupancy estimation error by 74%, and substantially minimizes bandwidth usage.

Conclusion: The attention-based set prediction approach effectively addresses multi-user HAR challenges, and the edge-cloud split architecture enables practical deployment with significant bandwidth savings.

Abstract: Wi-Fi-based human activity recognition (HAR) has emerged as a promising approach for contactless sensing, leveraging channel state information (CSI) collected from wireless transceivers. While existing studies have primarily concentrated on single-user scenarios, real-world deployments often involve multi-user settings where concurrent users' movements induce overlapping CSI patterns that challenge conventional classification methods. To address this limitation, this paper introduces an attention-based multi-user activity recognition (AMAR) framework that formulates HAR as a set prediction problem. The transformer-based architecture in AMAR leverages learnable query embeddings acting as specialized activity detectors, enabling the simultaneous identification of multiple activities from composite CSI representations. Moreover, to address deployment constraints, AMAR is designed in an edge-cloud split architecture form where lightweight convolutional networks on edge devices perform initial feature extraction, followed by residual vector quantization that achieves substantial bandwidth reduction while preserving activity-discriminative information. The cloud component performs final activity prediction through attention-based set matching, enabling the system to handle varying occupancy levels. Across classroom, meeting-room, and empty-room environments, on average AMAR nearly doubles the rate of perfectly predicting all concurrent activities compared to the best baseline. Moreover, it achieves an $F_1$-score of 53.4% compared to 45.6% for the best benchmark, and reduces occupancy estimation error by 74%, while minimizing bandwidth substantially.

</details>


### [162] [Microwave Linear Analog Computer (MiLAC)-Aided MIMO Radar Sensing: Transmit Beamforming Design and DoA Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21020)
*Ziang Liu,Zheyu Wu,Bruno Clerckx*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes using a microwave linear analog computer (MiLAC) to enable massive MIMO radar sensing by moving linear operations to the analog domain, achieving the same performance as fully-digital systems while significantly reducing hardware cost, power consumption, and digital processing complexity for both transmit beamforming and receiver DoA estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling MIMO radar to massive arrays improves sensing but increases hardware cost, power consumption, and digital processing complexity. The authors aim to tackle these challenges by leveraging MiLAC, which has shown benefits in communications, and identifying its potential for radar sensing.

Method: The paper proposes MiLAC-aided transmit beamforming (formulating a weighted CRB minimization problem under lossless/reciprocal MiLAC constraints, solved via a PDD-based iterative algorithm) and MiLAC-aided receiver 2D-DFT-based DoA estimation (implemented by lossless reciprocal MiLAC in the analog domain).

Result: MiLAC-aided and fully-digital beamforming achieve the same CRB; the 2D DFT can be implemented by lossless reciprocal MiLAC enabling analog-domain DoA estimation. Numerical results confirm identical performance, while hardware cost and power consumption are reduced (low-resolution DACs, no RF chains/ADCs at receiver, no digital DFT operations).

Conclusion: MiLAC-aided massive MIMO radar achieves identical sensing performance to fully-digital systems but with substantially lower hardware cost, power consumption, and digital processing complexity, making it a promising approach for practical high-resolution radar sensing.

Abstract: Multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) radar has waveform diversity and large spatial degrees of freedom (DoFs), making it attractive for high-resolution sensing. Scaling MIMO radar to massive arrays can further improve sensing performance, but it also increases hardware cost, power consumption, and digital processing complexity. The microwave linear analog computer (MiLAC) can tackle these challenges by moving linear operations from the digital domain to the analog domain. MiLAC has shown promising benefits for communications in recent studies and this paper identifies its potential for radar sensing. Specifically, we consider both MiLAC-aided transmit beamforming and receiver-side two-dimensional discrete Fourier transform (2D-DFT)-based direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation. For transmit beamforming, we formulate a weighted Cramer Rao bound (CRB) minimization problem under lossless and reciprocal MiLAC constraints and propose a penalty dual decomposition (PDD)-based iterative algorithm to address the non-convex problem. We further prove that MiLAC-aided and fully-digital beamforming achieve the same CRB. For receiver processing, we show that the 2D DFT can be implemented by a lossless reciprocal MiLAC, which enables analog-domain DoA estimation without digital optimization. Numerical results confirm the theoretical finding and show that the MiLAC-aided approach achieves the same CRB and DoA estimation performance as the fully-digital benchmark. Meanwhile, hardware cost and power consumption are reduced because only low-resolution DACs are required at the transmitter, while RF chains and ADCs are eliminated at the receiver. Moreover, performing the 2D DFT in the analog domain eliminates all digital DFT operations for DoA estimation.

</details>


### [163] [Optimal Time Window and Frequency Bandwidth Parameter Combination for Subject-Specific Motor Imagery EEG Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21319)
*Matthew A. McCartney,Liisa A. Kivioja,Sonal S. Baberwal,Shirley Coyle*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This study systematically optimizes both time window and frequency band parameters for MI-EEG classification using CSP+LDA across 109 subjects, finding optimal parameters at (0,4)s and (4,12)Hz while showing significant performance differences across parameter combinations, supporting personalized model selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MI-EEG classification performance varies widely across studies because they use different post-cue time windows and frequency bands, and no prior work has simultaneously optimized both parameters at scale to understand their combined impact.

Method: Iteratively trained and tested subject-specific CSP+LDA models across 23 frequency bandwidths and 5 time windows using 109 subjects, followed by repeated measures ANOVA for statistical analysis.

Result: Optimal parameters across all subjects were (0,4)s time window and (4,12)Hz frequency band, but significant accuracy differences existed across parameter combinations, with subjects showing similar performance for many combinations.

Conclusion: Personalized models are effective for detecting optimal temporal-spectral parameter combinations, as MI EEG signals inherently vary across subjects.

Abstract: Motor-imagery (MI) EEG can be classified using supervised machine learning techniques such as Linear Discriminant Analysis applied to features extracted by Common Spatial Patterns. Performance of these models varies widely, possibly due to MI studies commonly utilising differing post-cue time windows and frequency bands to one another. This study aims to assess how the simultaneous optimisation of both these parameters impact MI classification performance. This is done by iteratively training and testing a series of subject-specific models on different combinations of frequency bandwidth and time window options across 109 subjects. This is followed by a statistical analysis using repeated measures ANOVA to uncover significant differences between different bandwidths and time windows in terms of accuracy across the patient cohort. The resulting visualisations and statistical tests show that there are, indeed, significant differences between both specific time windows and specific bandwidths in terms of accuracy. While the comparison of classification accuracies across 23 frequency bandwidths during five different time windows demonstrates an optimal temporal and spectral scale combination of (0, 4) s at the range of (4, 12) Hz across all subjects, the subjects demonstrate similar accuracies for other parameter combinations. These findings highlight the efficacy of personalised models to detect optimal temporal and spectral parameter combinations to best classify MI EEG signals that inherently vary across subjects.

</details>


### [164] [Advancements in Non-Invasive Neuroimaging: Exploring the Potential of Radar Technology for Brain Imaging and Tumour Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21350)
*Keniel Peart,Indu Bodala,Shelly Vishwakarma*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This study explores radar technology using Patch and Vivaldi antennas for non-invasive brain imaging and tumour detection, finding Patch antennas best for tumour localization and Vivaldi antennas for broader scans, with potential as a safer, accessible alternative to MRI/CT in low-resource settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To offer a safer, more accessible alternative to MRI and CT scans for brain imaging and tumour detection, especially in resource-limited environments.

Method: Using Ansys HFSS to simulate electromagnetic interactions in brain tissues, evaluating Patch and Vivaldi antennas for penetration, signal strength, and safety.

Result: Patch antennas are optimal for tumour localization; Vivaldi antennas are better for broader scanning applications.

Conclusion: Radar-based imaging is promising for safer, non-invasive diagnostics, but further research with diverse models and real patient data is needed.

Abstract: This study investigates radar technology for non-invasive brain imaging and tumour detection, offering an alternative to MRI and CT scans. Using Ansys HFSS to simulate electromagnetic interactions in brain tissues, we evaluate the penetration, signal strength, and safety of Patch and Vivaldi antennas. Results show Patch antennas are optimal for tumour localization, while Vivaldi antennas suit broader scanning applications. Although promising for safer, more accessible imaging, especially in resource-limited environments, further research with diverse models and actual patient data is essential to advance this technology in non-invasive medical diagnostics.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [165] [Stacked Intelligent Metasurfaces for Resolution-Constrained Near-Field Range Extension in 6G Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20298)
*Yajun Zhao*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper investigates how stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs) extend the usable near-field distance (UNFD) for 6G systems by improving wavefront control, revealing that multilayer stacking mitigates the distance-resolution dilemma but gains saturate with accumulated losses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between classical Rayleigh distance and the engineering-usable near-field distance (UNFD) in conventional single-layer metasurfaces, and to quantify how stacked intelligent metasurfaces can extend this range for 6G communication, sensing, and ISAC systems.

Method: A unified framework combining effective-phase-distance and discrete Green's-function operator perspectives; low-complexity analytical models for the distance-resolution dilemma; engineering correction factors for practical imperfections; higher-order phase framework for extreme near-field operation; numerical simulations validating performance.

Result: Lateral resolution degrades linearly with distance, axial resolution degrades quadratically (dominant bottleneck); increasing SIM layer count pushes UNFD closer to the Rayleigh limit; however, gains saturate as accumulated losses offset control flexibility benefits.

Conclusion: Stacked intelligent metasurfaces can effectively extend usable near-field range via cascaded wavefront shaping, but practical trade-offs between control flexibility and insertion losses limit the benefits of adding more layers.

Abstract: Near-field electromagnetic focusing is central to 6G communication, sensing, and integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems. However, for a fixed aperture, the resolution-constrained usable range of conventional single-layer transmissive metasurfaces is far shorter than the classical Rayleigh distance. This discrepancy stems not from fundamental near-field physics limitations, but from inadequate wavefront control, implementation imperfections, and the quadratic degradation of axial resolution with distance.To quantify this gap, we distinguish the Rayleigh distance from the engineering-usable near-field distance (UNFD), defined as the maximum range where predefined focusing gain and resolution requirements are jointly satisfied. Under identical aperture, feed, and input power constraints, we investigate how stacked intelligent metasurfaces (SIMs) extend UNFD via cascaded wavefront shaping. A unified framework combining effective-phase-distance and discrete Green's-function operator perspectives is developed, interpreting multilayer SIM design as an operator approximation problem for ideal near-field focusing.We derive low-complexity analytical models revealing an inherent distance-resolution dilemma: lateral resolution degrades linearly with distance, while axial resolution degrades quadratically, making axial performance the dominant bottleneck. Multilayer stacking mitigates this by improving wavefront curvature matching and reducing residual phase error. Engineering correction factors for practical imperfections and a higher-order phase framework for extreme near-field operation are incorporated. Numerical simulations confirm that increasing layer count pushes UNFD closer to the Rayleigh limit, but gains saturate as accumulated losses offset control flexibility benefits.

</details>


### [166] [Super-Beamforming in Holographic MIMO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20377)
*Andrea Pizzo,Angel Lozano*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Mutual coupling can enable super-beams with quadratic gain scaling relative to antenna count, rather than conventional linear scaling, when antenna losses are low and spacings are slightly below half-wavelength.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the conventional assumption that beamforming gain scales linearly with the number of antennas and to explore whether mutual coupling can be exploited to achieve super-beamforming with better scaling.

Method: The paper theoretically analyzes beamforming gain under relaxed half-wavelength spacing constraints, considering antenna losses and mutual coupling effects to derive conditions for quadratic scaling of endfire gain.

Result: Endfire gain can scale quadratically with the number of antennas when antenna losses are sufficiently small and spacings are slightly below half-wavelength, without requiring vanishing spacings.

Conclusion: The linear scaling of beamforming gain is not a fundamental limit; mutual coupling can be harnessed for super-beamforming with quadratic gain scaling under appropriate spacing and low-loss conditions.

Abstract: The conventional linear scaling of beamforming gain with the number of antennas is not a fundamental physical limitation, but rather a consequence of the half-wavelength spacings that minimize mutual coupling. Relaxing this constraint facilitates beamforming gains exceeding those of uncoupled arrays along specific directions. This paper shows that, when antenna losses remain sufficiently small, mutual coupling enables the synthesis of super-beams whose endfire gain scales quadratically with the number of antennas. Notably, this quadratic scaling does not necessarily require vanishing spacings, but emerges for spacings slightly below half wavelength as the array aperture increases.

</details>


### [167] [Rotatable Coupler Antenna Enhanced Wireless Network: Modeling and Coupler Rotation Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20535)
*Xiaodan Shao,Chuangye Shan,Weihua Zhuang,Xuemin Shen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces a rotatable coupler antenna (RCA) system that uses one active antenna and multiple rotatable passive couplers to achieve mechanical beamforming, improving SNR and spectral efficiency while reducing active antennas and RF chains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable spectrum and energy efficient wireless communication cost-effectively by exploiting flexible coupler antenna systems with fewer active components and RF chains.

Method: The authors model the RCA-enhanced point-to-point communication system using multi-port circuit theory, characterize mutual coupling as a function of 3D coupler rotations, and optimize rotations to maximize SNR via a spherical-cap conditional-gradient-based algorithm with cross-entropy-method initialization.

Result: Simulation results show the proposed RCA system significantly improves communication performance over benchmark schemes while requiring substantially fewer active antennas and RF chains.

Conclusion: The RCA system is a promising cost-effective solution for achieving high spectral and energy efficiency in wireless communications through mechanical beamforming with passive couplers.

Abstract: Flexible coupler antenna systems have recently received significant research interest due to their capability to intelligently reconfigure wireless channels by controlling coupler positions and/or rotations and dynamically exploiting mutual coupling. In this paper, we investigate a new type of flexible coupler antenna, termed rotatable coupler antenna (RCA), for enabling spectrum and energy efficient wireless communication cost-effectively. Specifically, an RCA consists of one fixed active antenna and multiple low-cost passive couplers, each of which can independently rotate in three-dimensional (3D) space, so as to collaboratively achieve mechanical beamforming without requiring additional radio-frequency (RF) chains for the couplers. We study an RCA-enhanced point-to-point communication system, where one RCA is deployed at the transmitter to serve a single user equipped with a fixed antenna. Based on multi-port circuit theory, we establish the channel model and characterize the mutual coupling coefficients as a function of coupler rotations. We formulate a new problem to maximize the received signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) at the user by optimizing the 3D rotations of all couplers, subject to practical coupler rotation constraints. To tackle this nonconvex problem, we develop a spherical-cap conditional-gradient-based algorithm with cross-entropy-method initialization. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed RCA system can significantly improve communication performance in comparison with benchmark schemes, while requiring substantially fewer active antennas and RF chains.

</details>


### [168] [Flexible Coupler Antenna for Wireless Networks: Opportunities and Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20560)
*Xiaodan Shao,Chuangye Shan,Weihua Zhuang,Xuemin Shen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces Flexible Coupler Antenna (FCA), a new cost-effective technique that uses low-cost passive couplers around fixed-position active antennas to achieve mechanical beamforming, improving wireless communication performance while significantly reducing antenna and RF chain costs compared to conventional active arrays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve wireless communication network performance through a cost-effective approach that reduces antenna and RF chain costs of conventional active arrays, while providing directional signal enhancement, especially for devices with stringent size, weight, and power (SWAP) constraints.

Method: FCA employs low-cost passive couplers around fixed-position active antennas; couplers can independently control their positions/rotations to collaboratively achieve mechanical beamforming for signal enhancement or nulling.

Result: FCA enables multiple performance enhancements: mechanical beamforming gain, path-loss reduction, fading mitigation, spatial multiplexing gain, interference suppression, and geometric gain. Numerical results verify substantial capacity gains in wireless networks.

Conclusion: FCA is a promising low-cost, low-form-factor technique that delivers significant wireless performance gains while reducing antenna and RF chain costs, making it particularly suitable for SWAP-constrained devices.

Abstract: Flexible coupler antenna (FCA) is a new technique that aims to improve the performance of wireless communication networks by smartly translating low-cost passive couplers around fixed-position active antennas to reshape the induced currents on the passive elements for radiation. Specifically, different couplers can independently control their positions/rotations at the transceiver and thereby collaboratively achieve mechanical beamforming for directional signal enhancement or nulling. The position and/or rotation reconfiguration of passive couplers provides a new and cost-effective means of enhancing wireless communication performance, while significantly reducing the antenna and radio-frequency (RF) chain costs of conventional active arrays. The compact and low form-factor structure of the FCA makes it particularly appealing for devices with stringent size, weight, and power (SWAP) constraints. In this article, we provide an overview of FCA to reveal its promising capabilities in wireless networks, including its system modeling, practical implementation, and competitive advantages over existing techniques. We present a variety of FCA-enabled performance enhancements in terms of mechanical beamforming gain, path-loss reduction, fading mitigation, spatial multiplexing gain, interference suppression, and geometric gain. Furthermore, we elaborate on the design challenges of FCA as well as promising solutions, and discuss the key applications of FCA in wireless networks. Finally, numerical results are presented to verify the substantial capacity gains enabled by FCA-aided transmission in wireless networks.

</details>


### [169] [Forward asymmetric numeral systems coding for natural language text compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20826)
*Mykyta Kharin,Igor Zavadskyi*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper combines forward modeling with asymmetric numeral systems (ANS) to create an adaptive ANS algorithm that achieves high speed, compression near entropy, and sometimes even below-entropy compression estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive ANS has remained a long-standing scientific and practical problem; the authors aim to fill this gap by combining modeling and adaptive coding methods.

Method: Combining forward modeling of the information source with adaptive coding methods based on asymmetric numeral systems (ANS).

Result: Achieves high encoding/decoding speeds and compression ratios close to Shannon entropy; forward modeling can yield estimated compressed message sizes less than entropy.

Conclusion: The proposed approach successfully implements adaptive ANS, solving a key problem in the field while maintaining high performance and compression efficiency.

Abstract: Compression based on asymmetric numeral systems (ANS) combines high encoding and decoding speeds with a compression ratio close to Shannon entropy, while forward modeling of the information source makes it possible to obtain an estimated compressed message size that is less than the entropy. This paper proposes combining these modeling and adaptive coding methods. In addition to ensuring high data processing speeds and compression ratios, this approach enables one to implement the adaptive ANS, which has long remained an important scientific and practical problem.

</details>


### [170] [Partially Observable Restless Bandits for Age-Optimal Scheduling over Markov Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21016)
*Xijun Wang,Shuying Gan,Yanzhi Huang,Xiaoyu Zhao,Chao Xu,Xiang Chen*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper addresses a timely scheduling problem in IoT systems with time-correlated Markov channels, where channel states are unknown to the controller. The problem is formulated as a partially observable restless multi-armed bandit, solved via Whittle's index policy with closed-form index derivation, achieving near-optimal performance under bandwidth constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The proliferation of IoT applications demands fresh information delivery. To characterize freshness, Age of Information (AoI) is used, but scheduling under unknown, time-correlated channel states and strict bandwidth constraints remains challenging.

Method: Formulates the problem as a partially observable restless multi-armed bandit, applies Lagrangian relaxation to decouple into sub-problems, proves threshold structure of optimal policies, establishes indexability, and derives a closed-form Whittle-like index for low-complexity scheduling.

Result: Simulations show the proposed index-based policies outperform baselines, remain close to the optimal policy or relaxed lower bound, and are especially effective under limited scheduling resources or large network sizes.

Conclusion: The Whittle's index-based scheduling approach effectively minimizes AoI under bandwidth constraints with low complexity, making it suitable for large-scale IoT systems with unknown time-correlated channels.

Abstract: There is a surge of need for fresh information with the overwhelming proliferation of the Internet of Things (IoT) applications. To characterize the information freshness perceived by the destination, the age of information (AoI) has been proposed. In this paper, we consider an IoT system with multiple devices sending status update packets to a central controller through time-correlated Markov channels and assume that the instantaneous channel states are not available to the central controller before making scheduling decisions. To ensure information freshness, we investigate a timely scheduling problem that minimizes the total expected time-average AoI under a strict communications bandwidth constraint. We formulate this problem as a partially observable restless multi-armed bandit problem. Using Lagrangian relaxation, we decouple the relaxed problem into multiple sub-problems and prove the threshold structure of their optimal policies. Armed with this property, we establish the indexability for the decoupled problem and design an algorithm to compute the Whittle's index. To reduce implementation complexity, we further derive the Whittle-like index in closed-form for low-complexity scheduling. Simulation results show that the proposed index-based policies outperform the baselines, remain close to the optimal policy or relaxed lower bound, and are especially effective when scheduling resources are limited or the network size is large.

</details>


### [171] [On Unified and Sharpened CMI Bounds for Generalization Errors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21056)
*Yang Lu,Matthias Frey,Margreta Kuijper,Jingge Zhu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a unified framework for information-theoretic generalization bounds using conditional mutual information (CMI), based on leave-m-out cross-validation, which reproduces and improves upon existing bounds while bridging the gap between MI- and CMI-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified description of existing CMI-based generalization bounds, bridge the gap between MI-based and CMI-based bounds, and develop sharper bounds than existing ones.

Method: Uses leave-m-out (LmO) cross-validation error framework to propose a unified CMI-based bound that generalizes existing CMI bounds; tightens CMI quantities by reducing conditional terms when loss is bounded.

Result: The unified framework reproduces known CMI bounds, develops sharper new bounds, works where existing bounds fail, and empirical results show improvement over prior bounds.

Conclusion: The proposed CMI-based framework provides a unified, tighter, and more flexible approach to information-theoretic generalization bounds.

Abstract: We present a new family of information-theoretic generalization bounds within the framework of conditional mutual information (CMI). Most of our results are established based on the leave-$m$-out (L$m$O) cross-validation error, with $m$ denoting the number of the hold-out supersamples. Under this setting, we propose a unified CMI-based bound, allowing to envelop and reproduce many known CMI-based bounds and also bridge the gap between the MI- and CMI-based bounds when $m$ tends to infinity. The proposed framework not only provides a unified description of the existing bounds but also develops new, sharper bounds. We show the benefits of the proposed bounds through several simple examples, where the existing results are either inapplicable or looser. Moreover, under the premise that the loss function is bounded, we tighten the CMI quantities involved in the proposed bounds by reducing the number of conditional terms, thereby enhancing the proposed framework. We show empirically that the resulting new bounds improve upon the previously known ones.

</details>


### [172] [On the Identifiability of Semi-Blind Estimation in Cell-Free Massive MIMO Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21181)
*Christian Forsch,Laura Cottatellucci*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the identifiability conditions for semi-blind joint channel estimation and data detection in cell-free massive MIMO networks, modeling the system as a bipartite random geometric graph and developing a probabilistic framework to determine when reliable recovery is possible based on network density and connectivity parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address pilot contamination in CF-MaMIMO networks by understanding the fundamental identifiability conditions that enable semi-blind JCD methods to work, providing system-level design guidelines.

Method: The authors model the network as a BRGG with spatial PPP distributions of APs and UEs, approximate it with a surrogate independent-edge random graph, and develop a recursive probabilistic analysis to characterize identifiability conditions.

Result: The analysis reveals an identifiability region as a function of AP/UE densities and connectivity radius, validated by Monte Carlo simulations that also confirm the accuracy of the graph approximation.

Conclusion: The framework provides system-level insights and practical guidelines for selecting deployment parameters and pilot sequence lengths to enable reliable semi-blind recovery in large-scale CF-MaMIMO systems.

Abstract: Semi-blind joint channel estimation and data detection (JCD) is a promising approach to mitigate pilot contamination in cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-MaMIMO) networks. The effectiveness of such methods fundamentally depends on identifiability, i.e., the ability to unambiguously recover the unknown channel coefficients and transmitted data signals from the received uplink observations. In this work, we investigate the identifiability of semi-blind JCD from a large-scale system design perspective. We consider a CF-MaMIMO network in which access points (APs) and user equipments (UEs) are spatially distributed according to Poisson point processes (PPPs). The resulting network topology is modeled as bipartite random geometric graph (BRGG) that captures local connectivity induced by wireless propagation. To enable a tractable analysis, the spatially dependent graph model is approximated by a surrogate independent-edge random graph with matched degree distributions. Building on this model, we develop a recursive probabilistic analysis that characterizes the conditions under which semi-blind recovery succeeds with high probability. The proposed analysis reveals an identifiability region as a function of key system parameters, including AP and UE densities and the connectivity radius beyond which channel coefficients are assumed negligible. Monte Carlo simulations validate the predicted identifiability region and assess the accuracy of the proposed graph approximation. The proposed framework provides system level insights into how network density and connectivity affect identifiability in large-scale CF-MaMIMO systems and offers guidelines for selecting deployment parameters and pilot sequence lengths that enable reliable semi-blind recovery.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [173] [SOLAR: A Self-Optimizing Open-Ended Autonomous Agent for Lifelong Learning and Continual Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20189)
*Nitin Vetcha,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SOLAR is a self-optimizing LLM agent that uses parameter-level meta-learning and multi-level RL to adapt to new domains without gradient-based fine-tuning, outperforming baselines across diverse reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle in dynamic real-world settings due to concept drift and high cost of gradient-based adaptation; traditional fine-tuning fails on non-stationary data streams and causes catastrophic forgetting.

Method: SOLAR uses parameter-level meta-learning (treating weights as exploration environment), consolidates a strong common-sense prior, employs multi-level reinforcement learning for autonomous discovery of adaptation strategies, and maintains an evolving episodic memory buffer to balance plasticity and stability.

Result: SOLAR outperforms strong baselines on common-sense, mathematical, medical, coding, social, and logical reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: SOLAR marks significant progress toward autonomous agents capable of lifelong adaptation in evolving environments without gradient-based fine-tuning.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable success of large language models (LLMs), they still face bottlenecks while deploying in dynamic, real-world settings with primary challenges being concept drift and the high cost of gradient-based adaptation. Traditional fine-tuning (FT) struggles to adapt to non-stationary data streams without resulting in catastrophic for getting or requiring extensive manual data curation. To address these limitations within the streaming and continual learning paradigm, we propose the Self-Optimizing Lifelong Autonomous Reasoner (SOLAR) which is an open-ended autonomous agent that leverages parameter-level meta-learning to self-improve, treating model weights as an environment for exploration. It initiates the process by consolidating a strong prior over common-sense knowledge making it effective for transfer-learning. By utilizing a multi-level reinforcement learning approach, SOLAR autonomously discovers adaptation strategies, enabling efficient test-time adaptation to unseen domains. Crucially, SOLAR maintains an evolving knowledge base of valid modification strategies, implicitly acting as an episodic memory buffer to balance plasticity (adaptation to new tasks) and stability (retention of meta-knowledge). Experiments demonstrate that SOLAR outperforms strong baselines on common-sense, mathematical, medical, coding, social and logical reasoning tasks, marking a significant step toward autonomous agents capable of lifelong adaptation in evolving environments.

</details>


### [174] [Tool-Augmented Agent for Closed-loop Optimization,Simulation,and Modeling Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20190)
*Liyuan Deng,Shujian Deng,Yongkang Chen,Yongkang Dai,Zhihang Zhong,Linyang Li,Xiao Sun,Yilei Shi,Huaxi Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: COSMO-Agent is an RL framework that teaches LLMs to perform closed-loop CAD-CAE optimization by orchestrating external tools and revising parametric geometries under multi-constraint rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the CAD-CAE semantic gap where iterative design-simulation optimization is bottlenecked by the lack of automated feedback translation into valid geometric edits under coupled constraints.

Method: Proposes a tool-augmented RL framework (COSMO-Agent) that casts CAD generation, CAE solving, result parsing, and geometry revision as an interactive RL environment, with a multi-constraint reward for feasibility, toolchain robustness, and structured output validity. Also contributes an industry-aligned dataset with 25 component categories.

Result: COSMO-Agent training substantially improves small open-source LLMs for constraint-driven design, outperforming both large open-source and strong closed-source models in feasibility, efficiency, and stability.

Conclusion: The proposed RL framework effectively enables LLMs to learn closed-loop CAD-CAE optimization, making small models competitive with or superior to much larger counterparts.

Abstract: Iterative industrial design-simulation optimization is bottlenecked by the CAD-CAE semantic gap: translating simulation feedback into valid geometric edits under diverse, coupled constraints. To fill this gap, we propose COSMO-Agent (Closed-loop Optimization, Simulation, and Modeling Orchestration), a tool-augmented reinforcement learning (RL) framework that teaches LLMs to complete the closed-loop CAD-CAE process. Specifically, we cast CAD generation, CAE solving, result parsing, and geometry revision as an interactive RL environment, where an LLM learns to orchestrate external tools and revise parametric geometries until constraints are satisfied. To make this learning stable and industrially usable, we design a multi-constraint reward that jointly encourages feasibility, toolchain robustness, and structured output validity. In addition, we contribute an industry-aligned dataset that covers 25 component categories with executable CAD-CAE tasks to support realistic training and evaluation. Experiments show that COSMO-Agent training substantially improves small open-source LLMs for constraint-driven design, exceeding large open-source and strong closed-source models in feasibility, efficiency, and stability.

</details>


### [175] [OSCToM: RL-Guided Adversarial Generation for High-Order Theory of Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20423)
*Sharmin Sultana Srishty,Kazi Mahathir Rahman,Malaika Parizat Sakkhi,Samia Shahid Prianna,Shaikhul Islam Sinat*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OSCToM is a new approach that uses reinforcement learning, domain-specific languages, and surrogate models to generate observer-self belief conflicts for improving Theory of Mind reasoning in LLMs, achieving 76% accuracy on FANToM (vs 0.2% for ExploreToM) with 6x more efficient data synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with Theory of Mind reasoning in complex social settings requiring recursive belief tracking and handling information asymmetries. Existing benchmarks like ExploreToM fail to adequately test these nested belief conflicts.

Method: OSCToM combines reinforcement learning, an extended domain-specific language, and compositional surrogate models to generate observer-self conflict scenarios where an observer's view of another agent conflicts with their own belief state.

Result: OSCToM-8B achieves 76% accuracy on FANToM (vs ExploreToM's 0.2%), improves on ExploreToM's FANToM results, remains competitive on Hi-ToM and BigToM, and offers 6x more efficient data synthesis.

Conclusion: Targeted training data with observer-self conflicts can help smaller language models handle advanced cognitive reasoning more efficiently and effectively than prior approaches.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) perform well on many language tasks, but their Theory of Mind (ToM) reasoning is still uneven in complex social settings. Existing benchmarks, including ExploreToM, do not always test the recursive beliefs and information asymmetries that make these settings difficult. This paper presents OSCToM (Observer-Self Conflict Theory of Mind), an approach for modeling nested belief conflicts in LLM-based ToM tasks. The key case is one in which an observer's view of another agent conflicts with the observer's own belief state. Such cases go beyond simple perspective-taking and require recursive, multi-layered reasoning. OSCToM combines reinforcement learning (RL), an extended domain-specific language, and compositional surrogate models to generate observer-self conflicts. In our experiments, OSCToM-8B gives the best overall result among the systems tested. It improves on the reported ExploreToM results on FANToM and remains competitive on Hi-ToM and BigToM. On the information-asymmetric FANToM benchmark, OSCToM reaches 76% accuracy, compared with the 0.2% reported by ExploreToM. The data-synthesis procedure is also 6x more efficient, indicating that targeted training data can help smaller models handle advanced cognitive reasoning. The project code is available at https://github.com/sharminsrishty/osct.

</details>


### [176] [AgentCo-op: Retrieval-Based Synthesis of Interoperable Multi-Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20425)
*Shuaike Shen,Wenduo Cheng,Shike Wang,Mingqian Ma,Jian Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentCo-op is a retrieval-based framework that composes reusable skills, tools, and agents into executable workflows via typed artifact handoffs, with bounded self-guided repair when failures occur, achieving strong results on genomics case studies and six benchmarks while reducing costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing multi-agent workflows in open-ended scientific settings is difficult due to lack of curated training sets, reliable metrics, and standardized interfaces between existing tools and agents.

Method: AgentCo-op uses retrieval-based synthesis to compose reusable skills, tools, and external agents into workflows via typed artifact handoffs, then applies bounded self-guided local repair to fix implicated components when execution evidence indicates failure.

Result: In two open-world genomics case studies, AgentCo-op successfully composed independently developed agents without redesign or global search. On six benchmarks, it achieved best on four and best average score under unified backbone, while consistently reducing per-task cost relative to multi-agent baselines.

Conclusion: Retrieval-based synthesis can extend automated agentic workflow design beyond benchmark-optimized graphs to open-world workflows built from existing agents, tools, and typed artifacts.

Abstract: Designing multi-agent workflows is especially difficult in open-ended scientific settings where tasks lack curated training sets, reliable scalar evaluation metrics, and standardized interfaces between existing tools and agents. We propose AgentCo-op, a retrieval-based synthesis framework that composes reusable skills, tools, and external agents into executable workflows through typed artifact handoffs, then applies bounded self-guided local repair to implicated components when execution evidence indicates failure. In two open-world genomics case studies, AgentCo-op composes independently developed scientific agents and external tool repositories into auditable workflows without redesigning them or running global topology search. It coordinates specialized agents for spatial transcriptomics and gene-set interpretation to enable collaborative discovery from spatial transcriptomics data, and builds a parallel workflow for cross-modality marker analysis on single-cell multiome data. AgentCo-op can also import a searched workflow as a structural prior and improve it by grounding nodes with retrieved components and applying local repair, showing that synthesis and search are complementary. On six coding, math, and question-answering benchmarks, AgentCo-op achieves the best result on four benchmarks and the best average score under a unified backbone setting, while consistently reducing per-task cost relative to multi-agent baselines. Together, these results suggest that retrieval-based synthesis can extend automated agentic workflow design beyond benchmark-optimized agent graphs to open-world workflows built from existing agents, tools, and typed artifacts.

</details>


### [177] [High Quality Embeddings for Horn Logic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20467)
*Yifan Zhang,Yasir White,Dean Clark,Joseph Sanchez,Jevon Lipsey,Ashely Hirst,Jeff Heflin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes three improvements for training embeddings of logical statements using triplet loss: generating anchors with repeated terms, balancing easy/medium/hard examples, and emphasizing hard examples during training. Experiments compare embeddings across knowledge bases to identify characteristics suited to reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural networks can improve the efficiency of logical reasoning by ranking choices, but this requires effective numeric embeddings of logical statements. The paper aims to develop better embedding approaches that lead to improved downstream reasoning performance.

Method: Three ideas are introduced: (1) generating anchors more likely to have repeated terms, (2) generating positive/negative examples with a balance of easy, medium, and hard difficulties, and (3) periodically emphasizing the hardest examples during training using triplet loss.

Result: The embeddings are evaluated through experiments comparing different approaches across various knowledge bases, analyzing what characteristics make embeddings well-suited for specific reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed embedding generation techniques improve downstream reasoning performance, with different embedding characteristics proving advantageous for different reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Neural networks can be trained to rank the choices made by logical reasoners, resulting in more efficient searches for answers. A key step in this process is creating useful embeddings, i.e., numeric representations of logical statements. This paper introduces and evaluates several approaches to creating embeddings that result in better downstream results. We train embeddings using triplet loss, which requires examples consisting of an anchor, a positive example, and a negative example. We introduce three ideas: generating anchors that are more likely to have repeated terms, generating positive and negative examples in a way that ensures a good balance between easy, medium, and hard examples, and periodically emphasizing the hardest examples during training. We conduct several experiments to evaluate this approach, including a comparison of different embeddings across different knowledge bases, in an attempt to identify what characteristics make an embedding well-suited to a particular reasoning task.

</details>


### [178] [\ECUAS{n}: A family of metrics for principled evaluation of uncertainty-augmented systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20490)
*Lautaro Estienne,Erik Ernst,Matías Vera,Pablo Piantanida,Luciana Ferrer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper critiques current evaluation methods for uncertainty-augmented systems (outputting both predictions and uncertainty scores) and proposes a new family of metrics called ECUAS(n), which are proper scoring rules that better assess overall decision-making performance by balancing prediction errors and uncertainty quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation approaches for UA systems (using separate metrics, fixed rejection costs, or coverage-risk curves) are inadequate for assessing overall decision-making performance under uncertainty.

Method: Proposes ECUAS(n) metrics, a family of proper scoring rules where parameter n controls the trade-off between incorrect prediction costs and imperfect uncertainty costs, applicable to classification and generation tasks.

Result: Theoretical advantages of ECUAS(n) are demonstrated, and empirical validation is conducted on diverse classification and generation datasets, including a manually annotated TriviaQA subset.

Conclusion: ECUAS(n) metrics provide a more adequate and flexible framework for evaluating uncertainty-augmented systems in high-stakes decision-making.

Abstract: In high-stakes automated decision-making, access to predictive uncertainty is essential for enabling users -- human or downstream systems -- to accept or reject predictions based on application-specific cost trade-offs. Such uncertainty-augmented (UA) systems -- i.e., systems that output both predictions and uncertainty scores -- are currently being assessed in the literature in a variety of ways, using separate metrics to evaluate the predictions and the uncertainty scores, setting a cost function with a fixed rejection cost or integrating over a coverage-risk curve. We argue that these evaluation approaches are inadequate for assessing overall performance of the UA system for decision making under uncertainty and propose a novel family of metrics, \ECUAS{n}, formulated as proper scoring rules for the task of interest. The parameter $n$ controls the trade-off between the cost of incorrect predictions and imperfect uncertainties depending on the needs of the use-case. We demonstrate the advantages of the \ECUAS{n} metrics both theoretically and empirically, through experiments on diverse classification and generation datasets, including a manually annotated subset of TriviaQA.

</details>


### [179] [Open-World Evaluations for Measuring Frontier AI Capabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20520)
*Sayash Kapoor,Peter Kirgis,Andrew Schwartz,Stephan Rabanser,J. J. Allaire,Rishi Bommasani,Harry Coppock,Magda Dubois,Gillian K Hadfield,Andrew B. Hall,Sara Hooker,Seth Lazar,Steve Newman,Dimitris Papailiopoulos,Shoshannah Tekofsky,Helen Toner,Cozmin Ududec,Arvind Narayanan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that benchmark evaluations have limitations and proposes open-world evaluations—long-horizon, real-world tasks assessed qualitatively. It introduces CRUX for conducting such evaluations and demonstrates it with an AI agent developing and publishing an iOS app, showing early capability warnings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark evaluations can both overstate and understate real-world AI capabilities because they focus on precisely specified, easily gradable tasks with low budgets and short time horizons, missing messy, real-world performance.

Method: The authors propose open-world evaluations (long-horizon, real-world tasks with small-sample qualitative analysis) and introduce CRUX (Collaborative Research for Updating AI eXpectations) as a project for conducting them regularly. A case study tasked an AI agent with developing and publishing an iOS app to the App Store.

Result: The AI agent completed the iOS app development and publication task with only a single avoidable manual intervention, suggesting open-world evaluations can provide early warning of soon-to-be-widespread capabilities.

Conclusion: Open-world evaluations are a valuable complement to benchmarks. The authors recommend designing and reporting such evaluations to better track frontier AI progress in real-world contexts.

Abstract: Benchmark-based evaluation remains important for tracking frontier AI progress. But it can both overstate and understate deployed capability because it privileges tasks that can be precisely specified, automatically graded, easy to optimize for, and run with low budgets and short time horizons. We advocate for a complementary class of evaluations, which we term open-world evaluations: long-horizon, messy, real-world tasks assessed through small-sample qualitative analysis rather than benchmark-scale automation. In this paper we survey recent open-world evaluations, identify their strengths and limitations, and introduce CRUX (Collaborative Research for Updating AI eXpectations), a project for conducting such evaluations regularly. As a first instance, we task an AI agent with developing and publishing a simple iOS application to the Apple App Store. The agent completed the task with only a single avoidable manual intervention, suggesting that open-world evaluations can provide early warning of capabilities that may soon become widespread. We conclude with recommendations for designing and reporting open-world evals.

</details>


### [180] [AgentAtlas: Beyond Outcome Leaderboards for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20530)
*Parsa Mazaheri,Kasra Mazaheri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentAtlas introduces a comprehensive evaluation framework for LLM agents, revealing that removing explicit label guidance collapses all models to a narrow accuracy floor, and no single model excels across all key behavioral dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent benchmarks are fragmented, using different evaluation metrics (task success, tool-call validity, consistency, safety, robustness), and the field has recognized that a single accuracy score is insufficient for evaluating deployable agents.

Method: The paper proposes four components: (1) a six-state control-decision taxonomy (Act/Ask/Refuse/Stop/Confirm/Recover); (2) a nine-category trajectory-failure taxonomy with hierarchical labels; (3) taxonomy-aware vs. taxonomy-blind comparison to measure prompt-supervision effects; and (4) a benchmark-coverage audit across six behavioral axes. An eight-model evaluation (1,342 items) was conducted under both prompt modes.

Result: Removing explicit label menu drops trajectory accuracy by 14-40 percentage points, compressing all models to a 0.54-0.62 floor. No single model wins across all three evaluation dimensions: control accuracy, trajectory diagnosis, and tool-context utility retention.

Conclusion: The work serves as a measurement-protocol demonstration rather than a benchmark release, highlighting that prompt supervision significantly inflates perceived agent capability and that multidimensional evaluation is essential.

Abstract: Large language model agents now act on codebases, browsers, operating systems, calendars, files, and tool ecosystems, but the benchmarks used to evaluate them are fragmented: each emphasizes a different unit of measurement (final task success, tool-call validity, repeated-pass consistency, trajectory safety, or attack robustness). A line of 2024-2025 work has converged on the diagnosis that a single accuracy column is no longer the right unit of comparison for deployable agents. AgentAtlas extends this line of work with four components: (i) a six-state control-decision taxonomy (Act / Ask / Refuse / Stop / Confirm / Recover); (ii) a nine-category trajectory-failure taxonomy with two orthogonal hierarchical labels (primary_error_source, impact); (iii) a taxonomy-aware vs. taxonomy-blind methodology that measures how much of a model's apparent capability comes from the supervision in the prompt; and (iv) a benchmark-coverage audit mapping fifteen agent benchmarks against six behavioral axes. To demonstrate the methodology we run a small fixed eight-model set (1,342 generated items, four frontier closed and four open-weight) under both prompt modes. Removing the explicit label menu drops every model's trajectory accuracy by 14-40 pp to a tight 0.54-0.62 floor regardless of family, and no single model wins on all three of control accuracy, trajectory diagnosis, and tool-context utility retention. We treat the synthetic run as a measurement-protocol demonstration, not a benchmark release.

</details>


### [181] [Personality Engineering with AI Agents: A New Methodology for Negotiation Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20554)
*Michelle A. Vaccaro,Jared R. Curhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces "personality engineering," a methodology using AI agents to precisely manipulate and evaluate negotiator personality based on the interpersonal circumplex (warmth and dominance), enabling rigorous testing of classic negotiation theories that have been difficult to study under controlled conditions due to human limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans struggle to balance competing demands in negotiation (empathizing vs. asserting, concern for self vs. other), making it difficult for researchers to rigorously test classic negotiation theories under controlled conditions.

Method: The authors propose "personality engineering," a methodology leveraging AI agents to precisely parameterize, manipulate, and evaluate negotiator personality, using the interpersonal circumplex (warmth and dominance) as a foundational coordinate system.

Result: AI agents overcome human limitations (lack of precision, repertoire, consistency, scalability), enabling a new class of controlled experiments to advance negotiation theory.

Conclusion: This approach offers both a rigorous methodology for testing classic negotiation theories and a practical guide for designing the personalities of AI negotiation agents.

Abstract: According to canonical negotiation theory, people's success in a negotiation depends on how well they balance competing demands--empathizing and asserting, demonstrating concern for other and concern for self, being soft on the people and hard on the problem. Yet people struggle to manage these tensions, so researchers have lacked the ability to rigorously test the field's prescriptions under controlled conditions. AI agents do not face the same limitations, and their precision, repertoire, consistency, and scalability enable a new class of experiments to contribute to negotiation theory. In this article, we introduce personality engineering: a methodology that uses AI agents to precisely parameterize, manipulate, and evaluate negotiator personality. We propose using the interpersonal circumplex--and its two core dimensions of warmth and dominance--as a foundational coordinate system for the field. This approach offers both a rigorous methodology for testing classic negotiation theories and a practical guide for designing the personalities of AI negotiation agents.

</details>


### [182] [Mahjax: A GPU-Accelerated Mahjong Simulator for Reinforcement Learning in JAX](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20577)
*Soichiro Nishimori,Shinri Okano,Keigo Habara,Sotetsu Koyamada,Eason Yu,Masashi Sugiyama*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mahjax is a high-speed GPU-accelerated Riichi Mahjong environment in JAX, enabling up to 2M steps/s on 8 A100 GPUs, designed for tabula rasa reinforcement learning research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Riichi Mahjong presents complex imperfect-information challenges similar to real-world RL problems, but prior work relied on supervised pre-training from human logs. The authors aim to support tabula rasa (from-scratch) learning algorithms.

Method: The authors built Mahjax, a fully vectorized Riichi Mahjong environment implemented in JAX, leveraging GPU parallelism for large-scale rollout generation. They also provide a visualization tool for debugging.

Result: Mahjax achieves up to 2M (no-red) and 1M (red) steps/second on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. Agents trained in the environment improve their rank against baseline policies.

Conclusion: Mahjax successfully enables efficient tabula rasa RL training in Riichi Mahjong, offering high throughput and validated learning performance, making it a useful platform for future research.

Abstract: Riichi Mahjong is a multi-player, imperfect-information game characterized by stochasticity and high-dimensional state spaces. These attributes present a unique combination of challenges that mirror complex real-world decision-making problems in reinforcement learning. While prior research has heavily relied on supervised learning from human play logs to pre-train the policy, algorithms capable of learning \textit{tabula rasa} (from scratch) offer greater potential for general applicability, as evidenced by the AlphaZero lineage. To facilitate such research, we introduce \textbf{Mahjax}, a fully vectorized Riichi Mahjong environment implemented in JAX to enable large-scale rollout parallelization on Graphics Processing Units (GPUs). We also provide a high-quality visualization tool to streamline debugging and interaction with trained agents. Experimental results demonstrate that Mahjax achieves throughputs of up to \textbf{2 million} and \textbf{1 million steps per second} on eight NVIDIA A100 GPUs under the no-red and red rules, respectively. Furthermore, we validate the environment's utility for reinforcement learning by showing that agents can be trained effectively to improve their rank against baseline policies.

</details>


### [183] [From Automated to Autonomous: Hierarchical Agent-native Network Architecture (HANA)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20608)
*Binghan Wu,Shoufeng Wang,Yunxin Liu,Ya-Qin Zhang,Joseph Sifakis,Ye Ouyang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hierarchical multi-agent architecture with a Dual-Driven Orchestrator and self-aware agents that achieves Level 4/5 Autonomous Networks, validated in 5G Core environments with 86% MTTR reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous network operations rely on rigid scripts that lack cognitive agency, failing to handle off-nominal conditions, necessitating a shift from static automation to agent-native intelligence.

Method: A hierarchical multi-agent reference architecture featuring a Dual-Driven Orchestrator coordinating specialized Executive Agents, supported by shared Public Memory and integrated agent self-awareness for harmonizing deliberative strategic governance with reflexive fault recovery.

Result: The system sustains critical throughput under congestion and reduces Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) by 86% in a 5G Core environment.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture demonstrates efficacy in unifying strategic planning with operational resilience, confirming its potential for achieving high-level (Level 4/5) autonomous networks.

Abstract: Realizing Level 4/5 Autonomous Networks (AN) demands a shift from static automation to agent-native intelligence. Current operations, reliant on rigid scripts, lack the cognitive agency to handle off-nominal conditions. To address this, this letter proposes a hierarchical multi-agent reference architecture enabling high-level autonomy. The framework features a Dual-Driven Orchestrator that coordinates specialized Executive Agents, supported by a shared Public Memory for unified domain knowledge. A key innovation is the integration of agent self-awareness, which empowers the system to harmonize deliberative strategic governance with reflexive fault recovery. We instantiate and validate this architecture within a 5G Core environment. Case studies demonstrate that the system sustains critical throughput under congestion and reduces Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) by 86%, confirming its efficacy in unifying strategic planning with operational resilience.

</details>


### [184] [COAgents: Multi-Agent Framework to Learn and Navigate Routing Problems Search Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20618)
*Oleksandr Yakovenko,Mahdi Mostajabdaveh,Cheikh Ahmed,Abdullah Ali Sivas,Xiaorui Li,Zirui Zhou,Mao Kun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: COAgents is a cooperative multi-agent framework for Vehicle Routing Problems that models the search process as a Partial Search Graph, using specialized agents for node selection, move selection, and jump decisions to guide optimization. It achieves state-of-the-art results on VRPTW and competitive performance on CVRP benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vehicle Routing Problems are computationally intractable at scale. Traditional heuristics rely on handcrafted rules and struggle to generalize across diverse instances, motivating the need for a more adaptive and generalizable search framework.

Method: COAgents models the search process as a graph (nodes=solutions, edges=local refinements or jumps). It dynamically constructs a Partial Search Graph (PSG) and uses three agents: Node Selection Agent, Move Selection Agent (for intensification), and Jump Agent (for exploration/diversification). The framework separates problem-agnostic search control from domain-specific encoding.

Result: On CVRP: competitive with learn-to-search baselines. On VRPTW: new state-of-the-art among learning-based methods, reducing gap to best-known solutions by 14% (N=100) and 44% (N=50) vs POMO, and 21%/40% vs ALNS.

Conclusion: COAgents effectively combines multi-agent cooperation with graph-based search dynamics, demonstrating strong generalization and achieving superior results especially on the more complex VRPTW benchmark.

Abstract: Although Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP) are essential to many real-world systems, they remain computationally intractable at scale due to their combinatorial complexity. Traditional heuristics rely on handcrafted rules for local improvements and occasional \textit{jumps} to escape local minima, but often struggle to generalize across diverse instances. We introduce \textbf{COAgents}, a cooperative multi-agent framework that models the search process as a graph: nodes represent solutions, and edges correspond to either local refinements or large perturbations for diversification (i.e., jumps). A \textit{Partial Search Graph} (PSG) is dynamically constructed during search, enabling COAgents to train a Node Selection Agent and a Move Selection Agent to guide intensification, and a Jump Agent to trigger well-timed explorations of new regions. Unlike end-to-end learning approaches, COAgents cleanly separates problem-agnostic search control from compact domain-specific encoding, facilitating adaptability across tasks. Extensive experiments on the CVRP and VRPTW benchmarks show that COAgents remains competitive with several learn-to-search baselines on CVRP and sets a new state of the art among learning-based methods on the more challenging VRPTW instances, reducing the gap to the best-known solutions by 14\% at $N\!=\!100$ and 44\% at $N\!=\!50$ relative to the strongest neural solver (POMO), and by 21\% and 40\% respectively relative to ALNS.
  Code is available at https://github.com/mahdims/COAgents.

</details>


### [185] [Evaluating Temporal Semantic Caching and Workflow Optimization in Agentic Plan-Execute Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20630)
*Alimurtaza Mustafa Merchant,Krish Veera,Sajal Kumar Goyla,Shambhawi Bhure,Dhaval Patel,Kaoutar El Maghraoui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a temporal semantic cache and MCP workflow optimizations to reduce latency in industrial asset operations pipelines, achieving up to 30.6x speedup on cache hits and 1.67x overall speedup, while exposing semantic caching limitations for parameter-rich industrial queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial asset operations workflows are latency-sensitive due to coordination across sensor data, work orders, failure modes, forecasting tools, and domain-specific agents. Existing LLM caching techniques (KV-cache reuse, embedding-based semantic caching) designed for chatbot serving fail when output validity depends on time, asset, or sensor parameters.

Method: Two complementary optimization layers: (1) a temporal semantic cache that accounts for time-sensitive parameters, and (2) MCP workflow optimizations including disk-backed tool-discovery caching and dependency-aware parallel step execution.

Result: MCP workflow optimizations achieved 1.67x speedup and ~40% reduction in median end-to-end latency. The temporal cache achieved median 30.6x speedup on cache hits. The paper also identified concrete failure modes of pure semantic caching for parameter-rich industrial queries.

Conclusion: The proposed optimizations significantly reduce latency in industrial agent benchmarks, while highlighting how caching choices interact with evaluation correctness in MCP-backed agent benchmarks, particularly for parameter-rich and time-sensitive queries.

Abstract: Industrial asset operations workflows are latency-sensitive because a single user query may require coordination over sensor data, work orders, failure modes, forecasting tools, and domain-specific agents. We evaluate this problem on AssetOpsBench (AOB), an industrial agent benchmark whose plan-execute pipeline exposes repeated overhead from tool discovery, LLM planning, MCP tool execution, and final summarization. Existing LLM caching techniques such as KV-cache reuse and embedding-based semantic caching were designed for chatbot serving and break down when output validity depends on time, asset, or sensor parameters. We propose two complementary optimization layers for AOB plan-execute pipelines: a temporal semantic cache and a set of MCP workflow optimizations combining disk-backed tool-discovery caching and dependency-aware parallel step execution. MCP workflow optimizations corresponded to a 1.67x speedup and reduced median end-to-end latency by about 40.0% while the temporal-cache benchmark achieved a median of 30.6x speedup on cache hits. Beyond the speedup, our results expose a concrete failure mode of pure semantic caching for parameter-rich industrial queries, providing a critical analysis of how caching choices interact with evaluation correctness in MCP-backed agent benchmarks.

</details>


### [186] [Declarative Data Services: Structured Agentic Discovery for Composing Data Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20690)
*Shanshan Ye,Duo Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Declarative Data Services (DDS), an architecture using typed contracts and sub-agents to achieve structured agentic discovery of multi-system data backends, demonstrating convergence where unbounded discovery fails on a trading-backend workload.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-driven agentic discovery works for single-domain problems but fails for multi-system data backends because the search space is heterogeneous, verification requires a running stack, and composition knowledge is unevenly captured in pretraining.

Method: DDS decomposes global search into bounded sub-searches via four typed contracts (intent, operator DAG, per-system skills, runtime attribution). Sub-agents search each space while knowledge flows forward as skill citations and errors route backward as typed signals.

Result: On a trading-backend workload, DDS converges where unbounded agentic discovery does not; runtime failures become skill patches that subsequent deployments cite inline.

Conclusion: DDS is an early prototype that demonstrates structured agentic discovery with typed decomposition can successfully compose real-world data systems where unbounded search fails.

Abstract: Agentic discovery has shown that LLM-driven search can find novel algorithms, designs, and code under benchmark conditions. Translating the paradigm to multi-system data backends surfaces a harder problem: the search space is heterogeneous, the verifier is whether a deployed stack actually runs, and composition knowledge is unevenly captured in pretraining. Unbounded agentic discovery, a coding agent iterating on failure-log feedback, fails to converge consistently on a working stack even when iteration and explicit composition knowledge are added. We propose Declarative Data Services (DDS), an architecture for structured agentic discovery of data-system compositions from declarative user intent. The framework owns four typed contracts at successive layers (intent, operator DAG, per-system skills, runtime attribution) that decompose the global search into bounded sub-searches; sub-agents search each typed space, while the framework provides the channels by which knowledge flows forward as inline skill citations and errors route backward as typed signals. As a proof of life on a trading-backend workload, DDS converges where unbounded discovery does not; runtime failures become skill patches that the next deployment cites inline. We position this as an early prototype reporting lessons from real-world data-system composition.

</details>


### [187] [VBFDD-Agent for Electric Vehicle Battery Fault Detection and Diagnosis: Descriptive Text Modeling of Battery Digital Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20742)
*Joey Chan,Zhen Chen,Ershun Pan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VBFDD-Agent is an LLM-based battery fault detection and diagnosis agent that converts monitoring signals into structured text descriptions and generates interpretable diagnostic results and maintenance recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Battery systems are becoming more complex, and traditional fault detection methods lack cross-domain adaptability and human-AI collaboration. There is also a scarcity of open-source battery fault report corpora and unified maintenance knowledge representation.
