Method: CP-MoE proposes: (1) a transient expert that captures early task-specific updates, (2) a consistency-preserving routing bias that estimates representation similarity to steer routing toward compatible experts, and (3) a transient expert-guided regularization mechanism that protects important historical parameters during merging.

Result: On SuperNI benchmark, CP-MoE achieves SOTA performance with stronger zero-shot transfer to unseen tasks. On VQA v2, it reduces forgetting and outperforms strong MoE baselines in multimodal visual reasoning.

Conclusion: CP-MoE effectively reduces parameter interference and forgetting while preserving cross-task knowledge transfer, validated on both unimodal (LLM) and multimodal (VLM) continual learning benchmarks.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting remains a major obstacle to continual learning in large language models (LLMs) and vision--language models (VLMs). Although Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures offer an efficient path to scaling, existing LoRA-based MoE continual learning methods still face a fundamental trade-off: they either isolate experts too aggressively, limiting knowledge transfer across tasks, or allow task-specific updates to overwrite important existing parameters, leading to severe forgetting. To address this, we propose CP-MoE, a continual learning framework built around a transient expert that captures early task-specific updates and guides their integration into stable experts. CP-MoE introduces a consistency-preserving routing bias, which uses the transient expert to estimate representation similarity with stable experts and steer routing towards more compatible expert selection, and a transient expert-guided regularisation mechanism, which selectively protects important historical parameters during merging. Together, these components reduce parameter interference and forgetting while preserving cross-task knowledge transfer. We validate CP-MoE on both unimodal and multimodal continual learning benchmarks with LLM-based and VLM-based MoE models. On SuperNI benchmark, spanning diverse sequential language tasks, CP-MoE achieves state-of-the-art performance and stronger zero-shot transfer to unseen tasks. On VQA v2 dataset, it scales effectively to multimodal visual reasoning, consistently reduces forgetting, and outperforms strong MoE baselines.

</details>


### [214] [Graph Transductive Sharpening: Leveraging Unlabeled Predictions in Node Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20248)
*Brown Zaz,Mar Gonzàlez I Català,Ferran Hernandez Caralt,Moshe Eliasof,Pietro Liò*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transductive Sharpening (TS) improves semi-supervised node classification by minimizing prediction entropy on unlabeled nodes and balancing it on labeled nodes, achieving consistent gains without architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard supervised objectives discard predictions on unlabeled nodes because no ground-truth labels exist, but these predictions may contain useful training signal in the transductive setting.

Method: Proposes Transductive Sharpening (TS), a loss-level modification that minimizes prediction entropy on unlabeled nodes while counterbalancing this effect on labeled nodes, motivated by the decomposition of cross-entropy into label-dependent alignment and label-independent entropy terms.

Result: Consistent performance improvements across a wide range of node-classification benchmarks without requiring any changes to the backbone architecture.

Conclusion: The training objective is an orthogonal and effective axis for improving transductive semi-supervised node classification, beyond architectural innovation.

Abstract: In the transductive setting, where the full graph is observed but node labels are only partially available, progress in semi-supervised node classification has largely focused on architectural innovation. In this paper, we revisit an orthogonal axis: the training objective. We start from a simple observation: transductive models produce predictions for every node during training, including nodes without labels. These unlabeled-node predictions may contain useful training signal, but standard supervised objectives discard them because no ground-truth labels are available. Inspired by the decomposition of cross-entropy into a label-dependent alignment term and a label-independent entropy term, we propose prediction confidence as a natural way to extract this signal in the absence of labels. This motivates Transductive Sharpening (TS): a loss-level modification that minimizes prediction entropy on unlabeled nodes while counterbalancing this effect on labeled nodes. We evaluate Transductive Sharpening across a wide range of node-classification benchmarks and observe consistent performance improvements without requiring any changes to the backbone architecture. Code is available at https://github.com/transductive-sharpening/tunedGNN.

</details>


### [215] [Automated Kernel Discovery Towards Understanding High-dimensional Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20249)
*Taeyoung Yun,Woocheol Shin,Inhyuck Song,Jaewoo Lee,Jinkyoo Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An LLM-driven evolutionary framework for automated kernel discovery in high-dimensional Bayesian optimization that searches beyond predefined composition rules and uses a two-stage approach to generate validated kernel code, achieving state-of-the-art results (average rank 1.2/17).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GP kernel design for high-dimensional BO relies on manual engineering or limited automated searches (additions/multiplications of base kernels). LLM-based methods require raw observations which is infeasible due to context-length limits.

Method: Two-stage LLM-driven evolutionary framework: 1) LLM proposes novel mathematical forms, 2) A second LLM converts forms into validated executable code. Uses a leave-one-out continuous ranked probability score (LOO-CRPS) to penalize overfitted kernels.

Result: Achieves average rank of 1.2 out of 17 on five high-dimensional BO benchmarks, outperforming all competitive baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed Kernel Discovery framework effectively automates kernel design for high-dimensional BO by broadening the search space and avoiding observation conditioning, with further analysis identifying which kernels drive improvements.

Abstract: Gaussian Process (GP) kernels are central to Bayesian optimization (BO), yet designing effective kernels for high-dimensional problems still relies on extensive manual engineering. Existing automated approaches struggle in high dimensions for two bottlenecks: their kernel search space is limited to additions and multiplications of base kernels, and LLM-based approaches require conditioning on raw observations, which becomes infeasible due to context-length limits and the difficulty of extracting meaningful patterns. We introduce \textbf{Kernel Discovery}, a LLM-driven evolutionary framework for high-dimensional BO that searches a broader kernel space beyond predefined composition rules and does not require conditioning on observations. Motivated by the observation that directly prompting an LLM to generate kernel code yields syntactically varied but functionally identical kernels, we adopt a two-stage approach: an LLM first proposes novel mathematical forms, then a second LLM call converts each form into validated, executable code. We also propose a leave-one-out continuous ranked probability score (LOO-CRPS) as a selection criterion that penalizes overfitted kernels. On five high-dimensional BO benchmarks, our method achieves an average rank of \textbf{1.2 out of 17}, outperforming competitive baselines.
  We further analyze the discovered kernels to identify which kernels lead to improvements in high-dimensional BO.

</details>


### [216] [Physics-informed convolutional neural networks for fluid flow through porous media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20250)
*Rafał Topolnicki,Paweł Dłotko,Maciej Matyka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A neural network framework predicts pore-scale velocity fields in porous media using a CNN encoder-decoder with skip connections and a custom physics-informed loss function, achieving accurate predictions and accelerating Lattice-Boltzmann simulations by reducing iterations in over 90% of cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simulating fluid flow in porous media is computationally expensive due to complex geometries and slow convergence of standard solvers, especially when repeated simulations are needed.

Method: A convolutional encoder-decoder with skip connections extracts multi-scale features. A custom loss function enforces velocity reconstruction, incompressibility, no-flow in solids, periodicity, and global tortuosity. Multiple CNN backbones are compared, and generalization is tested on out-of-distribution samples.

Result: The model generalizes well to unseen geometries, boundary conditions, porosity changes, and realistic porous structures. Predicted velocity fields used as initial conditions for Lattice-Boltzmann simulations reduce iterations in over 90% of cases.

Conclusion: The proposed neural network framework provides accurate, robust velocity field predictions and serves as an effective warm-start strategy to accelerate pore-scale flow simulations.

Abstract: Accurate simulation of fluid flow in porous media is challenging due to complex pore-space geometries and the computational cost of solving the Navier-Stokes equations. This difficulty is particularly important when repeated simulations are required, as standard numerical solvers may converge slowly in intricate porous domains. We present a neural-network-based framework for predicting pore-scale velocity fields directly from sample geometry. The method uses a convolutional encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections to preserve spatial detail while extracting multi-scale features. Physical consistency is encouraged through a custom loss function combining velocity reconstruction with incompressibility, no-flow conditions inside solids, periodicity constraints, and agreement with the global tortuosity index. We analyze the influence of the corresponding loss weights and quantify the contribution of individual loss components to prediction accuracy. Several CNN backbones are evaluated to identify architectures providing accurate and robust predictions. The generalization ability of the trained model is tested on samples outside the training distribution, including changes in obstacle geometry, boundary conditions, porosity, and realistic porous structures. Finally, we demonstrate a practical use of the predicted velocity fields as initial conditions for Lattice-Boltzmann simulations. This warm-start strategy accelerates solver convergence, reducing the number of iterations in over 90% of tested cases.

</details>


### [217] [Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Safe Autonomous Driving Under Pedestrian Behavioral Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20255)
*Prakash Aryan,Kaushik Raghupathruni,Timo Kehrer,Sebastiano Panichella*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper uses multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to co-train a self-driving car (SDC) and 12 pedestrians with hidden jaywalking personality traits, showing that co-trained agents achieve higher goal completion (78% vs 35%) and lower collision rates (14% vs 33%) compared to rule-based baselines, with jaywalking accounting for 62% of collisions despite only 13% of crossing events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simulation-based testing of SDCs relies on unrealistic scripted pedestrian models that fail to capture the heterogeneity and uncertainty of real human crossing behavior, especially jaywalking governed by latent, unobservable personality traits.

Method: The authors use MAPPO to co-train an SDC and 12 pedestrians. Pedestrians use scripted Dijkstra pathfinding for locomotion with an RL policy for go/wait decisions. Each pedestrian has a hidden jaywalking personality trait sampled at episode start. A speed differential metric measures how the SDC anticipates jaywalkers vs. crosswalk users.

Result: Co-trained SDC reached 78% goals with 14% collisions (vs. 35% goals, 33% collisions for best baseline). SDC traveled 2.65 m/s faster near jaywalkers at close range (0-3 m), indicating unanticipated jaywalking. Jaywalking caused 62% of collisions despite 13% of events. MARL reduced collisions by 30% over single-agent RL.

Conclusion: MARL co-training produces more realistic SDC-pedestrian interaction scenarios and improves safety outcomes, but the persistent collision rate from jaywalking highlights the need for better anticipation of unpredictable pedestrian behaviors.

Abstract: Simulation-based testing of self-driving cars (SDCs) typically relies on scripted or simplified pedestrian models that do not capture the heterogeneity and uncertainty of real human crossing behavior. This limits the realism of safety assessments, especially in scenarios involving jaywalking, which is governed by latent personality traits that the vehicle cannot observe. We hypothesize that jointly training pedestrians and the SDC with multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) produces more realistic interaction scenarios than training the SDC against fixed pedestrian policies, and that the resulting behavior gap between predictable and unpredictable crossings can be measured directly from trajectories. This paper describes a MARL environment in which an SDC and 12 pedestrians are co-trained using Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO). Pedestrian locomotion follows scripted Dijkstra pathfinding, while an RL policy controls high-level go/wait decisions. Jaywalking probability depends on a per-pedestrian personality trait sampled at episode start and hidden from the SDC. In 500-episode evaluations, the co-trained SDC reached 78% of goals with a 14% collision rate, compared to 35% goals and 33% collisions for the best rule-based baseline. A speed differential metric shows that the SDC traveled 2.65 m/s faster near jaywalkers than near crosswalk users at close range (0-3 m), indicating that jaywalking encounters were not anticipated. Jaywalking accounted for 13% of crossing events but was associated with 62% of collisions. Co-training with MARL pedestrians reduced collisions by 30% relative to single-agent RL, as pedestrians learned to wait when the SDC approached at speed.

</details>


### [218] [FBOS-RL: Feedback-Driven Bi-Objective Synergistic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20256)
*Xikai Zhang,Yongzhi Li,Likang Xiao,Yingze Zhang,Yanhua Cheng,Quan Chen,Peng Jiang,Wenjun Wu,Liu Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FBOS-RL introduces a feedback-driven bi-objective reinforcement learning framework that addresses GRPO's limitation of stalling on hard tasks by combining exploration enhancement with two mutually reinforcing training objectives (exploitation-oriented policy alignment and exploration-oriented capability cultivation), leading to faster learning and higher performance ceilings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO's simple sampling scheme conditions all rollouts on the same prompt, so when tasks exceed the model's current capability, few high-quality rollouts are generated, leaving the policy without meaningful gradient directions and causing training to stall.

Method: FBOS-RL uses Feedback-Guided Exploration Enhancement (leveraging environmental feedback) and two synergistic objectives: Exploitation-oriented Policy Alignment (EPA) and Exploration-oriented Capability Cultivation (ECC), which mutually reinforce each other in a positive flywheel effect.

Result: FBOS-RL learns substantially faster than GRPO and feedback-based baselines under identical rollouts, achieves a higher performance ceiling, and exhibits higher policy entropy with lower gradient norms throughout training.

Conclusion: The EPA and ECC objectives are mutually reinforcing, forming a positive flywheel that significantly improves both training efficiency and final performance ceiling in reinforcement learning for large-scale models.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has become a cornerstone for aligning and unlocking the reasoning capabilities of large-scale models. At its core, the training loop of GRPO and its variants alternates between rollout sampling and policy update. Unlike supervised learning, where each gradient step is anchored to an explicit ground-truth target, the optimal gradient direction for updating model parameters in this setting is not known a priori; the high-quality rollouts drawn during the sampling stage therefore act as the implicit "teacher" that guides every parameter update. However, GRPO adopt a simple sampling scheme that conditions all rollouts on the same original prompt. When a task lies beyond the policy model's current capability, this sampling scheme rarely yields a high-quality rollout, leaving the policy model without a meaningful gradient direction when updating its parameters, which causes training to stall. To address this issue, we propose FBOS-RL, a Feedback-Driven Bi-Objective Synergistic reinforcement learning framework. Specifically, we let the model perform Feedback-Guided Exploration Enhancement based on the feedback provided by the environment, and on top of this we design two mutually reinforcing training objectives: Exploitation-oriented Policy Alignment(EPA) and Exploration-oriented Capability Cultivation(ECC). Extensive experiments demonstrate that EPA and ECC can mutually reinforce each other, forming a positive flywheel effect that significantly improves both the training efficiency and the final performance ceiling of reinforcement learning. Specifically, under an identical number of rollouts, FBOS-RL learns substantially faster than GRPO and feedback-based baselines and ultimately attains a higher performance ceiling, while exhibiting higher policy entropy and lower gradient norms throughout training.

</details>


### [219] [Instance Discrimination for Link Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20257)
*Valentin Cuzin-Rambaud,Mathieu Lefort,Rémy Cazabet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper adapts self-supervised instance discrimination models (originally for images/nodes) to link prediction by proposing community-based structural augmentations and two new models (L-GRACE and L-BGRL) that use link representations instead of node representations, achieving state-of-the-art performance on unattributed graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Instance discrimination models have proven effective for self-supervised learning in images and node classification, but link prediction has been underexplored. The authors aim to adapt and evaluate these methods for link prediction, particularly addressing the challenge of unattributed graphs.

Method: The paper (1) provides a rigorous evaluation of existing self-supervised models for link prediction, (2) proposes a novel structural augmentation based on community structure, and (3) introduces L-GRACE and L-BGRL, which operate on link representations rather than node representations.

Result: The proposed community-based augmentation improves performance. L-GRACE and L-BGRL outperform existing methods, especially on unattributed graphs, and achieve performance on par with state-of-the-art in both supervised and self-supervised contexts.

Conclusion: Self-supervised learning for link prediction benefits significantly from link-level representations and community-aware augmentations, bridging the gap between supervised and self-supervised methods for this task.

Abstract: Recently, instance discrimination models have emerged as a major solution for self-supervised learning. Having already demonstrated its effectiveness in the image domain, instance discrimination learning is now proving equally convincing in the graph domain, in particular for node classification. However, fewer contributions have tackled the link prediction task. In this contribution, we propose to adapt existing methods to this context. We first provide a rigorous evaluation of existing self-supervised models in the field of link prediction, showing that the main performance depends on the augmentation process (like in computer vision). We then propose a new structural augmentation based on the community structure that is relevant for link prediction. Our main contribution introduces two new models, L-GRACE and L-BGRL, based on link representations instead of node representations, which improve the performance of the existing methods, especially on unattributed graphs, and we show that they perform on par with the state of the art, both in supervised and self-supervised contexts.

</details>


### [220] [It Takes Two: Complementary Self-Distillation for Contextual Integrity in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20258)
*Sangwoo Park,Woongyeong Yeo,Seanie Lee,Yumin Choi,Hyomin Lee,Kangsan Kim,Jinheon Baek,Seong Joon Oh,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SELFCI is a self-distillation framework that decouples information suppression from task resolution to align LLMs with Contextual Integrity privacy norms, achieving better privacy-utility trade-offs without external supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs deployed as personal agents must adhere to Contextual Integrity (CI) norms for privacy, but existing models are unreliable in disclosure decisions and mitigation strategies degrade task performance.

Method: SELFCI jointly optimizes two independent reverse KL divergences over distinct teacher distributions—one for preserving task-relevant information (utility), one for enforcing minimal disclosure (privacy)—creating a Product-of-Experts (PoE) target that aligns policy with both capability and privacy requirements.

Result: SELFCI consistently outperforms competitive baselines (e.g., GRPO) without costly external supervision, and generalizes to out-of-domain settings including agentic workflows and accumulated private context.

Conclusion: SELFCI offers a practical, scalable path toward CI alignment for LLMs by decoupling utility and privacy objectives, effectively overcoming the privacy-utility trade-off.

Abstract: Contextual Integrity (CI) defines privacy not merely as keeping information hidden, but as governing information flows according to the norms of a given context. As large language models are increasingly deployed as personal agents handling sensitive workflows, adhering to CI becomes critical. However, even frontier models remain unreliable in making disclosure decisions, and existing mitigation strategies often degrade underlying task performance. To overcome this privacy-utility trade-off, we propose SELFCI, a complementary self-distillation framework that decouples information suppression from task resolution. SELFCI jointly optimizes two independent reverse KL divergences over distinct teacher distributions derived from feedback: one encourages preserving task-relevant information for utility, while the other enforces minimal and appropriate disclosure. This complementary formulation induces a Product-of-Experts (PoE) target, aligning the policy with the intersection of capability and privacy requirements. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that SELFCI, without relying on costly external supervision, consistently outperforms competitive baselines such as online reinforcement learning algorithms (e.g., GRPO). These trends further extend to out-of-domain settings involving agentic workflows and accumulated private context, suggesting that SELFCI provides a practical path toward CI alignment.

</details>


### [221] [Residual Paving: Diagnosing the Routing Bottleneck in Selective Refusal Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20262)
*Bryce Hinkley,Peyman Najafirad*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Residual Paving is a routed residual editing method for selectively controlling refusal behavior in instruction-tuned transformers by separating the routing decision (whether to intervene) from the edit content (what to apply), achieving strong edit success (88.6%→4.0% refusal) while preserving benign and harmful refusals, though learned routing remains the main bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable selective refusal editing as a three-way control problem: inducing non-refusal on specific edit prompts while maintaining benign behavior and harmful refusal capabilities outside the edit set.

Method: Residual Paving: a routed residual editing method using an early-layer router that predicts a scalar gate and expert mixture; when active, prompt-conditioned bottleneck residual experts apply later-layer updates while keeping the backbone unchanged.

Result: On Gemma-3-4B-IT: edit refusal reduced from 88.6% to 4.0%, with 95.5% benign and 87.3% harmful distribution preservation. Outperforms ActAdd and DIM-style steering. Oracle routing shows routing selectivity is the main bottleneck (median +12.9 pp gain).

Conclusion: The routing component is the primary bottleneck in selective refusal editing; the separation of route selectivity from residual editing capacity is effective, and oracle diagnostics confirm learned routing as the key limitation.

Abstract: We study selective refusal editing as a three-way control problem: induce non-refusal on designated edit prompts while preserving benign behavior and harmful refusals outside the edit set. We introduce Residual Paving, a routed residual editing method for frozen instruction-tuned transformers that separates route selectivity, whether to intervene, from residual-edit capacity, what edit to apply. An early-layer router predicts a scalar gate and expert mixture; when active, prompt-conditioned bottleneck residual experts apply later-layer residual updates while leaving the backbone unchanged. This decomposition supports an oracle-routing diagnostic where only the learned scalar gate is replaced with the held-out edit/keep label, leaving the residual editor and frozen backbone fixed. On the primary Gemma-3-4B-IT held-out split, learned Residual Paving reduces edit refusal from 88.6% to 4.0%, with 95.5% benign distribution preservation and 87.3% harmful distribution preservation. Same-protocol one-direction steering controls are much weaker on edit success, leaving edit refusal at 86.8% for Edit-target ActAdd and 78.9% for DIM-style refusal steering. The remaining failure is off-target harmful-keep degradation: harmful refusal remains below the frozen-base rate, 65.3% vs. 81.6%. Across six backbones, oracle routing improves the keep-side diagnostic score on every reported row, with median gain +12.9 pp, supporting the interpretation that learned route selectivity is the main observed bottleneck. Trajectory diagnostics on two backbones further suggest directed movement toward edit-target continuations rather than generic refusal suppression.

</details>


### [222] [Chronicle: A Multimodal Foundation Model for Joint Language and Time Series Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20268)
*Paul Quinlan,Jeremy Levasseur,Qingguo Li,Xiaodan Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chronicle is a 324M-parameter decoder-only transformer jointly pretrained from scratch on text and time series using a unified architecture. It matches pure language models on NLU and sets new SOTA on time series classification and multimodal forecasting, demonstrating that cross-modal capability emerges from shared parameters without needing a pretrained language model backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal time-series models adapt pretrained language models post hoc, inheriting representations never exposed to temporal data. They are also evaluated only against other multimodal baselines, not against strong unimodal foundation models, leaving it unclear if joint training is necessary.

Method: A 324M-parameter decoder-only transformer trained from scratch on both text and time series within a single architecture. Both modalities share all transformer blocks, attention mechanisms, and the residual stream. Most pretraining uses unimodal batches, so cross-modal capability emerges from shared parameters alone, with a short alignment stage that interleaves both modalities.

Result: Matches Gemma-3-270M-PT on 19 NLU tasks; sets new state-of-the-art for frozen-embedding time series classification on 24 UCR/UEA datasets; outperforms all supervised fusion baselines on Time-MMD multimodal forecasting.

Conclusion: Joint pretraining from scratch on text and time series is feasible and effective; cross-modal ability can emerge from shared parameters without post-hoc adaptation of a language model; multimodal models should be benchmarked against unimodal foundation models.

Abstract: Real-world time series come with text: metadata, descriptions, news, reports. Yet time series foundation models process numerical sequences in isolation, and the multimodal text-and-time-series models that attempt to bridge the two all adapt a pretrained language model post hoc, inheriting representations shaped without ever seeing temporal data. These models are also evaluated almost exclusively against other multimodal baselines, not against the strongest unimodal foundation models in either domain, leaving open whether joint training is needed at all. We present Chronicle, a compact 324M-parameter decoder-only transformer trained from scratch on natural language and time series within a single unified architecture. Both modalities share the same transformer blocks, attention mechanism, and residual stream; the bulk of pretraining uses unimodal batches so cross-modal capability emerges purely from shared parameters, with a short alignment stage that interleaves the two. To our knowledge, Chronicle is the first model jointly pretrained on text and time series from scratch, and the first multimodal model evaluated against dedicated foundation models in both domains. It matches Gemma-3-270M-PT on 19 NLU tasks, sets a new bar for frozen-embedding time series classification on 24 UCR/UEA datasets, and produces multimodal forecasts on Time-MMD that beat every supervised fusion baseline, all from a single backbone.

</details>


### [223] [Catching a Moving Subspace: Low-Rank Bandits Beyond Stationarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20269)
*Hamed Khosravi,Xiaoming Huo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies piecewise-stationary low-rank linear contextual bandits, where rewards lie on a low-dimensional latent subspace that shifts at unknown segment boundaries. The authors characterize the identification boundary for subspace recovery (requiring known noise variance, bounded state-noise coupling, and full-dimensional probe support), propose the SPSC algorithm achieving dynamic regret $\widetilde{O}(r\sqrt{T})+\widetilde{O}(T^{2/3})+O(W\,V_{\mathrm{in}})$ that replaces the ambient $d$ with intrinsic rank $r$, and demonstrate strong empirical performance on 11 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work handles stationary low-rank bandits (exploit rank but break under drift) and non-stationary linear bandits (adapt to drift but pay ambient rate $d\sqrt{T}$) only in isolation. This work bridges the gap by studying settings where both low-rank structure and subspace drift occur simultaneously.

Method: The authors propose SPSC (Subspace Piecewise-Stationary Contextual bandit), which interleaves isotropic probes with windowed projected ridge-UCB exploitation inside the learned $r$-dimensional subspace, and include a CUSUM-style variant for online segment boundary detection.

Result: The paper establishes (i) tight identification boundaries for subspace recovery under three necessary and jointly sufficient probe-side conditions; (ii) dynamic regret of $\widetilde{O}(r\sqrt{T})+\widetilde{O}(T^{2/3})+O(W\,V_{\mathrm{in}})$, replacing ambient dimension $d$ with intrinsic rank $r$; (iii) strong empirical performance across 11 diverse benchmarks.

Conclusion: This is the first work to simultaneously characterize the identification boundary and attain the intrinsic-rank dynamic-regret rate for piecewise-stationary low-rank linear contextual bandits.

Abstract: Many bandit deployments (recommendation, clinical dosing, ad targeting) share two facts prior work handles only in isolation: rewards live on a low-dimensional latent subspace, and that subspace drifts. Stationary low-rank bandits exploit rank but break under subspace change; non-stationary linear bandits adapt to drift but pay ambient rate $\widetilde{O}(d\sqrt{T})$. We study piecewise-stationary low-rank linear contextual bandits with scalar feedback: $θ_t = B_k^\star w_t$ with rank-$r$ factor $B_k^\star\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times r}$ constant within each of $K$ unknown segments and able to shift at boundaries. Our results are tight along three axes. (i) Identification boundary. With single-play scalar rewards, the moving subspace is recoverable through quadratic functionals of rewards iff three probe-side conditions hold: known noise variance, bounded state-noise coupling, and full-dimensional probe support. Each is necessary in the unrestricted-second-moment problem, and jointly they are sufficient, characterizing the boundary of the solvable region. (ii) Algorithm and dynamic regret. SPSC interleaves isotropic probes with windowed projected ridge-UCB exploitation inside the learned $r$-dimensional subspace; a CUSUM-style variant discovers segment boundaries online. The costed dynamic regret is $\widetilde{O}(r\sqrt{T})+\widetilde{O}(T^{2/3})+O(W\,V_{\mathrm{in}})$, replacing the ambient $d\sqrt{T}$ rate with the intrinsic rank. (iii) Empirics. On eleven benchmarks spanning synthetic, UCI/MovieLens, semi-synthetic clinical, and ZOZOTOWN production-log data, SPSC outperforms non-stationary and low-rank baselines whenever $d-r\gtrsim T^{1/6}$, matching the analytical crossover. To our knowledge, this is the first work to characterize the identification boundary and attain the intrinsic-rank dynamic-regret rate in this setting.

</details>


### [224] [Conformal Selective Acting: Anytime-Valid Risk Control for RLVR-Trained LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20270)
*Hamed Khosravi,Xiaoming Huo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CSA (Conformal Selective Acting) is a deployment wrapper that provides per-round safety certificates with anytime-pathwise selective-risk guarantees for locally fine-tuned specialist LLMs in regulated settings, using e-processes per threshold on a Bonferroni grid under RLVR filtration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regulated organizations deploying adaptive, online-updated LLMs need per-round safety certificates with selective risk control, but existing methods (offline conformal, online-conformal, A-RCPS) only offer marginal validity or long-run averages, failing the strict deployment requirements.

Method: CSA uses a (test statistic, validity guarantee, deployment rule) framework, maintaining a Ville-type e-process per threshold on a Bonferroni grid, evaluated against RLVR filtration. It provides predictable updates with isotonic-calibrated monotone risk.

Result: Proves anytime-pathwise selective-risk bound $R_T^{\mathrm{act}}\le α+O(N_T^{-1/2})$, rate-optimal certification $Θ(\barη^{-2}\log(1/δ))$, and horizon-independent release-rate gap. Across 48+16+5 cells (10,300+ rounds), CSA is the only method among ten compared satisfying pathwise validity and non-refusing deployment on every cell.

Conclusion: CSA is a deployment-side complement orthogonal to the model, enabling operators in regulated settings to deploy specialist LLMs safely without relying on frontier APIs, while filling a previously empty cell in the theoretical framework.

Abstract: A local specialist LLM, fine-tuned with reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) on operator-local data, is installed in a regulated organization with per-deployment error budget $α$. The operator needs a safety certificate for this deployment's stream at every round: no pooling across deployments, no waiting for a long-run average. Existing wrappers cannot deliver this on adaptive, online-updated streams: offline conformal-risk methods require exchangeability; online-conformal methods bound only long-run averages; non-exchangeable extensions are marginally valid; and the closest anytime wrapper, A-RCPS, controls marginal rather than selective risk. Using a (test statistic, validity guarantee, deployment rule) framework, we identify one empty cell forced by deployment requirements: e-process per threshold, selective risk, anytime-pathwise validity, max-certified-threshold rule. Conformal Selective Acting (CSA) fills it as a per-round wrapper maintaining a Ville-type e-process per threshold on a Bonferroni grid, evaluated against the RLVR filtration. Under predictable updates and isotonic-calibrated monotone risk we prove (i) an anytime-pathwise selective-risk bound $R_T^{\mathrm{act}}\leα+O(N_T^{-1/2})$, (ii) rate-optimal certification matching $Θ(\barη^{-2}\log(1/δ))$, and (iii) a horizon-independent release-rate gap. Across eight specialist benchmarks ($480$ streams), sixteen adversarial distribution-shift cells ($160$ streams), and five live Expert-Iteration RLVR cells with online LoRA over four base models in three architecture families ($10{,}300$ rounds), CSA is the only method among ten compared that satisfies pathwise validity and non-refusing deployment on every cell. We do not propose a new LLM, training algorithm, or policy class; CSA is the deployment-side complement, orthogonal to the model, for operators who cannot use a frontier API.

</details>


### [225] [Smaller Abstract State Spaces Enable Cross-Scale Generalization in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20272)
*Nasehatul Mustakim,Lucas Lehnert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a theoretical framework for achieving Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization in RL agents by extending state abstraction to POMDPs and introducing successor-weighted model reduction, deriving bounds on OOD test performance that show constraining agents to small abstract state spaces is key for generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans can easily generalize abstract concepts to larger/more complex tasks, but RL systems struggle with this OOD generalization. The authors aim to provide a theoretical foundation for how such generalization can be achieved.

Method: The authors (1) extend state abstraction frameworks and proof techniques to POMDPs, (2) define a successor-weighted model reduction that enables compression into smaller abstract spaces than previously possible, and (3) derive theoretical bounds on OOD test performance by decomposing loss into approximation and estimation errors.

Result: The bound shows that reducing an agent's abstract state space size improves test performance and OOD generalization. The analysis reveals that constraining agents to operate over a small, finite set of abstract states is necessary for generalization to more complex tasks.

Conclusion: This is the first theoretical model showing how OOD generalization can be achieved in RL agents, and it motivates further research into learning RL architectures that scale across tasks of varying complexity.

Abstract: While humans readily generalize abstract concepts to more complex or larger tasks, building Reinforcement Learning (RL) systems with this ability remains elusive. Here, we present the first theoretical model of how such Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization can be achieved in RL agents. Our approach considers Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (POMDPs) and assumes that an intelligent agent uses an abstraction function to determine which experiences can be treated as equivalent and which must be distinguished. First, we extend the existing state abstraction framework and proof techniques to POMDPs. Then, we define a successor-weighted model reduction, a model reduction variant that enables compression into smaller abstract spaces than prior definitions allow. We derive a bound on the agent's OOD test performance, thereby defining the conditions under which OOD generalization is achievable. This bound decomposes an agent's performance loss into approximation and estimation errors, revealing how reducing an agent's abstract state space size improves test performance and OOD generalization. Our analysis suggests that constraining an agent to operate over a small, finite set of abstract states is necessary for achieving generalization to more complex tasks. Our results motivate further research into learning RL architectures that scale across tasks of varying complexity levels.

</details>


### [226] [Modality-Decoupled Online Recursive Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20273)
*Siyuan Li,Youyuan Zhang,Fangming Liu,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M-ORE is a modality-decoupled online recursive editor for lifelong MLLM adaptation that addresses cross-modal conflict and inter-edit interference via a unified proximal-projection formulation with Sherman-Morrison recursion, achieving constant per-edit overhead and improved reliability, generality, and locality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-only LLM editors degrade on MLLMs due to (1) cross-modal conflict from visually dominant activations skewing update statistics, and (2) inter-edit interference as sequential writes become entangled in a shared edit space, amplifying long-horizon interference.

Method: M-ORE is derived from a unified proximal-projection formulation with a closed-form update using Sherman-Morrison recursion. It maintains module-wise locality statistics for the text stack and visual projector to prevent visually dominated update shaping, and performs continual updates in a fixed orthogonal low-rank edit subspace to mitigate long-horizon interference.

Result: Experiments on multiple MLLM backbones and online editing benchmarks show M-ORE consistently improves reliability, generality, and locality over strong baselines, while achieving favorable quality-efficiency scaling.

Conclusion: M-ORE effectively enables lifelong MLLM adaptation with constant per-edit overhead, addressing key challenges of cross-modal conflict and inter-edit interference in online model editing.

Abstract: Online model editing for multimodal large language models (MLLMs) requires assimilating a stream of corrections under tight compute and memory budgets. Yet editors developed for text-only LLMs often degrade on MLLMs: visually dominant activations skew the statistics that shape updates, causing cross-modal conflict, while sequential writes become entangled in a shared edit space and amplify long-horizon interference, causing inter-edit interference. To address these, we propose M-ORE, a modality-decoupled online recursive editor for lifelong MLLM adaptation. M-ORE is derived from a unified proximal-projection formulation and admits a closed-form update with a Sherman-Morrison recursion, yielding constant per-edit overhead. It maintains module-wise locality statistics for the text stack and the visual projector to avoid visually dominated update shaping and performs continual updates in a fixed orthogonal low-rank edit subspace via a Sherman-Morrison recursion to mitigate long-horizon interference. Experiments on multiple MLLM backbones and online editing benchmarks show that our M-ORE method consistently improves reliability, generality, and locality over strong baselines, while achieving favorable quality-efficiency scaling. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/lab-klc/M-ORE.

</details>


### [227] [OmniISR: A Unified Framework for Centralized and Federated Learning via Intermediate Supervision and Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20276)
*Wei-Bin Kou,Guangxu Zhu,Ming Tang,Chen Zhang,Lisheng Wu,Lei Zhou,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OmniISR is a unified framework that combines centralized learning (CL), federated learning (FL), and hybrid CL-FL training by adding intermediate supervision (mutual information) and regularization (negative entropy) signals at hidden layers, achieving theoretical convergence guarantees and improving model performance across both paradigms while reducing the CL-FL performance gap by 22.60%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global edge intelligence operates under heterogeneous legal frameworks where some regions permit centralized learning (CL) while others require federated learning (FL) due to data localization laws. This creates two incompatible optimization regimes (CL's unbiased gradients with covariate shift vs. FL's biased drift-prone updates), and naive integration lacks rigorous theoretical guarantees.

Method: OmniISR proposes (i) mutual-information (MI) as intermediate supervision to align shifting internal covariate in CL and client-drifting representations in FL, and (ii) negative-entropy (NE) as intermediate regularizer to penalize overconfident predictions and preserve representational uncertainty. Theoretically, it derives convergence bounds, drift-bounds, gradient-alignment guarantees, and escape-time bounds.

Result: OmniISR achieves O(1/sqrt(T)) non-asymptotic convergence, quantifies ISR-reduced client drift, provides gradient-alignment guarantees, and shows hybrid mixing accelerates escape from strict saddles. Empirically, it reduces the CL-FL gap by 22.60% and achieves 37/48 paired metric wins across multiple FL algorithms.

Conclusion: The proposed ISR signals provide a theoretically grounded, unified approach to bridging centralized and federated learning paradigms, enabling robust performance across heterogeneous deployment environments without violating standard SGD convergence.

Abstract: The global deployment of edge intelligence operates across heterogeneous legal frameworks. While some regions permit centralized learning (CL) via cloud data aggregation, others enforce strict data localization, necessitating federated learning (FL). This operational dichotomy introduces two incompatible optimization regimes (i.e., unbiased global gradients yet coupled with internal covariate shift in CL versus biased, drift-prone local updates in FL), resulting in that any naive integration of the two lacks rigorous theoretical guarantees. To fill this gap, we propose OmniISR, a unified framework that fuses pure CL, pure FL, and hybrid CL-FL training modes via equipping intermediate supervision and regularization (ISR) signals at multiple hidden layers. Specifically, we propose (i) to use mutual-information (MI) as intermediate supervision to align shifting internal covariate in CL and client-drifting representations in FL, and (ii) to adopt negative-entropy (NE) as intermediate regularizer to penalize overconfident prediction, preserve representational uncertainty, and avoid device-specific collapse. On the theory side, we derive (i) a unified, ISR-agnostic, and non-asymptotic O(1/sqrt(T)) convergence bound that shows the introduced ISR does not violate standard SGD convergence, (ii) a federated drift-bound that quantifies the ISR-reduced client drift, (iii) a gradient-alignment guarantee that ensures non-conflicting CL and FL updates under mild bias, and (iv) an explicit escape-time bound that indicates that CL-FL hybrid mixing enlarges effective stochasticity and accelerates escape from strict saddles. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OmniISR consistently improves model performance in both centralized and federated paradigms, reduces the CL-FL gap by 22.60%, and yields 37/48 paired metric wins across multiple FL algorithms.

</details>


### [228] [ClaimDiff-RL: Fine-Grained Caption Reinforcement Learning through Visual Claim Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20278)
*Tianle Li,Xuyang Shen,Yan Ma,Rongxin Guo,Shaoxiang Chen,Jiacheng Chen,Haochen Wang,Hongyang Tang,Yucong Zhou,Yu Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ClaimDiff-RL uses atomic claim differences (faithfulness/correctness of each claim) rather than holistic scores as reward units for RL fine-tuning of dense image captions. A multimodal judge verifies each claim against the image, enabling separate tuning of hallucination vs. omission, leading to better balanced captions that outperform Gemini-3-Pro-Preview on fine-grained tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL-based dense captioning uses holistic sequence-level rewards (e.g., pairwise preferences, scalar metrics), which compress local errors (hallucinated or missing visual claims) into a single signal, obscuring the tradeoff between factuality and coverage.

Method: Given an image, actor caption, and reference caption, a multimodal judge (1) enumerates visually grounded claim differences, (2) verifies each against the image, (3) assigns open-vocabulary error types and severity levels, and (4) produces per-difference statistics for composing rewards.

Result: On a 160-image human diagnostic benchmark, public captioning benchmarks, and VQA benchmarks, ClaimDiff-RL improves the hallucination-missing-fact balance, preserves general capability, and surpasses Gemini-3-Pro-Preview on fine-grained Capability dimensions (object counting, spatial relations, scene recognition).

Conclusion: Typed, verifiable claim differences are an effective reward unit for fine-grained and diagnosable caption RL, enabling explicit control over the faithfulness-coverage tradeoff that holistic rewards obscure.

Abstract: Long-form image captioning exposes a reward granularity problem in RL: captions are judged as whole sequences, while the important errors occur at the level of individual visual claims. A good dense caption should be both faithful and informative, avoiding hallucination without omitting salient details. Yet pairwise preferences, reference-based metrics, and holistic scalar rewards compress these local errors into a single sequence-level signal, obscuring the tradeoff between factuality and coverage. We introduce ClaimDiff-RL, a framework that uses reference-conditioned atomic claim differences as the reward unit for caption RL. Given an image, an actor caption, and a reference caption, a multimodal judge enumerates visually grounded differences, verifies each difference against the image, assigns open-vocabulary error types and severity levels, and produces per-difference statistics for reward composition. This makes hallucinated claims and omitted salient facts separately measurable and tunable. Experiments show that holistic scalar rewards can reduce hallucination by increasing missing facts, while ClaimDiff-RL exposes this faithfulness and coverage tradeoff and enables more balanced operating points. On a 160-image human-labeled diagnostic benchmark, public captioning benchmarks, and VQA benchmarks, ClaimDiff-RL improves the hallucination--missing-fact balance, preserves general capability, and even surpasses Gemini-3-Pro-Preview on several fine-grained Capability dimensions such as object counting, spatial relations, and scene recognition. These results suggest that typed, verifiable claim differences are an effective reward unit for fine-grained and diagnosable caption RL.

</details>


### [229] [Introspective X Training: Feedback Conditioning Improves Scaling Across all LLM Training Stages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20285)
*Brandon Cui,Ximing Lu,Jaehun Jung,Syeda Nahida Akter,Hyunwoo Kim,Yuxiao Qu,David Acuna,Shrimai Prabhumoye,Yejin Choi,Prithviraj Ammanabrolu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introspective Training (IXT) uses a thinking reward model to generate natural language feedback that conditions training data, enabling quality-aware token treatment from early stages and achieving up to 2.8x compute efficiency in LLM training at 7.5-12B scale up to 18T tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To scale LLM training more efficiently by leveraging insights from later pipeline stages (e.g., post-training) to inform earlier stages (e.g., pre-training), addressing the growing computational costs across many training stages.

Method: IXT uses a thinking reward model to annotate data with natural language critique feedback, then trains models by prefix-conditioning data with this feedback, enabling quality-aware training from the earliest pipeline stages, inspired by offline reward-conditioned reinforcement learning.

Result: On 7.5-12B dense LLMs trained from scratch up to 18T tokens, IXT achieves up to 2.8x compute efficiency and reaches performance levels unachievable by standard training in math and code domains.

Conclusion: IXT effectively bends scaling curves by injecting quality-awareness early in training, demonstrating that feedback from later stages can inform earlier stages for more efficient LLM scaling.

Abstract: We tackle the question of how to scale more efficiently across the many, ever-growing stages of current LLM training pipelines. Our guiding intuition stems from the fact that the dynamics of later stages of the pipeline, e.g. post-training, can be used to inform earlier stages such as pre-training. To this end, we propose Introspective Training (or IXT), inspired by offline reward-conditioned reinforcement learning and applicable to any stage of training. IXT uses a thinking reward model to annotate data with natural language critique based feedback, enabling quality aware training from the earliest stages of the pipeline. Models are then trained by prefix-conditioning the data with the generated feedback -- ensuring that not all tokens are treated equally starting much earlier in training than usual. Comprehensive experiments on 7.5-12B transformer-based dense LLMs trained from scratch all the way up to 18 Trillion tokens seen show that our method: bends scaling curves resulting in up to 2.8x more compute efficiency generally; and reaches performance levels unachievable for models trained otherwise in domains such as math and code.

</details>


### [230] [FusionCell: Cross-Attentive Fusion of Layout Geometry and Netlist Topology for Standard-Cell Performance Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20287)
*Haoyi Zhang,Kairong Guo,Bojie Zhang,Yibo Lin,Runsheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FusionCell is a dual-modality predictor that fuses layout geometry and netlist topology using a DeiT encoder and graph transformer, achieving 0.92% MAPE and orders-of-magnitude speedup over circuit simulation for standard cell characterization on a 7nm ASAP7 dataset of 19.5k cells.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard cell characterization relies on slow simulation sweeps, and existing fast predictors often ignore layout geometry, missing coupling and layout-dependent effects that critically influence delay and power.

Method: FusionCell uses a DeiT encoder for routed three-layer layouts and a graph transformer for heterogeneous device/net graphs, integrated via a topology-guided mechanism where the netlist queries relevant layout regions for joint reasoning.

Result: Achieves 0.92% average MAPE, improved Spearman/Kendall ranking, and orders-of-magnitude speedup over circuit simulation on a 7nm ASAP7 dataset with 19.5k cells across 149 types.

Conclusion: FusionCell demonstrates that explicit fusion of layout geometry and netlist topology enables accurate and fast standard cell performance prediction, significantly accelerating characterization.

Abstract: Standard cells form the building blocks of digital circuits, so their delay and power critically influence chip-level performance; yet characterization still relies on slow simulation sweeps, and many fast predictors ignore layout geometry, missing coupling and layout-dependent effects. The challenge is to jointly represent layout geometry and netlist topology so models capture fine-grained spatial details together with structural connectivity for accurate performance prediction. We introduce FusionCell, a dual-modality predictor that treats routed layout geometry and netlist topology as inputs and fuses them explicitly in a unified model. A DeiT encoder processes three-layer routed layouts, while a graph transformer models heterogeneous device/net graphs. The modalities are integrated through a topology-guided mechanism, where the netlist acts as a structural "map" to actively query relevant physical regions in the layout for joint geometric and topological reasoning. We build a 7nm dataset based on the ASAP7 PDK with over 19.5k cells spanning 149 types using automatic tools, targeting six metrics: signal rise/fall delay, transition, and power. Experimental results demonstrate that FusionCell reduces regression error, with an average MAPE of 0.92 percent, and improves Spearman/Kendall ranking over baselines, while accelerating the characterization process by orders of magnitude compared to circuit simulation.

</details>


### [231] [Plug-and-Play Spiking Operators: Breaking the Nonlinearity Bottleneck in Spiking Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20289)
*Xinzhe Yuan,Xiang Peng,Bin Gu,Huan Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a plug-and-play framework for converting Transformer nonlinearities (Softmax, SiLU, normalization) into spike-friendly approximations using LIF neuron groups and bit-shift scaling, enabling training-free ANN-to-SNN conversion of LLMs with less than 1% accuracy drop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ANN-to-SNN conversion pipelines focus on linear operations but lack support for key Transformer nonlinear operators (division, exponentiation, norm computations), which are not naturally supported by standard LIF dynamics, limiting compatibility with neuromorphic execution constraints.

Method: Decomposes nonlinear computations into three primitives (division, exponentiation, ℓ2 norms), realizes them via population computation using LIF neuron groups with lightweight bit-shift scaling to avoid floating-point arithmetic, and composes these as modular operator blocks to support common Transformer nonlinearities without fine-tuning.

Result: Selectively replacing targeted nonlinear operators incurs less than 1% accuracy drop across all evaluated tasks on a range of LLM Transformers.

Conclusion: The proposed plug-and-play framework successfully bridges the gap in ANN-to-SNN conversion for Transformer nonlinearities, offering a practical, training-free solution that maintains high accuracy while enabling neuromorphic-compatible execution.

Abstract: ANN-to-SNN conversion offers a practical, training-free route to spiking large language models. However, current pipelines primarily focus on spike-driven realizations for Transformer linear-algebra operations, while providing limited support for key nonlinear operators. This gap limits compatibility with neuromorphic-style execution constraints, where such nonlinearities typically require division, exponentiation, or norm computations that are not naturally supported by standard leaky integrate-and-fire dynamics. To solve this problem, we propose a plug-and-play framework that implements spike-friendly approximations for Transformer nonlinearities and integrates into existing ANN-to-SNN pipelines. Our method decomposes these nonlinear computations into three recurring primitives -- division, exponentiation, and $\ell_2$ norms -- and realizes them via population computation using LIF neuron groups, combined with lightweight bit-shift scaling to avoid floating-point arithmetic. By composing these primitives as modular operator blocks, our framework supports common Transformer nonlinearities (e.g., Softmax, SiLU, and normalization) without any fine-tuning. Experiments on a range of LLMs Transformers show that selectively replacing the targeted nonlinear operators incurs less than a $1\%$ accuracy drop across all evaluated tasks.

</details>


### [232] [Weasel: Out-of-Domain Generalization for Web Agents via Importance-Diversity Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20291)
*Fatemeh Pesaran zadeh,Seyeon Choi,Xing Han Lù,Siva Reddy,Gunhee Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weasel is a trajectory selection method for offline training of web agents that selects diverse, important trajectory steps with AXTree pruning, achieving ~10-12x training speedups and better out-of-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based web agents fine-tuned on specific trajectories struggle with out-of-domain generalization, and offline training is compute-inefficient due to noisy, redundant trajectories and long AXTree states.

Method: Weasel selects a fixed-budget subset of trajectory steps by optimizing an objective balancing unary importance with pairwise diversity over states, websites, and interaction patterns using a greedy algorithm. It also uses target-centered AXTree pruning and replaces expert traces with model-generated, style-consistent rationales.

Result: Across multiple datasets (AgentTrek, NNetNav), benchmarks (WebArena, WorkArena, MiniWob), and models (Qwen2.5-7B, Gemma3-4B, Qwen3-8B), Weasel improves out-of-domain performance while achieving ~9.7-12.5x training speedups over standard fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Weasel effectively addresses both generalization and efficiency challenges in offline web agent training through principled trajectory selection and pruning, substantially reducing training cost while improving OOD performance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled web agents that follow natural language goals through multi-step browser interactions. However, agents fine-tuned on specific trajectories and domain often struggle to generalize out of domain, and offline training can be compute-inefficient due to noisy, redundant trajectories and long accessibility-tree (AXTree) states. To address both issues, we propose Weasel, a trajectory selection method for offline training of web agents. Weasel selects a fixed-budget subset of trajectory steps by optimizing an objective that balances unary importance with pairwise diversity over states, websites, and interaction patterns, solving efficiently with a greedy algorithm. We further improve efficiency with target-centered AXTree pruning that keeps only content around the ground-truth action target, and we mitigate style mismatch for reasoning-native models by replacing expert traces with model-generated, style-consistent rationales. Across AgentTrek and NNetNav training datasets, evaluations in WebArena, WorkArena, and MiniWob, and experiments with Qwen2.5-7B, Gemma3-4B, and Qwen3-8B, Weasel improves out-of-domain performance while reducing training cost, producing roughly 9.7-12.5$\times$ training speedups over standard fine-tuning. We make the code available at https://github.com/fatemehpesaran310/weasel.

</details>


### [233] [TreeText-CTS: Compact, Source-Traceable Tree-Path Evidence for Irregular Clinical Time-Series Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20292)
*Kwanhyung Lee,Juhwan Choi,Jongheon Kim,Joohyung Lee,Hyeongwon Jang,Eunho Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TreeText-CTS converts irregular EHR time-series data into human-readable, source-traceable tree-path evidence units using frozen XGBoost models, achieving state-of-the-art performance among text-based EHR interfaces while maintaining inspectability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-based EHR interfaces are either too lengthy/raw (serialization) or lack traceability (free-form summaries). The authors aim to bridge readability with source-traceability for clinical time-series data.

Method: TreeText-CTS routes multi-scale window summaries through frozen XGBoost models, verbalizes activated tree paths as deterministic threshold-condition evidence units, selects an informative subset via an evidence selector, and integrates them using a language-model encoder for prediction.

Result: Achieves best AUROC and AUPRC among text-based EHR time-series interfaces on PhysioNet 2012 mortality, MIMIC-III mortality, and PhysioNet 2019 sepsis-onset forecasting; improves AUPRC by 6.0-9.7 absolute percentage points over the prior best text-based interface.

Conclusion: TreeText-CTS demonstrates that decomposing numerical model decisions into traceable, tree-path evidence units can match numerical model performance while providing fully inspectable, source-traceable evidence for each prediction.

Abstract: Numerical time-series models can effectively process irregular electronic health record (EHR) trajectories, but they do not naturally expose the measurements and temporal patterns supporting each risk estimate as readable evidence. Existing text-based interfaces improve readability, but typically rely on either raw serialization, which is lengthy and redundant, or patient-level free-form summaries, which are difficult to trace to source measurements and time windows. To bridge this gap, we introduce TreeText-CTS (Clinical Time-Series), which converts irregular EHR trajectories into human-readable, compact, source-traceable tree-path evidence units without patient-level summarization or inference-time autoregressive decoding. TreeText-CTS routes multi-scale window summaries through frozen XGBoost models and verbalizes activated tree paths as deterministic, source-traceable evidence units composed of threshold conditions. An evidence selector assembles an informative subset of these units, which a language-model encoder then integrates for prediction. Across PhysioNet 2012 mortality, MIMIC-III mortality, and PhysioNet 2019 sepsis-onset forecasting, TreeText-CTS achieves the best AUROC and AUPRC among evaluated text-based EHR time-series interfaces, improving AUPRC by 6.0 to 9.7 absolute percentage points over the strongest prior text-based interface while remaining competitive with numerical time-series models. Ablations show that tree-path evidence construction, evidence selection, and language-model composition each contribute to performance. Because every span passed to the language-model encoder is constructed from activated tree-path threshold conditions, TreeText-CTS makes the evidence supplied to the final predictor inspectable and source-traceable.

</details>


### [234] [Closed-form predictive coding via hierarchical Gaussian filters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20293)
*Aleksandrs Baskakovs,Sylvain Estebe,Kenneth Enevoldsen,Kristoffer Nielbo,Chris Mathys,Nicolas Legrand*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies that fixing precision matrices to identity in predictive coding networks causes slow training and poor depth scaling, and proposes restoring precision-weighted prediction errors via hierarchical Gaussian filters. This yields dynamic uncertainty estimates and Hebbian update rules, achieving near backpropagation efficiency on FashionMNIST while outperforming in online learning, data efficiency, and concept drift tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive coding networks are biologically plausible alternatives to backpropagation but are slower and degrade with depth. The authors trace this to a single simplification: fixing the precision matrix to identity, which discards precision-weighted prediction errors that are essential for fast, local, and Bayesian learning.

Method: The authors cast predictive coding networks as deep hierarchical Gaussian filters (HGFs), restoring precision-weighted message passing. This enables dynamic uncertainty estimation and Hebbian-compatible update rules at each layer. The model jointly learns activations, weights, and precisions under a single free-energy objective without global error signals, iterative inference, or automatic differentiation.

Result: On FashionMNIST, the proposed method approaches backpropagation in wall-clock cost per epoch while converging in fewer epochs. It outperforms backpropagation on online learning, data efficiency, and concept-drift tasks.

Conclusion: Closed-form variational inference with online precision learning provides a tractable foundation for deep predictive coding networks, retaining biological and interpretative advantages without requiring iterative relaxation or global error signals.

Abstract: Predictive coding (PC) offers a local and biologically grounded alternative to backpropagation in the training of artificial neural networks, yet to date, it remains slower, and performance degrades sharply as network depth increases. We trace both problems to a single simplification: current PC networks fix the precision matrix to the identity, discarding precision-weighted prediction errors that the variational derivation requires to be fast, local, and Bayesian. We close this gap by expressing predictive coding networks as deep hierarchical Gaussian filters (HGFs) and restore precision-weighted message passing, yielding dynamic uncertainty estimates and Hebbian-compatible update rules at every layer. The resulting networks can simultaneously learn activations, weights, and precisions under a single free-energy objective, with no global error signal, and resolve inference without requiring iterations or automatic differentiation. On FashionMNIST, our solution approaches backpropagation in epoch-level wall-clock cost while converging in fewer epochs, and outperforms it on online, data efficiency, and concept-drift tasks. We thus establish that closed-form variational inference with online precision learning provides a tractable foundation for deep predictive coding networks, retaining biological and interpretative advantages, without requiring iterative relaxation or global error signals.

</details>


### [235] [Quant.npu: Enabling Efficient Mobile NPU Inference for on-device LLMs via Fully Static Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20295)
*Jinghe Zhang,Daliang Xu,Chenghua Wang,Weikai Xie,Tao Qi,Yun Ma,Mengwei Xu,Gang Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Quant.npu is a fully static quantization framework enabling LLMs to run on mobile NPUs, achieving comparable accuracy to dynamic methods while reducing inference latency by up to 15.1%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PTQ methods use dynamic activation quantization incompatible with NPU hardware constraints. There is a gap between high-fidelity PTQ and NPU-constrained inference that needs to be bridged.

Method: Proposes an integer-only fully static quantization framework with: (1) learnable quantization parameters and rotation matrices, (2) rotation-and-bit-width-aware initialization, (3) distribution-aware selective optimization (two-stage pipeline), (4) sensitivity-guided adaptive mixed-precision scheme.

Result: Achieves comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art methods while reducing inference latency by up to 15.1% on real-world mobile NPUs.

Conclusion: Quant.npu successfully bridges the gap between high-fidelity PTQ and NPU hardware constraints, enabling efficient LLM deployment on mobile devices with fully static quantization.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed on mobile devices, where Neural Processing Units (NPUs) necessitate fully static quantization for optimal inference efficiency. However, existing post-training quantization (PTQ) methods predominantly rely on dynamic activation quantization, rendering them incompatible with NPU hardware constraints. To bridge the gap between high-fidelity PTQ and NPU-constrained inference, we propose Quant.npu, a integer-only fully static quantization framework. It incorporates learnable quantization parameters and rotation matrices, enabling low-bit activation-weight quantization without runtime quantization parameters re-computation. Crucially, we identify that initialization and selective optimization of quantization parameters is pivotal for optimization stability, as improper initialization and naive joint optimization induce gradient instability that disrupts the optimization of rotation matrices. To address this, we propose a rotation-and-bit-width-aware initialization tailored to diverse activation profiles and a distribution-aware selective optimization (two-stage quantization pipeline) tailored to rotated and unrotated tensors. Furthermore, we introduce a sensitivity-guided adaptive mixed-precision scheme to balance accuracy with inference efficiency. Extensive experiments on real-world mobile NPUs demonstrate that Quant.npu achieves comparable accuracy to state-of-the-art methods, while reducing inference latency by up to 15.1%.

</details>


### [236] [Spectral Unforgetting: Post-Hoc Recovery of Damaged Capabilities Without Retraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20296)
*Aarash Abro,Muhammad Tahir*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DG-Hard is a post-hoc spectral repair method that filters fine-tuning weight updates via Donoho-Gavish hard thresholding to recover damaged capabilities without data, achieving strong balanced repair across 14 settings and restoring safety alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning language models for target tasks degrades capabilities not threatened by training data (catastrophic forgetting). Existing solutions either require data or simply revert to base, and evaluation metrics like average accuracy hide per-benchmark failures.

Method: DG-Hard treats the fine-tuning weight update Δ = W_ft - W_base as a low-rank task-aligned signal plus IID-like noise residual. It applies Donoho-Gavish hard singular-value thresholding to each weight-delta matrix, keeping structured high-energy components and removing spectral bulk, requiring no data-dependent tuning.

Result: Across 14 (model, task) settings and 9 cross-domain held-out benchmarks, DG-Hard achieves the strongest balanced repair among post-hoc baselines. It also restores safety alignment degraded by benign fine-tuning on three independent safety axes without using alignment data.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning-induced capability loss is not an unavoidable consequence of specialization but a removable spectral residue in the weight update itself. DG-Hard provides a data-free, closed-form repair solution.

Abstract: Fine-tuning a language model for a target task routinely degrades capabilities the training data never explicitly threatened. We study this phenomenon, known as catastrophic forgetting, and propose a post-hoc repair solution that uses only the pretrained checkpoint $W_{\mathrm{base}}$ and its fine-tuned descendant $W_{\mathrm{ft}}$. The goal is not merely to revert the model toward the base checkpoint, but to recover capabilities damaged by fine-tuning while preserving both the target-task gains and any beneficial held-out improvements. We introduce DG-Hard, a checkpoint-only spectral repair method for the fine-tuning update $Δ= W_{\mathrm{ft}} - W_{\mathrm{base}}$. DG-Hard treats $Δ$ as a low-rank task-aligned signal embedded in an IID-like noise residual that gradient descent has no incentive to remove, and applies the Donoho-Gavish hard singular-value threshold to each weight-delta matrix, keeping the structured high-energy part of the update and removing the spectral bulk. This reduces repair to a closed-form SVD filtering step requiring no data-dependent tuning. A central difficulty is evaluation: average accuracy hides per-benchmark failures, while naive recovery scores reward models that simply revert toward the base. We therefore introduce a partition-conditional metric that separately tracks healing, preservation, non-damage, and target-task retention. Across $14$ (model, task) settings and nine cross-domain held-out benchmarks, DG-Hard achieves the strongest balanced repair among post-hoc baselines. DG-Hard also restores safety alignment degraded by benign fine-tuning on three independent safety axes, despite using no alignment data. These results suggest that part of fine-tuning-induced capability loss is not an unavoidable consequence of specialization, but a removable spectral residue in the weight update itself.

</details>


### [237] [Mechanisms of Misgeneralization in Physical Sequence Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20299)
*Kento Nishi,Raphael Tang,Karun Kumar,Core Francisco Park,Hidenori Tanaka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep learning models trained on trajectory data can produce correct-looking individual trajectories but misrepresent the aggregate distribution over physical quantities like distance or energy—a failure called physical misgeneralization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and explain why generative sequence models trained on curated demonstrations can violate the intended aggregate distribution over physical quantities (e.g., travel distance, mechanical energy), despite producing plausible individual trajectories.

Method: The authors develop a mechanistic account using a data deviation kernel to estimate how local prediction errors propagate through physical measurements, shifting the recovered distribution. They validate on synthetic tasks, maze navigation, and double-pendulum motion tasks.

Result: Physical misgeneralization occurs when local errors typical of the model class propagate through the physical measurement function. The data deviation kernel can predict which physical quantities gain or lose mass.

Conclusion: Understanding the mechanism enables identification of promising mitigation strategies; the authors propose a kernel-informed intervention to address physical misgeneralization.

Abstract: Generative sequence models are often trained to plan motion in physical domains, from robotics to mechanical simulations. When constructing a dataset to train such a model, engineers may curate demonstrations to specify how trajectories should be distributed over a physical quantity like travel distance or mechanical energy. For example, a roboticist building a maze navigation agent might choose demonstrations whose travel distances cover a fixed range uniformly, hoping to constrain the agent's expected power usage. We find that standard deep learning can violate this intent: each generated trajectory can seem plausible on its own, but the aggregate distribution over the physical quantity is wrong. We call this failure physical misgeneralization, and develop an account of its mechanism. Using controlled synthetic tasks, we show that physical misgeneralization arises when local errors typical of the model class propagate through the physical measurement to shift the recovered distribution. We estimate these errors with a data deviation kernel, and we use it to predict which physical quantities gain or lose mass in both our synthetic and more applied maze navigation and double-pendulum motion tasks. Finally, our mechanistic interpretation helps identify which mitigation strategies are structurally promising, and we use it to propose a kernel-informed intervention.

</details>


### [238] [Robust Subspace-Constrained Quadratic Models for Low-Dimensional Structure Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20300)
*Zheng Zhai,Xiaohui Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a robust subspace-constrained quadratic model (SCQM) for learning low-dimensional structure from high-dimensional data, accommodating diverse noise distributions (generalized Gaussian and radial Laplace) and using a gradient-based algorithm with backtracking line-search for efficient optimization. Experiments show superior robustness and reconstruction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To robustly learn low-dimensional structure from high-dimensional data under a broad class of noise distributions (both heavy-tailed and light-tailed), overcoming limitations of existing methods that lack such flexibility.
