Method: The SCQM model extends the SQMF framework to handle generalized Gaussian and radial Laplace noise. A gradient-based algorithm with backtracking line-search is developed for nonconvex optimization. Sensitivity analysis of ℓ_p^p and ℓ_2 loss functions is provided.

Result: Extensive numerical experiments show the proposed approach consistently outperforms existing methods in robustness and reconstruction accuracy.

Conclusion: The proposed SCQM offers a robust and flexible solution for low-dimensional structure learning under diverse noise conditions, with strong empirical performance.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a robust subspace-constrained quadratic model (SCQM) for learning low-dimensional structure from high-dimensional data. Building upon the subspace-constrained quadratic matrix factorization (SQMF) framework, the proposed model accommodates a broad class of noise distributions, including generalized Gaussian and radial Laplace models. This generalization enables reliable performance under both heavy-tailed and light-tailed noise, thereby substantially enhancing robustness across diverse data regimes. To efficiently address the resulting nonconvex optimization problem, we develop a gradient-based algorithm equipped with a backtracking line-search strategy that ensures stable and efficient convergence. In addition, we present a sensitivity analysis of the $\ell_p^p$ and $\ell_2$ loss functions, elucidating their distinct behaviors under varying noise characteristics. Extensive numerical experiments corroborate the theoretical analysis and demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms existing methods in terms of robustness and reconstruction accuracy.

</details>


### [239] [Neural Collapse by Design: Learning Class Prototypes on the Hypersphere](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20302)
*Panagiotis Koromilas,Theodoros Giannakopoulos,Mihalis A. Nicolaou,Yannis Panagakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that cross-entropy and supervised contrastive learning are both forms of prototype contrast on the unit hypersphere, and proposes NTCE/NONL losses that fix CE's shortcomings and SCL with fixed prototypes that eliminates the need for linear probing, achieving Neural Collapse efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Both dominant supervised learning paradigms (CE and SCL) fail to reach Neural Collapse (NC) — the theoretical optimum — due to specific structural deficiencies: CE leaves radial degrees of freedom unconstrained leading to degenerate geometry, while SCL discards its learned NC structure during the linear probing phase.

Method: (1) From CE side: Proposes NTCE and NONL — normalized losses that incorporate contrastive optimization's benefits (large effective negative set, decoupled alignment/uniformity terms) into classifier learning. (2) From SCL side: Proves SCL's objective already optimizes for a classifier using class mean embeddings as weights, and proposes using fixed prototypes instead of linear probing.

Result: NTCE and NONL surpass CE accuracy on 4 benchmarks (incl. ImageNet-1K), achieve ≥95% NC, match CE's converged NC on 4/5 metrics in <7.5% iterations. SCL with fixed prototypes matches linear probing without classifier training. Transfer learning improves by +5.5% mean relative, up to +8.7% under severe class imbalance, and lower mCE on ImageNet-C.

Conclusion: Supervised learning can be recast as prototype learning on the hypersphere, where Neural Collapse is reached by design on both CE and SCL paths, closing the gap between theory and practice.

Abstract: Supervised classification has a theoretical optimum, Neural Collapse (NC), yet neither of its two dominant paradigms reaches it in practice. Cross entropy (CE) leaves radial degrees of freedom unconstrained and converges to a degenerate geometry, while supervised contrastive learning (SCL) drives features toward NC during pretraining but discards this structure in a post hoc linear probing phase. We show that both paradigms are different appearances of the same method, prototype contrast on the unit hypersphere, and that closing the gap requires fixing each at its specific point of failure. From the CE side, we propose NTCE and NONL, two normalized losses that import contrastive optimization's missing ingredients into classifier learning: a large effective negative set and decoupled alignment and uniformity terms. From the SCL side, we prove that SCL's objective already optimizes throughout training for a principled classifier whose weights are the class mean embeddings, making linear probing both redundant and harmful. Empirically, on four benchmarks including ImageNet-1K, NTCE and NONL surpass CE accuracy, closely approximate NC ($\geq 95\%$), and match CE's converged NC on 4/5 metrics in under $7.5\%$ of its iterations, while SCL with fixed prototypes matches linear probing without the hours-long classifier training phase. The learned geometry yields $+5.5\%$ mean relative improvement in transfer learning, up to $+8.7\%$ under severe class imbalance, and lower mCE on ImageNet-C, recasting supervised learning as prototype learning on the hypersphere, with NC reached by design on both paths.

</details>


### [240] [AirfoilGen: A valid-by-construction and performance-aware latent diffusion model for airfoil generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20303)
*Zhijie Yang,Min Tang,Qiang Zou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AirfoilGen is a latent diffusion model for airfoil design that ensures geometric validity through a circle sweeping representation and enables explicit control over aerodynamic performance (lift/drag coefficients) via conditional latent diffusion, achieving 98.41% conditioning accuracy on a new 200,000+ airfoil dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep generative approaches for airfoil design suffer from limited geometric validity (producing invalid shapes) and poor physical controllability (cannot effectively condition on aerodynamic performance like lift and drag coefficients).

Method: (1) Circle sweeping representation - a novel valid-by-construction airfoil representation scheme that constrains generation to respect essential airfoil characteristics. (2) Transformer-based encoder to map airfoils into latent embeddings. (3) Conditional diffusion model that denoises Gaussian noise into these latent embeddings conditioned on target aerodynamic performance. (4) A new large-scale dataset of 200,000+ airfoils.

Result: AirfoilGen achieves far greater geometric validity and aerodynamic performance controllability than previous methods, with an average performance-conditioning accuracy of 98.41%.

Conclusion: The proposed valid-by-construction representation and conditional latent diffusion framework successfully address key limitations of prior airfoil generation approaches, enabling reliable and controllable airfoil shape design.

Abstract: Airfoil shape design is a fundamental task in aerospace engineering, with a direct impact on flight stability and fuel consumption. Deep learning has recently emerged as a promising tool for this task, but existing deep generative approaches remain limited in both geometric validity and physical controllability. They offer little control over the generated shapes, yielding invalid geometries, and they typically do not condition effectively on aerodynamic performance. To address these issues, this paper proposes AirfoilGen, a valid-by-construction and performance-aware latent diffusion model for airfoil. It first introduces a novel airfoil representation scheme, the circle sweeping representation, to constrain the generative process so that output shapes respect essential airfoil characteristics. It then enables explicit control over aerodynamic performance (e.g., lift and drag coefficients) by operating in a learned latent space: a transformer model encodes airfoil shapes into vector embeddings, and a conditional diffusion model denoises Gaussian noise into these latent embeddings while incorporating target aerodynamic performance. In addition, this paper presents a new dataset of over 200,000 airfoils, which is substantially larger than the widely used UIUC airfoil dataset (1,650 airfoils) and more suitable for training modern deep generative models. Experiments demonstrate that AirfoilGen enables airfoil generation with far greater geometric validity and aerodynamic performance controllability than previously achievable, with an average performance-conditioning accuracy of 98.41%.

</details>


### [241] [WaveGraphNet: Physics-Consistent Guided-Wave Damage Localization through Coupled Inverse-Forward Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20311)
*Vinay Sharma,Aditya Bharade,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WaveGraphNet is a coupled inverse-forward graph learning framework that improves guided-wave damage localization in CFRP plates by using a graph representation of sparse transducer networks and a forward branch as a physics-consistent regularizer, showing better extrapolation to unseen regions compared to baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Guided-wave damage localization models trained on limited damage locations generalize poorly to unseen regions of the structure due to weakly constrained spatial inference from sparse piezoelectric transducer networks.

Method: WaveGraphNet models the sensing layout as a graph (transducers as nodes, propagation paths as edges). It uses an inverse branch to map graph-structured spectral descriptors to a damage location, and a forward branch to predict path-wise energy-deviation patterns. The forward branch acts as a physics-consistent regularizer during training.

Result: The graph-based formulation provides strong localization for sparse guided-wave sensing and shows improved robustness in extrapolation to held-out regions compared to non-graph and graph baselines.

Conclusion: Coupled inverse-forward graph learning is an effective strategy for guided-wave damage localization under limited spatial coverage.

Abstract: Guided-wave structural health monitoring enables damage localization in composite plates using sparse networks of bonded piezoelectric transducers. However, inferring the spatial location of defects from pitch-catch measurements remains weakly constrained when only a limited set of damage locations is available for training. As a result, models trained to predict defect locations may perform well on seen cases but generalize poorly to unseen regions of the structure.
  This paper proposes WaveGraphNet, a coupled inverse--forward graph learning framework for guided-wave damage localization in Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer (CFRP) plates. The sensing layout is explicitly modeled as a graph, where transducers are represented as nodes and measured propagation paths define the graph connectivity. An inverse branch maps graph-structured spectral descriptors of differential guided-wave responses to a damage location, while a forward branch predicts the path-wise energy-deviation patterns of measured wave responses associated with a candidate location. During training, the forward branch serves as a physics-consistent regularizer, discouraging location estimates that are numerically plausible but inconsistent with the measured redistribution of wave-response energy. This coupling encourages agreement between inferred damage coordinates and the underlying wave propagation behavior.
  Within this benchmark, the proposed graph-based formulation provides a strong localization model for sparse guided-wave sensing and demonstrates improved robustness in extrapolation to held-out regions compared to both non-graph and graph baselines. These results highlight the potential of coupled inverse-forward graph learning as an effective strategy for guided-wave localization under limited spatial coverage.

</details>


### [242] [Less Data, Faster Training: repeating smaller datasets speeds up learning via sampling biases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20314)
*Jingwen Liu,Ezra Edelman,Surbhi Goel,Bingbin Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the "small-vs-large gap" phenomenon where training on smaller datasets with more repetitions can yield compute savings compared to using larger datasets, attributing this to layer-wise growth enabled by sampling biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why training on fewer samples with more repetitions can be computationally more efficient than using larger datasets, a phenomenon unexplained by prior theory.

Method: Theoretical analysis combined with empirical evidence from various interventions, examining layer-wise growth and sampling biases across different algorithmic tasks, architectures, and optimizers.

Result: Smaller datasets with more repetitions can lead to compute savings during training, and this speedup is attributed to appropriate layer-wise growth enabled by sampling biases.

Conclusion: Using smaller datasets with more repetitions is not merely a fallback under data scarcity, but can be proactively leveraged as a favorable inductive bias for optimization, especially in reasoning tasks.

Abstract: This work investigates the ``small-vs-large gap'', where repeating on fewer samples can lead to compute saving during training compared to using a larger dataset. This is observed across algorithmic tasks, architectures and optimizers and cannot be explained using prior theory. We argue that the speedup comes from appropriate layer-wise growth enabled by sampling biases, which is more pronounced when the dataset size is smaller. We provide both theoretical analysis and empirical evidence from various interventions. Our results suggest that using a smaller dataset with more repetitions is not just a fallback strategy under data scarcity, but can be proactively leveraged as a favorable inductive biases for optimization, particularly in reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [243] [Causal Unlearning in Collaborative Optimization: Exact and Approximate Influence Reversal under Adversarial Contributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20341)
*Ali Mahdavi,Azadeh Zamanifar,Amirfarhad Farhadi,Omid Kashefi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HF-KCU efficiently removes client data from federated learning models using Krylov subspace influence approximation, achieving ~48x speedup over retraining with minimal accuracy loss (within 0.60%) and provable privacy restoration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning systems must support data deletion requests for privacy compliance, but retraining from scratch after each deletion is computationally prohibitive. An efficient unlearning method is needed.

Method: HF-KCU approximates influence functions using conjugate gradient iterations in Krylov subspaces (O(kd) complexity), combined with a causal weighting mechanism that restricts parameter updates only to clients holding deleted data.

Result: On CIFAR-10 with Dirichlet (alpha=0.5) partitioning: 47.75x speedup over retraining, test accuracy within 0.60% of baseline (71.16% vs 71.76%), and membership inference attack success rate of 0.499 matching retrained model. Validated on ResNet-18, SimpleCNN, and ViT-Lite across three datasets.

Conclusion: HF-KCU provides efficient, precise, and interpretable federated unlearning with convergence guarantees and graceful degradation under adversarial threats, suitable for production systems with asynchronous deletion requests and constrained computational budgets.

Abstract: Federated learning systems must support data deletion requests to comply with privacy regulations, yet retraining from scratch after each deletion is computationally prohibitive. We present HF-KCU, a method that removes a client's contribution by approximating the influence function through conjugate gradient iterations in Krylov subspaces, reducing complexity from O(d^3) to O(kd) where k<<d.A causal weighting mechanism ensures that only clients holding the deleted data receive parameter updates, preventing spurious changes to unaffected clients. Our method is designed to handle bounded adversarial perturbations to the Hessian and gradient, providing graceful degradation under realistic threat models. We validate HF-KCU across convolutional (ResNet-18, SimpleCNN) and transformer (ViT-Lite) architectures on CIFAR-10, MNIST, and Fashion-MNIST. On CIFAR-10 under Dirichlet (alpha=0.5) partitioning, HF-KCU achieves 47.75 times speedup over retraining while maintaining test accuracy within 0.60% of the rational baseline(71.16 vs 71.76 %). Membership inference attacks on the forget set yield success rates of 0.499 matching the retrained model and confirming effective privacy restoration. We provide convergence guarantees showing that the Krylov approximation error decreases as O((k ^1/2-1)/(k^1/2+1)) where k is the Hessian condition number. The causal weighting mechanism ensures surgical updates, where only clients holding deleted data are modified, preserving model quality for unaffected participants and avoiding the instability of gradient-based approaches in asynchronous federated settings. This design provides interpretability as each update is directly traceable to the influence of the deleted data. The method's efficiency and precision make it suitable for production federated systems where deletion requests arrive asynchronously and computational budgets are constrained.

</details>


### [244] [Symmetrization of Loss Functions for Robust Training of Neural Networks in the Presence of Noisy Labels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20347)
*Alexandre Lemire Paquin,Brahim Chaib-Draa,Philippe Giguère*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies symmetrization of loss functions for label noise robustness, deriving a multi-class unhinged loss from cross-entropy symmetrization, proving its uniqueness as a convex multi-class symmetric loss, and introducing SGCE and α-MAE interpolating losses that show competitive performance on noisy-label benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Labeling training data is expensive and error-prone, motivating the need for loss functions robust to label noise. The symmetry condition offers theoretical robustness guarantees, but existing symmetric losses in the multi-class setting are not well understood.

Method: The authors study symmetrization via decomposing multi-class loss into symmetric and class-insensitive components, derive the multi-class unhinged loss from cross-entropy symmetrization, prove its uniqueness as a convex multi-class symmetric loss, and introduce SGCE and α-MAE losses that interpolate between the unhinged loss and MAE with controllable β-smoothness.

Result: The multi-class unhinged loss is the unique convex multi-class symmetric loss, and its linear approximation around equal-score vectors locally characterizes any symmetric loss. SGCE and α-MAE achieve competitive performance on standard noisy-label benchmarks.

Conclusion: Symmetrization provides a principled way to design robust loss functions; the multi-class unhinged loss is fundamental, and interpolated losses like SGCE and α-MAE offer practical robustness with smoothness control.

Abstract: Labeling a training set is often expensive and susceptible to errors, making the design of robust loss functions for label noise an important problem. The symmetry condition provides theoretical guarantees for robustness to such noise. In this work, we study a symmetrization method arising from the unique decomposition of any multi-class loss function into a symmetric component and a class-insensitive term. In particular, symmetrizing the cross-entropy loss leads to a linear multi-class extension of the unhinged loss. Unlike in the binary case, the multi-class version must have specific coefficients in order to satisfy the symmetry condition. Under suitable assumptions, we show that this multi-class unhinged loss is the unique convex multi-class symmetric loss. We also show that it has a fundamental local role: the linear approximation of any symmetric loss around score vectors with equal components is equivalent to the multi-class unhinged loss. We then introduce SGCE and alpha-MAE, two loss functions that interpolate between the multi-class unhinged loss and the Mean Absolute Error while allowing control of the beta-smoothness of the loss. Experiments on standard noisy-label benchmarks show competitive performance compared with existing robust loss functions.

</details>


### [245] [Consistently Informative Soft-Label Temperature for Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20357)
*Hoang-Chau Luong,Nghia Van Vo,Kaiqi Zhao,Lingwei Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CIST introduces separate sample-wise adaptive temperatures for teacher and student in knowledge distillation to produce consistently informative soft labels and improve performance across vision and language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed-temperature KD is sample-agnostic, causing inconsistent entropy across teacher soft labels (some too sharp, some too smooth) and imposes rigid logit-scale alignment between mismatched teacher-student capacities.

Method: CIST assigns separate adaptive temperatures per sample to teacher and student, producing consistently informative soft labels while relaxing strict logit-scale matching, and reweights distillation loss based on teacher confidence and student difficulty.

Result: Experiments on vision and language tasks show consistent improvements over standard KD and strong baselines with negligible computational overhead.

Conclusion: Adaptive sample-wise temperature addresses fixed-temperature entropy inconsistency and capacity mismatch, providing a principled and efficient improvement for knowledge distillation.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation (KD) transfers knowledge from a high-capacity teacher to a compact student by matching their predictive distributions, with temperature scaling serving as a central mechanism for smoothing teacher predictions and exposing informative "dark knowledge" beyond the hard label. However, the standard fixed-temperature design is inherently sample-agnostic. Since samples differ in logit scale and learning difficulty, a single global temperature produces teacher soft labels with highly inconsistent entropy: some predictions remain overly sharp and provide limited inter-class information, whereas others become over-smoothed and lose class-discriminative information. Moreover, sharing the same temperature between teacher and student further imposes rigid logit-scale alignment despite their capacity mismatch. To address these limitations, we propose CIST (Consistently Informative Soft-label Temperature), which assigns separate sample-wise adaptive temperatures to the teacher and student. This design produces consistently informative teacher soft labels while relaxing rigid teacher--student logit-scale matching. It also reweights the distillation objective according to teacher confidence and student learning difficulty. Theoretically, we show that teacher-label entropy is largely governed by the ratio between the maximum teacher logit and the temperature, providing a principled basis for adaptive smoothing. Empirically, CIST mitigates the inconsistency induced by fixed temperature, and experiments on both vision and language distillation tasks show consistent improvements over standard KD and strong baselines with negligible computational overhead.

</details>


### [246] [Nonlocal operator learning for fMRI encoding and decoding tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20389)
*Andreas Kramer,Saugat Acharya,Alice Giola,Emanuele Zappala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes neural integral-operator models for fMRI encoding/decoding, showing that broader spatiotemporal context improves performance and latent-space structure across two datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: fMRI data has high-dimensional spatiotemporal structure that makes prediction and decoding difficult; there is a need for models that can capture nonlocal spatiotemporal dependencies.

Method: A latent neural integral operator framework that performs fixed point iterations in an auxiliary space, with a decoder for classification and stimuli prediction. Evaluated on two open-source fMRI datasets.

Result: Larger temporal windows improve results and produce more structured latent representations. The learned latent space shows clearer class separation than raw data in decoding. Encoding performance remains moderate.

Conclusion: Neural integral operators are promising for modeling fMRI dynamics; broader spatiotemporal context benefits prediction and representation learning; models must be specifically designed to capture distributed nonlocal dependencies in brain dynamics.

Abstract: Functional MRI data exhibit high-dimensional spatiotemporal structure, making both prediction and decoding challenging. In this work, we investigate neural integral-operator-based models for encoding and decoding tasks in fMRI, with particular emphasis on the role of nonlocal spatiotemporal context. We implement a latent neural integral operator framework that performs fixed point iterations in an auxiliary space from which classification and stimuli prediction is performed via a decoder. We evaluate our model on two open-source fMRI datasets.
  Our experiments examine both decoding of stimuli from fMRI recordings and encoding of fMRI dynamics from stimulus representations. A main focus is the effect of spatiotemporal context: we systematically compare short and long temporal windows, as well as the use of visual cortex vs whole brain recordings, and analyze their influence on performance and latent-space geometry. Across tasks and datasets, larger temporal windows generally improve results and produce more structured learned representations. In decoding experiments, the learned latent space often provides clearer class separation than the raw data. In encoding experiments, although absolute performance remains moderate due to the difficulty of the task, longer temporal windows still yield consistent gains.
  These findings suggest that neural integral operators provide a promising framework for modeling fMRI dynamics and that broader spatiotemporal context can be beneficial for both prediction and representation learning. More broadly, the results indicate that exploiting distributed nonlocal structure in brain dynamics requires model architectures specifically designed to capture such dependencies.

</details>


### [247] [Score-Based Causal Discovery of Latent Variable Causal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20396)
*Ignavier Ng,Xinshuai Dong,Haoyue Dai,Biwei Huang,Peter Spirtes,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops score-based methods (building on GES) for identifying causal structures with latent variables, proving score equivalence and consistency, characterizing degrees of freedom under various structural assumptions, and unifying several existing constraint-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing constraint-based methods for causal discovery with latent variables suffer from testing-order dependency, error propagation, and significance level selection issues. Score-based methods like GES work well without latent variables but are difficult to formulate when latent variables are present. The authors aim to bridge this gap.

Method: The authors formulate a scoring function that achieves score equivalence and consistency for latent variable causal models. They characterize degrees of freedom for the marginal distribution over observed variables under different structural assumptions and develop both exact and continuous score-based methods.

Result: The proposed score-based methods provide a unified view of several existing constraint-based methods, and experimental results validate their effectiveness.

Conclusion: Score-based methods can be effectively extended to causal discovery with latent variables, offering advantages over constraint-based methods and unifying multiple existing approaches under a single framework.

Abstract: Identifying latent variables and the causal structure involving them is essential across various scientific fields. While many existing works fall under the category of constraint-based methods (with e.g. conditional independence or rank deficiency tests), they may face empirical challenges such as testing-order dependency, error propagation, and choosing an appropriate significance level. These issues can potentially be mitigated by properly designed score-based methods, such as Greedy Equivalence Search (GES) (Chickering, 2002) in the specific setting without latent variables. Yet, formulating score-based methods with latent variables is highly challenging. In this work, we develop score-based methods that are capable of identifying causal structures containing causally-related latent variables with identifiability guarantees. Specifically, we show that a properly formulated scoring function can achieve score equivalence and consistency for structure learning of latent variable causal models. We further provide a characterization of the degrees of freedom for the marginal over the observed variables under multiple structural assumptions considered in the literature, and accordingly develop both exact and continuous score-based methods. This offers a unified view of several existing constraint-based methods with different structural assumptions. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

</details>


### [248] [Decomposing MXFP4 quantization error for LLM reinforcement learning: reducible bias, recoverable deadzone, and an irreducible floor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20402)
*Xiaocan Li,Shiliang Wu,Zheng Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper decomposes MXFP4 quantization error into three components (scale bias, deadzone truncation, grid noise) that each damage distinct RL training pathways, and proposes targeted corrections that recover near-BF16 accuracy in LLM post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MXFP4 quantization can accelerate RL post-training of LLMs but introduces severe accuracy degradation. Existing work treats quantization error as monolithic noise, missing the distinct mechanisms by which it damages training.

Method: The paper proves an exact three-way decomposition of MXFP4 quantization error into: (1) scale bias from power-of-two rounding, (2) deadzone truncation from zeroing small values, and (3) grid noise from rounding to nearest 4-bit grid. Three targeted corrections are proposed: Macro-block scaling, Outlier Fallback, and Adaptive Quantization Noise (AQN).

Result: On Qwen2.5-3B dense and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base MoE models, the targeted corrections recover BF16 accuracy to within 0.7% and 3.0% respectively.

Conclusion: Each error component dominates a distinct RL failure mode, and the proposed component-targeted corrections effectively mitigate accuracy degradation from MXFP4 quantization in RL post-training of LLMs.

Abstract: MXFP4 arithmetic can dramatically accelerate reinforcement learning (RL) post-training of large language models (LLMs), yet the quantization error introduces severe accuracy degradation. Existing work treats the quantization error as a monolithic noise term, missing the distinct mechanisms upon interpreting how quantization error damages training. We prove an exact three-way decomposition of quantization error and show how each component dominates a distinct RL training pathway. Our theoretical and empirical analysis decomposes the MXFP4 quantization error into three additive components: "scale bias" from power-of-two rounding, "deadzone truncation" from zeroing small values, and "grid noise" from rounding to the nearest 4-bit grid. Each component dominates a distinct RL failure mode: scale bias accumulates multiplicatively through the backward pass, affecting gradient accuracy; deadzone truncation degrades rollout quality; and grid noise raises the policy's entropy. We combine corrections that are RL failure mode-targeted but not component-exclusive: Macro-block scaling to reduce scale bias, Outlier Fallback recovers deadzone entries, but also partially reduces scale bias induced error, and Adaptive Quantization Noise (AQN) for controlling the policy entropy. On Qwen2.5-3B dense and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Base mixture-of-experts model, the targeted corrections recover BF16 accuracy to within 0.7% and 3.0% respectively.

</details>


### [249] [Spectral Souping: A Unified Framework for Online Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20408)
*Yinlam Chow,Guy Tennenholtz,Ted Yun,James Harrison,Arthur Gretton,Andre Barreto,Bo Dai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spectral Souping introduces a universal spectral representation in LLMs that enables efficient offline learning of specialized policy bases and online model merging at inference time for dynamic, individualized preference alignment without costly retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLHF aligns LLMs with aggregate human preferences but fails to address diverse, conflicting individual user needs.

Method: A two-phase approach: (1) offline learning of specialized policy bases for distinct preference dimensions using a discovered universal spectral representation, (2) online "souping" algorithm that merges outputs or parameters at inference time for rapid adaptation.

Result: Significant performance improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods on online preference alignment benchmarks.

Conclusion: Spectral Souping provides a scalable, computationally efficient solution for dynamically adapting LLMs to individual user preferences without costly online retraining.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) effectively aligns Large Language Models (LLMs) with aggregate human preferences but often fails to address the diverse and conflicting needs of individual users. To overcome this issue, we introduce Spectral Souping, a unified framework for efficient, online preference alignment. Our contribution is the discovery of a universal spectral representation within LLMs, which is proven to be highly amenable to model merging. This theoretical insight enables a two-phase methodology: we first learn a basis of specialized policies offline, each focused on a distinct, fine-grained preference dimension. An online adaptation algorithm then efficiently ``soups'' these policies at inference time, either by merging their outputs or parameters, enabling rapid model adaptation without the need for costly online retraining w.r.t. tailored preference rewards. Experiments on online preference alignment benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves significant performance improvements over existing state-of-the-art approaches, presenting a scalable and computationally efficient solution for dynamically adapting LLMs to individual user preferences.

</details>


### [250] [Supervised Latent Restructuring for Small-Data Quantum Learning in Plant Phenomics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20413)
*Alakananda Mitra,David H. Fleisher,Vangimalla Reddy,Chittaranjan Ray*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hybrid workflow combining PCA compression (1280D→64D), supervised LDA restructuring (64D→11D), and GPU-accelerated Quantum Kernel Alignment on NVIDIA L40S for classifying high-dimensional plant phenomics data with very small sample sizes. The LDA restructuring improves geometric separability (Silhouette from -0.006 to 0.197), but the 11D bottleneck degrades some classical models (RBF-SVM, Random Forest) while improving others (Linear SVM, XGBoost). QKA under constrained optimization remains challenging, showing that good latent geometry alone is insufficient for strong quantum performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional biological data with very small sample sizes make reliable classification difficult, as kernel methods lose discriminative power when latent compression fails to preserve class-separating structure. The paper aims to address this mismatch in fine-grained plant phenomics.

Method: A hybrid workflow: (1) compress 1280D deep image embeddings to 64D via PCA, (2) restructure to 11D supervised latent space via Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), (3) apply GPU-accelerated Quantum Kernel Alignment (QKA) on NVIDIA L40S hardware for classification.

Result: Supervised LDA restructuring improves Silhouette coefficient from -0.006 (PCA-64) to 0.197 (LDA-11). Classical evaluation shows mixed results: Linear SVM and XGBoost improve, while RBF-SVM and Random Forest degrade under the 11D bottleneck. QKA remains challenging under constrained optimization budget.

Conclusion: Representation geometry is a central design variable in small-data quantum learning, but latent geometry alone is insufficient for strong trainable quantum performance. The practical difficulty of recovering nonlinear discriminative structure from aggressively compressed biological representations is exposed.

Abstract: High-dimensional biological data often exhibit a severe mismatch between feature dimensionality and sample size, making reliable classification difficult in extremely small-data regimes. In these settings, kernel methods can lose discriminative power when latent compression fails to preserve class-separating structure. We study this problem in fine-grained plant phenomics and propose a hybrid workflow that compresses 1280-dimensional deep image embeddings into a 64-dimensional PCA space and then restructures them into an 11-dimensional supervised latent space using Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), followed by GPU-accelerated Quantum Kernel Alignment (QKA) on NVIDIA L40S hardware. Empirically, supervised latent restructuring substantially improves the geometric separability of the compressed representation, increasing the Silhouette coefficient from 0.003 in the raw embedding space and -0.006 in PCA-64 to 0.197 in the supervised LDA-11 space. However, downstream classical evaluation reveals a clear compression trade-off: Linear SVM and XGBoost improve in the restructured latent space, whereas RBF-SVM and Random Forest degrade under the same 11-dimensional bottleneck. Under a constrained optimization budget, QKA in this regime remains challenging, indicating that latent geometry alone is not sufficient for strong trainable quantum performance. These findings position representation geometry as a central design variable in small-data quantum learning and expose the practical difficulty of recovering nonlinear discriminative structure from aggressively compressed biological representations.

</details>


### [251] [Miller-Index-Based Latent Crystallographic Fracture Plane Reasoning with Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20416)
*Qinwu Xu,Yifan Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can use crystallographic plane indices (Miller indices) as a structured latent representation for reasoning about fracture geometry. Experiments show MLLMs can perform latent inference in idealized settings and reject the representation when physics doesn't support it, suggesting they can function as physics-aware reasoning systems with explicit validity modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if MLLMs can leverage structured crystallographic priors (Miller indices) for physics-aware reasoning about fracture geometry, bridging between visual observations and physical latent representations.

Method: Formulate Miller indices as a latent variable governing planar fracture; evaluate latent inference (mapping visuals to plane hypotheses) and latent applicability assessment (determining if representation is meaningful); experiments on synthetic data, 2D-3D geometric pairs, and real fracture images across ceramics, glass, metals, and concrete.

Result: MLLMs reliably perform latent inference in idealized settings and can appropriately reject the latent representation when the underlying physics does not support it.

Conclusion: MLLMs can act as physics-aware reasoning systems conditioned on structured latent priors, provided the domain of validity is explicitly modeled.

Abstract: We study whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can leverage crystallographic plane indices (Miller indices) as a structured latent representation for reasoning about fracture geometry. We formulate Miller indices $z = (h,k,l)$ as a latent variable governing idealized planar fracture and evaluate two complementary capabilities: (i) latent inference, where the model maps visual observations to plane hypotheses under physically valid conditions, and (ii) latent applicability assessment, where the model determines whether such a representation is meaningful for a given fracture image.
  Through extensive experiments spanning synthetic data, controlled 2D--3D geometric pairs, and real-world fracture images across multiple material classes -- including ceramics, glass, metals, and concrete -- we show that MLLMs can reliably perform latent inference in idealized settings and, critically, can reject the latent representation when the underlying physics does not support it. These results suggest that MLLMs can act as physics-aware reasoning systems conditioned on structured latent priors, provided that the domain of validity is explicitly modeled.

</details>


### [252] [Can Conversational XAI Improve User Performance? An Experimental Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20439)
*Sven Kruschel,Julian Rosenberger,Lasse Bohlen,Mathias Kraus,Patrick Zschech*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes an experimental design to evaluate conversational XAI assistants against Q&A-based assistance, finding that both help users outperform a predictive model but show no significant difference between assistance types, with plans for refinement in a full study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: XAI techniques often fail to improve user performance; conversational XAI assistants promise to overcome these limitations, but empirical evidence on their objective impact is limited.

Method: Proposes an experimental design with an explainable-by-design model; compares conversational assistance vs. Q&A-based assistance; measures prediction accuracy, model understanding, and error identification.

Result: Preliminary results (N=42) show both treatments significantly outperform the model, but no significant performance differences between assistance types and modest engagement overall.

Conclusion: Findings inform refinements for a full study, including enhanced engagement interventions and investigation of mechanisms driving improved predictions.

Abstract: Explainable AI (XAI) techniques aim to provide insights into predictive models and enhance user performance, yet they often fall short of these expectations. Conversational XAI assistants promise to overcome such limitations, but empirical evidence on their impact on objective performance measures remains limited. We propose an experimental design for evaluating explanation assistance through prediction accuracy, model understanding, and error identification. Using an explainable-by-design prediction model, we create conditions where users can outperform the model by identifying and compensating for systematic errors. We compare conversational assistance against Q&A-based assistance to assess which better supports users in working with model explanations. Preliminary results from testing our experimental design show that participants (N=42) in both treatments significantly outperformed the model but reveal no performance differences between assistance types and modest engagement overall. These findings inform refinements for our planned full study, including enhanced engagement interventions and investigation of the mechanisms driving improved predictions.

</details>


### [253] [Mechanistic Interpretability for Learning Assurance of a Vision-Based Landing System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20607)
*Romeo Valentin,Olivia Beyer Bruvik,Marc R. Schlichting,Mykel J. Kochenderfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method to provide representation-level assurance evidence for neural networks in aviation, specifically for a vision-based aircraft landing system. By decomposing a Vision Transformer's patch embeddings into content (task-relevant) and style (domain-specific) atoms via K-SVD sparse dictionary learning, the authors show that the model relies primarily on contentful representations. They also introduce out-of-model-scope (OOMS) detection as a runtime monitoring technique, addressing EASA's learning-assurance guidance requirements for data-driven aviation systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EASA's learning-assurance guidance requires data-driven aviation systems to build and monitor their own situation representation, but neural networks currently lack the technical means to provide such evidence. This paper aims to address this gap.

Method: The authors train a Vision Transformer for runway keypoint regression on LARDv2, decompose per-patch embeddings into interpretable atoms via K-SVD sparse dictionary learning, separate content (task-relevant) from style (domain-specific) atoms, and define out-of-model-scope (OOMS) detection for runtime monitoring.

Result: Contentful atoms track task-relevant runway structure, stylistic atoms track domain-specific appearance, and the regression head places almost all linear weight on contentful atoms. OOMS detection complements existing OOD monitoring approaches.

Conclusion: This work delivers the first concrete piece of representation-level evidence that EASA learning-assurance guidance demands, pointing to mechanistic interpretability as a practical building block for future aviation safety cases.

Abstract: EASA's learning-assurance guidance requires data-driven aviation systems to build and monitor their own situation representation, yet for neural networks the technical means to provide such evidence remain an open problem. We address this gap for a vision-based aircraft landing system: we propose that a minimally assurable model must at least be shown to separate content from style in its own situation representation. Showing that the model's predictions then rely largely on the contentful representation components leads to a concrete assurance path. To demonstrate this assurance path on a concrete model we train a vision transformer model for runway keypoint regression on the LARDv2 dataset. The model, which acts as the subject for our assurance demonstration, produces per-patch embeddings that we decompose into interpretable atoms via K-SVD sparse dictionary learning. A qualitative visualization confirms that contentful atoms track task-relevant runway structure and stylistic atoms track domain-specific appearance, and the regression head is shown to place almost all of its linear weight on contentful atoms. We further build on the content/style separation and define out-of-model-scope (OOMS) detection, a novel runtime assurance approach directly monitoring the model's situation representation. OOMS monitoring is complementary to operational design domain and output-space out-of-distribution monitoring and addresses concrete requirements of the recent EASA guidance. By directly analyzing a model's situation representation both at test time and runtime, this work delivers the first concrete piece of the representation-level evidence that EASA learning-assurance guidance demands, and points to mechanistic interpretability as a practical building block of future aviation safety cases.

</details>


### [254] [Group-Algebraic Tensors: Provably-optimal Equivariant Learning and Physical Symmetry Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20440)
*Paulina Hoyos,Shashanka Ubaru,Dongsung Huh,Vasileios Kalantzis,Kenneth L. Clarkson,Misha Kilmer,Haim Avron,Lior Horesh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the $\star_G$ tensor algebra, making group equivariance an intrinsic algebraic property rather than an architectural constraint. It provides three theoretical pillars: Eckart-Young optimality for $\star_G$-SVD, Kronecker factorization for composing symmetries, and a Lean 4 formalization. Empirically, it recovers Wigner-Eckart selection rules from QM9 data and achieves closed-form predictions with ~50-90x fewer parameters than MLPs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To make equivariance an intrinsic algebraic property rather than an architectural constraint, enabling capabilities that equivariant neural networks structurally cannot provide.

Method: The $\star_G$ tensor algebra framework with three theoretical pillars: (i) Eckart-Young optimality for $\star_G$-SVD, (ii) Kronecker factorization for composing symmetries, and (iii) Lean 4 formalization. Empirical validation on QM9 molecular geometry over chiral octahedral subgroup of SO(3).

Result: Recovers Wigner-Eckart selection rules from data alone without quantum mechanical input; $\star_G$-SVD with ridge regression achieves closed-form predictions at ~50-90x fewer parameters than MLPs on full QM9 (130,831 molecules).

Conclusion: Algebraic equivariance complements architectural equivariance by offering provably-optimal symmetry-preserving compression, per-irrep interpretability, and data-driven physical discovery.

Abstract: We introduce the $\star_G$ tensor algebra, in which any finite group $G$ defines the multiplication rule, making equivariance an intrinsic algebraic property rather than an architectural constraint. The framework rests on three machine-verified theoretical pillars: (i)~an Eckart-Young optimality guarantee for the $\star_G$-SVD: the first such result for symmetry-preserving tensor approximation, exact and polynomial-time; (ii)~a Kronecker factorization that composes multiple symmetries by replacing $F_G$ with $F_{G_1} \otimes F_{G_2}$ with no architectural redesign; and (iii)~a 600-line Lean~4 formalization of the $\star_G$ algebra. The framework provides capabilities that equivariant neural networks (ENNs) structurally cannot: a closed-form per-irreducible-representation decomposition of every prediction, and data-driven discovery of the symmetry group that best fits a dataset. As a non-trivial empirical demonstration, decomposing QM9 molecular geometry over the chiral octahedral subgroup of SO(3) recovers the Wigner--Eckart selection rules of angular momentum from data alone, with no quantum mechanical input: scalar properties are A$_1$-dominated, dipole components are T$_1$-dominated, the isotropic polarizability is uniquely insensitive to $l\!=\!1$ as the rank-2-trace decomposition $l\!=\!0 \oplus l\!=\!2$ requires, and the T$_1$/A$_1$ predictive-power ratio separates vector observables from scalar observables by a factor of five. On full QM9 (130{,}831 molecules), $\star_G$-SVD with ridge regression provides closed form predictions at $\sim50-90\times$ fewer parameters than parameter-matched MLPs. Algebraic equivariance thus complements architectural equivariance not as a faster-better-cheaper alternative but as a different mathematical affordance: provably-optimal symmetry-preserving compression, per-irrep interpretability, and data-driven physical discovery.

</details>


### [255] [Design for Manufacturing: A Manufacturability Knowledge-Integrated Reinforcement Learning Framework for Free-Form Pipe Routing in Aeroengines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20644)
*Caicheng Wang,Zili Wang,Shuyou Zhang,Yongzhe Xiang,Zheyi Li,Liangyou Li,Jianrong Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FPRO integrates manufacturing knowledge into reinforcement learning for aeroengine pipe routing, using Frenet frame representation and PPO optimization to directly generate manufacturable, collision-free paths validated on a six-axis free-bending machine.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current pipe routing design is decoupled from downstream manufacturing, requiring labor-intensive trial-and-error iterations to achieve manufacturable designs in aeroengine development.

Method: FPRO formulates routing as a boundary value problem in the Frenet frame, represents paths via curvature/torsion profiles with cubic Hermite interpolation, embeds manufacturing constraints, and optimizes using PPO with stage-guided reward. Optimized paths are mapped to bending die trajectories for fabrication.

Result: FPRO generates collision-free manufacturable paths with smoother profiles, faster convergence, and superior performance in terminal alignment, path length, obstacle avoidance, and manufacturability versus Cartesian-based methods and RL baselines. Real-world validation shows close geometric correspondence between manufactured pipe and digital design.

Conclusion: The Frenet-based framework effectively bridges design and manufacturing for free-form pipe routing, demonstrating practical feasibility for aeroengine applications.

Abstract: Design for manufacturing plays a critical role in advanced aeroengine development, where complex components necessitate careful consideration of manufacturability. However, current practices in pipe routing remain largely decoupled from down-stream manufacturing, leading to labor-intensive, trial-and-error iterations to achieve manufacturable designs. To address this problem, this study proposes the Frenet-based pipe routing optimization (FPRO) framework, a manufacturability knowledge-integrated reinforcement learning approach for free-form pipe design in aeroengines. FPRO formulates the routing problem as a boundary value problem in the Frenet frame. In this framework, the pipe path is represented by curvature and torsion profiles, which are generated using cubic Hermite interpolation. To integrate design and manufacturing, domain-specific manufacturing knowledge is embedded as constraints on the permissible ranges of curvature and torsion. The path optimization is performed using the proximal policy optimization algorithm with stochastic exploration and a stage-guided reward mechanism. A unified mapping formulation then translates the optimized path into motion trajectories for the bending die, enabling direct fabrication on a six-axis free-bending machine. Experimental results demonstrate that FPRO consistently generates collision-free, manufacturable paths with smoother geometric profiles compared to Cartesian-based methods. It also achieves faster convergence and superior performance in terminal alignment, path length, obstacle avoidance, and manufacturability compared to state-of-the-art reinforcement learning baselines. Real-world validation confirms the close geometric correspondence between the manufactured pipe and its digital design, validating the practical feasibility of FPRO.

</details>


### [256] [Weight Decay Regimes in Grokking Transformers: Cheap Online Diagnostics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20441)
*Lucky Verma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weight decay acts as a control parameter for memorization-generalization-collapse transitions in transformers trained on modular arithmetic, with cheap attention-based diagnostics tracking these regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and cheaply diagnose phase transitions between memorization, generalization (grokking), and collapse in transformers, using weight decay as a scalar control parameter and attention-based metrics as low-cost alternatives to loss-landscape diagnostics.

Method: Train transformers (0.82M–85M params) on modular arithmetic tasks across 11 conditions. Introduce mean pairwise attention-head cosine similarity and entropy standard deviation as online diagnostics. Fit logistic (memorization-to-developmental boundary λ_c=0.0158) and power-law (ν=0.757) models. Validate with multi-task replication (n=280), attention-head re-initialization experiments, weight-norm clipping, and cross-architecture probes (MLP, LSTM, Mamba).

Result: Weight decay separates memorization, developmental grokking, and collapse. λ_c=0.0158 (95% CI [0.0109, 0.0200], N=210). Empirical exponent ν=0.757 (CI [0.725, 0.799])—excludes ν=1/2 and 3D Ising ν≈0.63. Re-initialization at λ=0.05 changes Phase-2 amplitude (Cohen's d=-1.190, p_t=4.5×10⁻³); weight-norm clipping does not. Cross-architecture probes replicate the transition with architecture-specific λ_c.

Conclusion: Weight decay controls transition regimes in modular arithmetic transformers. Attention-based diagnostics are effective and cheap. The empirical power-law exponent is identified but universality class is deferred. Scope is limited to small transformer attention models on modular arithmetic; non-attention models are probes only.

Abstract: Transformers trained on modular arithmetic exhibit sharp transitions between memorization, generalization, and collapse. We show that weight decay acts as a scalar empirical control parameter for these regimes, and introduce two cheap online diagnostics, mean pairwise attention-head cosine similarity and entropy standard deviation, that track training dynamics from attention activations alone and complement loss-landscape diagnostics at lower compute cost. Across eleven experimental conditions and three model scales (0.82M to 85M parameters), the weight-decay axis separates memorization, developmental grokking, and collapse. A near-transition logistic fit localizes the memorization-to-developmental boundary at $λ_c=0.0158$ (95% CI [0.0109, 0.0200], N=210); a power-law fit gives an empirical exponent $ν=0.757$ (CI [0.725, 0.799]). Reference exponents $ν=1/2$ and 3D Ising $ν\approx 0.63$ lie outside this empirical CI under our four-bin grid, so we report $ν$ as empirical and defer universality-class identification to denser finite-size-scaling work. A horizon-matched multi-task replication (n=280, four modular operations) preserves the weight-decay control pattern; a paired attention-head re-initialization experiment at $λ=0.05$ changes Phase-2 amplitude (Cohen's $d=-1.190$, n=10, $p_t=4.5 \times 10^{-3}$), while matched weight-norm clipping does not. Three cross-architecture probes (4L MLP, 4L LSTM, and 4L Mamba; each n=70) replicate the weight-decay-controlled transition with architecture-specific $λ_c$ values. Main diagnostic claims are scoped to modular arithmetic in small transformer attention models; the non-attention experiments are scope probes, and architecture-wide, language-model, and universality-class claims are out of scope.

</details>


### [257] [LLM Pretraining Shapes a Generalizable Manifold: Insights into Cross-Modal Transfer to Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20449)
*Alexis Roger,Prateek Humane,Zhenghan Tai,Gwen Legate,Andrei Mircea,Vasilii Feofanov,Irina Rish*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language-pretrained transformers can effectively forecast time series because pretraining builds a reusable manifold; frozen LLMs decode realistic trajectories via linear probes, and finetuning aligns task-relevant dynamics through low-dimensional updates rather than learning from scratch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why language-pretrained transformers can be effective time-series forecasters and to explain the cross-modal transfer mechanism.

Method: Linear probe on frozen LLM states, retrieval in projected space, analysis of optimization dynamics (gradients, loss landscape), and low-rank/subspace analysis of finetuning.

Result: Frozen LLMs decode realistic time-series trajectories; competitive forecasts via retrieval; pretrained initialization yields coherent gradients and anisotropic loss landscape; finetuning performs low-dimensional alignment, reusing directions for periodicity, trend, and repetition.

Conclusion: LLM-to-time-series transfer follows a geometric account: language pretraining builds a reusable manifold, and finetuning projects numerical dynamics onto task-relevant directions.

Abstract: Can language-pretrained transformers become effective time-series forecasters, and why? In this paper, we show that cross-modal transfer arises because language pretraining preconditions time series training with a reusable manifold. A linear probe on frozen LLM states decodes realistic time-series trajectories without paired supervision, and retrieval in this projected space yields competitive forecasts, showing that structure and dynamics exist before finetuning. Pretrained initialization also improves optimization, producing coherent gradients and a highly anisotropic loss landscape unlike random initialization. Finetuning then acts as low-dimensional alignment, reusing existing directions rather than learning temporal primitives from scratch, as evidenced by low-rank updates, subspace alignment, and shared features for periodicity, trend, and repetition. Together, these results support a geometric account of LLM-to-time-series transfer: language pretraining builds the manifold, and finetuning projects numerical dynamics onto task-relevant directions.

</details>


### [258] [SMA-DP: Spectral Memory-Aware Differential Privacy for Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20450)
*Mohammad Partohaghighi,Roummel Marcia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMA-DP-SGD enhances differentially private SGD with a spectral memory-aware branch that uses previously privatized noisy releases and power-law spectral exponents to adapt memory depth, improving accuracy on challenging datasets like CIFAR-100/10 at the cost of ~2.94× runtime overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP-SGD suffers from high-variance updates that reduce utility on challenging datasets due to per-example clipping and Gaussian noise.

Method: SMA-DP-SGD augments DP-SGD with a fractional memory branch built from past privatized noisy releases, guided by WeightWatcher-inspired power-law spectral exponents for layer-wise adaptation, with private-history alignment, norm matching, and warm-up activation to stabilize the memory contribution.

Result: Competitive or superior accuracy on CIFAR-100, CIFAR-10, and MNIST over several DP baselines; largest gains on CIFAR-100 and CIFAR-10; controlled memory depth and small memory-branch ratio; ~2.94× runtime overhead vs DP-SGD.

Conclusion: SMA-DP-SGD offers a clean conditional sensitivity structure with transparent privacy guarantees, enabling adaptive private memory that improves utility but introduces a practical trade-off between accuracy gains and computational cost.

Abstract: Differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) enables private deep learning through per-example clipping and calibrated Gaussian noise, but its high-variance updates can reduce utility on challenging datasets. We propose \textbf{SMA-DP-SGD}, a \textbf{Spectral Memory-Aware Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent} method that augments DP-SGD with a fractional memory branch built only from previously privatized noisy releases. WeightWatcher-inspired power-law spectral exponents provide group-wise reliability signals, instantiated layer-wise in our experiments, to adapt the decay and effective memory depth. Private-history alignment, norm matching, and warm-up activation stabilize the memory contribution. Privacy remains transparent: conditioned on the private release history, the memory branch is fixed, and the only newly data-dependent term is the current clipped sum scaled by a fixed coefficient \(β\). Hence, SMA-DP-SGD preserves a clean conditional sensitivity structure and exactly recovers group-wise DP-SGD when \(β=1\). Experiments on CIFAR-100, CIFAR-10, and MNIST show competitive or superior accuracy over several DP optimization baselines, with the largest gains on CIFAR-100 and CIFAR-10. CIFAR-10 ablations show that \(β\) controls the privacy--utility trajectory, while spectral and memory diagnostics confirm a controlled short-to-moderate effective memory depth and a small memory-branch ratio. Runtime analysis shows that the mechanism incurs additional overhead, about \(2.94\times\) DP-SGD in our CIFAR-10 implementation, revealing a practical trade-off between adaptive private memory and computational cost.

</details>


### [259] [CASCADE Conformal Prediction: Uncertainty-Adaptive Prediction Intervals for Two-Stage Clinical Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20468)
*Ricardo Diaz-Rincon,Muxuan Liang,Adolfo Ramirez-Zamora,Benjamin Shickel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CASCADE introduces a conformal prediction framework that uses epistemic uncertainty from a screening classifier to dynamically scale prediction intervals for medication dose forecasting in Parkinson's Disease, achieving narrower intervals for confident cases while maintaining robust coverage for uncertain ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Effective medication management in Parkinson's Disease is challenging due to heterogeneous progression and variable patient responses; standard uncertainty quantification fails to communicate prediction reliability, treating high and low confidence decisions identically.

Method: A conformal prediction framework (CASCADE) that propagates epistemic uncertainty from a screening classifier (identifying whether medication change is needed) to adapt downstream regression predictions (predicting how much change), using Venn-Abers multi-probabilistic uncertainty mapped to non-conformity scores for continuous risk adaptation.

Result: 38.9% narrower prediction intervals for confident patients compared to standard conformal baselines, while automatically expanding intervals for uncertain cases to ensure robust coverage.

Conclusion: The cascade effect bridges the gap between discrete clinical decision-making and continuous dose forecasting in PD, offering a principled way to adapt prediction uncertainty based on classifier confidence.

Abstract: Effective medication management in Parkinson's Disease (PD) is challenging due to heterogeneous disease progression, variable patient response, and medication side effects. While AI models can forecast levodopa equivalent daily dose (LEDD) as a measure of medication needs, standard uncertainty quantification often fails to communicate the reliability of these predictions, treating high and low confidence clinical decisions identically. We introduce CASCADE (Calibrated Adaptive Scaling via Conformal And Distributional Estimation), a novel conformal prediction framework that propagates epistemic uncertainty from a screening classifier to adapt downstream predictions. Unlike standard conformal methods that rely on auxiliary residual regression, we leverage epistemic uncertainty from a primary classification task (identifying whether a medication change is needed) to dynamically scale the prediction intervals of a secondary regression task (predicting how much change). By mapping Venn-Abers multi-probabilistic uncertainty directly to non-conformity scores, our framework achieves continuous risk adaptation. We demonstrate that this ``cascade effect'' produces highly efficient intervals for confident patients (38.9% narrower than standard conformal baselines) while automatically expanding intervals to ensure robust coverage for uncertain cases, bridging the gap between discrete clinical decision-making and continuous dose forecasting in PD.

</details>


### [260] [Training Language Agents to Learn from Experience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20477)
*Yuval Shalev,Zifeng Ding,Mateja Jamnik*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces In-context Training (ICT) for language agents, where a reflector model learns to generate system prompts that improve an actor's performance on unseen tasks, using RL training without human examples. Results show cross-task generalization across ALFWorld, MiniHack, and even to unseen environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reflection-based language agents can only self-correct within a single task instance; it remains unclear whether such experience can be distilled into reusable lessons that improve performance on future unseen tasks.

Method: The authors propose the In-context Training (ICT) task, where a reflector model observes actor trajectories and generates system prompts to improve future performance. They train the reflector via reinforcement learning without human-provided examples, and introduce MetaGym, a Python library for meta-environments.

Result: Trained reflectors outperform untrained baselines on most held-out task families in ALFWorld and MiniHack, and in some cases generalize to substantially different environments beyond the training benchmark.

Conclusion: The ability to learn from experience can itself be learned, enabling cross-task self-improvement in language agents. MetaGym facilitates further research in this direction.

Abstract: Language agents can adapt from experience in interactive environments, but current reflection-based methods can only self-correct within a single task instance. Whether such experience can be distilled into reusable lessons that improve performance on future unseen tasks remains unclear. We address this problem by introducing the In-context Training (ICT) task, a framework for evaluating cross-task self-improvement in language agents. In ICT, a reflector model observes trajectories collected by an actor model and generates system prompts intended to improve the actor's performance on future unseen tasks. We then propose an RL-based training pipeline for learning such reflections directly from experience, without human-provided examples. Across ALFWorld and MiniHack, our trained reflectors outperform an untrained baseline on most held-out task families, showing that the ability to learn from experience can itself be learned. In some cases, we observe generalisation beyond the benchmark on which the reflector was trained, to substantially different environments. Finally, we introduce MetaGym, a generic Python library for constructing meta-environments, enabling future research on self-improving language agents.

</details>


### [261] [Quadratic Characterizations for Reachability Analysis of Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20482)
*Elias Khalife,Mazen Farhood,Pierre-Loic Garoche*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a framework for constructing verified quadratic characterizations of scalar relations on bounded domains using convex quadratic programs and sum-of-squares certificates, improving reachability analysis for neural networks and other nonlinear systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadratic constraints (QCs) are widely used to characterize nonlinearities and uncertainties, but generic analytical characterizations can be conservative on bounded domains.

Method: Candidate quadratic inequalities are locally generated by solving convex quadratic programs using samples from the relation and exterior sample points, then verified globally using sum-of-squares certificates over exact or relaxed polynomial descriptions.

Result: The method yields domain-dependent quadratic characterizations for smooth activations (e.g., tanh) and reduces conservatism for ReLU networks by exploiting dependencies between neurons and tighter local bounds.

Conclusion: Numerical examples demonstrate improved reachability results for smooth activations, reduced conservatism for ReLU networks, and applicability beyond neural networks (e.g., saturation).

Abstract: Quadratic constraints (QCs) are widely used to characterize nonlinearities and uncertainties, but generic analytical characterizations can be conservative on bounded domains. This paper develops a framework for constructing verified quadratic characterizations of scalar relations in the two-dimensional real plane. Candidate quadratic inequalities are locally generated by solving convex quadratic programs using samples from the relation and exterior sample points. They are then verified globally using sum-of-squares certificates over an exact semialgebraic description or, in the case of nonpolynomial relations, over relaxed polynomial descriptions. The resulting verified constraints define a sound overapproximation of the scalar relations over the considered domains. These constraints are directly compatible with existing analysis frameworks based on QCs and pointwise integral quadratic constraints (IQCs) for static nonlinearities and uncertainties, and they can also be embedded in QC-based semidefinite programs for reachability and safety analysis of feedforward neural networks. For smooth activations such as $\tanh$, the method yields domain-dependent quadratic characterizations that constitute an alternative to generic sector- or slope-based descriptions. For ReLU networks, we give methods to reduce conservatism in QC-based reachability analysis of feedforward networks by exploiting dependencies between neurons and tighter local bounds. Numerical examples demonstrate improved reachability results for smooth activations, reduced conservatism for ReLU networks, and applicability beyond neural networks through an example involving saturation.

</details>


### [262] [ZEBRA: Zero-shot Budgeted Resource Allocation for LLM Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20485)
*May Hamri,Inbal Talgam-Cohen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ZEBRA is a zero-shot framework that reduces multi-phase budget allocation in multi-agent pipelines to a continuous nonlinear knapsack problem, outperforming LLM-direct budget allocation on coding and QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing budget-aware methods control reasoning within a single agent or learn resource allocation via RL, but none address how to split a budget across composing phases of a multi-agent pipeline at inference time under fixed monetary budgets.

Method: ZEBRA reduces multi-phase budget allocation to a continuous nonlinear knapsack problem: an LLM controller estimates per-phase utility curves, and water-filling search on the Lagrange multiplier returns the optimal per-phase split. Both additive and multiplicative aggregations are unified under the same solver.

Result: On APPS coding benchmark, ZEBRA variants outperform LLM-direct on every aggregate metric. At α=0.5 budget, ZEBRA recovers 94.4% of unconstrained quality vs 88.1% for LLM-direct. On HotpotQA, ZEBRA beats LLM-direct by 14.3pp, showing adaptation (near-balanced split vs skewed split on APPS).

Conclusion: Lightweight algorithmic guidance at inference time can improve the economic behavior of autonomous multi-agent systems; ZEBRA's statistical advantage is significant and transfers beyond coding tasks.

Abstract: As autonomous agents increasingly execute end-to-end tasks under fixed monetary budgets, the pressing open question shifts from whether the budget is respected, to how to spend it effectively. Existing budget-aware methods typically control reasoning step-by-step within a single agent, or learn resource allocation policies via RL. None address how to split a budget across the composing phases of a multi-agent pipeline at inference time. We propose ZEBRA, a zero-shot framework that reduces multi-phase budget allocation to a continuous nonlinear knapsack problem: an LLM controller estimates per-phase utility curves, and a water-filling search on the Lagrange multiplier returns the per-phase split. Additive and multiplicative aggregations are unified under the same solver. On a $150$-task APPS coding benchmark, both ZEBRA variants outperform LLM-direct (budget allocation directly by an LLM) on every aggregate metric. At a budget of $α= 0.5$ of the unconstrained spend, ZEBRA recovers $94.4\%$ of unconstrained quality, versus $88.1\%$ for LLM-direct. The advantage is statistically significant and transfers beyond coding: on a $3$-phase HotpotQA pipeline, ZEBRA beats LLM-direct by $14.3$pp, with allocations empirically robust to curve-estimation noise. On HotpotQA, ZEBRA arrives at a different budget split (near-balanced) compared to the APPS one (skewed towards a refinement phase), showing adaptation to the pipeline structure. More broadly, we show that lightweight algorithmic guidance at inference time can improve the economic behavior of autonomous multi-agent systems.

</details>


### [263] [A 10,000-Year Global Stochastic Tropical Cyclone Catalog with Wind-Dependent Track Transitions (WHITS)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20494)
*Jennifer Nakamura,Upmanu Lall*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WHITS is a non-parametric TC track generator that extends HITS by conditioning transitions on local wind speed, sharpening kernel selection, and applying smoothing, producing a 10,000-yr global synthetic catalog that reproduces observed TC statistics for catastrophe risk applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable TC risk assessment is limited by the short, sparse historical record, especially for rare high-intensity landfalls that drive insured losses.

Method: WHITS extends HITS with three key improvements: (1) conditioning transitions on local wind speed plus position/age/forward vector; (2) kernel sharpening to suppress dynamically inconsistent jumps; (3) smoothing window across transitions to remove discontinuities.

Result: The 10,000-yr global synthetic catalog reproduces observed track density and annual hurricane/typhoon-force wind-hit probability across all six basins.

Conclusion: The catalog is useful for catastrophe-risk applications needing a large, low-bias sample of physically plausible tracks rather than small statistically corrected ones.

Abstract: Reliable assessment of tropical cyclone (TC) risk is limited by the brevity and spatial sparsity of the historical record, particularly for the rare, high-intensity landfalls that dominate insured loss. We present WHITS (Wind-focused Hurricane Interactive Track Simulator), a non-parametric semi-Markov track generator that extends the HITS framework of Nakamura et al. (2015) in three ways: transitions between historical track segments are conditioned on local wind speed in addition to position, age, and forward vector; the kernel selection on the comparative-vector term is sharpened to suppress dynamically inconsistent jumps; and a short smoothing window is applied across each transition to remove the position and wind discontinuities reported by downstream surge users. WHITS is fit to the full available best-track record in each of six basins in IBTrACS, extending in the North Atlantic to 1851 and in other basins to the earliest year of reliable best-track data. The resulting 10,000-yr global synthetic catalog reproduces observed track density and the annual hurricane/typhoon-force wind-hit probability across all basins. The catalog is intended for catastrophe-risk applications where a large, low-bias sample of physically plausible tracks is more useful than a small, statistically corrected one.

</details>


### [264] [Tippett-minimum Fusion of Representation-space Diffusion Models for Multi-Encoder Out-of-Distribution Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20502)
*Neelkamal Bhuyan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes EncMin2L, a multi-encoder fusion method using per-encoder diffusion models and a two-level min-gate to detect various OOD shifts without OOD labels, achieving ≥0.94 AUROC across all shift types at 2.3× lower parameter cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
