Motivation: Existing OOD detection methods struggle to handle the full spectrum of distribution shifts (global, semantic, texture, covariate) simultaneously, and multi-encoder approaches are computationally expensive.

Method: Uses per-encoder representation-space diffusion models (RDMs); introduces EncMin2L (encoder-agnostic two-level min-gate) combining per-encoder likelihood detectors; employs η² (class-conditional F-test) and Δμ (log-likelihood shift under synthetic corruptions) for ID-data diagnostics; uses Tippett minimum p-value combination for calibration-stable OOD signal.

Result: Achieves ≥0.94 AUROC across all four shift types simultaneously, outperforms state-of-the-art representation-space diffusion OOD detectors, with 2.3× lower parameter cost than monolithic multi-encoder baselines.

Conclusion: EncMin2L effectively fuses multiple encoders for comprehensive OOD detection without requiring OOD labels, achieving strong performance across diverse distribution shifts with improved efficiency.

Abstract: We address out-of-distribution (OOD) detection across the full spectrum of distribution shifts -- global domain changes, semantic divergence, texture differences, and covariate corruptions -- through a multi-encoder fusion of per-encoder representation-space diffusion models (RDMs). We statistically identify each encoder's sensitivity to specific shift types from ID data alone and introduce EncMin2L -- an encoder-agnostic two-level $\min(\cdot)$-gate that combines and calibrates per-encoder diffusion-based likelihood detectors without OOD labels, outperforming monolithic multi-encoder baselines at $2.3\times$ lower parameter cost. Two ID-data diagnostics: $η^2$ (class-conditional F-test) and $Δμ$ (log-likelihood shift under synthetic corruptions) -- quantify encoder specialization, while a Tippett minimum $p$-value combination aggregates per-encoder scores into a single, calibration-stable OOD signal. EncMin2L achieves $\geq 0.94$ AUROC across all four shift types simultaneously, outperforming the state-of-the-art representation-space diffusion OOD detectors across overlapping benchmarks.

</details>


### [265] [Reinforcing Human Behavior Simulation via Verbal Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20506)
*Weiwei Sun,Xuhui Zhou,Jiarui Liu,Weihua Du,Haojia Sun,Yiqing Xie,Qianou Ma,Sihao Chen,Mengting Wan,Longqi Yang,Pei Zhou,Sherry Wu,Sean Welleck,Graham Neubig,Yiming Yang,Maarten Sap*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DITTO uses verbal feedback in reinforcement learning to train LLMs for more human-like behavior, achieving 36% improvement over base models and outperforming GPT-5.4 on 6/10 tasks across a new benchmark called SOUL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans learn social norms from verbal feedback, but LLM training from feedback has focused narrowly on verifiable domains (code, math) with scalar rewards. As LLMs simulate human behavior (users, patients, personas), there's a need for training signals that are verbal, subjective, and multi-faceted.

Method: DITTO treats verbal feedback as a first-class signal in RL: after each rollout, it receives verbal feedback, generates a feedback-conditioned improved rollout, and jointly optimizes both outputs using GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization), distilling verbal guidance into the base policy without requiring feedback at test time.

Result: DITTO achieves an average 36% improvement over the base model and exceeds GPT-5.4 on 6 of 10 SOUL benchmarks.

Conclusion: RL with verbal feedback is a promising direction for training LLMs to simulate human-like behavior across diverse social and interactive tasks.

Abstract: Humans learn social norms and behaviors from verbal feedback (e.g., a parent saying "that was rude" or a friend explaining "here's why that hurt"). Yet, learning from feedback for LLMs has largely focused on domains like code and math, where RL rewards are directly verifiable and condensed into scalar values. As LLMs are increasingly used to simulate human behavior, e.g., standing in for users, patients, students, and other personas, there is a pressing need to make them more human-like, which requires embracing a fundamentally different kind of signal: feedback that is verbal, subjective, and multi-faceted. We present DITTO, a model trained by treating verbal feedback as a first-class signal in reinforcement learning. After each rollout, DITTO receives verbal feedback and generates a feedback-conditioned improved rollout; both outputs are jointly optimized with GRPO, distilling verbal guidance into the base policy without requiring feedback at test time. We also introduce SOUL (Simulation gym Of hUman-Like behavior), a unified benchmark and training data suite spanning 10 tasks across six categories: Theory of Mind, character role play, social skill, learner simulation, user simulation, and persona simulation. DITTO achieves an average 36% improvement over the base model and exceeds GPT-5.4 on 6 of 10 SOUL benchmarks, demonstrating that RL with verbal feedback is a promising direction for training LLMs to simulate human behavior.

</details>


### [266] [Fast Reconstruction of Exact Maxwell Dynamics from Sparse Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20514)
*Dan DeGenaro,Xin Li,Obed Amo,Michael Pokojovy,Sarah Adel Bargal,Markus Lange-Hegermann,Bogdan Raiţă*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FLASH-MAX is a shallow neural network that encodes exact Maxwell equation solutions into its architecture, achieving sub-1% error from sparse 3D observations in seconds while maintaining zero PDE residual.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the trade-off between precision and optimization speed in scientific ML by embedding governing physical equations directly into the network architecture rather than treating them as loss penalties.

Method: A shallow neural network where each hidden neuron represents an exact analytical solution to Maxwell's equations, ensuring the model satisfies PDEs symbolically by construction, with a proven universal approximation theorem.

Result: Sub-1% relative validation error from ~1K sparse pointwise observations, zero PDE residual, single-digit errors from only 100 3D observations, all achieved within seconds of training.

Conclusion: Shifting governing structure from the loss function into the hypothesis class dramatically improves the precision-speed trade-off in scientific machine learning.

Abstract: We introduce FLASH-MAX, a shallow, exact-by-construction neural network architecture for predicting homogeneous electromagnetic fields from sparse pointwise observations. Each hidden neuron represents a separate exact solution to Maxwell's equations, so that the network satisfies the governing equations symbolically by construction and can be trained end-to-end from sparse data within seconds. We prove a universal approximation result showing that this exact model class remains universal on arbitrary domains. FLASH-MAX reaches sub-1% relative validation error from about 1K sparse pointwise observations in seconds, all while maintaining a zero PDE residual, and keeps single-digit errors even for only 100 observations sampled from 3D space. These results suggest that moving governing structure from the loss into the hypothesis class can dramatically improve the trade-off between precision and optimization speed in scientific machine learning.

</details>


### [267] [An exponential mechanism based on quadratic approximations for fine-tuning machine learning models with privacy guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20521)
*Hoang Tran,Jorge Ramirez,Jiayi Wang,Alberto Bocchinfuso,Christopher Stanley,M. Paul Laiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a differentially private fine-tuning algorithm using the exponential mechanism with a quadratic utility function that enables closed-form sampling from a multivariate normal distribution, and introduces random projections for scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning pretrained models on sensitive datasets risks memorizing private data, making models vulnerable to information extraction attacks. Existing differentially private fine-tuning methods lack efficiency or theoretical guarantees.

Method: The method constructs a utility function combining a local quadratic approximation of the pretrained model with new dataset information, then applies the exponential mechanism to enable exact closed-form sampling from a multivariate normal distribution. A random-projection strategy is added for scalability to high-dimensional models.

Result: Theoretical privacy guarantees, sensitivity bounds, and accuracy estimations are established. Experiments on MNIST and MIMIC datasets show competitive performance against existing differentially private fine-tuning techniques.

Conclusion: The proposed exponential mechanism-based fine-tuning provides robust differential privacy guarantees with efficient closed-form sampling and competitive accuracy, with the random-projection variant extending scalability.

Abstract: Fine-tuning adapts a pretrained machine learning model to a small, sensitive dataset, but this process risks memorizing individual new data points, making the model vulnerable to adversaries who seek to extract sensitive information. In this work, we develop a randomized algorithm based on the exponential mechanism for fine-tuning while ensuring differential privacy. Our key idea is to construct a simple utility function that combines a local quadratic approximation of the pretrained model with information from the new dataset. The resulting exponential mechanism admits exact sampling from a multivariate normal distribution in closed form. We establish theoretical privacy guarantees, sensitivity bounds, and accuracy estimations for our method. We further introduce a random-projection strategy that makes the approach scalable to high-dimensional models. Numerical experiments on the MNIST benchmark and the MIMIC clinical dataset demonstrate competitive performance against existing differentially private fine-tuning techniques.

</details>


### [268] [Machine-Learning-Enhanced Non-Invasive Testing for MASLD Fibrosis: Shallow-Deep Neural Networks Versus FIB-4, Tabular Foundation Models, and Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20523)
*Athanasios Angelakis,Gabriele De Vito,Eleni-Myrto Trifylli,Filomena Ferrucci*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Machine learning models, especially a shallow-deep neural network (s-DNN) with only 354 parameters, improve advanced fibrosis detection in MASLD over FIB-4 alone while using the same clinical variables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FIB-4's fixed formula underuses diagnostic information; the authors aim to enhance advanced fibrosis detection without expanding clinical data requirements.

Method: Three biopsy-confirmed MASLD cohorts (n=784; China, Malaysia, India). Compared FIB-4 with s-DNN, TabPFN, and GPT-4o using five variables (age, FIB-4, AST, PLT, ALT).

Result: s-DNN achieved best external AUCs (0.77 Malaysia, 0.67 India) with only 354 parameters; FIB-4 got 0.75 and 0.60. AST and FIB-4 were dominant features.

Conclusion: Compact non-linear MLE-NITs can enhance FIB-4-based fibrosis assessment without new clinical data, with s-DNN offering balanced, efficient performance.

Abstract: Advanced fibrosis is a major determinant of liver-related morbidity in metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD). FIB-4 is widely used as a first-line non-invasive test, but its fixed formula may underuse diagnostic information contained in age, aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, and platelet count. We evaluated whether machine-learning-enhanced non-invasive testing (MLE-NIT) can improve advanced fibrosis detection while preserving this FIB-4 variable space.
  We used three biopsy-confirmed MASLD cohorts from China, Malaysia, and India (n=784). The Chinese cohort was split into 486 training and 54 internal validation/tuning patients; final performance was reported only on the Malaysian and Indian external cohorts. Models used five variables: age, FIB-4, aspartate aminotransferase, platelet count, and alanine aminotransferase. We compared FIB-4 with a shallow-deep neural network (s-DNN), TabPFN, and gpt-4o-2024-08-06.
  FIB-4 achieved external ROC-AUCs of 0.75 and 0.60 in Malaysia and India, respectively. TabPFN achieved 0.69 and 0.66, fine-tuned GPT-4o achieved 0.75 and 0.63, and the s-DNN achieved 0.77 and 0.67, respectively. The s-DNN contained only 354 trainable parameters, compared with 7,244,554 for TabPFN, yet provided a more balanced external operating profile. Calibration showed s-DNN Brier scores of 0.18 and 0.22, and permutation importance identified AST and FIB-4 as dominant variables. Compact non-linear MLE-NITs may enhance FIB-4-based fibrosis assessment without increasing clinical data requirements.

</details>


### [269] [Ada2MS: A Hybrid Optimization Algorithm Based on Exponential Mixing of Elementwise and Global Second-Moment Estimates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20533)
*Meng Zhu,Quan Xiao,Weidong Min*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Ada2MS is a new optimization algorithm that smoothly transitions between AdamW-like and momentum-SGD-like behavior via continuous exponential interpolation of second-moment estimates, achieving competitive results on visual tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To balance the strengths and weaknesses of AdamW (stable, robust but weaker generalization) and Momentum SGD (better generalization but sensitive to hyperparameters), by creating a unified algorithm that bridges both paradigms.

Method: Ada2MS performs continuous exponential interpolation between elementwise second-moment estimates (AdamW-like) and global second-moment estimates (momentum-SGD-like) to achieve smooth behavioral transition.

Result: On visual tasks evaluated under a unified optimizer-comparison protocol, Ada2MS obtains competitive results.

Conclusion: Ada2MS effectively combines the robustness of AdamW with the generalization strengths of Momentum SGD through a smooth interpolation mechanism, achieving competitive performance on vision tasks.

Abstract: Optimization algorithms are core methods by which machine learning models iteratively minimize loss functions, update parameters, learn from data, and improve performance. Momentum SGD and AdamW represent two important optimization paradigms. AdamW produces stable updates and usually has strong robustness across training scenarios, but its generalization performance is sometimes weaker than that of momentum methods. Momentum SGD can often obtain better generalization after careful tuning, but it is more sensitive to gradient-scale variation and hyperparameter settings. To balance the strengths and weaknesses of the two paradigms, this paper proposes Ada2MS, an optimization algorithm that achieves a smooth transition between AdamW-like behavior and momentum-SGD-like behavior through continuous exponential interpolation between elementwise second-moment estimates and global second-moment estimates. On the visual tasks evaluated in this study, Ada2MS obtains competitive results under a unified optimizer-comparison protocol. The code will be released at https://github.com/mengzhu0308/Ada2MS

</details>


### [270] [Axiomatizing Neural Networks via Pursuit of Subspaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20534)
*Mehmet Yamac,Mert Duman,Ugur Akpinar,Felix Rojas Casadiego,Serkan Kiranyaz,Marcel van Gerven,Moncef Gabbouj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes the Pursuit of Subspaces (PoS) hypothesis, an axiomatic geometric framework to theoretically understand neural networks, providing unified explanations for representation, computation, and generalization in deep learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep neural networks are black boxes with poor theoretical understanding despite empirical success. The gap between performance and theory is analogous to pre-axiomatic geometry, motivating the need for a principled theoretical foundation.

Method: Introduces the Pursuit of Subspaces (PoS) hypothesis as an axiomatic framework using geometric postulates. The axioms and their derived consequences offer a unified perspective spanning representation, computation, and generalization across shallow and deep architectures.

Result: The framework yields geometric explanations for fundamental deep learning questions, including representation structure, architectural mechanisms, and generalization behavior.

Conclusion: The PoS hypothesis offers a principled step toward a coherent theoretical foundation for deep learning, analogous to how axioms structured classical geometry.

Abstract: While deep neural networks have achieved remarkable success across a wide range of domains, their underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood, and they are often regarded as black boxes. This gap between empirical performance and theoretical understanding poses a challenge analogous to the pre-axiomatic stage of classical geometry. In this work, we introduce the Pursuit of Subspaces (PoS) hypothesis, an axiomatic framework that formulates neural network behavior through a set of geometric postulates. These axioms, together with their derived consequences, provide a unified perspective on representation, computation, and generalization in both shallow and deep architectures. We show that this framework yields geometric explanations for fundamental questions in deep learning, including representation structure, architectural mechanisms, and generalization behavior, offering a principled step toward a coherent theoretical foundation.

</details>


### [271] [OpenSeisML: Open Large-Scale Real Seismic and well-log Dataset for Generative AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20539)
*Ipsita Bhar,Huseyin Tuna Erdinc,Thales Souza,Charles Jones,Felix J. Herrmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OpenSeisML is a collection of real seismic datasets from public UK data, curated via an automated pipeline to support generative AI for seismic inversion and uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML seismic inversion methods lack realistic velocity models due to private data ownership; OpenSeisML bridges this gap by providing publicly available, curated seismic datasets.

Method: Datasets are sourced from the UK National Data Repository, using checkshot data for time-depth conversion and interpolation for velocity model construction, with an automated curation pipeline ensuring reproducibility.

Result: A reproducible, automated pipeline that produces realistic velocity models from public post-stack seismic data, suitable for training generative AI models.

Conclusion: OpenSeisML enables training generative models for subsurface property synthesis and uncertainty quantification, providing a prior for seismic inversion.

Abstract: The advent of machine learning (ML) and computer vision has significantly accelerated seismic inversion workflows by reducing the computational cost of traditionally expensive iterative methods. However, the development and evaluation of ML methods remain limited by the scarcity of realistic velocity models, as most high-quality data are privately owned by oil and gas companies. To address this gap, we present OpenSeisML, a collection of real seismic datasets designed to support generative AI (Gen-AI) workflows for seismic inversion. The datasets are curated from publicly available surveys in the UK National Data Repository (NDR). When seismic volumes are in the time domain and wells are in depth, a time-to-depth conversion is required. We use checkshot data to establish the time-depth relationship and construct a velocity model through interpolation for accurate conversion of post-stack seismic data. Here, we present an automated data curation pipeline that enables seismic data preparation while ensuring reproducibility. The objective is to train a generative model that captures the statistical distribution of subsurface properties, enabling the synthesis of multiple statistically consistent realizations for uncertainty quantification which can act as a prior for seismic inversion.

</details>


### [272] [Latent Process Generator Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20547)
*Lukas Billera,Hedwig Nora Nordlinder,Ben Murrell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces latent process generator matching, a framework that extends generator matching from static latent variables to time-dependent latent conditional processes by treating the observed generative state as a deterministic image of a tractable Markov process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing flow-matching and diffusion models rely on auxiliary stochastic dynamics during training, but the auxiliary state is either intractable to sample at generation time or not part of the desired output. The authors aim to generalize generator matching to handle time-dependent latent processes.

Method: The authors propose a general framework where the observed generative state is treated as a deterministic image $X_t = \Phi(Y_t)$ of a tractable Markov process $Y_t$, and show that the generator of a stochastic process on the image space can be learned to match the one-time marginal distributions of the projected process.

Result: The framework generalizes and subsumes discrete latent process results from existing literature, and extends generator matching from static latent variables to time-dependent latent conditional processes.

Conclusion: This work provides a unified theoretical foundation for learning generative models with latent stochastic processes, broadening the applicability of generator matching beyond static latent variable settings.

Abstract: Many recent flow-matching and diffusion-style generative models rely on auxiliary stochastic dynamics during training: a richer process is simulated to define conditional targets, but the auxiliary state is either intractable to sample at generation time or simply not part of the desired output. Existing Generator Matching theory formalises conditioning on static latent random variables, and several recent papers prove special cases of projection results for particular augmented-state constructions. We introduce latent process generator matching, a general framework that treats the observed generative state as a deterministic image $X_t=Φ(Y_t)$ of a tractable Markov process $Y_t$. We show that in this setting one may learn the generator of a stochastic process on the image space which has the same one-time marginal distributions as the projected process. This generalizes and subsumes the discrete latent process results from the literature, and extends Generator Matching from static latent variables to a rich family of time-dependent latent conditional processes.

</details>


### [273] [Complementing reinforcement learning with SFT through logit averaging in the post training of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20555)
*Xingwei Gan,Ying Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes replacing KL regularization with logit averaging between a frozen reference policy and a trainable policy in GRPO, showing improved or comparable accuracy on MATH, cn-k12, and MMLU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To avoid KL regularization and critic in RLVR while leveraging both the reasoning expertise of the trainable policy and the formatting advantage of SFT.

Method: Averages logits of a frozen reference policy (e.g., SFT) and a trainable policy, incorporated into GRPO without KL regularization or critic.

Result: Higher or comparable accuracy relative to canonical KL-regularized GRPO on MATH, cn-k12, and MMLU.

Conclusion: Logit averaging is an effective alternative to KL regularization for coupling trainable and reference policies in GRPO.

Abstract: We introduce a novel method that averages the logits of a frozen reference policy (e.g., SFT) and a trainable policy, and incorporate the method into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). In contrast to Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods, our proposal does not involve a Kullback Leibler (KL) regularization or critic; the trainable policy and the reference anchor are coupled through the logit averaging structure to leverage the reasoning expertise of the trainable policy while maintaining the formatting advantage of SFT. Our method is evaluated on MATH, cn-k12, and MMLU, and the results show a higher accuracy or at least comparable accuracy relative to the canonical KL-regularized GRPO.

</details>


### [274] [Deep Learning Surrogates for Emulating Stochastic Climate Tipping Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20580)
*Adeline Hillier,Jennifer Sleeman,Jay Brett,Caroline Tang,Jenelle Millison,Anand Gnanadesikan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A dynamics-informed Temporal Fusion Transformer achieves 465x speedup over Earth system simulations while accurately forecasting ocean collapse events across thousands of time steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To replace computationally expensive numerical Earth system simulations with a fast, differentiable data-driven surrogate that can forecast tipping events in ocean transport dynamics.

Method: Modified Temporal Fusion Transformer with dynamics-informed architecture and objective function changes, trained on multivariate non-stationary time series and static covariates from global ocean transport simulations.

Result: The surrogate accurately predicts timing of Atlantic and Pacific collapses, captures stochastic uncertainty across ensemble predictions, and achieves 465x computational speedup while maintaining differentiability.

Conclusion: Dynamics-informed TFTs are effective as fast, differentiable surrogates for Earth system simulations, enabling rapid exploration of tipping point dynamics.

Abstract: This work explores a dynamics-informed Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) as a data-driven surrogate for computationally intensive Earth system simulations. Focusing on multivariate time series describing global ocean transport, we demonstrate the surrogate's ability to forecast tip events across thousands of time steps. The data involve up to 21 non-stationary time series in addition to static covariates describing free parameters and initial conditions. Modifications to the architecture and objective function yield a surrogate that anticipates the timing of Atlantic and Pacific collapses to high fidelity and captures the stochastic uncertainty in transition timing across ensemble predictions. The learned surrogate achieves a 465x computational speedup over the numerical simulator while maintaining differentiability with respect to parameters and initial conditions.

</details>


### [275] [TriForces: Augmenting Atomistic GNNs for Transferable Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20581)
*Ali Ramlaoui,Alexandre Duval,Hannah Bull,Victor Schmidt,Hugues Talbot,Fragkiskos D. Malliaros,Joseph Musielewicz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TriForces is a model-agnostic framework that separates composition and structure information using self-supervised learning, improving MLIP transferability and achieving significant error reductions (e.g., 57% energy MAE at 20K samples) without requiring DFT labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLIPs trained on large DFT data need adaptation to target chemistries using small datasets, but transfer inconsistently and lose composition/structure information.

Method: A three-stream framework that separates composition and structure information, combined with self-supervised learning to preserve transferable representations.

Result: Improves performance on MatBench and QM9 without DFT labels; reduces energy MAE by 57% at 20K samples on OMat24 and improves force MAE across sample sizes.

Conclusion: TriForces enables efficient similar structure retrieval and effective transfer learning for MLIPs, with pretrained variants released across multiple architectures.

Abstract: Machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) achieve excellent accuracy when trained on large Density Functional Theory (DFT) data. To be useful in practice, they must often be adapted to target chemistries using small and expensive task-specific datasets. However, MLIPs transfer inconsistently across domains, with representations that often loose accessible composition and structure information. To address this, we present TriForces, a model-agnostic three-stream framework that separates composition and structure information, combined with self-supervised learning to preserve transferable representations. TriForces improves performance on MatBench and QM9 over baselines without needing DFT labels and enables efficient similar structure retrieval through its learned latent space. On OMat24, in limited-data training regime, TriForces reduces energy MAE by 57% at 20K samples only and improves force MAE across sample sizes. We release pretrained TriForces variants across multiple MLIP architectures with code at https://github.com/Ramlaoui/triforces.

</details>


### [276] [ReversedQ: Opportunities for Faster Q-Learning in Episodic Online Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20592)
*Sofia R. Miskala-Dinc,Aviva Prins*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper improves model-free Q-learning in finite-horizon episodic MDPs by fixing delayed learning issues in posterior-sampling methods. The authors propose ReversedQ, which modifies value-function update order, update frequencies, and initialization, achieving substantial improvements (78.78% and 61.81% better scaled mean cumulative reward) over RandomizedQ in test environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the delayed learning problem in nascent model-free posterior-sampling Q-learning methods for finite-horizon episodic MDPs, which limits theoretical guarantees and practical performance.

Method: Identifies three key modifications: (i) changing value-function update order, (ii) adjusting update frequencies, and (iii) altering value-function initialization. These changes are applied to Wang et al.'s RandomizedQ framework to create ReversedQ.

Result: ReversedQ improves scaled mean cumulative reward from 9.53% to 78.78% in BDCL and from 21.76% to 61.81% in a chain MDP compared to RandomizedQ.

Conclusion: Simple modifications to update order, frequency, and initialization can substantially improve the empirical performance of posterior-sampling Q-learning methods, suggesting delayed learning is a key bottleneck.

Abstract: We study model-free Q-learning in finite-horizon episodic Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with stationary dynamics across episodes. We identify a central issue in nascent model-free posterior-sampling works: the reliance on delayed learning in order to prove theoretical guarantees. In particular, we identify three opportunities for faster learning - (i) value-function update order, (ii) update frequencies, and (iii) value-function initialization. Using Wang et al.'s RandomizedQ as a basis, we illustrate these changes and their individual (as well as cumulative) impact in multiple empirical studies. We find that our combined modifications, termed ReversedQ, improve scaled mean cumulative reward compared to RandomizedQ, from 9.53% to 78.78% in the Bidirectional Diabolical Combination Lock (BDCL), and from 21.76% to 61.81% in a chain MDP.

</details>


### [277] [Unsupervised clustering and classification of upper limb EMG signals during functional movements: a data-driven](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20599)
*L. F. Salazar Álvarez,D. Escobar-Saltarén,M. B. Salazar Sánchez,S. C. Henao-Aguirre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a four-stage pipeline for clustering and classifying upper-limb sEMG signals (NINAPRO DB4, 52 gestures), using preprocessing (0.6 Hz low-pass filter + Hilbert envelope), feature extraction/reduction to 5 key features, hierarchical clustering (Mahalanobis distance) to select 6 representative gestures, and classifier evaluation showing Extra Trees and ANN as top performers with a 200 ms optimal window, supporting adaptive myoelectric prosthesis control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve myoelectric prosthesis control by developing a robust, low-latency pipeline for clustering and classifying upper-limb sEMG signals during functional reach and grasp movements, balancing biomechanical diversity with computational efficiency.

Method: A four-stage pipeline: (1) preprocessing using 4th-order low-pass filter (0.6 Hz) and Hilbert envelope; (2) extracting 26 temporal/frequency features, reduced to 5 via visual analysis, mutual information, PCA, and decision tree importance; (3) gesture selection via hierarchical clustering with Mahalanobis distance yielding 6 representative movements; (4) classifier evaluation using PyCaret (identifying Extra Trees and ANN) followed by independent training to confirm generalization.

Result: A 200 ms window was optimal. Extra Trees (ET) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) were top performers; ANN showed progressive learning, while ET maintained robust, consistent results, confirming stability and generalization capacity.

Conclusion: The proposed pipeline supports adaptive, low-latency myoelectric prosthesis control strategies and provides a scalable framework for future real-time applications.

Abstract: This study presents a comprehensive approach for the clustering and classification of upper-limb surface electromyography (sEMG) signals during functional reach and grasp movements. The methodology was applied to the NINAPRO DB4 dataset, which provides multichannel EMG recordings of 52 gestures. A four-stage pipeline was designed, including signal preprocessing, fea-ture extraction, gesture selection via hierarchical clustering, and comparative model evaluation. Preprocessing involved a fourth-order low-pass filter (0.6 Hz) and Hilbert envelope transformation, effectively reducing noise and enhancing signal clarity. Feature extraction yielded 26 temporal and frequency-domain met-rics, which were later refined using visual analysis, mutual information, principal component analysis, and decision tree importance scores. A final subset of five key features was selected for classification tasks. Gesture selection was per-formed through hierarchical clustering using Mahalanobis distance, resulting in six representative movements that balanced biomechanical diversity and compu-tational efficiency. A 200 ms window was identified as optimal for temporal seg-mentation based on stability and physiological plausibility. Classifier models were evaluated in two stages. Automated comparison using PyCaret identified Extra Trees (ET) and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) as top performers. Sub-sequent independent training confirmed their stability and generalization capac-ity, with ANN showing progressive learning and ET maintaining robust, con-sistent results. The findings support the implementation of adaptive, low-latency control strategies for myoelectric prostheses and provide a scalable pipeline for future real-time applications.

</details>


### [278] [Compositional Transduction with Latent Analogies for Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20609)
*Junseok Kim,Dohyeong Kim,Mineui Hong,Songhwai Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes analogy transduction for offline GCRL using context-invariant representations to enable compositional generalization beyond seen analogy-context pairs, outperforming prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing trajectory stitching methods in offline GCRL are limited by temporal contiguity and cannot compose behaviors across varying contexts for unseen goals.

Method: Formalizes analogy transduction, introduces a novel analogy representation that captures task-endogenous changes invariant to context, and enables generalization to unseen analogy-context combinations.

Result: Substantially outperforms prior methods on OGBench manipulation environments.

Conclusion: Analogy transduction with context-invariant representations is an effective approach for compositional generalization in offline GCRL.

Abstract: Compositional generalization is essential for reaching unseen goals under novel contextual variations in offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL), where a generalist goal-reaching agent must be learned from limited data. Most prior approaches pursue this via trajectory stitching over temporally contiguous segments, which limits composing behaviors across varying contexts. To overcome this limitation, we formalize analogy transduction as synthesizing new plans by composing task-endogenous analogies with given contexts and propose a novel analogy representation tailored for it. Grounded in our theory, this analogy representation captures what changes under optimal task execution, remains invariant to contextual variations, and is sufficient for optimal goal reaching. We further contend that generalization to unseen analogy-context pairs is a practical obstacle in analogy transduction, and introduce a new approach for offline GCRL that enables analogy transduction beyond seen pairs to unseen combinations. We empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on OGBench manipulation environments, substantially outperforming prior methods that do not perform analogy transduction. Project page: https://rllab-snu.github.io/projects/CTA/

</details>


### [279] [Matryoshka Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20612)
*Ziye Chen,Hongbin Lin,Xinyue Xu,Jie Li,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MCBM proposes a nested concept hierarchy within a single model, enabling adaptive concept granularity and reducing expert intervention costs from O(K) to O(log K).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CBMs suffer from high test-time intervention costs because they either use a fixed concept set (requiring exhaustive annotation) or train multiple models for different budgets (heavy overhead). The goal is to enable efficient, adaptive concept usage without retraining.

Method: MCBM organizes concepts into a nested Matryoshka hierarchy based on maximum relevance and minimum redundancy, allowing inference at multiple granularity levels within a single unified model. Inspired by Matryoshka Representation Learning.

Result: Theoretically achieves O(log K) expected intervention costs with monotonic performance improvement guarantees. Empirically matches independently trained models' performance while enabling dynamic expert interaction.

Conclusion: MCBM offers a practical, cost-efficient solution for interpretable CBMs by unifying multi-granularity concept learning into one architecture, significantly reducing human annotation burden at test time.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have emerged as a prominent paradigm for interpretable deep learning, learning by grounding predictions in human-understandable concepts. However, their practical deployment is hindered by the high cost of test-time intervention, as correcting model errors typically requires human experts to manually inspect and verify a large set of predicted concepts. Existing approaches suffer from a fundamental structural limitation: they either adopt a single static concept set, forcing experts to exhaustively annotate concepts and incurring prohibitive intervention costs, or train multiple models tailored to different concept budgets, resulting in substantial computational and maintenance overhead. To address this challenge, we propose the Matryoshka Concept Bottleneck Model (MCBM), a unified architecture that enables adaptive concept utilization within a single model. Inspired by Matryoshka Representation Learning, MCBM organizes concepts into a nested hierarchy based on maximum relevance and minimum redundancy, allowing inference at multiple levels of conceptual granularity without retraining. Theoretically, we show that MCBM reduces the expected intervention costs from linear to logarithmic order, $O(\log K)$, while guaranteeing monotonic performance improvement. Empirically, extensive experiments demonstrate that MCBM matches the performance of independently trained models while enabling dynamic and efficient expert interaction.

</details>


### [280] [SURF: Steering the Scalarization Weight to Uniformly Traverse the Pareto Front](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20619)
*Liuyuan Jiang,Chentong Huang,Lisha Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SURF proposes a principled weight sampling method based on inverting the arc-length CDF of the scalarization path to achieve uniform Pareto front coverage in multi-objective optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uniformly sampling scalarization weights often leads to non-uniform coverage of the Pareto front. The paper aims to explain and correct this mismatch through geometric analysis.

Method: SURF analyzes the scalarization path's traversal speed, derives an arc-length CDF map, and inverts it to obtain PF-aware weight sampling rules. For structured problems (e.g., bi-objective bandits), closed-form solutions are provided; for general problems, it alternates between CDF reconstruction and weight sampling.

Result: Theoretically, SURF converges linearly to a finite-sampling floor. Empirically, it achieves more uniform PF coverage than baselines on bandits, multi-objective-gymnasium, and multi-objective LLM alignment tasks.

Conclusion: SURF provides a theoretically grounded and empirically effective method for achieving uniform Pareto front coverage via principled weight selection.

Abstract: Scalarization is widely used in multi-objective optimization owing to its simplicity and scalability. In many applications, the goal is to generate solutions that represent diverse user preferences, ideally with uniform coverage of the Pareto front (PF). However, uniformly sampling scalarization weights usually induces non-uniform coverage of the PF. We explain this mismatch through a geometric analysis of the scalarization path. As the scalarization weight varies, the corresponding solutions trace the PF with a generally non-uniform traversal speed. This speed induces an arc-length cumulative distribution function (CDF); inverting this CDF map yields a principled rule for selecting weights that produce uniform PF coverage. Building on this insight, we propose SURF (Sampling Uniformly along the PaReto Front). For structured problems, including bi-objective bandits, we derive closed-form expressions for this CDF map and the resulting PF-aware weight sampling rule. For general problems, SURF alternates between CDF reconstruction and weight sampling. Theoretically, we show that under provable conditions, SURF converges linearly to an unavoidable finite-sampling floor. Empirically, experiments on bandits, multi-objective-gymnasium, and multi-objective LLM alignment demonstrate that SURF efficiently achieves more uniform PF coverage than baselines.

</details>


### [281] [Dynamic Shapley Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20620)
*Xuan Yang,Hsi-Wen Chen,Ming-Syan Chen,Jian Pei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: D-Shap is a dynamic Shapley-based data valuation framework that represents contributions as a matrix and efficiently updates it using utility/coalition locality, reducing computation by orders of magnitude.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Shapley data valuation methods are one-shot and computationally expensive, making them impractical for dynamic settings where tasks and training players evolve.

Method: D-Shap formulates dynamic valuation as a structured matrix maintenance problem, leveraging utility locality and coalition locality. It uses structure-aware interpolation for new tasks and local block updates for new players, plus self-valuation to eliminate pre-specified evaluation tasks.

Result: Task updates in milliseconds, player updates reduced by up to three orders of magnitude, with competitive valuation quality compared to full recomputation.

Conclusion: D-Shap enables efficient, reusable Shapley-based data valuation in dynamic environments without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Shapley-based data valuation provides a principled way to quantify the contribution of training data, but its high computational cost makes it impractical in dynamic settings where tasks and training players evolve. Existing methods treat Shapley computation as a one-shot process and collapse contributions into aggregated scores, preventing reuse and requiring recomputation under any change. We introduce a new perspective that represents Shapley values as a player-by-task matrix and formulates dynamic valuation as a structured matrix maintenance problem. We exploit the fact that each task depends on a small subset of training players and that similar tasks yield similar valuations, leading to utility locality and coalition locality. Based on these insights, we propose D-Shap, a dynamic valuation framework that enables efficient updates by modifying only a small portion of the matrix: new task valuations are inferred via structure-aware interpolation, while updates induced by new players are confined to affected local matrix blocks. To eliminate the need for pre-specified evaluation tasks, we introduce self-valuation, which constructs the initial matrix directly from training data, supported by scalable subset reuse and coverage-aware anchor selection. Experiments across diverse models show that D-Shap performs task updates in milliseconds and reduces the cost of player updates by up to three orders of magnitude, while achieving valuation quality competitive with full recomputation.

</details>


### [282] [The General Theory of Localization Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20635)
*Congwei Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the "localization method," a machine learning framework based on localization kernels and local means, which underpin self-attention. It formalizes this via local models and the localization trick, connects it to many existing methods (e.g., kernel methods, MeanShift, Transformers), and explores advanced extensions like adaptive kernels and hierarchical models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified theoretical framework that generalizes diverse machine learning models (from kernel methods to Transformers) under a single lens, clarifying their common foundations and enabling systematic design of new learning systems.

Method: The localization method is defined via two pillars: (1) formulation of the local(-ized) model and (2) the localization trick. The framework is built on localization kernels and local means. The paper systematically analyzes connections to existing models and extends the framework with adaptive kernels, hierarchical local models, and non-local models.

Result: The framework unifies a wide range of ML models (kernel methods, lazy learning, MeanShift, relaxation labeling, Hopfield networks, LLE, fuzzy inference, DAEs). Notably, the Transformer architecture is shown to be constructible via hierarchical local models, demonstrating the framework's unifying power.

Conclusion: This work provides a unified theoretical lens for reinterpreting existing models and offers new methodological tools for designing flexible, data-adaptive learning systems, with the localization method serving as a generalizable foundation.

Abstract: This paper proposes a general machine learning framework called the localization method, which is fundamentally built on two core concepts: localization kernels and local means -- key components that underpin the self-attention mechanism. To establish a rigorous theoretical foundation, the framework is formally defined through two essential pillars: the formulation of the local(-ized) model and the localization trick. We systematically investigate the connections between the localization method and a wide range of existing machine learning models/methods, including (but not limited to) kernel methods, lazy learning, the MeanShift algorithm, relaxation labeling, Hopfield networks, local linear embedding (LLE), fuzzy inference, and denoising autoencoders (DAEs). By dissecting these relationships, we clarify the broader theoretical significance of the localization method and demonstrate its practical applicability across diverse machine learning tasks. Furthermore, we explore advanced extensions of the framework, such as adaptive kernels, hierarchical local models, and non-local models. Notably, we show that the Transformer -- a cornerstone of modern sequence modeling -- can be constructed using hierarchical local models, revealing the ability of the localization method to unify and generalize state-of-the-art architectures. This work not only provides a unified theoretical lens to reinterpret existing models but also offers new methodological tools for designing flexible, data-adaptive learning systems.

</details>


### [283] [Same Target, Different Basins: Hard vs. Soft Labels for Annotator Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20642)
*Mirerfan Gheibi,Gashin Ghazizadeh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies hard-label delivery methods (multipass and stochastic label sampling) as alternatives to soft-label training when annotators disagree, finding they improve performance with limited annotations and match soft-label training when full distributions are available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When annotators disagree, that disagreement reflects epistemic uncertainty rather than simple label noise. The paper seeks alternatives to collapsing votes to a single label or training on empirical soft-label distributions.

Method: Two hard-label methods are studied: multipass (cycling through observed votes while keeping dataset size fixed) and stochastic label sampling (SLS) (sampling one label per example at each epoch start). Controls include deterministic multipass ablation and shuffled SLS.

Result: On CIFAR-10H, hard-label delivery improves over soft-label training when few annotations are available, especially when sparse targets are farther from full distributions. With full distributions, both methods match soft-label training. Hard-label delivery converges to flatter basins with better OOD detection on SVHN and CIFAR-100.

Conclusion: Multipass is a strong default when raw vote counts are available; SLS is a lightweight alternative competitive with few votes and matches soft-label training with full distributions.

Abstract: When annotators disagree, that disagreement can reflect epistemic uncertainty rather than simple label noise. We study hard-label delivery as an alternative to the usual choices of collapsing votes to a single label or training directly on the empirical soft-label distribution. We focus on two primary hard-label methods: multipass, which cycles through observed votes while keeping the dataset size fixed, and stochastic label sampling (SLS), which samples one label per example at the start of each epoch. On CIFAR-10H, we find that when only a small number of annotations per example is available, hard-label delivery improves over soft-label training, with larger improvements where the sparse empirical target is farther from the full annotator distribution. When full annotator distributions are available, both hard-label methods match soft-label training. We use deterministic control as an ablation of multipass and shuffled SLS as a control that breaks the example-to-distribution match. We also show that SLS and soft-label cross-entropy optimize the same expected objective. Hard-label delivery also converges to flatter basins, with supporting descriptive evidence from OOD detection on SVHN and CIFAR-100. Overall, these results suggest that multipass is a strong practical default when raw vote counts are available, while SLS offers a lightweight alternative that remains competitive when only a few votes per example are available and matches soft-label training when full annotator distributions are available.

</details>


### [284] [AVSD: Adaptive-View Self-Distillation by Balancing Consensus and Teacher-Specific Privileged Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20643)
*Duy Nguyen,Hanqi Xiao,Archiki Prasad,Zaid Khan,Anirban Das,Austin Zhang,Sambit Sahu,Hyunji Lee,Elias Stengel-Eskin,Mohit Bansal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AVSD introduces adaptive-view self-distillation that separates cross-view consensus from view-specific residuals, outperforming single-view baselines and GRPO on math and code benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-distillation for language models suffers from asymmetric information between teacher and student, and the optimal choice of privileged information view is task-dependent, making it hard to select a single teacher view.

Method: AVSD uses multiple privileged-information views, reconstructs token-level supervision by separating stable cross-view consensus (reliable update direction) from view-specific residual signals (selectively added to adjust magnitude when aligned with consensus).

Result: On AIME24, AIME25, HMMT25 (math) and Codeforces, LiveCodeBench v6 (code), AVSD consistently outperforms single-view self-distillation and GRPO, achieving Avg@8 gains of 3.1% (Qwen3-8B) and 2.2% (Qwen3-4B) over strongest baselines in math, and 2.4% improvement on code benchmarks.

Conclusion: AVSD effectively addresses the teacher-student asymmetry and view selection challenges in self-distillation by leveraging multiple views with consensus-residual decomposition, leading to superior performance across math and code tasks.

Abstract: Self-distillation enables language models to learn on-policy from their own trajectories by using the same model as both student and teacher, with the teacher being conditioned on privileged information unavailable to the student. Such information can come in different types or views, such as solutions, demonstrations, feedback, or final answers. This setup provides dense token-level feedback without relying on a separate external model, but creates a fundamental asymmetry: the teacher may rely on view-specific information that the student cannot access at inference time. Moreover, the best type of privileged information is often task-dependent, making it difficult to choose a single teacher view. In this work, we address both these challenges jointly by introducing AVSD (Adaptive-View Self-Distillation), a novel method of self-distillation with multiple privileged-information views, which reconstructs token-level supervision by separating stable cross-view consensus from view-specific residual signals. AVSD identifies the consensus signal shared across views, which provides a reliable update direction, and then selectively adds the view-specific residual signal to adjust the update magnitude when it both aligns with the consensus direction and remains proportionate to the consensus signal. Experiments on math competition benchmarks (AIME24, AIME25, and HMMT25) show that AVSD consistently outperforms both single-view self-distillation baselines and GRPO, achieving average Avg@8 gains of 3.1% and 2.2% over the strongest baselines on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-4B, respectively. Moreover, on code-generation benchmarks (Codeforces, LiveCodeBench v6) using Qwen3-8B, AVSD outperforms the single-view self-distillation baseline by 2.4% on average.

</details>


### [285] [REFLECTOR: Internalizing Step-wise Reflection against Indirect Jailbreak](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20654)
*Jiachen Ma,Jiawen Zhang,Xiangtian Li,Bo Zou,Chaochao Lu,Chao Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reflector is a two-stage framework that internalizes self-reflection in LLM generation trajectories to defend against multi-step jailbreak attacks, achieving >90% defense success rates while also improving general task performance like GSM8K by 5.85%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs remain vulnerable to sophisticated, multi-step jailbreak attacks that bypass surface-level safety alignment by exploiting the internal generation process.

Method: A two-stage framework: (1) teacher-guided generation creates high-quality reflection data for SFT to establish structured reflection patterns; (2) Reinforcement Learning with outcome-driven and reward-validity supervision instills robust autonomous self-reflection.

Result: Defense Success Rates (DSR) exceeding 90% against complex indirect attacks; 5.85% gain on GSM8K; improved performance on knowledge-intensive benchmarks; robust generalization across diverse threat scenarios.

Conclusion: Internalizing trajectory-level safety overcomes surface alignment limitations without significant computational overhead, offering an efficient and scalable solution for safe and capable LLMs.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities, they remain susceptible to sophisticated, multi-step jailbreak attacks that circumvent conventional surface-level safety alignment by exploiting the internal generation process. To address these vulnerabilities, we propose Reflector, a principled two-stage framework that internalizes self-reflection within the generation trajectory. Reflector first leverages teacher-guided generation to produce high-quality reflection data for supervised fine-tuning (SFT), establishing structured reflection patterns. It subsequently uses Reinforcement Learning (RL) with outcome-driven and reward-validity supervision to instill robust, autonomous self-reflection capabilities. Empirical results show that Reflector achieves Defense Success Rates (DSR) exceeding 90% against complex indirect attacks while generalizing robustly across diverse threat scenarios. Notably, the framework enhances both task-specific and general utility, yielding a 5.85% gain on GSM8K alongside improved performance on knowledge-intensive benchmarks. By internalizing trajectory-level safety, Reflector overcomes the fundamental limitations of surface alignment without significant computational overhead, offering an efficient and scalable solution for the development of safe and capable LLMs.

</details>


### [286] [LT2: Linear-Time Looped Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20670)
*Chunyuan Deng,Yizhe Zhang,Rui-Jie Zhu,Yuanyuan Xu,Jiarui Liu,T. S. Eugene Ng,Hanjie Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LT2 replaces quadratic softmax attention in Looped Transformers with linear-time attention (linear or sparse), enabling iterative memory refinement and expanded receptive fields. The hybrid variants (GDN+DSA and Full+GDN) match or surpass standard LTs in quality and efficiency. A converted 1.4B model outperforms industry 1B models and competes with 4B models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Looped Transformers suffer from quadratic complexity due to full attention, making them computationally expensive and slow. The goal is to make looped transformers more scalable and efficient while retaining their iterative refinement benefits.

Method: Proposes LT2 family with two variants: LT2-linear (linear attention) and LT2-sparse (sparse attention). Explores hybrid models combining different attention types in looped settings, including LT2-hybrid (GDN+DSA) for efficiency and LT2-hybrid (Full+GDN) for quality. Also presents a method to convert pre-trained LT into LT2-hybrid via ~1B token training.

Result: Looping synergizes with linear/sparse attention: enables iterative memory refinement in linear attention and progressive receptive field expansion in sparse attention. Hybrid variants match or surpass standard LTs. The converted Ouro-hybrid-1.4B outperforms industry 1B models and competes with 4B models.

Conclusion: Linear-time attention in looped transformers is a viable path toward scalable, efficient small language models, demonstrating that quality can be maintained or even improved while drastically reducing computational cost.

Abstract: Looped Transformers (LT) have emerged as a powerful architecture by iterating their layers multiple times before decoding the final token. However, pairing them with full attention retains quadratic complexity, making them computationally expensive and slow. We introduce LT2 (Linear-Time Looped Transformers), a family of looped architectures that replace quadratic softmax attention with subquadratic, linear-time attention. We study two variants: LT2-linear with linear attention and LT2-sparse with sparse attention. We find that looping uniquely synergizes with these variants: it enables iterative memory refinement in linear attention and progressively expands the effective receptive field in sparse attention. We formalize these benefits theoretically and demonstrate consistent empirical gains across controlled recall, state-tracking, and language modeling tasks. We then explore LT2-hybrid, which combines different attention variants in a looped setting. Two variants are especially promising: LT2-hybrid (GDN+DSA), which interleaves linear and sparse attention to maximize efficiency and matches the standard looped transformer's quality at fully linear-time cost; and LT2-hybrid (Full+GDN), which interleaves GDN with a small fraction of full attention layers to maximize quality, surpassing the standard looped transformer in both performance and efficiency. We also show how to convert a pre-trained LT into an LT2-hybrid model. With about 1B tokens of training, our converted model, Ouro-hybrid-1.4B, outperforms industry-level 1B models and is competitive with industry-level 4B models while retaining the speed benefits of linear-time attention. Together, these results show a clear path toward making looped transformers more scalable and advancing efficient, capable small language models.

</details>


### [287] [Modular Multimodal Classification Without Fine-Tuning: A Simple Compositional Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20674)
*Herman Bergström,Aditya Mehrotra,Rahul G. Krishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoMET is a zero-training multimodal classification method that uses PCA to compress frozen pre-trained backbone embeddings and feeds them into a Tabular Foundation Model, achieving SOTA results with no fine-tuning needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the necessity of complex end-to-end multimodal training pipelines by showing that simple composition of frozen foundation models with TFMs can achieve strong performance without any training.

Method: Extract features from each modality using frozen pre-trained backbones, compress with PCA, concatenate, and feed into a Tabular Foundation Model for prediction. Also proposes PALPooling for adaptive token pooling when CLS tokens are suboptimal.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results across diverse multimodal benchmarks without any training. Handles datasets with 500K+ samples and 2,000+ classes without fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Composing foundation models is a simple yet powerful out-of-the-box solution for multimodal learning, challenging the necessity of complex end-to-end training pipelines.

Abstract: We introduce CoMET, \textit{\textbf{C}omposing \textbf{M}odality \textbf{E}ncoders with \textbf{T}abular foundation models}, a simple yet highly competitive method for multimodal classification: pass each modality through a frozen pre-trained backbone, compress the resulting embeddings with PCA, and concatenate as input into a Tabular Foundation Model (TFM) for prediction. We show that PCA alone suffices to act as an adaptor yielding strong, robust performance across modalities. When the \texttt{CLS} tokens of the foundation model align poorly with downstream tasks, we propose \textbf{PALPooling}, a lightweight adaptive token pooler that consistently improves representation quality. By composing strong frozen representation learning backbones with TFMs, our approach achieves state-of-the-art results across diverse multimodal benchmarks without any training. On hierarchical tasks with large fine-grained class spaces, our approach enables fast and scalable classification, handling datasets with over 500,000 samples and 2,000 classes without any fine-tuning. Overall, our results show that the composition of foundation models is a simple, yet powerful, out-of-the-box solution for multimodal learning, challenging the necessity of complex, end-to-end training pipelines for new problems.

</details>


### [288] [Dynamic TMoE: A Drift-Aware Dynamic Mixture of Experts Framework for Non-Stationary Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20678)
*Jiawen Zhu,Shuhan Liu,Di Weng,Yingcai Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic TMoE is a non-stationary time series forecasting framework that uses MMD-based distribution shift detection to dynamically add/remove experts, plus a temporal memory router for context-aware expert selection, achieving 10.4% MSE and 7.8% MAE reduction over SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary time series face evolving distribution shifts that static models can't capture. Existing MoE approaches are limited by fixed expert pools and memoryless routing, unable to adapt to abrupt regime shifts.

Method: Uses Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD) to detect distribution shifts, dynamically instantiates heterogeneous experts and prunes redundant ones. A temporal memory router employs recurrent states and an anomaly repository for stable, context-aware expert selection without test-time updates.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on nine benchmarks, reducing MSE by 10.4% and MAE by 7.8%.

Conclusion: Dynamic TMoE effectively unifies architectural evolution with temporal continuity, outperforming existing methods for non-stationary time series forecasting.

Abstract: Non-stationary time series forecasting is challenged by evolving distribution shifts that static models struggle to capture. While Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures offer a promising paradigm for decoupling complex drift patterns, existing approaches are limited by fixed expert pools and memoryless routing, hampering their ability to adapt to abrupt regime shifts. To address this, we propose Dynamic TMoE, a framework that unifies architectural evolution with temporal continuity during learning phase. By detecting distribution shifts via Maximum Mean Discrepancy (MMD), we dynamically instantiate heterogeneous experts and prune redundant ones to optimize capacity. Additionally, a temporal memory router leverages recurrent states and an anomaly repository to ensure stable, context-aware expert selection without requiring test-time updates. Experiments on nine benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, reducing MSE by 10.4% and MAE by 7.8%. Code is available at https://github.com/andone-07/Dynamic-TMoE.

</details>


### [289] [Distributed Direct Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20696)
*Zhanhong Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides the first theoretical convergence and time-complexity analysis of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) in distributed (federated and decentralized) settings, deriving rates that quantify the impact of client drift, communication frequency, preference heterogeneity, and graph spectral connectivity, with empirical validation on alignment benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preference-based RL's theoretical behavior in distributed settings with fragmented, heterogeneous preference data is poorly understood, and DPO lacks convergence guarantees under federated/decentralized training with communication constraints and non-IID preferences.

Method: The authors model personalized offline RL with user-specific preference distributions, characterize the global optimization landscape, and derive convergence rates for both federated DPO (accounting for client drift, communication frequency, preference heterogeneity) and decentralized DPO (accounting for spectral connectivity of communication graphs).

Result: First convergence and time-complexity analysis of DPO in distributed environments; theoretical rates quantifying the effects of client drift, communication frequency, preference heterogeneity, and graph connectivity; empirical validation showing strong theoretical guarantees and robust scalable performance.

Conclusion: Distributed DPO enjoys strong theoretical guarantees and delivers robust, scalable performance in practice, with provable convergence influenced by key distributed system parameters.

Abstract: Preference-based reinforcement learning (RL) is a key paradigm for aligning policies with human judgments, yet its theoretical behavior in distributed settings where preference data are fragmented across heterogeneous users remains poorly understood. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) avoids explicit reward modeling but lacks convergence guarantees under federated and decentralized training, where communication constraints and non-IID preferences fundamentally alter optimization dynamics. We provide the first convergence and time-complexity analysis of DPO in distributed environments. Modeling personalized offline RL with user-specific preference distributions, we characterize the induced global optimization landscape. For federated DPO, we derive convergence rates that quantify the impact of client drift, communication frequency, and preference heterogeneity; for decentralized DPO, we establish convergence over general communication graphs and show how spectral connectivity governs optimization speed and consensus. Empirically, we corroborate our theoretical insights on standard alignment benchmarks, demonstrating that our proposed methods not only enjoy strong theoretical guarantees but also deliver robust and scalable performance in practice. The code base is available here.

</details>


### [290] [Decision-Path Patterns as Tree Reliability Signals: Path-based Adaptive Weighting for Random Forest Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20716)
*Youngjoon Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a class-conditional ratio weighting method that uses topological patterns (label flips along decision paths) to reweight trees in random forests per-sample, achieving statistically significant accuracy gains over standard RF without the class bias issues of naive approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard random forests treat all trees equally via simple majority voting, ignoring that some trees are more reliable for specific samples. The topological pattern of class label flips along decision paths may contain exploitable reliability signals, but using it naively introduces class bias.

Method: The authors observe tree-specific topological patterns (where/how often the dominant class label flips along root-to-leaf paths) as a reliability signal. They propose a class-conditional ratio weighting scheme that guarantees zero expected class bias by construction, applied to reweight tree votes per sample.

Result: On 30 binary classification benchmarks, the proposed method is the only one among four compared schemes (RF, weighted RF, KNORA-Eliminate, KNORA-Union) to achieve statistically significant accuracy improvement over RF (Wilcoxon p=0.018). It has no majority-recall regressions and only 3/30 datasets with minority-recall regressions, outperforming dynamic ensemble selection methods.

Conclusion: Exploiting topological label-flip patterns via class-conditional ratio weighting yields robust accuracy gains across forest sizes (100-1000 trees) without introducing class bias or severe regression issues, outperforming both standard RF and dynamic ensemble selection methods.

Abstract: Random forests aggregate tree votes by simple majority, treating all trees as equally informative. We observe that the topological pattern along each tree's root-to-leaf decision path -- where and how often the dominant class label flips along it -- carries a signal of tree reliability that is exploitable for per-sample reweighting. The naive use of this signal is structurally confounded with the predicted class, so we propose a class-conditional ratio weighting that guarantees zero expected class bias by construction. On 30 binary classification benchmarks under a shared-forest, shared-split protocol with 30 repeats, the proposed method is the only one among four compared schemes -- RF, weighted RF, KNORA-Eliminate, KNORA-Union -- to yield a statistically significant accuracy improvement over RF (Wilcoxon p = 0.018), while the three alternatives all fail to do so (p > 0.5). It is also the only scheme without majority-recall regressions, with minority-recall regressions limited to 3/30 datasets -- a one-sided loss to which classical dynamic ensemble selection methods are susceptible. The gain is robust across forest sizes from 100 to 1000 trees.

</details>


### [291] [Robust Recommendation from Noisy Implicit Feedback: A GMM-Weighted Bayes-label Transition Matrix Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20721)
*Zongyu Li,Xuanyu Liu,Gongce Cao,Shirui Sun,Yaqi Fang,Yongshuai Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RGBT uses GMM-derived reliability scores to calibrate Bayes-label transition matrix, enabling full sample utilization with consistent and lower-variance estimation for learning from noisy implicit feedback in recommender systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Implicit feedback in recommender systems suffers from label noise. Existing denoising methods either discard noisy data (low utilization) or use BLTM (biased estimates). RGBT aims to overcome both limitations simultaneously.

Method: Proposes a Robust GMM-weighted Bayes-label Transition Matrix (RGBT) framework that employs Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to compute instance-specific reliability scores, which calibrate the BLTM estimation to reduce bias while maintaining full data usage.

Result: Experiments on real-world and synthetically flipped datasets show RGBT outperforms both reliable sample-based denoising methods (better noisy sample utilization) and transition matrix-based methods (superior calibration capability).

Conclusion: RGBT achieves full sample utilization, consistent estimation, and significantly reduced estimation variance, offering a robust solution for learning from noisy implicit feedback in recommendation.

Abstract: Learning from implicit feedback in recommender systems is fundamentally challenged by pervasive label noise. While conventional denoising approaches often discard noisy instances to ensure robustness, this strategy inevitably suffers from low data utilization. Alternative methods that employ a Bayes-label transition matrix (BLTM) can leverage all available data, but their estimates tend to be biased in practical recommendation scenarios. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a Robust GMM-weighted Bayes-label Transition Matrix framework (RGBT). Our solution utilizes a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to derive instance-specific reliability scores, which systematically calibrate the BLTM estimation to mitigate bias. Theoretical analysis confirms that our approach, by leveraging the BLTM framework with GMM calibration, simultaneously ensures full sample utilization, delivers consistent estimation, and critically, achieves a significant reduction in estimation variance. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world and synthetically flipped datasets demonstrate that RGBT not only utilizes noisy samples more effectively than mainstream reliable sample-based denoising methods, but also achieves significantly superior calibration capability of the transition matrix compared to state-of-the-art transition matrix-based denoising approaches.

</details>


### [292] [AGPO: Adaptive Group Policy Optimization with Dual Statistical Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20722)
*Miaobo Hu,Shuhao Hu,Bokun Wang,Ruohan Wang,Xin Wang,Xiaobo Guo,Daren Zha,Jun Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AGPO proposes adaptive clipping and bidirectional adaptive temperature sampling to replace fixed hyperparameters in GRPO, improving LLM reasoning on math/STEM benchmarks across multiple model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PPO/GRPO use fixed clipping thresholds and decoding temperatures, making training brittle and requiring heavy hyperparameter tuning.

Method: AGPO uses a shared probe-derived statistical state to drive two controllers: (i) adaptive clipping based on reward dispersion, skewness, probe vote entropy, policy entropy, and KL drift; (ii) bidirectional adaptive temperature sampling that adjusts decoding temperature dynamically around a base temperature.

Result: On nine math/STEM benchmarks, Qwen2.5-14B with AGPO achieves 67.3% on GSM8K and 40.5% on MATH, outperforming PPO/GRPO under the same token budget. Gains transfer to Llama-3-8B and Gemma-2-9B.

Conclusion: AGPO's adaptive modules are complementary and improve training stability and final performance across model families, with public implementation available.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning improves LLM reasoning, but PPO/GRPO typically use fixed clipping and decoding temperature, which makes training brittle and tuning-heavy. We propose Adaptive Group Policy Optimization (AGPO), a critic-free refinement of GRPO that uses group-level statistics to control both update magnitude and exploration. AGPO uses a shared probe-derived statistical state to drive two controllers: (i) adaptive clipping, which sets the trust-region size from reward dispersion and skewness, probe vote entropy, policy entropy, and step-wise KL drift; and (ii) bidirectional adaptive temperature sampling, which heats or cools decoding around a base temperature according to centered uncertainty relative to a running baseline. On nine English and Chinese math/STEM benchmarks, Qwen2.5-14B trained with AGPO outperforms PPO/GRPO under the same generated-token budget, reaching 67.3% on GSM8K and 40.5% on MATH. Gains transfer to Llama-3-8B and Gemma-2-9B, and ablations confirm both modules are complementary. Our implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/wandugu/paper_agpo.

</details>


### [293] [Memory-Efficient Partitioned DNN Inference on Resource-Constrained Android Crowds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20723)
*Lakshani Manamperi,Disumi Pathirana,Thiwanka Pathirana,Nipun Premarathna,Kutila Gunasekera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CROWDio enables large DNN inference on memory-constrained Android devices using distributed pipeline scheduling without model modification, achieving 43±2 MB peak RSS and 50±3 mAh battery draw per run.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying large transformers on memory-constrained mobile devices (3.3-7.4 GB RAM) is difficult; existing compression techniques are insufficient.

Method: Five mechanisms: JIT deferred partition loading, single-partition-resident constraint, 4-tier affinity scheduler, zlib-compressed tensor transport, streaming 1:1 dependency model.

Result: On DistilBERT (67M params, SST-2) across 5 Android handsets: 43±2 MB peak RSS, 50±3 mAh battery draw, 34% lower latency than barrier synchronization.

Conclusion: Distributed pipeline scheduling can enable practical ONNX inference on commodity mobile devices without modifying model architecture.

Abstract: Deploying large deep neural networks on memory-constrained mobile devices is a central challenge in edge ML. While compression, pruning, and quantization reduce per-parameter cost, transformer-based models remain too large for the 3.3-7.4 GB RAM envelope of commodity Android handsets.
