Abstract: Conformal triage converts predictive scores into deployment actions that either release a case, flag it for urgent attention, or defer it to human review. Under prevalence shift, however, the usual summaries of marginal coverage and human-review rate can miss the safety-critical question of whether patients who truly experience the target event are released without review. To address this gap, we introduce a leakage-aware deployment audit for release-side conformal triage. It first assigns target subjects to three non-overlapping roles: prevalence correction, conformal calibration, and held-out release-safety evaluation. This separation then lets the audit evaluate release directly: how many event-positive patients are cleared without review, whether the pilot has enough event labels for calibration, and how the safety-review trade-off shifts. Applying this audit to a retrospective NSCLC pilot shows why lower review can be misleading: after prevalence correction, the pooled conformal branch lowers review by releasing more patients, some of whom are event-positive. Within the audit, the classwise branch acts as a scarcity diagnostic: the pilot has too few event labels to certify safe low-review release.

</details>


### [321] [Choose Wisely and Privately: Proactive Client Selection for Fair and Efficient Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20975)
*Adda Akram Bendoukha,Heber Hwang Arcolezi,Nesrine Kaaniche,Aymen Boudguiga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a proactive client selection framework for Federated Learning that uses mutual information from differentially private contingency tables to find optimal client federations before training, balancing data utility and fairness via a Potential Federation Loss optimized through simulated annealing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Averaging-based Federated Learning suffers from non-IID data, causing slow convergence and poor accuracy. Existing reactive approaches (discarding/down-weighting noisy clients) waste computational resources, increase communication rounds, and expose unnecessary privacy risks.

Method: The framework uses mutual information from differentially private contingency tables to quantify cross-feature correlation relevance. A Potential Federation Loss (PFL) balances utility and fairness. Client selection is formulated as an optimal subset search solved via simulated annealing with strong differential privacy.

Result: On four benchmarks, models trained on optimally found federations achieve faster convergence, higher accuracy, and better fairness compared to uniform sampling, even outperforming state-of-the-art adaptive aggregation/sampling strategies.

Conclusion: Proactive client selection before training begins is more effective than reactive approaches, offering computational efficiency, privacy preservation, and improved model performance in non-IID Federated Learning settings.

Abstract: Federated Learning enables collaborative model training across decentralized data sources without data transfer. Averaging-based FL is limited by the presence of non-IID data, which negatively impacts convergence speed and final model accuracy. Conventional alternatives suffer from significant inefficiency. Clients with noisy or highly heterogeneous data contribute expensive gradient computations that are either discarded or heavily down-weighted before aggregation. These reactive approaches waste computational resources, require more communication rounds and result in unnecessary privacy exposure. In this paper, we propose a proactive client selection framework that aims to find an optimal federation of clients whose combined data match utility and fairness requirements before training begins. Our method relies on mutual information computed from differentially private contingency tables to quantify the relevance of cross-feature correlations in the union dataset. We introduce a Potential Federation Loss (PFL) over the set of fixed-size federations, which balances two objectives. Maximizing collective data utility while ensuring fair cross-features correlations to prevent group unfairness. Client selection is expressed as an optimal subset search problem over the PFL objective, which we solve using simulated annealing under strong differential privacy guarantees for clients' local statistics. Experimental results on four benchmarks show faster, fairer, and more accurate models trained on optimally found federations, compared to uniform sampling, even when state-of-the-art adaptive aggregation or sampling strategies are employed.

</details>


### [322] [Point Cloud Sequence Encoding for Material-conditioned Graph Network Simulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20978)
*Philipp Dahlinger,Balázs Gyenes,Niklas Freymuth,Luca Geminiani,Tobias Würth,Johannes Mitsch,Nadja Klein,Luise Kärger,Gerhard Neumann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PEACH introduces a point cloud-based in-context learning framework that enables graph network simulators to adapt to unseen physical properties without requiring mesh reconstruction, achieving accurate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNSs assume access to material parameters (e.g., stiffness, viscosity), limiting real-world applicability. Reconstructing meshes from observed scenes is challenging, creating a gap between simulation and real-world deployment.

Method: PEACH uses a spatio-temporal point cloud sequence encoder with in-context learning to adapt simulators to unseen physical properties. Two forms of auxiliary supervision are employed to improve simulation fidelity.

Result: PEACH achieves accurate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on challenging dynamic scenes, and outperforms mesh-based baselines in prediction accuracy while being more practical for real-world deployment.

Conclusion: PEACH effectively removes the dependency on mesh reconstruction and prior knowledge of material parameters, making GNSs more practical for realistic experimental settings.

Abstract: Graph Network Simulators (GNSs) have emerged as powerful surrogates for complex physics-based simulation, offering inherent differentiability and orders-of-magnitude speedups over traditional solvers. However, GNSs typically assume access to the underlying material parameters, such as stiffness or viscosity, severely limiting their utility in realistic experimental settings. While recent meta-learning approaches address the parameter dependency by inferring properties from mesh trajectories, reconstructing a mesh from an observed scene is challenging. In this work, we introduce Point Cloud Encoding for Accurate Context Handling (PEACH), a novel framework that applies in-context learning on point clouds to adapt a learned simulator to unseen physical properties during inference. Our approach relies on a novel spatio-temporal point cloud sequence encoder, as well as two forms of auxiliary supervision to help improve simulation fidelity. We demonstrate that PEACH is capable of accurate zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on a challenging, dynamic scene. Experiments on simulation scenes show that PEACH even outperforms mesh-based baselines on prediction accuracy, while being much more practical for real-world deployment.

</details>


### [323] [A Sharper Picture of Generalization in Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20988)
*Paul Lintilhac,Sair Shaikh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies transformer generalization on boolean functions via Fourier spectra and PAC-Bayes theory, showing that sparse, low-degree spectra enable flat minima with non-vacuous generalization bounds, supported by empirical and mechanistic interpretability evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand transformers' generalization behavior on boolean domains through the lens of Fourier spectra, moving beyond prior Rademacher complexity-based bounds to explore PAC-Bayes theory for potentially tighter, non-vacuous bounds.

Method: The authors show that any boolean function with sparsity no greater than the context length admits flat minima, then apply PAC-Bayes bounds to an idealized low-sharpness learner to derive non-vacuous generalization guarantees.

Result: Sparse Fourier spectra concentrated on low-degree components enable low-sharpness constructions with good generalization; PAC-Bayes bounds yield non-vacuous generalization guarantees for such functions.

Conclusion: The theoretical construction is supported by empirical evaluation and mechanistic interpretability analysis, suggesting the approach is realistic for real transformers.

Abstract: We study transformers' generalization behavior on boolean domains from the perspective of the Fourier Spectra of their target functions. In contrast to prior work (Edelman et al., 2022; Trauger and Tewari, 2024), which derived generalization bounds from Rademacher complexity, we investigate the feasibility of obtaining generalization bounds via PAC-Bayes theory. We show that sparse spectra concentrated on low-degree components enable low-sharpness constructions with good generalization properties. Our idea is to show the existence of flat minima implementing any boolean function of sparsity no greater than the context length, and then apply a PAC-Bayes bound to an idealized low-sharpness learner, resulting in a non-vacuous generalization bound. We evaluate predictions empirically and conduct a mechanistic interpretability study to support the realism of our theoretical construction in real transformers.

</details>


### [324] [Modeling Temporal scRNA-seq Data with Latent Gaussian Process and Optimal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20989)
*Mehmet Yigit Balik,Harri Lähdesmäki*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A generative framework combining Hilbert-space approximated Gaussian processes with optimal transport for inferring temporal dynamics from static single-cell RNA sequencing data, capturing biological heterogeneity via cell-specific latent times and cell type conditioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neural differential equation and flow-based methods for inferring temporal processes from scRNA-seq data are prone to overfitting and lack proper consideration of biological variability.

Method: Uses a latent heteroscedastic Gaussian process approximated by Hilbert space methods, with an optimal transport objective to align generated and observed distributions, incorporating cell-specific latent time and cell type conditioning.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on complex interpolation and extrapolation benchmarks; introduces a gradient-based strategy for perturbation trajectory inference.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively captures biological heterogeneity and temporal dynamics from static scRNA-seq snapshots, outperforming existing approaches on benchmark tasks.

Abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing provides insights into gene expression at single-cell resolution, yet inferring temporal processes from these static snapshot measurements remains a fundamental challenge. Current approaches utilizing neural differential equations and flows are sensitive to overfitting and lack careful considerations of biological variability. In this work, we propose a generative framework that models population trends using a latent heteroscedastic Gaussian process (GP) approximated by Hilbert space methods. To address the absence of genuine cell trajectories, we leverage an optimal transport (OT) objective that aligns generated and observed population distributions. Our method explicitly captures biological heterogeneity by incorporating cell-specific latent time and cell type conditioning to disentangle temporal asynchrony and trajectories to different cell types. We demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on complex interpolation and extrapolation benchmarks and introduce a novel gradient-based strategy for inferring perturbation trajectories.

</details>


### [325] [Beyond the Bellman Recursion: A Pontryagin-Guided Framework for Non-Exponential Discounting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20996)
*Hojin Ko,Jeonggyu Huh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PG-DPO replaces broken Bellman recursions (which fail under non-exponential discounting) with a Pontryagin-based variational framework using Adjoint-MC projections, achieving better accuracy and stability on hyperbolic and survival-discount benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bellman-style recursions collapse under non-exponential discounting (common in human preferences and survival processes) because exponential discounting sits at a fragile intersection of multiplicativity and time homogeneity; violating either property breaks standard dynamic programming.

Method: Proposes Pontryagin-Guided Direct Policy Optimization (PG-DPO), a variational framework that abandons recursion and couples the Pontryagin Maximum Principle with Monte Carlo rollouts via an Adjoint-MC projection enforcing pointwise Hamiltonian maximization.

Result: Across multi-dimensional hyperbolic and survival-discount benchmarks, PG-DPO improves accuracy and stability where equation-driven solvers and critic-based baselines diverge.

Conclusion: The Pontryagin-based variational approach effectively handles non-exponential discounting scenarios where traditional Bellman recursions fail, offering a more robust alternative for human-like and survival-based preferences.

Abstract: Most value-based and actor--critic reinforcement learning methods rely on Bellman-style recursions, yet these recursions collapse under non-exponential discounting common in human preferences and survival processes. We show the breakdown is structural: exponential discounting sits at a fragile intersection of multiplicativity and time homogeneity, and violating either property breaks standard dynamic programming. To overcome this, we propose Pontryagin-Guided Direct Policy Optimization (PG-DPO), a variational framework that abandons recursion and couples the Pontryagin Maximum Principle with Monte Carlo rollouts via an Adjoint-MC projection enforcing pointwise Hamiltonian maximization. Across multi-dimensional hyperbolic and survival-discount benchmarks, PG-DPO improves accuracy and stability where equation-driven solvers and critic-based baselines diverge.

</details>


### [326] [Efficient Banzhaf-Based Data Valuation for $k$-Nearest Neighbors Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21033)
*Guangyi Zhang,Lutz Oettershagen,Lixu Wang,Aristides Gionis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops efficient exact and Monte Carlo algorithms for computing Banzhaf values in kNN classifiers, proving #P-hardness yet achieving practical linear-time solutions via dynamic programming.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data valuation is crucial for quantifying data contribution to model performance, but game-theoretic approaches like Banzhaf value suffer from exponential computational complexity.

Method: The authors prove #P-hardness for computing Banzhaf values in kNN classifiers, then exploit locality properties to develop a pseudo-polynomial DP algorithm (O(Wkn²)) for weighted kNN and a linear-time algorithm (O(nk²)) for unweighted kNN, plus efficient Monte Carlo estimation.

Result: Achieves O(nk²) time complexity for unweighted kNN (linear in data points) and O(Wkn²) for weighted kNN; experiments on real datasets verify practical efficiency.

Conclusion: Despite theoretical #P-hardness, practical exact algorithms for Banzhaf values in kNN classifiers are feasible due to locality properties, enabling effective real-world data valuation.

Abstract: Data valuation, the task of quantifying the contribution of individual data points to model performance, has emerged as a fundamental challenge in machine learning. Game-theoretic approaches, such as the Banzhaf value, offer principled frameworks for fair data valuation; however, they suffer from exponential computational complexity. We address this challenge by developing efficient algorithms specifically tailored for computing Banzhaf values in $k$-nearest neighbor ($k$NN) classifiers. We first establish the theoretical hardness of the problem by proving that it is \#P-hard. Despite this intractability, we exploit the locality properties of $k$NN classifiers to develop practical exact algorithms. Our main contribution is a dynamic programming framework that achieves significant computational improvements: we present a pseudo-polynomial algorithm with $O(Wkn^2)$ time complexity for weighted $k$NN classifiers, where $W$ is the maximum sum of top-$k$ weights, and a specialized algorithm for unweighted $k$NN that achieves $O(nk^2)$ time complexity, that is, linear in the number of data points. We also offer efficient Monte Carlo estimation methods. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the practical efficiency of our approach and its effectiveness in data valuation applications.

</details>


### [327] [A Dialogue between Causal and Traditional Representation Learning: Toward Mutual Benefits in a Unified Formulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21058)
*Yan Li,Yuewen Sun,Shaoan Xie,Gongxu Luo,Yunlong Deng,Kun Zhang,Guangyi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper bridges causal representation learning (CRL) and traditional representation learning by proposing a unified formulation with two components: task (what information to preserve) and constraint (what structure to impose). Experiments on CausalVerse show that the effectiveness of causal constraints depends on the task they are paired with.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between CRL (focused on theoretical identifiability) and traditional representation learning (focused on empirical applications), which has led to disconnected terminology, formulations, and evaluations.

Method: The authors introduce a unified formulation characterizing representation learning by two components: a task component (what information to preserve) and a constraint component (what structure is imposed on the latent space). They experimentally evaluate CRL methods under different task-constraint pairings on CausalVerse.

Result: The effectiveness of causal constraints depends strongly on the tasks with which they are paired, demonstrating that CRL and traditional representation learning can mutually benefit each other.

Conclusion: CRL and traditional representation learning should be brought into dialogue rather than treated as separate paradigms, as each offers complementary insights: CRL provides theoretical tools for structured constraints, while traditional RL provides practical insights on task design.

Abstract: Causal representation learning (CRL) and traditional representation learning have largely developed along different trajectories. Traditional representation learning has been driven mainly by applications and empirical objectives, whereas CRL has focused more on theoretical questions, particularly identifiability. This difference in emphasis has created a gap between the two fields in terminology, problem formulation, and evaluation, limiting communication and sometimes leading to disconnected or redundant efforts. In this paper, we argue that these two fields should be brought into dialogue rather than treated as separate paradigms. To this end, we introduce a unified formulation in which the representation learning is characterized by two components: a task component, which specifies what information the learned representation is required to preserve, and a constraint component, which specifies what structure is imposed on the latent space. Under this formulation, the benefits run in both directions. CRL provides theoretical tools for understanding when structured latent constraints are useful or necessary, while traditional representation learning offers practical insights on task design and objective choice that can improve the development of CRL methods. To illustrate this interaction, we experimentally study how different task components affect the behavior of CRL methods under different structured constraints. Results on CausalVerse show that the effectiveness of causal constraints depends strongly on the tasks with which they are paired.

</details>


### [328] [Divide et Calibra: Multiclass Local Calibration via Vector Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21060)
*Cesare Barbera,Lorenzo Perini,Giovanni De Toni,Andrea Passerini,Andrea Pugnana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a compositional calibration method for multiclass classification that uses Vector Quantization (VQ) to partition the latent space into regions, then applies shared codeword-dependent Dirichlet concentration parameters to create region-specific calibration maps that generalize well even in sparse regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multiclass calibration methods struggle: global approaches assume homogeneous errors across the latent space, while local methods rely on dimensionality reduction that causes information loss.

Method: A compositional approach using Vector Quantization (VQ) to create structured partitions of the representation space, combined with indexed Dirichlet concentrations for parameter sharing across regions, enabling heterogeneous and generalizable calibration maps.

Result: Significant improvements in local calibration on benchmark datasets, while maintaining competitive global calibration and predictive performance.

Conclusion: The proposed compositional calibration method effectively handles multiclass calibration by learning heterogeneous maps that generalize across sparse regions, outperforming both global and local approaches.

Abstract: Accurate and well-calibrated Machine Learning (ML) models are mandatory in high-stakes settings, yet effective multiclass calibration remains challenging: global approaches assume calibration errors are homogeneous across the latent space, while local methods often rely on latent-space dimensionality reduction, which leads to information loss. To address these issues, we propose a compositional approach to multiclass calibration, where region-specific calibration maps are constructed from shared codeword-dependent factors. We instantiate this idea via Vector Quantization (VQ), which induces a structured partition of the representation space, and an indexed parameterization of Dirichlet concentrations that enables parameter sharing across regions. Our approach learns heterogeneous calibration maps that generalize well even to sparse regions of the latent space. Experiments on benchmark datasets show significant improvements in local calibration while maintaining competitive global calibration and predictive performance.

</details>


### [329] [Robust Personalized Recommendation under Hidden Confounding in MNAR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21066)
*Zongyu Li,Wanting Su,Tianyu Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes PUID/BPUID frameworks that estimate personalized (user-item level) sensitivity bounds to handle hidden confounding in recommender systems, outperforming global sensitivity methods without requiring RCT data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods rely on RCTs (costly) or global sensitivity bounds (assume uniform effect of hidden confounders), which neglect heterogeneity across user-item interactions. Need for personalized, assumption-relaxed approach.

Method: Estimates user-item level sensitivity bounds (PUID) plus adversarial optimization; BPUID variant incorporates pre-trained models as stabilizing references via benchmark-guided strategy.

Result: Significantly outperforms global sensitivity methods on three real-world datasets under hidden confounding, without needing RCT data.

Conclusion: Personalized sensitivity bounds effectively address hidden confounding heterogeneity in recommender systems, offering a practical alternative to RCT-dependent or globally uniform approaches.

Abstract: Recommender systems often rely on observational user--item interaction data, which is prone to selection bias due to users' selective interactions with items. Inverse propensity weighting and doubly robust estimators effectively mitigate selection bias under observed confounding, but are unreliable in the presence of hidden confounders. Existing approaches relying on randomized controlled trials (RCTs) or global sensitivity bounds are constrained in practice: RCTs demand costly experimental data, while global sensitivity bounds presume a uniformly bounded effect of unmeasured confounders on propensities through sensitivity analysis, thereby neglecting heterogeneity across user--item interactions. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel framework, which estimates user--item level sensitivity bounds, thereby substantially relaxing the homogeneity assumption inherent in global sensitivity bounds named Personalized Unobserved-Confounding-aware Interaction Deconfounder (PUID). To ensure both robustness and predictive accuracy, we further develop an adversarial optimization strategy and propose a benchmark-guided variant (BPUID) that incorporates pre-trained models as stabilizing references. Extensive experiments on three real-world datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms global methods under hidden confounding, without requiring RCT data.

</details>


### [330] [Towards Understanding Self-Pretraining for Sequence Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21070)
*Omar Coser,Loredana Zollo,Paolo Soda,Antonio Orvieto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper replicates and ablates Amos et al. (2024)'s self-pretraining (SPT) method for Transformers. It finds SPT helps by learning proximity interactions (biasing attention scores toward nearby tokens) and that supervised training can be locally blind to certain attention directions that masked reconstruction can detect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why self-pretraining (SPT) improves Transformer accuracy beyond standard supervised training, and to identify the fundamental bottlenecks in supervised learning for Transformers.

Method: Replication and systematic ablation of Amos et al. (2024)'s experiments; minimal setup analysis to isolate SPT effects; simplified theoretical setup to analyze attention-score learning dynamics.

Result: The central bottleneck is not depth or generalization alone, but the difficulty of learning useful query-key attention patterns from random initialization under label supervision. SPT helps by learning proximity interactions (converting absolute positional encodings into proximity-biased attention scores). Supervised training can be locally blind to certain attention-score directions detectable by masked reconstruction.

Conclusion: Self-pretraining improves Transformers by enabling attention patterns (especially proximity biases) that supervised learning alone struggles to learn from scratch. The findings highlight a fundamental limitation of label supervision in discovering certain useful attention structures.

Abstract: Amos et al. (2024) showed that the accuracy of Transformer models in sequence classification can be significantly improved by first pretraining with a masked token prediction objective without external data or augmentation, a procedure referred to as self-pretraining (SPT). While the primary objective of Amos et al. (2024) was to showcase that Transformers can achieve strong performance on the Long-Range Arena (LRA), their pipeline raises more fundamental questions: How does SPT drive optimization to better solutions? Why can standard supervised training fail in Transformers? To better understand this, we replicate and systematically ablate the findings of Amos et al. (2024). Our ablations suggest that a central bottleneck in the studied settings is not depth or generalization alone, but the ability of label supervision to learn useful query-key Attention patterns from random initialization. With a minimal setup, we identify learning proximity interactions - turning absolute positional encodings into proximity-biased Attention scores - as a key source of the improvements brought by SPT. Finally, in a simplified theoretical setup, we show that label supervision can be locally blind to certain Attention-score directions that are instead detectable through masked reconstruction.

</details>


### [331] [Reviving Error Correction in Modern Deep Time-Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21088)
*Minh Hoang Nguyen,Dai Do,Huu Hiep Nguyen,Dung Nguyen,Kien Do,Hung Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes UEC-STD, a universal error correction module that improves long-term time-series forecasting by decomposing predictions into trend and seasonal components and correcting each separately, outperforming across 4 backbones and 10 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models suffer from error accumulation during autoregressive long-term prediction, and existing error correction mechanisms are not effective for modern deep forecasting models.

Method: UEC-STD (Universal Error Corrector with Seasonal-Trend Decomposition) is an architecture-agnostic module that decomposes predictions into trend and seasonal components and trains a corrector to adjust each component separately, without requiring retraining of the base forecaster.

Result: The method significantly improves correction accuracy and robustness across 4 backbone architectures and 10 diverse datasets.

Conclusion: UEC-STD provides a practical, plug-and-play tool for enhancing long-term forecasts and offers new insights into mitigating autoregressive errors in deep time-series models.

Abstract: Modern deep-learning models have achieved remarkable success in time-series forecasting. Yet, their performance degrades in long-term prediction due to error accumulation in autoregressive inference, where predictions are recursively used as inputs. While classical error correction mechanisms (ECMs) have long been used in statistical methods, their applicability to deep learning models remains limited or ineffective. In this work, we revisit the error accumulation problem in deep time-series forecasting and investigate the role and necessity of ECMs in this new context. We propose a simple, architecture-agnostic error correction model that can be integrated with any existing forecaster without requiring retraining. By explicitly decomposing predictions into trend and seasonal components and training the corrector to adjust each separately, we introduce the Universal Error Corrector with Seasonal-Trend Decomposition (UEC-STD), which significantly improves correction accuracy and robustness across 4 backbones and 10 datasets. Our findings provide a practical tool for enhancing forecasts while offering new insights into mitigating autoregressive errors in deep time-series models. Code is available at https://github.com/DA2I2-SLM/UEC-STD.

</details>


### [332] [UOTIP: Unbalanced Optimal Transport Map for Unpaired Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21094)
*Donggyu Lee,Taekyung Lee,Jaewoong Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes UOTIP, an Unbalanced Optimal Transport-based method for solving unpaired image inverse problems, achieving robustness and state-of-the-art performance by relaxing exact marginal constraints and using a likelihood-based cost function.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address unpaired image inverse problems where only independent, non-paired sets of noisy measurements and clean target signals are available, overcoming limitations of paired-data approaches.

Method: Formulates reconstruction as learning an Unbalanced Optimal Transport Map from noisy measurement distribution to clean signal distribution, using a likelihood-based cost function that relaxes exact marginal constraints.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on unpaired image inverse problem benchmarks across both linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

Conclusion: UOT framework provides robustness to multi-level noise, adaptability to class imbalance, and generalizability to diverse noise types; quadratic cost term ensures existence and uniqueness of the transport map via the twist condition.

Abstract: We investigate unpaired image inverse problems, a challenging setting where only independent, non-paired sets of noisy measurements and clean target signals are available for training. We propose a novel inverse problem solver based on Unbalanced Optimal Transport, called Unbalanced Optimal Transport Map for Inverse Problems (UOTIP). Our method formulates the reconstruction task, predicting clean target signals from noisy measurements, as learning a UOT Map from noisy measurement distribution to clean signal distribution by incorporating a likelihood-based cost function. By relaxing the exact marginal constraint, the UOT framework provides key advantages to our model: robustness to multi-level observation noise, adaptability to class imbalance between noisy and clean datasets, and generalizability to diverse noise-type scenarios. Furthermore, we theoretically demonstrate that incorporating a quadratic cost term ensures the existence and uniqueness of the transport map by satisfying the twist condition, even for ill-posed inverse problems. Our experiments demonstrate that UOTIP achieves state-of-the-art performance on unpaired image inverse problem benchmarks, across linear and nonlinear inverse problems.

</details>


### [333] [A Typed Tensor Language for Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21103)
*Theofilos Mailis,Kalliopi-Christina Despotidou,Konstantinos Filippopolitis,Yannis Foufoulas,Thanasis-Michail Karampatsis,Andreas Ktenidis,Evdokia Mailli,Theodore Papamarkou,Yannis Ioannidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a typed tensor language that formalizes federated learning and analytics, distinguishing between client-partitioned (federated) and globally available (shared) tensors. It proves a shared-state factorization theory showing one-round programs factor through fixed-dimensional shared state, provides a converse representability result, and develops a differentiable fragment for gradient-based learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning and analytics are often described as separate protocols despite sharing the same mathematical form (client-local computation, mergeable aggregation, shared post-processing). The authors aim to formalize this common structure through a unified typed tensor language.

Method: The paper develops a typed tensor language distinguishing federated tensors (partitioned across clients) from shared tensors (globally available). Semantics are defined via comparison with a virtual global tensor. The main analysis involves shared-state factorization theory, proving that typed one-round programs factor through fixed-dimensional shared state, with a converse representability result.

Result: (1) Typed one-round programs factor through fixed-dimensional shared state whose size is independent of client/record count. (2) A converse representability result linking factorizations to typed programs. (3) Extension to iterative programs with cross-round shared state. (4) A differentiable fragment showing that per-record gradients, when summed along the record axis, yield the global gradient, enabling server-side gradient descent and second-order updates.

Conclusion: The framework provides a formal account of federated computations expressible as encode, merge, and decode procedures, characterizing a broad class of federated learning computations where communication passes through fixed-dimensional shared state.

Abstract: Federated learning and analytics are often described as collections of separate protocols, even when they share the same mathematical form: client-local tensor computation, mergeable aggregation into shared state, and shared-only post-processing. We introduce a typed tensor language that formalizes this structure. The language distinguishes federated tensors, whose records are partitioned across clients along a tracked record axis, from shared tensors, which are available globally. Its semantics are defined by comparison with a virtual global tensor, used only as a reference object. The main result is a shared-state factorization theory. We show that typed one-round programs factor through fixed-dimensional shared state whose size is independent of the number of clients and records, computed from client-local tensor expressions and merged across clients. We also prove a converse representability result; factorizations whose encoders and decoders are expressible in the language are realized by typed one-round programs, and the correspondence extends to iterative programs whose cross-round state is shared. This gives a formal account of the computations in the language that can be expressed as encode, merge, and decode procedures. We then develop a differentiable fragment for learning. If a per-record loss and its per-record gradient are represented by client-local tensor expressions, the global gradient is represented by record-axis summation of the federated gradient tensor. This yields typed iterative programs for server-side gradient descent and shared-linear-algebra second-order updates. The framework characterizes a broad class of federated learning computations whose communication passes through fixed-dimensional shared state.

</details>


### [334] [HORST: Composing Optimizer Geometries for Sparse Transformer Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21104)
*Tom Jacobs,Rohan Jain,Rebekka Burkholz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HORST is a new optimizer that combines adaptive methods' stability with L1 sparsity bias via hyperbolic mirror map, significantly outperforming AdamW for sparse transformer training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard optimizers struggle to simultaneously encourage sparsity and maintain training stability for transformers; adaptive optimizers have L∞ bias (stability) while sparsity requires L1 bias.

Method: Proposes composing optimizer steps as non-commutative operators to combine optimization geometry, yielding HORST (Hyperbolic Operator for Robust Sparse Training) which uses a hyperbolic mirror map to induce L1 sparsity bias while inheriting adaptive methods' stability.

Result: HORST consistently and significantly outperforms AdamW baselines across all sparsity levels on both vision and language transformer tasks, with large gains at higher sparsity.

Conclusion: The modular optimizer effectively integrates sparsity into adaptive training by principled composition of non-commutative operators, providing a robust solution for sparse transformer training.

Abstract: Sparsifying transformers remains a fundamental challenge, as standard optimizers fail to simultaneously encourage sparsity and maintain training stability. Effective adaptive optimizers exhibit an implicit $L_{\infty}$ bias favoring stability, yet, sparsity requires an $L_1$ bias. To integrate sparsity, we propose a composition of optimizer steps, which we cast as non-commutative operators to analyze and combine their optimization geometry in a principled way. This yields HORST (Hyperbolic Operator for Robust Sparse Training), a modular optimizer that inherits stability from adaptive methods while inducing $L_1$ sparsity bias through a hyperbolic mirror map. Our experiments demonstrate its utility for sparse training of transformers on both vision and language tasks. HORST consistently and significantly outperforms AdamW baselines across all sparsity levels, with large gains at higher sparsity.

</details>


### [335] [Improved Guarantees for Constrained Online Convex Optimization via Self-Contraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21107)
*Dhruv Sarkar,Abhishek Sinha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a projection-based algorithm for Constrained Online Convex Optimization (COCO) that significantly improves cumulative constraint violation bounds: achieving O(log T) CCV for strongly convex losses (exponential improvement) and O(√T) CCV for convex losses, while maintaining optimal regret.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing algorithms for COCO suffered from suboptimal constraint violation bounds: O(√(T log T)) for strongly convex and O(√T log T) for convex losses. The paper aims to close this gap and achieve tighter, optimal bounds.

Method: A simple projection-based algorithm leveraging a recent geometric result for self-contracted curves to achieve superior constraint violation guarantees.

Result: For strongly convex losses: O(log T) regret and O(log T) CCV (exponential improvement). For convex losses: O(√T) regret and O(√T) CCV (improved from O(√T log T)).

Conclusion: The proposed projection-based algorithm simultaneously achieves optimal regret and significantly improved constraint violation bounds for both strongly convex and convex loss settings, enabled by a geometric insight on self-contracted curves.

Abstract: We consider Constrained Online Convex Optimization (COCO) with adversarially chosen constraints. At each round, the learner chooses an action before observing the loss and constraint function for that round. The goal is to achieve small static regret against the best point satisfying all constraints while also controlling cumulative constraint violation ($\mathsf{CCV}$). For strongly convex losses, state-of-the-art algorithms achieve $O(\log T)$ regret and $O(\sqrt{T \log T})$ $\mathsf{CCV}.$ The corresponding best-known bounds for convex losses is $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret and $O(\sqrt{T} \log T)$ $\mathsf{CCV}$. In this paper, we give a simple projection-based algorithm that simultaneously achieves $O(\log T)$ regret and $O(\log T)$ $\mathsf{CCV}$ for strongly-convex losses, yielding an exponential improvement in the $\mathsf{CCV}$. For the convex losses, our algorithm improves the $\mathsf{CCV}$ to $O(\sqrt{T})$ while maintaining the optimal $O(\sqrt{T})$ regret. The key to our improvement is a recent geometric result for self-contracted curves, which may be of independent interest.

</details>


### [336] [Efficient Learning of Deep State Space Models via Importance Smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21108)
*John-Joseph Brady,Nikolas Nusken,Yunpeng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PVMC is a new training method for deep state space models that combines variational and sequential Monte Carlo approaches, achieving 10x speedup and state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training deep state space models (DSSMs) at scale remains difficult; existing auto-encoding and SMC-based methods each have limitations, with SMC approaches scaling poorly on modern hardware due to sequential forward passes.

Method: Parallel Variational Monte Carlo (PVMC) — a training method that bridges auto-encoding DSSMs (variational lower bound) and SMC-based DSSMs (backprop through SMC), enabling parallel computation for both discriminative and generative tasks.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art or better results on baseline experiments; trains 10× faster than the fastest competing SMC approach.

Conclusion: PVMC effectively bridges two distinct training paradigms for DSSMs, offering robust, scalable, and high-performance training suitable for both generative and discriminative tasks.

Abstract: Latent state space systems are ubiquitous in statistical modelling, arising naturally when a time series is observed through a noisy measurement function, however training deep state space models (DSSM) at scale remains difficult. Two largely distinct strategies and literatures have developed around the training of DSSMs. Firstly, auto-encoding DSSMs train generative DSSMs by optimising a variational lower bound. Secondly, DSSMs trained by back-propagating the outputs of a classical sequential Monte Carlo algorithm (SMC). Such approaches can train DSSMs for discriminative as well as generative tasks, however, due to the sequentiality of their forward pass, scale poorly on modern hardware. We propose a new training method \emph{parallel variational Monte Carlo} (PVMC) that bridges the gap between the paradigms, and can be used robustly to train DSSMs for both discriminative and generative tasks. Our method achieves state-of-the-art or better results on a set of baseline experiments and trains $10\times$ faster than the fastest competing SMC approach.

</details>


### [337] [A Unified Framework for Uncertainty-Aware Explainable Artificial Intelligence: A Case Study in Power Quality Disturbance Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21114)
*Yinsong Chen,Samson S. Yu,Zhong Li,Chee Peng Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes explanation distributions for Bayesian neural networks and proposes UA-RAO, a family of operators to summarize these distributions. Evaluated on power quality disturbance classification, it shows improved localization and reveals uncertainty patterns not captured by deterministic XAI methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-hoc XAI methods produce deterministic attribution maps, but Bayesian neural networks induce a distribution over explanations. Capturing the variability of this distribution is important for uncertainty-aware decision-making, yet existing methods do not formalize or summarize this distribution effectively.

Method: Formalizes the explanation distribution as the push-forward measure of the BNN posterior through any Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator. Proposes UA-RAO (uncertainty-aware relevance attribution operator), which summarizes the explanation distribution using mean, variance, coefficient of variation, quantiles, and set-theoretic aggregation. Provides theoretical support via Monte Carlo accessibility and Wasserstein approximation bounds.

Result: Deep ensembles with the mean UA-RAO improve localisation over the deterministic baseline on a 15-class PQD benchmark. Other UA-RAO summaries reveal uncertainty patterns absent from point-estimate attributions. Qualitative results on measured signals suggest these patterns generalise beyond synthetic training distribution.

Conclusion: The framework is domain-agnostic and can be applied to any BNN paired with a Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator, enabling uncertainty-aware XAI beyond deterministic attribution maps.

Abstract: Post-hoc explainable AI (XAI) methods typically produce deterministic attribution maps, whereas Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) induce a distribution over explanations. Capturing the variability of this distribution is important for uncertainty-aware decision-making. This paper formalises the \emph{explanation distribution} as the push-forward measure of the BNN posterior through any Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator. It further proposes the uncertainty-aware relevance attribution operator (UA-RAO), a general family of operators that summarises the explanation distribution using the mean, variance, coefficient of variation, quantiles, and set-theoretic aggregation measures. Theoretical support is provided through Monte Carlo accessibility and Wasserstein approximation bounds. The framework is evaluated on a 15-class power quality disturbance (PQD) classification benchmark, comparing three BNN approximations paired with three attribution operators using relevance mass accuracy and intersection-over-union as localisation metrics. Results show that deep ensembles with the mean UA-RAO improve localisation over the deterministic baseline, while other UA-RAO summaries reveal uncertainty patterns absent from point-estimate attributions. Qualitative results on measured signals further suggest that these patterns generalise beyond the synthetic training distribution. The framework is domain-agnostic and can be applied to any BNN paired with a Lipschitz-continuous attribution operator.

</details>


### [338] [Advantage Collapse in Group Relative Policy Optimization: Diagnosis and Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21125)
*Xixiang He,Qiyao Sun,Ao Cheng,Xingming Li,Xuanyu Ji,Hailun Lu,Runke Huang,Qingyong Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRPO suffers from advantage collapse due to homogeneous rewards; the authors propose ACR (a diagnostic metric) and AVSPO (an extension injecting virtual reward samples) that reduces collapse by 58-63% and improves accuracy by 4-6 percentage points across 0.5B-14B models on math reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO is prone to advantage collapse, where homogeneous group rewards (all correct or all incorrect) produce near-zero advantages and vanishing gradients, hindering effective training of LLMs.

Method: Introduce ACR (Advantage Collapse Rate) as the first diagnostic metric to quantify ineffective gradient batches, and propose AVSPO (Adaptive Virtual Sample Policy Optimization) that injects virtual reward samples guided by real-time ACR monitoring, without requiring additional model rollouts.

Result: ACR strongly predicts training stagnation and final performance across 0.5B-14B models. AVSPO reduces advantage collapse by 58-63% relative to GRPO and yields consistent accuracy gains of 4-6 percentage points across all model scales, with maintained generalization on out-of-domain tasks.

Conclusion: AVSPO effectively addresses GRPO's advantage collapse problem, providing a lightweight, roll-out-free solution that improves reasoning capabilities of LLMs across diverse model scales.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a prominent algorithm within the Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) framework, has achieved strong results in improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs). However, GRPO is prone to advantage collapse, a failure mode where homogeneous rewards within a group (e.g., all correct or all incorrect answers) yield near-zero advantages and vanishing gradients. To address this, we introduce the Advantage Collapse Rate (ACR), the first diagnostic metric quantifying the proportion of training batches with ineffective gradients. Across models from 0.5B to 14B parameters on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, we show that ACR strongly predicts training stagnation and final performance. We then propose Adaptive Virtual Sample Policy Optimization (AVSPO), a lightweight extension of GRPO that injects virtual reward samples, guided by real-time ACR monitoring, to enable learning from homogeneous groups without additional model rollouts. AVSPO reduces advantage collapse by 58-63% relative to GRPO and yields consistent accuracy gains of 4-6 percentage points across all model scales, while maintaining generalization on the evaluated out-of-domain task. Code and datasets are available at https://qingyonghu.github.io/AVSPO.

</details>


### [339] [Reasoning-Trace Collapse: Evaluating the Loss of Explicit Reasoning During Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21127)
*Lukas Twist,Helen Yannakoudakis,Jie M. Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fine-tuning reasoning models on instruction-response data (without reasoning traces) causes reasoning-trace collapse, where models produce correct answers but lose valid intermediate reasoning. The paper proposes structural evaluation metrics and shows loss-masking can mitigate this issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The mismatch between explicit reasoning training and downstream fine-tuning (which typically uses ordinary instruction-response data without reasoning traces) can degrade a model's reasoning capabilities. Existing answer-only metrics fail to detect this degradation.

Method: The authors develop a structural evaluation framework separating answer correctness from reasoning-trace validity, measuring valid, empty, missing, and truncated reasoning. They study four open-weight reasoning models under standard supervised fine-tuning and test loss-masking strategies.

Result: Standard supervised fine-tuning rapidly suppresses valid reasoning traces. Answer-only metrics obscure this failure: performance conditional on valid reasoning remains high but the rate of valid reasoning drops sharply. Loss-masking substantially mitigates collapse without needing teacher-generated reasoning traces.

Conclusion: Evaluations of fine-tuned reasoning models should report structural reasoning reliability metrics alongside final-answer performance, especially when adaptation data lacks explicit reasoning traces.

Abstract: Explicit reasoning models are trained to produce intermediate reasoning traces before final answers, but downstream fine-tuning is often performed on ordinary instruction-response data that contains no such traces. We show that this mismatch can induce reasoning-trace collapse: a fine-tuned model continues to produce plausible final answers while losing the structurally valid explicit reasoning traces that made it a reasoning model in the first place. We introduce a structural evaluation framework that separates answer correctness from reasoning-trace validity, measuring valid, empty, missing, and truncated reasoning alongside reasoning-conditioned task performance. Using this framework, we study four open-weight reasoning models and find that standard supervised fine-tuning can rapidly suppress valid reasoning traces, and that answer-only metrics can substantially obscure this failure: in several settings, performance conditional on valid reasoning remains high while the rate of valid reasoning falls sharply. We further show that simple loss-masking strategies can substantially mitigate collapse without requiring teacher-generated reasoning traces. These results suggest that evaluations of fine-tuned reasoning models should report structural reasoning reliability metrics in addition to final-answer performance, especially when adaptation data does not contain explicit reasoning traces.

</details>


### [340] [Behavior-Consistent Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21214)
*Marcel Hussing,Liv G. d'Aliberti,Claas Voelcker,Benjamin Eysenbach,Eric Eaton*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper formalizes behavior-consistent RL to reduce cross-run policy divergence, proposes QED (Q-value Expectile Disagreement) - a state-dependent temperature schedule using double-critic disagreement as a proxy, and demonstrates two orders of magnitude reduction in across-run divergence across 18 continuous-control tasks without performance sacrifice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL exhibits high variance across training runs, causing unreliable performance and hindering real-world deployment. The authors aim to address cross-run policy divergence by obtaining policies that are both high-performing and distributionally similar across runs.

Method: The authors formalize behavior-consistent RL and prove that for Boltzmann policies, choosing temperature proportional to Q-function disagreement bounds pairwise KL divergence. They propose QED (Q-value Expectile Disagreement), a state-dependent temperature schedule that uses double-critic disagreement as a single-run proxy for cross-run disagreement.

Result: Across 18 continuous-control tasks, QED reduces across-run divergence by two orders of magnitude without sacrificing performance, resulting in considerable reduction in return variance at modest sample-efficiency costs.

Conclusion: Maximum-entropy RL with appropriate temperature tuning (specifically QED) can effectively control cross-run behavioral divergence while maintaining performance, addressing the reliability challenge in RL deployment.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) often exhibits high variance across training runs, leading to unreliable performance and posing a major challenge to deployment in real-world domains. In this work, we address the challenge of cross-run policy divergence by formalizing the problem of behavior-consistent RL, where the objective is to obtain policies that are both high-performing and distributionally similar across training runs. Our key observation is that maximum-entropy RL provides a direct mechanism for controlling behavioral divergence by anchoring runs to a common (uniform) prior. We prove that, for Boltzmann policies, choosing the temperature proportional to $Q$-function disagreement bounds the pairwise KL divergence between the induced policies. However, we also show that naïvely increasing entropy might impair policy optimization while amplifying off-policy error. Building upon these observations, we propose $Q$-value Expectile Disagreement (QED), a state-dependent temperature schedule that uses double-critic disagreement as a single-run proxy for cross-run disagreement. Empirically, we demonstrate that across 18 continuous-control tasks, QED reduces across-run divergence by two orders of magnitude without sacrificing performance, resulting in a considerable reduction in return variance at modest sample-efficiency costs.

</details>


### [341] [SMoA: Spectrum Modulation Adapter for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21147)
*Yongkang Liu,Xing Li,Mengjie Zhao,Shanru Zhang,Zijing Wang,Qian Li,Shi Feng,Feiliang Ren,Daling Wang,Hinrich Schütze*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMoA is a novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that uses spectrum modulation and block-wise low-rank adaptation to achieve broader spectral coverage than LoRA under smaller parameter budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA faces a dilemma: increasing rank improves performance by preserving more principal singular directions, but also increases computational cost. The authors aim to enlarge accessible spectrum-aware updates under a smaller parameter budget.

Method: SMoA partitions the layer into multiple aligned spectral blocks and applies one in-block Hadamard-modulated low-rank branch to each diagonal block, yielding broader coverage of pretrained spectral directions.

Result: SMoA improves average performance in lower-budget settings over LoRA and competitive LoRA-style baselines, supported by both theoretical analysis and empirical results on multiple tasks.

Conclusion: SMoA effectively overcomes the LoRA dilemma by achieving broader spectral coverage with fewer parameters, making it a superior PEFT method for resource-constrained fine-tuning of large language models.

Abstract: As the number of model parameters increases, parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) has become the go-to choice for tailoring pre-trained large language models. Low-rank Adaptation (LoRA) uses a low-rank update method to simulate full parameter fine-tuning, which is widely used to reduce resource requirements. However, decreasing the rank encounters challenges with limited representational capacity. Theory suggests that LoRA fine-tuning with rank r converges toward the top r singular values of the pre-trained weight matrix. As the rank increases, more principal singular directions are preserved, which generally improves the model's performance. However, a larger rank also introduces more trainable parameters, leading to higher computational cost. To overcome this dilemma, we propose SMoA, a \textbf{S}pectrum \textbf{Mo}dulation \textbf{A}dapter that enlarges the accessible family of spectrum-aware updates under a smaller parameter budget. SMoA partitions the layer into multiple aligned spectral blocks and applies one in-block Hadamard-modulated low-rank branch to each diagonal block, yielding broader coverage of pretrained spectral directions. We provide theoretical analysis and empirical results on multiple tasks. In our experiments, SMoA improves average performance in the current lower-budget setting over LoRA and competitive LoRA-style baselines.

</details>


### [342] [PREFINE: Preference-Based Implicit Reward and Cost Fine-Tuning for Safety Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21225)
*Richa Verma,Bavish Kulur,Sanjay Chawla,Balaraman Ravindran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PREFINE adapts DPO to fine-tune pre-trained RL policies for safety using trajectory-level preference data, reducing violations by over 60% while maintaining rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Making pre-trained RL policies safety-aware without retraining from scratch, especially when costs are given as preferences rather than numerical values.

Method: PREFINE adapts Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to sequential decision making by constructing policy-sampled counterfactual trajectories and jointly optimizing reward retention and safety alignment.

Result: Reduces constraint violations and catastrophic failures by over 60% while maintaining original reward behavior, with improved data and computational efficiency over offline RL or imitation learning.

Conclusion: PREFINE bridges preference alignment and safe policy adaptation in continuous domains, offering an efficient approach to safety-aware fine-tuning of pre-trained RL policies.

Abstract: We address the problem of making a pre-trained reinforcement learning (RL) policy safety-aware by incorporating cost constraints without retraining it from scratch. While costs could be numerically encoded, we assume a more general setting is when costs are provided as preferences. Given a reward-optimized policy and a small dataset of preferred (low-cost) and dispreferred (high-cost) trajectories, our goal is to fine-tune the policy to generate low-cost behaviors while retaining high rewards. Unlike standard RLHF in language models, where preferences are defined over responses to the same prompt, our setting involves trajectory-level preferences in continuous control environments. We introduce PREFINE: Preference-based Implicit Reward and Cost Fine-Tuning for Safety Alignment which is a preference-based fine-tuning method that adapts Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), which is now widely used for LLM fine-tuning, to the sequential decision making setting. PREFINE constructs policy-sampled counterfactual trajectories to establish meaningful preference contrasts and jointly optimizes for reward retention and safety alignment. Empirically, PREFINE reduces constraint violations and catastrophic failures by over 60% while maintaining original reward behavior. PREFINE produces policies that achieve low-cost, high-reward performance with significantly improved data and computational efficiency compared to full offline RL or imitation learning, bridging preference alignment and safe policy adaptation in continuous domains.

</details>


### [343] [Learning First Integrals via Backward-Generated Data and Guided Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21160)
*Jingfeng Zhong,Zhengxiang Liu,Zhijie Wang,Shuai Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FISolver is an LLM-based solver that discovers first integrals in dynamical systems using a "Backward Generation" algorithm to create training data, followed by supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, outperforming larger models and commercial solvers at lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing symbolic computation tools and LLMs struggle to discover first integrals due to scarce high-quality training data and reliance on mathematical intuition, creating a critical bottleneck in automated conservation law discovery.

Method: (1) A "Backward Generation" algorithm generates large-scale datasets by deriving differential equations from sampled first integrals; (2) Supervised fine-tuning on a compact mathematical model; (3) Reinforcement learning with Levenshtein Distance-based shaped reward; (4) Data synthesis and blending strategies for adaptation to difficult problem families.

Result: FISolver significantly outperforms larger mathematical LLMs and commercial solvers like Mathematica on challenging benchmarks, while requiring substantially lower computational cost.

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates a promising data-driven route for automated discovery of first integrals, effectively overcoming data scarcity via backward generation and reinforcement learning.

Abstract: The discovery of first integrals is of fundamental scientific importance for understanding conservation laws in dynamical systems. However, existing symbolic computation tools and Large Language Models (LLMs) remain limited on this task because high-quality training data are scarce and successful solutions often depend on mathematical intuition. This paper presents FISolver, an LLM-based solver developed to address this challenge. First, we introduce a "Backward Generation" algorithm that systematically builds large-scale datasets of (differential equation, first integral) pairs by deriving differential equations from sampled integrals, thereby alleviating the data scarcity bottleneck. Second, we apply supervised fine-tuning to a compact mathematical model and further improve its performance through reinforcement learning with a Levenshtein Distance-based shaped reward. In addition, we design data synthesis and blending strategies that support effective adaptation to difficult problem families from sparse examples. Experiments show that FISolver, while requiring substantially lower computational cost, significantly outperforms larger mathematical LLMs and commercial solvers such as Mathematica on challenging benchmarks, indicating a new data-driven route for automated discovery of first integrals.

</details>


### [344] [OCTOPUS: Optimized KV Cache for Transformers via Octahedral Parametrization Under optimal Squared error quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21226)
*Mark Boss,Vikram Voleti,Simon Donné,Shimon Vainer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OCTOPUS is a new KV cache compression codec that jointly quantizes rotated coordinate triplets using an octahedral parameterization, achieving state-of-the-art compression across modalities with a fused implementation that adds no decode-time overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reducing memory bandwidth and footprint of KV cache in long-context autoregressive inference, which dominates performance bottlenecks.

Method: Joint quantization of rotated coordinate triplets via octahedral parameterization, mapping triplet directions to a square, then applying Lloyd-Max quantization to the two resulting coordinates and norm against implementation-matched marginals, with per-triplet squared error optimization for non-uniform bit allocation.

Result: Matches or beats all prior rotation codecs across text, video, and audio at every reported bit width and metric, with growing lead at extreme compression; fused Triton implementation adds no decode-time bandwidth or latency.

Conclusion: OCTOPUS advances rotation-preconditioned KV compression through structured triplet quantization, offering a data-oblivious, online, deterministic codec with practical inference speed benefits.

Abstract: The key-value (KV) cache dominates memory bandwidth and footprint in long-context autoregressive inference. Recent rotation-preconditioned codecs (TurboQuant, PolarQuant) show that a structured random rotation followed by a per-coordinate scalar quantizer matched to an analytically tractable marginal is a near-optimal recipe for KV compression. OCTOPUS advances this paradigm through joint quantization of rotated coordinate triplets. Each triplet's direction is mapped to a square via an octahedral parameterization, and the two resulting coordinates and the triplet norm are Lloyd-Max quantized against implementation-matched marginals. Optimizing the per-triplet squared error gives a strictly non-uniform bit allocation depending only on the total dimensionality of the keys. We find the finite-dimensional quality optimum with sweeps to be constant on every real decoder we test. The codec is data-oblivious, online, and deterministic given a seed. Across text, video, and audio, OCTOPUS matches or beats every prior rotation codec at every reported bit width and metric, with a lead that grows as bits drop for extreme compression. Furthermore, a fused Triton implementation reconstructs keys on the fly without materializing the uncompressed key, so the codec adds no decode-time bandwidth or latency over the existing dequantization. Project Page: https://octopus-quant.github.io/

</details>


### [345] [Q-SYNTH: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Adversarial Augmentation for Imbalanced Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21164)
*Adam Innan,Mansour El Alami,Nouhaila Innan,Muhammad Shafique,Mohamed Bennai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Q-SYNTH is a hybrid quantum-classical GAN that generates synthetic fraud samples to address class imbalance in credit card fraud detection, offering a balanced compromise between statistical fidelity and downstream detection performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extreme class imbalance in credit card fraud detection biases supervised learners toward the legitimate class, resulting in poor fraud-class recall and F1-score despite high overall accuracy.

Method: Q-SYNTH combines a parameterized quantum circuit (generator) with a classical neural network (discriminator) in a GAN framework to synthesize minority-class fraud samples for tabular data.

Result: Q-SYNTH reduces marginal distribution mismatch compared to a classical GAN baseline and maintains competitive downstream fraud-detection performance, though SMOTE achieves the best feature-wise similarity and classical GAN attains the highest downstream performance in some settings.

Conclusion: Q-SYNTH offers a favorable compromise between distributional fidelity and downstream performance, demonstrating the feasibility of hybrid quantum augmentation for imbalanced fraud detection.

Abstract: Credit card fraud detection is fundamentally challenged by extreme class imbalance, where fraudulent transactions are rare yet operationally critical. This imbalance often biases supervised learners toward the legitimate class, leading to high overall accuracy but weaker fraud-class recall and F1-score. This paper introduces Q-SYNTH, a hybrid classical--quantum generative adversarial framework in which a parameterized quantum circuit serves as the generator and a classical neural network serves as the discriminator. Q-SYNTH is designed for minority-class fraud synthesis in tabular data and is evaluated along two dimensions: statistical fidelity to real fraud samples and downstream performance for fraud detection. To this end, generated samples are assessed using distributional similarity measures based on Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistics and Wasserstein distances, real-vs-synthetic detectability measured by AUC-ROC, and downstream classification performance across both quantum and classical classifiers. Under the reported protocol, Q-SYNTH reduces marginal distribution mismatch relative to a classical GAN baseline while maintaining competitive downstream fraud-detection performance. Although SMOTE achieves the strongest feature-wise similarity and the classical GAN attains the highest downstream performance in several settings, Q-SYNTH offers a favorable compromise between distributional fidelity and downstream performance, supporting the feasibility of hybrid quantum augmentation for imbalanced fraud detection.

</details>


### [346] [ChunkFT: Byte-Streamed Optimization for Memory-Efficient Full Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21177)
*Yongkang Liu,Zijing Wang,Mengjie Zhao,Ercong Nie,Mingyang Wang,Qian Li,Feiliang Ren,Shi Feng,Daling Wang,Hinrich Schütze*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ChunkFT is a memory-efficient fine-tuning framework that enables full-parameter fine-tuning by dynamically activating working sets, achieving comparable or better performance than standard fine-tuning while using drastically less GPU memory (e.g., 13.72GB for 7B models).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the massive GPU memory requirements of full-parameter fine-tuning large language models, enabling fine-tuning of models like Llama 3-70B on limited hardware (e.g., a single RTX 4090).

Method: ChunkFT reformulates full-parameter fine-tuning around a dynamically activated working set, computing gradients for arbitrary sub-tensors without modifying the network architecture, thus avoiding standard dense gradient computation.

Result: Fine-tuned Llama 3-8B on a single RTX 4090 (24GB) and Llama 3-70B on 2× H800 (80GB). Full fine-tuning of a 7B model with 1K input used only 13.72GB GPU memory. Outperformed existing memory-efficient baselines on language understanding, math reasoning, and MT-Bench. Achieved performance comparable to or exceeding standard full-parameter fine-tuning.

Conclusion: ChunkFT is an effective memory-efficient fine-tuning framework that bridges the gap between parameter-efficient and full-parameter fine-tuning, making large model fine-tuning accessible on limited hardware without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: This work presents \textsc{ChunkFT}, a memory-efficient fine-tuning framework that reformulates full-parameter fine-tuning around a dynamically activated working set. \textsc{ChunkFT} enables gradient computation for arbitrary sub-tensors without modifying the network architecture, providing an algorithmic foundation for optimizing arbitrary sub-networks while avoiding standard dense gradient computation. We provide a theoretical convergence analysis of \textsc{ChunkFT} in the deterministic setting. Empirically, we apply \textsc{ChunkFT} to fine-tune Llama 3-8B and Llama 3-70B using a single RTX 4090-24GB GPU and 2$\times$ H800-80GB GPUs, respectively. Full-parameter fine-tuning of a 7B model with a 1K input length requires only 13.72GB of GPU memory. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of \textsc{ChunkFT} in memory usage, running time, and optimization quality. Moreover, downstream evaluations on language understanding, mathematical reasoning, and MT-Bench show that \textsc{ChunkFT} consistently outperforms existing memory-efficient baselines. Notably, \textsc{ChunkFT} achieves performance comparable to, and in some cases exceeding, full-parameter fine-tuning. Our repository is on https://github.com/misonsky/chunk.

</details>


### [347] [APEX: Autonomous Policy Exploration for Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21240)
*Yibo Li,Jiashuo Yang,Zhi Zheng,Zhiyuan Hu,Yuan Sui,Shizun Wang,Yufei He,Bryan Hooi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: APEX addresses exploration collapse in self-evolving LLM agents by maintaining an explicit strategy space (DAG-based strategy map) with Fork Discovery and Policy Selection, achieving strong results on text-adventure games and web tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-evolving agents suffer from exploration collapse—as memory grows, behavior concentrates on familiar high-reward routines, reducing discovery of better alternatives.

Method: APEX builds a strategy map (a DAG of milestones with dependency edges). Fork Discovery expands the map with evidence-grounded unexplored directions, and Policy Selection balances exploration and exploitation during planning.

Result: Outperforms all baselines on nine Jericho text-adventure games and WebArena; ablations validate each component's contribution and show robustness.

Conclusion: APEX demonstrates sustained exploration and outperforms baselines on Jericho games and WebArena, with ablation studies confirming each component's contribution.

Abstract: LLM agents have shown strong performance across a wide range of complex tasks, including interactive environments that require long-horizon decision making. But these agents cannot learn on the fly at test time. Self-evolving agents address this by accumulating memory and reflection across episodes rather than requiring model-weight updates. However, these agents often suffer from exploration collapse: as memory grows, behavior concentrates around familiar high-reward routines, reducing the chance of discovering better alternatives. To address this problem, we propose Autonomous Policy EXploration (APEX), which builds and maintains an explicit strategy space through a strategy map-a directed acyclic graph of milestones with prerequisite dependency edges. In APEX, Fork Discovery expands the map with evidence-grounded unexplored directions, while Policy Selection balances exploration and exploitation during planning. Evaluated on nine Jericho text-adventure games and WebArena, a realistic web interaction benchmark, APEX outperforms all baselines. Extensive ablations validate each component's contribution and demonstrate robustness across diverse settings, demonstrating APEX's effectiveness for sustained exploration in self-evolving agents.

</details>


### [348] [Domain-Adaptable Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation with Dense Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21180)
*Erfan Aghadavoodi Jolfaei,Daniel Maninger,Abhinav Anand,Mert Tiftikci,Mira Mezini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a reinforcement learning framework using PPO to fine-tune LLMs for code generation, with a customizable execution-aware reward that captures syntax, correctness, style, security, and simulator executability. It achieves 19% absolute improvement on MBPP and 51% reduction in execution failures on RoboEval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs for code generation lack guarantees for correctness, quality, safety, and domain-specific constraints (e.g., robotics). Existing methods struggle to align generated code with execution-aware and domain-specific requirements.

Method: A reinforcement learning framework fine-tuning pre-trained LLMs via PPO, with a customizable execution-aware reward formula covering syntax, functional correctness, code style, security, and simulator executability. A token-level reward mapping mechanism assigns credits from execution outcomes to individual tokens.

Result: 19% absolute pass@1 improvement on MBPP, 51% reduction in execution failures on RoboEval, demonstrating substantial gains in functional correctness and simulator executability.

Conclusion: Structured reinforcement learning can effectively align LLMs to correct program generation and domain-specific requirements, bridging the gap between general-purpose code generation and specialized domains like robotics.

Abstract: Large language models show strong potential for automated code generation, but lack guarantees for correctness, quality, safety, and domain-specific constraints. For instance in robotics, where code generation is increasingly being used for planning and executing actions, awareness of the environment and physical constraints is critical. To facilitate the adaption of code-generating LLMs to diverse requirements, including domain-specific ones, we present a reinforcement learning framework that fine-tunes pre-trained LLMs using proximal policy optimization. Our customizable execution-aware reward formula captures and optimizes syntax, functional correctness, code style, security, and simulator executability. A token-level reward mapping mechanism enables effective credit assignment from execution outcomes to generated tokens. The framework is evaluated on general-purpose code generation (MBPP/MBPP+) and robotic program synthesis (RoboEval). The results show substantial improvements in functional correctness and simulator executability, including an absolute pass@1 increase of 19% on MBPP and a reduction in execution failures by 51% on RoboEval.
