This can be inefficient because target examples may differ in their relevance to the current model: examples closer to the model's current behavior provide more actionable guidance than those farther away. We propose PRISM (PReference-aware Influence-function-based Data Selection Method for Efficient Fine-Tuning), which uses the current model's preference to weight target examples and construct a preference-aware target representation. PRISM then scores candidate training samples by their alignment with this representation, concentrating the data budget on samples more likely to move the model toward the target behavior. Theoretical analysis shows that this preference weighting yields a more effective first-order direction for increasing target-behavior preference. Experiments across model families and scales show that PRISM improves both efficient fine-tuning and safety-oriented SFT repair, demonstrating that precise target-behavior characterization is key to budget-efficient data selection.

</details>


### [377] [Adaptive Signal Resuscitation: Channel-wise Post-Pruning Repair for Sparse Vision Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21426)
*Qishi Zhan,Ziheng Chen,Minxuan Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Adaptive Signal Resuscitation (ASR), a training-free, channel-wise post-pruning repair method that addresses accuracy collapse in high-sparsity neural networks by matching repair granularity to damage granularity, using variance-matching corrections with a shrinkage rule.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-shot magnitude pruning causes severe accuracy collapse in high-sparsity regimes. Existing layer-wise activation repair methods fail because they apply a single correction to the whole layer, over-amplifying damaged channels while trying to restore layer-level signal.

Method: ASR estimates a variance-matching correction for each output channel and stabilizes it with a data-driven shrinkage rule, suppressing unreliable corrections for damaged channels while preserving corrections for healthier ones. Applied before BatchNorm recalibration, it requires only forward passes on a small calibration set with no retraining.

Result: On ResNet-50 at 90% sparsity, ASR recovers 55.6% top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-10 vs 41.0% for layer-wise repair and 28.0% for BatchNorm-only recalibration. Improvements are consistent across three datasets, four architectures, and both unstructured/structured sparsity settings.

Conclusion: Granularity mismatch is a key failure mode in post-pruning repair. Channel-wise repair with shrinkage (ASR) outperforms layer-wise methods, especially in high-sparsity regimes. Naive channel-wise variance matching alone is insufficient; shrinkage is crucial for stabilization.

Abstract: One-shot magnitude pruning can cause severe accuracy collapse in the high-sparsity regime, even when the pruning mask preserves the largest weights. We argue that this failure reflects a granularity mismatch in post-pruning repair. Under global magnitude pruning, nearly collapsed channels can coexist with channels that retain informative activation variance within the same layer. Existing layer-wise activation repair methods apply a single correction to the whole layer, and can therefore over-amplify damaged channels while trying to restore the layer-level signal. We propose Adaptive Signal Resuscitation (ASR), a training-free channel-wise repair method that matches the granularity of repair to the granularity of damage. ASR estimates a variance-matching correction for each output channel and stabilizes it with a data-driven shrinkage rule, suppressing unreliable corrections for channels with weak post-pruning signal while preserving corrections for healthier channels. Applied before BatchNorm recalibration, ASR requires only forward passes on a small calibration set and no retraining. Across three datasets, four convolutional architectures, and both unstructured and structured sparsity settings, ASR generally improves over layer-wise repair, with the clearest gains in high-sparsity regimes. On ResNet-50 at 90% sparsity, ASR recovers 55.6% top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-10, compared with 41.0% for layer-wise repair and 28.0% for BatchNorm-only recalibration. Ablations show that naive channel-wise variance matching is insufficient, and that shrinkage stabilizes post-pruning repair.

</details>


### [378] [Polynomial-Time Robust Multiclass Linear Classification under Gaussian Marginals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21428)
*Ilias Diakonikolas,Giannis Iakovidis,Mingchen Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies agnostic learning of multiclass linear classifiers under Gaussian distribution, revealing that the standard perceptron algorithm fails for k≥3, and proposes efficient pairwise improper-learning and localization-based frameworks achieving dimension-independent error guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While binary (k=2) linear classifiers have well-developed algorithmic theory, much less is known for k≥3. Prior robust algorithms for k=3 incur exponential dependence on inverse accuracy. The goal is to design fully polynomial-time learners with dimension-independent error guarantees for multiclass settings.

Method: 1) Analyzing the standard multiclass perceptron algorithm to show it requires super-polynomially many samples/updates even with clean labels; 2) Developing a pairwise improper-learning framework achieving error Õ(k^{3/2}√opt)+ε; 3) Designing a sharper localization-based framework achieving error O(opt)+ε for k=3 and poly(k)opt+ε for regular k-class classifiers.

Result: The multiclass perceptron algorithm fails (super-polynomial sample/update complexity) for k≥3. The pairwise framework yields error Õ(k^{3/2}√opt)+ε. The localization framework achieves O(opt)+ε for k=3 and poly(k)opt+ε for regular cases, with polynomial time and dimension-independent guarantees.

Conclusion: The work reveals a fundamental hardness distinction between binary and multiclass linear classification, and provides the first efficient robust learners for multiclass settings with dimension-independent guarantees, overcoming exponential barriers in prior work.

Abstract: We study the task of agnostic learning of multiclass linear classifiers under the Gaussian distribution. Given labeled examples $(x, y)$ from a distribution over $\mathbb{R}^d \times [k]$, with Gaussian $x$-marginal, the goal is to output a hypothesis whose error is comparable to that of the best $k$-class linear classifier. While the binary case $k=2$ has a well-developed algorithmic theory, much less is known for $k \ge 3$. Even for $k=3$, prior robust algorithms incur exponential dependence on the inverse of the desired accuracy in both complexity and representation size. In this work, we develop new structural results for multiclass linear classifiers and use them to design fully polynomial-time robust learners with dimension-independent error guarantees. Our first result shows that the standard multiclass perceptron algorithm requires super-polynomially many samples and updates, even with clean labels and Gaussian marginals, revealing a basic obstruction absent in the binary case. Our main positive result is a pairwise improper-learning framework which yields an efficient learner with error $\widetilde O(k^{3/2}\sqrt{\mathrm{opt}})+ε$ for general $k$. Additionally, we develop a sharper localization-based framework which leads to error $O(\mathrm{opt})+ε$ for $k=3$, and error $\mathrm{poly}(k)\mathrm{opt}+ε$ for geometrically regular $k$-class linear classifiers.

</details>


### [379] [Gaussian Sheaf Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21435)
*André Ribeiro,Ana Luiza Tenório,Tiago da Silva,Diego Mesquita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework (GSNNs) that adapts graph neural networks to handle Gaussian-distributed node features by using sheaf theory to preserve geometric structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GNNs treat node features as vectors, but when features are probability distributions (e.g., Gaussians with means and covariances), naive concatenation discards the geometric and algebraic structure. A principled method is needed to incorporate these inductive biases.

Method: Proposes Gaussian Sheaf Neural Networks (GSNNs), building on cellular sheaf theory to define a new Laplacian operator that generalizes the sheaf Laplacian for Gaussian-distributed features while preserving key properties.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world data demonstrate the practical relevance and effectiveness of GSNNs compared to standard approaches.

Conclusion: GSNNs provide a principled way to handle distribution-valued node features in graph learning, preserving the inherent geometric structure of Gaussians.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become the de facto standard for learning on relational data. While traditional GNNs' message passing is well suited for vector-valued node features, there are cases in which node features are better represented by probability distributions than real vectors. Concretely, when node features are Gaussians, characterized by a mean and a covariance matrix, naively concatenating their parameters into a single vector and applying standard message passing discards the geometric and algebraic structure that governs means and covariances. We propose Gaussian Sheaf Neural Networks (GSNNs), a principled framework that incorporates these inductive biases into graph-based learning. Building on the theory of cellular sheaves, we derive a new Laplacian operator that generalizes the sheaf Laplacian to this setting and preserves its key properties. We complement our theoretical contributions with experiments on synthetic and real-world data that illustrate the practical relevance of GSNNs.

</details>


### [380] [Mitigating Label Bias with Interpretable Rubric Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21455)
*Calvin Isley,Johann D. Gaebler,Sharad Goel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes rubric embeddings—a representation framework using expert-defined criteria instead of black-box embeddings—to train models on biased historical labels (e.g., hiring, admissions), showing theoretically and empirically that this approach reduces group disparities while improving cohort quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Statistical decision algorithms are deployed in domains where ground-truth labels are hard to obtain (e.g., hiring, admissions, content moderation). Models trained on historical human evaluations may inherit unjust biases if past decisions favored certain groups, motivating the need for methods that guard against biased proxy signals.

Method: The method replaces standard black-box embeddings with "rubric embeddings"—features derived from expert-defined criteria aligned with the underlying construct of interest. Predictions are anchored to semantically meaningful dimensions rather than biased proxy signals.

Result: Empirical evaluation on a novel dataset of applications to a large master's program shows that models trained on rubric embeddings reduce group disparities while improving measures of cohort quality. Theoretical evidence also supports that the approach mitigates label bias under plausible conditions.

Conclusion: Basing predictions on interpretable, domain-grounded representations (rubric embeddings) offers a practical approach to learning in the presence of biased labels, reducing bias without sacrificing predictive utility.

Abstract: Statistical decision algorithms are increasingly deployed in domains where ground-truth labels are hard to obtain, such as hiring, university admissions, and content moderation. In these settings, models are typically trained on historical human evaluations -- for example, using past hiring decisions as a proxy for true applicant quality. However, if past evaluations unjustly favor certain groups, models trained on these labels may inherit those biases. To address this problem, we propose basing predictions on rubric embeddings, a representation framework that replaces standard black-box embeddings with features derived from expert-defined criteria that align with the underlying construct of interest. By anchoring predictions to semantically meaningful dimensions, this approach guards against biased proxy signals. We provide both theoretical and empirical evidence that rubric embeddings mitigate label bias under plausible conditions. Empirically, we evaluate our method on a novel dataset of applications to a large master's program. We find that models trained on rubric embeddings reduce group disparities while improving measures of cohort quality. Our results suggest that basing predictions on interpretable, domain-grounded representations offers a practical approach to learning in the presence of biased labels.

</details>


### [381] [A Machine Learning Framework for Weighted Least Squares GNSS Positioning based on Activation Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21461)
*Pin-Hsun Lee,Harry Leib*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a machine learning-based weighted least squares (WLS) algorithm using activation functions to improve GNSS positioning accuracy in urban canyons by identifying and down-weighting poor-quality signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GNSS signals in urban canyons suffer from obstructions, NLOS reception, and multipath effects, causing severe positioning errors. Multi-constellation GNSS alone is insufficient because including degraded signals can worsen accuracy.

Method: The method uses ensemble learning with signal quality indicators to assign quality scores, then applies activation functions (e.g., sigmoid) to transform scores into weights for WLS positioning.

Result: Experiments on real-world Hong Kong and Tokyo datasets show sigmoid activation functions consistently improve accuracy across different ML algorithms and GNSS configurations, with strong geographical transferability.

Conclusion: The proposed framework significantly reduces positioning errors in both single- and multi-constellation scenarios and performs comparably when trained on data from other similarly urbanized regions.

Abstract: Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS) are widely used to provide position, velocity, and timing (PVT) information for various applications, including transportation, location-based communication services, and intelligent agriculture. In urban canyons, high-rise buildings and narrow streets can cause signal obstruction, non-line-of-sight (NLOS) reception, and multipath effects that introduce errors in GNSS pseudorange measurements. Although multi-constellations GNSS effectively increase the number of available satellites, the inclusion of degraded signals can lead to severe positioning errors. This study proposes a machine learning framework for the weighted least squares (WLS) algorithm incorporating activation functions to enhance positioning accuracy. Several signal quality indicators are employed as training features for ensemble learning algorithms to identify poor quality signals by providing quality scores. Then, activation functions are employed to transform the machine learning predicted scores to appropriate weights for WLS positioning. To evaluate the performance of our approach, experiments are conducted using real-world datasets from Hong Kong and Tokyo urban areas. Comparative analysis of activation functions reveals that sigmoid functions consistently yield the greatest improvements with different machine learning algorithms and GNSS constellation configurations. The proposed algorithm demonstrates substantial reductions in positioning errors for both single- and multiconstellation scenarios. Furthermore, our results indicate that the proposed algorithm exhibits strong geographical transferability. The proposed algorithm maintains comparable level of performance when trained on data from other regions with similar levels of urbanization.

</details>


### [382] [DelTA: Discriminative Token Credit Assignment for Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21467)
*Kaiyi Zhang,Wei Wu,Yankai Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces DelTA, a discriminative token credit assignment method for RLVR that improves reasoning in LLMs by reshaping policy-gradient updates to amplify discriminative token directions and downweight shared patterns like formatting tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Response-level RLVR rewards poorly translate into token-level probability changes because the implicit discriminator in policy-gradient updates is dominated by shared high-frequency patterns (e.g., formatting tokens), diluting sparse yet discriminative directions that better distinguish high-reward from low-reward responses.

Method: DelTA estimates token coefficients to amplify side-specific token-gradient directions and downweight shared/weakly discriminative ones, then reweights a self-normalized RLVR surrogate to make side-wise centroids more contrastive.

Result: DelTA outperforms strongest same-scale baselines by 3.26 points on Qwen3-8B-Base and 2.62 points on Qwen3-14B-Base across seven math benchmarks, with additional gains on code generation, different backbones, and out-of-domain evaluations.

Conclusion: The discriminator view reveals limitations of standard RLVR, and DelTA's discriminative token credit assignment effectively addresses these issues, showing strong generalization across tasks and backbones.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a central technique for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. Despite its effectiveness, how response-level rewards translate into token-level probability changes remains poorly understood. We introduce a discriminator view of RLVR updates, showing that the policy-gradient update direction implicitly acts as a linear discriminator over token-gradient vectors and thereby determines which token probabilities are increased or decreased during learning. Under standard sequence-level RLVR, this discriminator is constructed from positive- and negative-side centroids formed by advantage-weighted averaging of token-gradient vectors. However, such centroid construction can be dominated by shared high-frequency patterns, such as formatting tokens, diluting sparse yet discriminative directions that better distinguish high-reward responses from low-reward ones. To address this limitation, we propose $\textbf{DelTA}$, a discriminative token credit assignment method that estimates token coefficients to amplify side-specific token-gradient directions and downweight shared or weakly discriminative ones. These coefficients reweight a self-normalized RLVR surrogate, making the effective side-wise centroids more contrastive and thereby reshaping the RLVR update direction. On seven mathematical benchmarks, DelTA outperforms the strongest same-scale baselines by 3.26 and 2.62 average points on Qwen3-8B-Base and Qwen3-14B-Base, respectively. Additional results on code generation, a different backbone, and out-of-domain evaluations further demonstrate the generalization ability of DelTA.

</details>


### [383] [You Only Need Minimal RLVR Training: Extrapolating LLMs via Rank-1 Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21468)
*Zhepei Wei,Xinyu Zhu,Wei-Lin Chen,Chengsong Huang,Jiaxin Huang,Yu Meng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR weight trajectories are extremely low-rank and highly predictable; RELEX extrapolates future checkpoints via linear regression on a rank-1 subspace, matching RLVR performance using as few as 15% of training steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the underlying geometry of RLVR parameter trajectories in LLMs, which remains underexplored despite the paradigm's growing dominance.

Method: Proposes RELEX: estimates rank-1 subspace from a short observation window of RLVR checkpoints, then extrapolates future checkpoints via linear regression on that subspace.

Result: RELEX matches/exceeds RLVR performance on in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks with as few as 15% of training steps; can extrapolate 10-20x beyond observed prefix (e.g., 50 steps → 1000 steps).

Conclusion: RLVR trajectories are highly predictable (rank-1, near-linear); RELEX succeeds via a "denoising" effect that discards stochastic optimization noise; minimal sufficiency is confirmed (neither higher rank nor non-linear models improve extrapolation).

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become a dominant paradigm for improving reasoning in large language models (LLMs), yet the underlying geometry of the resulting parameter trajectories remains underexplored. In this work, we demonstrate that RLVR weight trajectories are extremely low-rank and highly predictable. Specifically, we find that the majority of downstream performance gains are captured by a rank-1 approximation of the parameter deltas, where the magnitude of this projection evolves near-linearly with training steps. Motivated by this, we propose a simple and compute-efficient method RELEX (REinforcement Learning EXtrapolation), which estimates the rank-1 subspace from a short observation window and extrapolates future checkpoints via linear regression, with no learned model required. Across three models (i.e., Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B, Qwen3-4B-Base, and Qwen3-8B-Base), RELEX produces checkpoints that match or exceed RLVR performance on both in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, requiring as few as 15% steps of full RLVR training. Remarkably, RELEX is able to extrapolate far beyond the observation window at no training cost, predicting checkpoints up to 10-20$\times$ beyond the observed prefix with continued improvement (e.g., observe only the first 50 steps and extrapolate to 1000 steps). Our ablation analysis confirms the minimalist sufficiency of RELEX: neither increasing the subspace rank nor employing non-linear modeling yields further gains in extrapolation. Finally, we show that RELEX's success stems from a "denoising" effect: by projecting updates onto the rank-1 subspace, the model discards stochastic optimization noise that would otherwise degrade performance during extrapolation. Our code is available at https://github.com/weizhepei/RELEX.

</details>


### [384] [Is Fixing Schema Graphs Necessary? Full-Resolution Graph Structure Learning for Relational Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21475)
*Yi Huang,Qingyun Sun,Jia Li,Xingcheng Fu,Jianxin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FROG proposes a learnable framework for relational deep learning that treats table roles as optimizable components (nodes/edges) in message passing, with functional dependency constraints, outperforming fixed-structure methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RDL methods use fixed graph structures based on full-resolution design, limiting flexibility. The authors aim to make graph structure learnable while preserving relational semantics.

Method: Formulates structure learning as a learnable table role modeling problem; designs role-driven message passing to capture relational semantics; introduces functional dependency constraints for semantic consistency across table and entity levels.

Result: FROG outperforms existing approaches; reveals how different table roles impact downstream task performance.

Conclusion: Learnable graph structures with role-driven message passing and functional dependency constraints offer better performance and new insights for graph construction in RDL.

Abstract: Relational prediction tasks are fundamental in many real-world applications, where data are naturally stored in relational databases (RDBs). Relational Deep Learning (RDL) addresses this problem by modeling RDBs as graphs and applying graph neural networks (GNNs) for end-to-end learning. However, the full-resolution property is commonly adopted as a design principle in graph construction for RDBs to preserve relational semantics, which leads most existing methods to rely on fixed graph structures. In this paper, we propose FROG, a Full-Resolution and Optimizable Graph Structure Learning} framework for RDL that formulates relational structure learning as a learnable table role modeling problem, allowing tables to contribute as nodes and edges in message passing. We further design role-driven message passing mechanisms to capture relational semantics, enabling joint optimization of graph structure and GNN representations. To ensure semantic consistency, we introduce functional dependency constraints that regularize representations across table and entity levels. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms existing approaches and reveal how table roles impact downstream tasks, offering new insights into graph construction for RDL

</details>


### [385] [EvoStruct: Bridging Evolutionary and Structural Priors for Antibody CDR Design via Protein Language Model Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21485)
*Mansoor Ahmed,Sujin Lee,Umar Khayaz,Murray Patterson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EvoStruct bridges a frozen PLM with an E(3)-equivariant GNN via cross-attention to solve vocabulary collapse in antibody CDR design, achieving 16% higher sequence recovery, 43% lower perplexity, and 2.3x greater amino acid diversity than prior GNN methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing equivariant GNN methods for CDR design suffer from severe vocabulary collapse (over-predicting few amino acids like tyrosine/glycine) because they learn amino acid distributions from limited structural data, ignoring evolutionary substitution patterns encoded in protein databases.

Method: EvoStruct bridges a frozen protein language model (PLM) with E(3)-equivariant GNN via cross-attention adapter, using progressive PLM unfreezing and R-Drop consistency regularization to specifically address vocabulary collapse in CDR design.

Result: On CHIMERA-Bench, EvoStruct achieves highest recovery and lowest perplexity, improving sequence recovery by 16%, reducing perplexity by 43% vs best GNN baselines, with 2.3x greater amino acid diversity and highest binding-pair correlation.

Conclusion: Bridging PLM evolutionary knowledge with equivariant GNN structural context via targeted regularization effectively resolves vocabulary collapse, substantially outperforming prior methods for antibody CDR design.

Abstract: Equivariant graph neural network (GNN) methods for antibody complementarity-determining region (CDR) design achieve the highest sequence recovery but suffer from severe vocabulary collapse. The current best GNN methods over-predict very few amino acids, such as tyrosine and glycine, while ignoring functionally important residues. We trace this failure to GNN encoders learning amino acid distributions de novo from limited structural data, discarding substitution patterns encoded in evolutionary databases. To resolve this, we propose EvoStruct, which bridges a frozen protein language model (PLM) with 3D structural context from an E(3)-equivariant GNN via a cross-attention adapter. Unlike prior PLM-structure adapters for general protein design, EvoStruct targets the vocabulary collapse problem specific to CDR design through progressive PLM unfreezing and R-Drop consistency regularization. On the CHIMERA-Bench dataset, EvoStruct achieves the highest amino acid recovery and lowest perplexity among several antibody design methods, improving sequence recovery by 16% and reducing perplexity by 43% relative to the best GNN baselines, while recovering 2.3x greater amino acid diversity and the highest binding-pair correlation with ground truth.

</details>


### [386] [Equilibrium Reasoners: Learning Attractors Enables Scalable Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21488)
*Benhao Huang,Zhengyang Geng,Zico Kolter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Equilibrium Reasoners (EqR), which frame iterative latent reasoning as learning task-conditioned attractors. By scaling test-time compute along depth (more iterations) and breadth (multiple trajectories), EqR achieves dramatic accuracy gains (2.6%→99% on Sudoku-Extreme) through convergence toward solution-aligned fixed points, with compute adaptively allocated based on task difficulty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why iterative latent state update models generalize beyond memorization, hypothesizing that they learn task-conditioned attractors where fixed points correspond to valid solutions.

Method: Equilibrium Reasoners (EqR) scale test-time compute along two axes: depth (running more iterations) and breadth (aggregating stochastic trajectories from multiple initializations), without external verifiers or task-specific priors.

Result: Gains from test-time scaling are tightly coupled with convergence toward solution-aligned attractors. On Sudoku-Extreme, accuracy rises from 2.6% (feedforward) to over 99% by unrolling up to 40,000 layers. Simple cases converge in 1-5 steps, while harder cases benefit from massive scaling.

Conclusion: Learned attractor landscapes provide a useful mechanistic lens for understanding scalable reasoning in iterative latent models, enabling adaptive allocation of test-time compute based on task difficulty.

Abstract: Scaling test-time compute by iteratively updating a latent state has emerged as a powerful paradigm for reasoning. Yet the internal mechanisms that enable these iterative models to generalize beyond memorized patterns remain unclear. We hypothesize that generalizable reasoning arises from learning task-conditioned attractors: latent dynamical systems whose stable fixed points correspond to valid solutions.
  We formalize this process through Equilibrium Reasoners (EqR), which enable test-time scaling without external verifiers or task-specific priors. EqR scales internal dynamics along two axes: depth, by running more iterations, and breadth, by aggregating stochastic trajectories from multiple initializations. Empirically, gains from test-time scaling are tightly coupled with stronger convergence toward solution-aligned attractors.
  This attractor perspective allows neural networks to adaptively allocate test-time compute based on task difficulty. While simple cases converge within 1 to 5 iteration steps, harder cases benefit from massive test-time scaling. By unrolling up to the equivalent of 40,000 layers, scalable latent reasoning boosts accuracy from 2.6% for feedforward models to over 99% on Sudoku-Extreme. These results suggest that learned attractor landscapes provide a useful mechanistic lens for understanding scalable reasoning in iterative latent models.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [387] [Disentangling Sampling from Training Budget in Class-Imbalanced CT Body Composition Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20405)
*Iason Skylitsis,Dimitrios Karkalousos,Ivana Išgum*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper adapts episodic sampling from few-shot learning for class-balanced batch construction in fully supervised medical image segmentation, showing comparable results to random/weighted sampling under full data but superior performance in low-data regimes due to training iteration budget effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Class imbalance in medical image segmentation causes rare classes to be underrepresented. Existing loss-based reweighting and sampling strategies do not explicitly control which classes appear in each batch, leaving rare-class exposure only partially addressed.

Method: Episodic sampling from few-shot learning is decoupled from metric learning and applied to fully supervised body composition segmentation in CT. It constructs class-balanced batches by episodically sampling. Evaluated on 9 muscle/adipose tissue classes from 210 SAROS CT scans under full-data and low-data regimes, with training iteration budget controlled.

Result: Under full-data: all three methods comparable (episodic Dice 0.882 vs. 0.878 random/weighted). Low-data: episodic outperformed (0.787 vs. 0.758/0.762), driven by 12× iteration difference. Under matched budget, episodic improved for ~3× more iterations before plateauing; random/weighted overfit earlier.

Conclusion: Training iteration budget is an under-recognized confound in sampling strategies. Episodic sampling provides implicit regularization via class-balanced batches, offering a low-cost, model-agnostic strategy for class-imbalanced medical image segmentation.

Abstract: Class imbalance is a fundamental challenge in medical image segmentation, where frequent classes typically dominate training at the expense of rare classes. Loss-based approaches mitigate imbalance by reweighting the per-pixel loss within the batch, while sampling strategies control which images enter the batch. Yet neither explicitly controls which classes appear within the batch, leaving rare-class exposure only partially rebalanced. In this work, we adopt episodic sampling from few-shot learning to promote class-balanced batch construction in a fully supervised setting. We decouple episodic sampling from its conventional metric-learning context and evaluate it in body composition segmentation in CT. We compare episodic sampling against random and weighted sampling on nine muscle and adipose tissues, derived from 210 scans of the public SAROS dataset. Training is performed under full- and low-data regimes, with additional comparisons under matched training iteration budgets. Under full-data training, all three strategies performed comparably (mean Dice 0.882 for episodic, 0.878 for random and weighted). Under low-data training, episodic sampling outperformed random and weighted (0.787 vs. 0.758 and 0.762), driven by a 12-fold difference in training iterations. Under matched training budgets, random and weighted overfit earlier, while episodic improved for approximately three times more iterations before plateauing. Our findings identify the training iteration budget as under-recognized confound in sampling strategies, motivating iteration-aware evaluation protocols for small datasets. Furthermore, the residual advantage of episodic sampling is consistent with an implicit regularization effect of class-balanced batches, offering a low-cost, model-agnostic strategy for class-imbalanced medical image segmentation. Code is available at https://github.com/iasonsky/episodic-sampling.

</details>


### [388] [LANCE: Locally Adaptive Neural Context Estimation for Overfitted Image Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20672)
*Martin Benjak,Jörn Ostermann*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: LANCE extends overfitted image compression (Cool-Chic) with a forward-signaled spatial hyperprior and predictive coding (MED + lightweight learned model) to enable regional entropy model adaptation, achieving ~1.4-3% BD-rate reductions on Kodak/CLIC datasets with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional overfitted image compression methods use lightweight autoregressive networks with globally signaled parameters, which perform poorly on non-stationary image statistics. The paper aims to enable regional adaptation without excessive overhead.

Method: LANCE incorporates a forward-signaled spatial hyperprior for regional entropy model adaptation, combined with a predictive coding scheme using a static Median Edge Detector (MED) and a lightweight learned context model to minimize signaling overhead.

Result: BD-rate reductions of 1.40% (Kodak) and 1.97% (CLIC 2020) over Cool-Chic 4.0 at 606-1481 MAC/pixel; at lower complexity, outperforms by 2.41% (Kodak) and 2.99% (CLIC).

Conclusion: The learned spatial hyperprior effectively segments images into regions with similar statistics, providing automated content-aware adaptation, improving compression efficiency over global parameter approaches.

Abstract: This paper introduces Locally Adaptive Neural Context Estimation (LANCE), a novel extension for overfitted image compression (OIC) frameworks like Cool-Chic. While traditional OIC methods rely on lightweight autoregressive networks with globally signaled parameters, they struggle with non-stationary image statistics. LANCE addresses this by incorporating a forward-signaled spatial hyperprior that enables regional adaptation of the entropy model. To minimize overhead, we employ a predictive coding scheme that combines a static Median Edge Detector (MED) with a lightweight learned context model.
  Experiments demonstrate that LANCE achieves BD-rate reductions of 1.40% on the Kodak dataset and 1.97% on CLIC 2020 over Cool-Chic 4.0 at the high end of our decoder complexity range of 606-1481 MAC/pixel. At the low end of the complexity range, we outperform Cool-Chic 4.0 by 2.41% and 2.99% on Kodak and CLIC, respectively. Qualitative analysis reveals that the learned spatial hyperprior effectively segments image regions into areas of similar image statistics, providing an automated, content-aware adaptation layer.

</details>


### [389] [Motion-Robust Deep Reconstruction for Free-Breathing Cardiac Cine MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20687)
*Mahmut Yurt,Kanghyun Ryu,Zhitao Li,Xucheng Zhu,Xianglun Mao,Martin Janich,Marcus Alley,Kawin Setsompop,John Pauly,Shreyas Vasanawala,Ali Syed*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Cine-DL is a deep learning framework for free-breathing cardiac cine MRI that combines k-space preprocessing (retrospective binning, respiratory gating, and Streak Optimized Coil Compression) with an unrolled reconstruction network (ResNet proximal operator + conjugate gradient data consistency) to reduce streak artifacts and enable high-quality, clinically deployable reconstruction without breath-holding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional breath-hold Cartesian cardiac cine MRI is prone to motion artifacts and is uncomfortable or infeasible for noncompliant patients (e.g., pediatric). Free-breathing radial acquisitions address these issues but suffer from prominent streak artifacts at high acceleration rates, limiting robust reconstruction.

Method: Proposes Cine-DL: (1) retrospective cardiac binning and respiratory gating on raw free-breathing radial data; (2) Streak Optimized Coil Compression (SOC) to preserve cardiac signals and suppress peripheral interference; (3) unrolled network with ResNet proximal operator and conjugate-gradient-based data consistency updates; (4) memory-efficient training strategy.

Result: Cine-DL consistently improves quantitative metrics and visual fidelity over baselines (k-t SENSE, iGRASP) on free-breathing volunteer data, and demonstrates successful clinical translation on newly acquired patient data in a hospital deployment.

Conclusion: Cine-DL provides a practical, clinically oriented route toward routine adoption of free-breathing cine MRI in time-sensitive clinical settings.

Abstract: Conventional cardiac cine MRI relies on breath-hold Cartesian acquisitions, which are vulnerable to motion artifacts and can be uncomfortable or infeasible, particularly for pediatric and other noncompliant patients who cannot reliably hold their breath. Free-breathing radial acquisitions can alleviate these limitations, but robust reconstruction at high acceleration remains challenging due to prominent streak artifacts. To address these limitations, we propose Cine-DL, a clinically oriented framework that couples targeted k-space preprocessing with fast, model-based deep reconstruction. In this pipeline, raw free-breathing radial data undergo retrospective cardiac binning and respiratory gating to resolve cardiac phases and discard motion-corrupted spokes. We then introduce Streak Optimized Coil Compression (SOC), which explicitly preserves cardiac signals while suppressing peripheral interference that typically drives the streak artifacts. The resulting 2D+t cine series is reconstructed with an unrolled network that alternates a ResNet proximal operator with physics-based data consistency updates solved via conjugate gradient. We further employ a memory-efficient training strategy that reduces peak memory usage. We evaluate Cine-DL on free-breathing volunteer data against established baselines (k-t SENSE and iGRASP) and demonstrate clinical translation via hospital deployment on newly acquired patient data. Our experiments show that Cine-DL consistently improves quantitative metrics and visual fidelity, supporting a practical route toward routine, time-sensitive clinical adoption of free-breathing cine MRI.

</details>


### [390] [Parallel Context Modeling for Sliding Window Attention in Neural Video Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20977)
*Alexander Kopte,André Kaup*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: P-SWA enables parallel decoding in neural video codecs using diagonal wavefronts, achieving 36% faster decoding than parallel VCT and up to 10.0% BD-rate savings over SWA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural video codecs suffer from error propagation (temporal conditioning) or high complexity and inferior RD performance (VCT). SWA improves complexity and RD but creates a decoding latency bottleneck due to sequential raster-scan order.

Method: P-SWA uses diagonal wavefronts for parallel decoding, embeds a hyperprior, and introduces an accumulator to fuse side information with local spatial context.

Result: 36% faster decoding speed over parallel VCT; up to 10.0% BD-rate savings for I-frames and 7.1% for P-frames over SWA.

Conclusion: P-SWA resolves the sequential decoding bottleneck in SWA while maintaining strong RD performance, offering a practical parallel neural video codec.

Abstract: Most neural video codecs rely on temporal conditioning, which makes them susceptible to error propagation over long sequences. While Transformer-based architectures like the VCT offer a drift-free alternative, they suffer from high computational complexity and inferior RD performance. The recent SWA addresses these shortcomings by reducing complexity and enhancing RD performance, yet it restricts decoding to a strictly sequential raster-scan order, creating a critical bottleneck in decoding latency. To resolve this, we propose P-SWA, utilizing diagonal wavefronts to enable parallel decoding. By embedding a hyperprior and introducing an accumulator to fuse side information and local spatial context, our method increases decoding speed by 36% over the parallel VCT. Simultaneously, it achieves Bjøntegaard Delta-rate savings of up to 10.0% for I-frames and 7.1% for P-frames over the SWA baseline.

</details>


### [391] [Transcoding a 3D Gaussian Splatting Model from a Plenoptic Point Cloud or Mesh without the Original Multi-view Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21051)
*Maja Krivokuća,Riad Bendouro,Neus Sabater*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an end-to-end pipeline to convert existing 3D point cloud or mesh models into 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) models without needing original multi-view images, using custom initialization for faster convergence and cleaner surfaces with fewer splats.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create 3D Gaussian splatting models from existing 3D point cloud or mesh models when the original multi-view images of the captured 3D object or scene are not available.

Method: An end-to-end transcoding pipeline with custom initialization that guides 3DGS model learning using constraints to ensure alignment with the input point cloud or mesh surface.

Result: The pipeline produces high visual quality 3DGS models with many fewer splats than original dense point clouds, and the custom initialization leads to much faster convergence and cleaner surface representation compared to default SfM-based initialization.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enables efficient conversion of existing 3D representations to compact, high-quality 3DGS models without requiring original multi-view imagery.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose an end-to-end transcoding pipeline, to create 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) models from existing 3D plenoptic point cloud or mesh models, when the original multi-view images of the captured 3D object or scene are not available. We also propose a custom initialisation to guide the 3DGS model learning, with constraints to ensure that the final 3DGS model aligns closely with the input point cloud or mesh surface. Tests on a high-quality, standard plenoptic point cloud dataset show that our pipeline produces 3DGS models of high visual quality, with many fewer splats than points in the original dense point clouds. Additionally, our custom initialisation leads to much faster convergence and cleaner surface representation than when starting from the default SfM-based initialisation that is typically used for 3DGS model learning.

</details>


### [392] [Joint Alignment and Denoising for Event-Based Vision Sensors Using Regret-based Pareto Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21096)
*Shimpei Harada,Junya Hara,Hiroshi Higashi,Yuichi Tanaka*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a joint optimization method for event alignment and denoising in event-based vision sensors using a bi-objective Pareto optimization approach based on contrast maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods treat event alignment and denoising as separate modules, creating a dilemma where alignment is biased by noise without denoising, and denoising struggles without alignment.

Method: A bi-objective Pareto optimization is formulated using a contrast map. Event alignment maximizes contrast map variance while denoising minimizes it. A regret strategy solves these conflicting objectives.

Result: The method achieves improvements over alternative approaches in both denoising and motion estimation tasks.

Conclusion: Jointly optimizing event alignment and denoising via Pareto optimization effectively resolves the dilemma of treating them separately.

Abstract: This paper proposes a joint alignment and denoising method for event-based vision sensors (EVSs). Existing signal processing methods for EVSs typically perform event alignment (EA) and event denoising (ED) as separate modules. However, this separation creates a dilemma: without ED, EA is biased by noise, whereas without EA, ED struggles to distinguish signal events from noise ones. To address this dilemma, we jointly optimize EA and ED by formulating a bi-objective Pareto optimization problem. Our formulation is built upon a contrast map that counts the number of events localized in each pixel. With a contrast map, we can formulate EA as maximizing its variance and ED as minimizing the variance. We cast these two conflicting problems as a Pareto optimization and use a regret strategy to obtain a solution. Experimental results on denoising and motion estimation demonstrate that our method achieves improvements against alternative ones.

</details>


### [393] [GeoDiff-SAR II: 3D-Driven Foundation Diffusion Models for SAR Generation via Decoupled Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21116)
*Xuanting Wu,Fan Zhang,Fei Ma,Yingbing Liu,Lingxiao Peng,Qiang Yin,Yongsheng Zhou*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: GeoDiff-SAR II is a 3D model-guided framework for controllable SAR image generation that uses Geometric-Electromagnetic Conditioning Maps (GECM) combined with ControlNet, LoRA, and FLUX backbone to achieve physically consistent control over azimuth angle, depression angle, and polarization mode.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SAR generation methods lack reliable controllability over key imaging parameters (azimuth angle, depression angle, polarization mode). The preliminary GeoDiff-SAR only supported limited azimuth completion and failed for large missing sectors without unified multi-parameter control.

Method: Proposes a 3D model-guided decoupled framework using Geometric-Electromagnetic Conditioning Maps (GECM) to encode target pose and scattering centers, decoupling macroscopic geometry from microscopic scattering. GECMs derived from real images during training, rendered from 3D CAD models during inference. Uses ControlNet for spatial guidance, LoRA on FLUX backbone, and text conditions for imaging parameters.

Result: Demonstrates controllable generation over key SAR parameters, stable generalization across large azimuth gaps, and consistent improvements in image fidelity, physical consistency, and downstream ATR performance on both simulated and real datasets.

Conclusion: The unified geometric-electromagnetic conditioning and parameter-aware generation framework effectively addresses the controllability limitations in SAR image generation, enabling physically consistent control across large viewpoint gaps.

Abstract: Existing Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) image generation methods still lack reliable controllability over key imaging parameters, particularly azimuth angle, depression angle, and polarization mode. Our preliminary GeoDiff-SAR supported limited azimuth completion, but remained ineffective for large missing azimuth sectors and did not provide unified control over multiple imaging conditions. To address this problem, we propose GeoDiff-SAR II, a 3D model-guided decoupled framework for controllable SAR image generation. The proposed framework imposes controllability through physically grounded geometric-electromagnetic cues rather than image intensity alone. We introduce a Geometric-Electromagnetic Conditioning Map (GECM), a structured intermediate representation that encodes the target pose map and dominant scattering centers, thereby decoupling macroscopic geometry from microscopic scattering responses. During training, GECMs are derived from real sparse-azimuth SAR images. During inference, the same representation is rendered directly from a 3D CAD model under specified azimuth, depression angle, and polarization conditions, enabling physically consistent control across large viewpoint gaps. The imaging parameters are further converted into text conditions, while the GECM is injected through ControlNet to provide explicit spatial guidance. Combined with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on a FLUX backbone, the proposed framework unifies geometric-electromagnetic conditioning and parameter-aware generation within a single process. Experiments on simulated and real datasets demonstrate controllable generation over key SAR imaging parameters, stable generalization across large azimuth gaps, and consistent improvements in image fidelity, physical consistency, and downstream Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) performance.

</details>


### [394] [Local-sensitive connectivity filter (ls-cf): A post-processing unsupervised improvement of the frangi, hessian and vesselness filters for multimodal vessel segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21251)
*Erick O Rodrigues,Lucas O Rodrigues,João HP Machado,Dalcimar Casanova,Marcelo Teixeira,Jeferson T Oliva,Giovani Bernardes,Panos Liatsis*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: An unsupervised multimodal method (LS-CF) improves Frangi filter-based retinal vessel segmentation by adding pixel-level continuity and local tolerance to fill discontinuities, achieving competitive or superior results across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retinal vessel analysis is important for assessing eye risks; existing Frangi filter-based methods suffer from vessel discontinuities, motivating a need for better unsupervised segmentation.

Method: Proposes a local-sensitive connectivity filter (LS-CF) that computes pixel-level vessel continuity with a local tolerance heuristic to fill gaps in Frangi filter responses, compared against naive connectivity filters and morphological closing.

Result: Outperformed all state-of-the-art methods on OSIRIX angiographic dataset; outperformed 4/5 methods on IOSTAR; surpassed several methods on DRIVE and STARE; outperformed 6/10 on CHASE-DB and all unsupervised methods on CHASE-DB.

Conclusion: The LS-CF is a robust unsupervised approach achieving competitive multimodal vessel segmentation results, particularly excelling on angiographic and challenging datasets.

Abstract: A retinal vessel analysis is a procedure that can be used as an assessment of risks to the eye. This work proposes an unsupervised multimodal approach that improves the response of the Frangi filter, enabling automatic vessel segmentation. We propose a filter that computes pixel-level vessel continuity while introducing a local tolerance heuristic to fill in vessel discontinuities produced by the Frangi response. This proposal, called the local-sensitive connectivity filter (LS-CF), is compared against a naive connectivity filter to the baseline thresholded Frangi filter response and to the naive connectivity filter response in combination with the morphological closing and to the current approaches in the literature. The proposal was able to achieve competitive results in a variety of multimodal datasets. It was robust enough to outperform all the state-of-the-art approaches in the literature for the OSIRIX angiographic dataset in terms of accuracy and 4 out of 5 works in the case of the IOSTAR dataset while also outperforming several works in the case of the DRIVE and STARE datasets and 6 out of 10 in the CHASE-DB dataset. For the CHASE-DB, it also outperformed all the state-of-the-art unsupervised methods.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [395] [Adaptive Human-Robot Collaboration for Masonry Construction Under Material and Assembly Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20264)
*Jutang Gao,Arash Adel*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents an adaptive human-robot collaborative masonry workflow using projector-based guidance for adhesive application and laser scanning for pose correction, improving precision and reducing tolerance accumulation in brickwork construction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human-robot collaboration in construction faces challenges due to limited robot-to-human communication and tolerance accumulation from material and assembly uncertainties.

Method: Two complementary mechanisms: 1) end-effector-mounted projector for spatially registered projection guidance, and 2) laser scanning for feedback-driven grasping and placement pose correction.

Result: Projection guidance improved adhesive application consistency and reduced application time; laser-based correction maintained level courses and avoided collision-prone failures compared to open-loop execution.

Conclusion: Integrating spatial projection with feedback-driven adaptation can mitigate tolerance accumulation and improve precision and robustness in human-robot collaborative construction.

Abstract: Human-robot collaboration in construction is often challenged by limited robot-to-human communication and the need to adapt to tolerance accumulation arising from material and assembly uncertainties. We present an adaptive human-robot collaborative workflow for masonry construction that addresses communication limitations and tolerance accumulation, demonstrated through a brickwork case study in which a robot places bricks while a human applies adhesive. This workflow is enabled by two complementary mechanisms: 1) an end-effector-mounted projector that provides spatially registered, just-in-time projection guidance for manual adhesive application, and 2) laser scanning for feedback-driven grasping and placement pose correction. Together, these mechanisms enable adjustment of human and robotic actions in response to material variability and accumulated assembly tolerances. Full-scale experiments across conventional running-bond and nonstandard configurations demonstrate that projection guidance improves adhesive application consistency and reduces application time, while laser-based correction maintains level courses and avoids collision-prone failures associated with open-loop execution. These results indicate that integrating spatial projection with feedback-driven adaptation, enabled by material and as-built sensing, can mitigate tolerance accumulation and improve precision and robustness in human-robot collaborative construction.

</details>


### [396] [Terrestrial Soft Mobile Robots: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20304)
*Dimuthu D. K. Arachchige*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A comprehensive review of wheelless terrestrial soft mobile robots, covering locomotion strategies, actuation methods, modeling, and control, along with key challenges and future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft mobile robots have potential in search-and-rescue, service, surveillance, exploration, and manufacturing, but a systematic review of wheelless terrestrial systems was needed to consolidate past and current developments.

Method: The paper provides a comprehensive review covering locomotion strategies, actuation methods, modeling approaches, and control systems for wheelless terrestrial soft mobile robots.

Result: Identified key research challenges that must be overcome for widespread adoption of soft mobile robots in various applications.

Conclusion: This article serves as a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners interested in soft mobile robots and soft robotics, highlighting both progress and remaining obstacles.

Abstract: Soft mobile robots have emerged as a promising area of research with potential applications in various disciplines including but not limited to search-and-rescue, service, surveillance, explorations, and manufacturing. In this article, we provide a comprehensive review of the current state of soft mobile robot research, focusing on wheelless terrestrial locomotive systems. We include past and present developments in locomotion strategies, actuation methods, modeling approaches, and control systems. Further, we identify key research challenges that must be overcome to enable the widespread adoption of soft mobile robots in various applications. Overall, this article provides a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners interested in the field of soft mobile robots and soft robotics.

</details>


### [397] [Proximal State Nudging: Reducing Skill Atrophy from AI Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20355)
*Megha Srivastava,Jonathan Ouyang,Eric Zhou,Andrew Silva,Emily Sumner,Dorsa Sadigh,Yuchen Cui,Deepak Gopinath,Guy Rosman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes Proximal State Nudging (PSN), a shared autonomy algorithm that optimizes both skill development and task performance by nudging users toward learnable states. PSN outperforms baselines in simulated environments and shows 7x skill gains with 50% fewer collisions in human subject studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Skill atrophy under AI assistance poses safety risks in shared-control systems, where operators may lose the ability to distinguish their own inputs from autonomous corrections.

Method: Proximal State Nudging (PSN), a shared autonomy algorithm that jointly optimizes for skill development and task performance by nudging users toward states estimated to be most learnable.

Result: PSN outperforms existing shared autonomy baselines in simulated LunarLander environment. In human subject studies (n=60) across two driving tasks, PSN produces up to 7x larger gains in unassisted skill than standard blended shared autonomy, with 50% fewer collisions than unassisted self-practice.

Conclusion: PSN effectively balances skill development and task performance, reducing skill atrophy while improving safety compared to both standard shared autonomy and unassisted practice.

Abstract: Skill atrophy, the gradual decline of human capability under AI assistance, poses a safety risk in shared-control of semi-autonomous systems, where operators may be unable to distinguish their own inputs from autonomous corrections. We propose Proximal State Nudging (PSN), a shared autonomy algorithm that jointly optimizes for skill development and task performance by nudging users toward states estimated to be most learnable. We first show that PSN outperforms existing shared autonomy baselines in balancing student improvement in unassisted reward with overall shared performance, using simulated students in the classic LunarLander environment. We then present, to the best of our knowledge, the first human subject studies of a planner incorporating learning-compatible shared autonomy: across two driving tasks in the CARLA simulator (High Performance Racing and Parallel Parking, n = 60), PSN produces up to 7x larger gains in unassisted skill than standard blended shared autonomy, while incurring 50% fewer collisions than unassisted self-practice.

</details>


### [398] [SUGAR: A Scalable Human-Video-Driven Generalizable Humanoid Loco-Manipulation Learning Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20373)
*Tianshu Wu,Xiangqi Kong,Yue Chen,Qize Yu,Hang Ye,Jia Li,Yizhou Wang,Hao Dong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SUGAR is a scalable framework that converts diverse human videos into deployable humanoid loco-manipulation skills through three stages: extracting kinematic priors from videos, refining them with physics-based simulation, and distilling into hierarchical autonomous policies, achieving zero-shot real-world transfer without task-specific reward engineering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for humanoid whole-body loco-manipulation rely on laborious task-specific reward engineering, rigid reference motions that fail to generalize, or costly teleoperation that limits scalability. Human videos offer scalable data but contain occlusion, contact artifacts, and retargeting errors that make them unsuitable for direct policy learning.

Method: Three-stage framework: (1) Automated pipeline extracts kinematic interaction priors (human-object motion trajectories and contact labels) from unstructured human videos; (2) Privileged physics-based refiner uses unified mimic reward and progressive state pool to transform imperfect priors into physically feasible skills; (3) Refined skills are distilled into hierarchical autonomous policy (command generator + command tracker).

Result: Outperforms reference-tracking baselines on six loco-manipulation tasks; performance scales with amount of human video data; achieves zero-shot real-world transfer with closed-loop execution, autonomous failure recovery, and stable long-horizon performance under perturbations.

Conclusion: SUGAR demonstrates that imperfect human video priors can be effectively transformed into deployable humanoid skills through physics-based refinement and distillation, enabling scalable and generalizable whole-body loco-manipulation without task-specific engineering.

Abstract: Building humanoid robots capable of generalizable whole-body loco-manipulation in the real world remains a fundamental challenge. Existing methods either rely on laborious task-specific reward engineering, rigidly replay reference motions that fail to generalize, or depend on costly teleoperation that limits scalability. While human videos capture diverse human behaviors, motion priors inferred from them are inherently imperfect, suffering from occlusion, contact artifacts, and retargeting errors that render them unsuitable for direct policy learning. To address this, we present SUGAR, a scalable data-driven framework that converts diverse human videos into deployable humanoid loco-manipulation skills, without any task-specific reward engineering or reference-motion conditioning at inference. SUGAR proceeds in three stages. First, a fully automated pipeline extracts kinematic interaction priors including human-object motion trajectories and contact labels from unstructured human videos. Second, a privileged physics-based refiner uses a unified mimic reward and progressive state pool to transform imperfect priors into physically feasible, high-fidelity skills. Third, refined skills are distilled into a hierarchical autonomous policy consisting of a command generator and a command tracker. We evaluate SUGAR on six representative loco-manipulation tasks in simulation and real-world humanoid hardware. Our method substantially outperforms reference-tracking baselines, and performance scales clearly with the amount of human video data. It also achieves zero-shot real-world transfer with reliable closed-loop execution, autonomous failure recovery, and stable long-horizon performance under external perturbations. Project Page: https://tianshuwu.github.io/sugar-humanoid/

</details>


### [399] [VBT-MPC: Vision-Based Tactile MPC for Contour Following](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20392)
*Edison Velasco-Sanchez,Luis F. Recalde,Guanrui Li,Pablo Gil*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A vision-based tactile model predictive control framework for robotic contour following that operates directly in contour feature space, eliminating the need for pose estimation or complex force control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile sensing is crucial for robotic manipulation tasks like surface inspection, but requires maintaining contact while accurately tracking object contours.

Method: Proposed VBT-MPC framework using eye-in-hand vision-based tactile sensor, operating directly in contour feature space without separate pose-estimation or force-control modules.

Result: Compared against visual-servoing strategies adapted to tactile features; evaluated on objects with diverse geometries and materials in simulation and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: VBT-MPC effectively enables contour following across diverse geometries and materials, outperforming tactile-adapted visual servoing approaches.

Abstract: Tactile sensing plays a key role in robotic manipulation, particularly in tasks like surface inspection. Successful execution requires maintaining contact while accurately tracking object contours. In this work, we propose a Vision-Based Tactile Model Predictive Control (VBT-MPC) framework for robotic contour following using a Vision-Based Tactile Sensor (VBTS) mounted in an eye-in-hand configuration. The proposed controller operates directly in contour features space, thereby avoiding the need for separate pose-estimation modules or complex force-control architectures. We further compare our VBT-MPC with visual-servoing strategies adapted to tactile features, and evaluate contour tracking on objects with diverse geometries and materials in both simulation and real-world experiments.

</details>


### [400] [Scalable Multi-robot Motion Planning via Hierarchical Subproblem Expansion and Workspace Decomposition Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20395)
*Isaac Ngui,Courtney McBeth,James D. Motes,Marco Morales,Nancy M. Amato*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-robot motion planning method that uses discrete workspace decomposition search to coordinate robots, reducing planning time by an order of magnitude through iteratively refining workspace representation to enable decoupled configuration space searches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve efficient multi-robot motion planning by avoiding the computational expense of searching the joint configuration space while maintaining sufficient inter-robot coordination.

Method: Uses discrete search over a workspace decomposition to provide coordination, then iteratively refines the workspace representation to allow the planner to search smaller, decoupled configuration spaces.

Result: Achieves improvement in planning time up to an order of magnitude compared to prior approaches.

Conclusion: Iterative workspace refinement can effectively decouple robot configuration space searches, dramatically reducing computational cost while maintaining necessary coordination.

Abstract: A fundamental challenge in multi-robot motion planning is achieving sufficient coordination to avoid inter-robot conflicts without incurring the large computational expense of searching the joint configuration space of the robot group. In this work, we present a method for multiple mobile robot motion planning that achieves an improvement in planning time up to an order of magnitude by leveraging the insight that we can use discrete search over a workspace decomposition to provide coordination between robots during planning. While prior work uses workspace topology to inform when coordination between robots is needed and then composes robots into their joint configuration space, we take a step further by iteratively refining our workspace representation to allow our planner to search smaller, decoupled configuration spaces.

</details>


### [401] [Spacetime Optimal-Transport Attention for Visuo-Haptic Imitation Learning of Contact-Rich Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20433)
*Yue Feng,Weicheng Huang,I-Ming Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SO-TA is a tri-modal fusion backbone using Optimal Transport attention to combine vision, force/torque, and proprioceptive signals for contact-rich manipulation, achieving 100% success on tight peg-in-hole and robust performance under perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contact-rich manipulation tasks are difficult for data-driven controllers due to discontinuous contact dynamics, partial observability, and safety constraints. No single sensing modality suffices, and existing multi-modal fusion methods lack structured inductive biases for contact-rich scenarios.

Method: SO-TA replaces softmax-normalized patch attention with entropy-regularized Optimal Transport alignment between force-pose-derived sub-queries and visual patches, using explicit marginal constraints as structured inductive bias, paired with a diffusion-based sequence policy.

Result: 100% success on tight peg-in-hole (vs 93% for cross-attention), 82.5% success under perturbations (vs 43.5% for baseline), validated on three real-robot tasks with ~200 rollouts each.

Conclusion: Optimal Transport attention with marginal constraints provides effective tri-modal fusion for contact-rich manipulation, offering both superior performance and interpretable phase-dependent diagnostics.

Abstract: Contact-rich manipulation tasks such as tight-clearance insertion, connector mating, polishing, and surface-conforming wiping remain difficult for data-driven controllers because they couple discontinuous contact dynamics, partial observability, and strict safety constraints. No single sensing modality suffices: vision supplies global context before contact, force/torque (F/T) feedback governs interaction after contact, and proprioceptive pose provides a consistent kinematic backbone. Most prior imitation-learning policies for contact-rich tasks operate on uni- or bi-modal signals, and the few that fuse three modalities typically adopt off-the-shelf attention modules with no explicit prior on how attention mass should be distributed across task-relevant regions. We present Spacetime Optimal-Transport Attention (SO-TA), a tri-modal fusion backbone that replaces softmax-normalized patch attention by an entropy-regularized Optimal Transport (OT) alignment between force-pose-derived sub-queries and visual patches. Explicit marginal constraints act as a structured inductive bias for contact-rich tasks, encouraging conditioning-aware spatial selection that is stable across illumination, distractors, and partial occlusion. SO-TA is paired with a diffusion-based sequence policy mapping observation windows to pose-action chunks. We evaluate SO-TA on three real-robot tasks: tight peg-in-hole assembly, BCM wiring-connector insertion, and curved-surface mark erasing. With ~200 rollouts per condition, SO-TA reaches 100% success on tight peg-in-hole versus 93% for cross-attention at matched capacity, and retains 82.5% success under illumination, distractor, and partial-occlusion perturbations where a concatenation baseline drops to 43.5%. OT-derived patch heatmaps and leave-one-out modality-influence ratios provide interpretable, phase-dependent diagnostics.

</details>


### [402] [Enhancing Graph-Based SLAM in GNSS-Denied environments by leveraging leg odometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20484)
*Léon Perruchot-Triboulet,Luc Jaulin,Kai Xiao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A factor graph architecture augmenting LIO-SAM with proprioceptive leg odometry reduces elevation drift in GNSS-denied legged robot navigation from over 30m to under 30cm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous navigation in GNSS-denied environments remains challenging for legged robots because exteroceptive sensors like LiDAR suffer from elevation drift in geometrically sparse or repetitive scenes.

Method: Augments LIO-SAM framework with a parallel kinematic lane driven by proprioceptive leg odometry, coupled to the main LiDAR-inertial lane via an identity relative pose constraint with selective noise model.

Result: On a Linxai D50 quadruped across two outdoor loops (>1 km), elevation drift reduced from over 30m to under 30cm; the approach enables convergence where baseline pipeline fails entirely.

Conclusion: Proprioceptive data already computed onboard for gait control is a lightweight and effective vertical anchor for SLAM in GNSS-denied settings.

Abstract: Autonomous navigation in GNSS-denied environments remains a core challenge for legged robots, where exteroceptive sensors such as LiDAR are prone to elevation drift in geometrically sparse or repetitive scenes. We present a factor graph architecture that augments the LIO-SAM framework with a parallel kinematic lane driven by proprioceptive leg odometry, coupled to the main LiDAR-inertial lane via an identity relative pose constraint with a selective noise model. Applied to a Linxai D50 quadruped platform across two outdoor loops totaling over one kilometer, our approach reduces elevation drift from over 30m to under 30cm and enables convergence in a scene where the baseline pipeline fails entirely. These results suggest that proprioceptive data, already computed onboard for gait control, constitutes a lightweight and effective vertical anchor for SLAM in GNSS-denied settings.

</details>


### [403] [The Yes-Man Syndrome: Benchmarking Abstention in Embodied Robotic Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20544)
*Doguhan Yeke,Elif Su Temirel,Ananth Shreekumar,Brandon Lee,Dongyan Xu,Z Berkay Celik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces RoboAbstention, a taxonomy and benchmark for evaluating when vision-language models should abstain from planning in embodied robotics contexts (e.g., ambiguous, infeasible, or false-premise instructions). Current VLMs perform poorly (best abstention rate 39%), but defensive prompting and in-context learning can boost performance to over 93%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing abstention benchmarks are text-only and fail to capture perceptual grounding, physical constraints, and sensory limitations inherent to embodied robotics environments where VLMs must recognize when instructions are ambiguous, physically infeasible, based on false premises, or otherwise unresolvable.

Method: The authors propose a taxonomy of abstention categories for embodied robotics and build RoboAbstention via a three-phase pipeline: (1) structured visual grounding, (2) deterministic constraint derivation, and (3) controlled instruction generation using category-specific templates, producing 6,069 verifiable benchmark instructions from images across five robotics datasets.

Result: Frontier VLMs show significant abstention weaknesses — Gemini 2.5 Flash abstains on only 39.0% of instructions, Gemini Robotics ER 1.6 Preview on just 16.5%. Defensive prompting and in-context learning substantially improve performance (Gemini Robotics ER 1.6 Preview reaches 93.6%, GPT 5.4 Mini reaches 88.6%), but no method fully solves the problem.

Conclusion: Abstention in embodied VLMs remains an open challenge; while prompting techniques can help, no current approach achieves perfect abstention, highlighting the need for further research in this direction.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are used as high-level planners for embodied agents, translating natural language instructions and visual observations into action plans. While prior work has studied abstention in LLMs, existing benchmarks are largely text-only and do not capture the perceptual grounding and physical constraints inherent to embodied robotics environments. In such settings, abstention requires recognizing when instructions are ambiguous, physically infeasible, based on false premises, or otherwise unresolvable given the available sensory modalities and context. To address this gap, we introduce a taxonomy to categorize abstention in the context of embodied robotics and present RoboAbstention, a scalable and auditable framework for generating abstention instructions grounded in images gathered from five robotics datasets. RoboAbstention instantiates the taxonomy through a three-phase pipeline: (1) structured visual grounding, (2) deterministic constraint derivation, and (3) controlled instruction generation via category-specific templates. This enables the construction of a diverse dataset with verifiable abstention conditions. We evaluate several frontier VLMs and find that all models exhibit significant weaknesses in abstention, including those with advanced reasoning capabilities. The best-performing model, Gemini 2.5 Flash, abstains on only 39.0% of our 6,069 benchmark instructions, while the embodied planner Gemini Robotics ER 1.6 Preview abstains on just 16.5%. We further explore methods for improving abstention in VLM planners, such as defensive prompting and in-context learning, and find that these interventions substantially improve performance, reaching 93.6% abstention rate for Gemini Robotics ER 1.6 Preview and 88.6% for GPT 5.4 Mini, yet no approach fully solves the problem. We open-source RoboAbstention at https://purseclab.github.io/RoboAbstention/.

</details>


### [404] [Fault-Tolerant, Rigidity-Preserving Control of Inflatable Truss Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.20561)
*James Wade,Isaac Weaver,Mihai Stanciu,Nathan Usevitch*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a fault-tolerant control framework for inflatable robotic trusses that handles motor failures by extending kinematic optimization with equality constraints, introducing DTCBF constraints for rigidity, and implementing closed-loop position control, achieving >69% workspace preservation and >25% tracking accuracy improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motor failures in operational environments severely limit the capabilities of isoperimetric robotic trusses, which otherwise have advantages like high strength-to-weight ratio and shape reconfigurability. A fault-tolerant approach is needed to maintain functionality despite such failures.

Method: Three contributions: 1) Extended kinematic optimization with equality constraints to handle arbitrary motor failure combinations; 2) Discrete-time control barrier function (DTCBF) constraints for structural rigidity guarantees while maximizing workspace; 3) Closed-loop position control with onboard encoder feedback and forward kinematics-based state estimator.

Result: Validated through simulation and hardware on a 2D isoperimetric truss testbed (6 actuators): >69% workspace preservation under single-motor failures, >25% improvement in tracking accuracy with closed-loop control.
