In addition, informed by shared design choices in prior work, we develop a streamlined, efficient 3DGS-based rendering pipeline that is deliberately kept lightweight, improving efficiency while leaving greater representational capacity to the front-end latent module. Extensive evaluations on RLBench, ManiSkill2, and a real-robot platform demonstrate consistent gains in task success, sample efficiency, and robustness to viewpoint and scene variations over strong baselines. Ablation studies further confirm that each component of our framework is critical to overall performance.

</details>


### [432] [Learning Robust Dexterous In-Hand Manipulation from Joint Sensors with Proprioceptive Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21330)
*Senlan Yao,Chenyu Yang,Jaehoon Kim,Aristotelis Sympetheros,Robert K. Katzschmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents the Proprioceptive Transformer (PT), a method that enables a tendon-driven dexterous hand to perform continuous cube rotation using only joint sensing (no vision or tactile feedback), achieving 3.1x faster rotation and 23.4% lower position estimation error than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dexterous manipulation approaches rely heavily on vision or tactile sensing for tracking object states. Joint sensing, though the most readily available modality on robotic hands, is largely overlooked—especially for tendon-driven hands. The authors investigate whether joint-only control can achieve competitive real-world performance without external perception.

Method: A teacher policy is trained via reinforcement learning with privileged object information, then distilled into the Proprioceptive Transformer (PT), which operates solely on joint position and velocity histories. The Transformer architecture extracts implicit object state information from temporal patterns in joint sensor readings.

Result: On the real ORCA hand, PT achieves 3.1x higher rotation speed than baselines and 23.4% lower RMSE for cube position estimation compared to the MLP baseline.

Conclusion: Joint sensing alone, when combined with a Transformer architecture, can effectively extract exteroceptive information from proprioceptive sources, enabling competitive dexterous manipulation without external perception.

Abstract: In-hand object manipulation is a fundamental yet challenging capability for dexterous robots. Despite significant progress in dexterous manipulation, existing approaches rely heavily on vision or tactile sensing to track object states, while joint sensing -- the most readily available modality on any robotic hand -- remains largely overlooked, particularly for tendon-driven hands. In this paper, we study how far joint sensing alone can go by asking: (i) whether motor encoders or direct joint sensing provides better proprioceptive feedback, (ii) how to extract environment information from joint measurements, and (iii) whether joint-only control can achieve competitive real-world performance without external perception. We present the Proprioceptive Transformer (PT), an exteroceptive-free approach for continuous cube rotation on a tendon-driven dexterous hand that uses only joint sensing feedback. A teacher policy is first trained via reinforcement learning with privileged object information, then distilled into PT, which operates solely on joint position and velocity histories. The Transformer architecture effectively extracts implicit object state information from temporal patterns in joint sensor readings. Experiments on the real ORCA hand show that our approach achieves 3.1x higher rotation speed than baselines. We also demonstrate that our PT achieves a 23.4% lower RMSE for cube position estimation than the MLP baseline, indicating superior extraction of exteroceptive information from proprioceptive sources.

</details>


### [433] [From swept contact to pose: Probe-aware registration via complementary-shape docking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21398)
*Chen Chen,Yunwen Li,Yifan Xu,Xiangjie Yan,Chang Shu,Jianxia Hou,Shiji Song,Xiang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a calibration-free contact registration method that treats the problem as complementary-shape docking between an object and a probe's swept volume, using 3D FFT correlation for global search and Lie-algebra refinement for metric-grade convergence, achieving sub-millimeter accuracy on a tooth-preparation robot without optical sensors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optical registration methods suffer from long calibration chains, line-of-sight constraints, and fabrication errors, motivating a calibration-free alternative for high-precision robotic manipulation.

Method: Formulates contact registration as complementary-shape docking, using a global-to-local search with 3D FFT correlation over low-discrepancy SO(3) samples, followed by continuous SE(3) refinement via Lie-algebra updates and analytic contact sensitivities.

Result: Simulation achieved sub-0.04 mm and sub-0.4° accuracy; on a tooth-preparation robot, achieved 0.42 mm and 3.75°, outperforming an optical tracker without external sensors.

Conclusion: The proposed method provides a practical, precise, and sensor-free registration strategy suitable for surgical and industrial robotic applications.

Abstract: Accurate registration between a prior model and the real scene is essential for high-precision robotic manipulation, yet optical methods suffer from long calibration chains, line-of-sight constraints, and fabrication errors. We propose a calibration-free alternative that reformulates contact registration as complementary-shape docking between the object and the probe's swept volume, explicitly accounting for probe geometry and leveraging both contact and non-contact evidence. Our solver integrates a global-to-local search via 3D FFT correlation over low-discrepancy SO(3) samples, then followed by continuous SE(3) refinement using Lie-algebra updates and analytic contact sensitivities. This pipeline yields efficient exploration and metric-grade convergence without fragile point correspondences. Simulation across free-form meshes achieved sub-0.04 mm and sub-0.4° accuracy and robustness to pose noise and contact loss. On a tooth-preparation robot, our method attained 0.42 mm and 3.75°, outperforming an optical tracker registration while requiring no external sensors. These results demonstrate a practical and precise registration strategy for surgical and industrial robots.

</details>


### [434] [MC-Risk: Multi-Component Risk Fields for Risk Identification and Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21406)
*Maximilian Link,Yingjie Xu,Yingbai Hu,Yinlong Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MC-Risk is a multi-component risk field that combines motorized-agent, VRU, and road penalty modules to achieve early, calibrated risk localization, achieving top performance on RiskBench and enabling plug-and-play risk-aware MPC planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a planner-aligned risk field that provides early, calibrated, and class-aware risk localization for autonomous driving, addressing the lack of standardized quantitative evaluation in prior risk-field formulations.

Method: Three interpretable modules: (1) motorized-agent field fusing multimodal trajectory prediction with Gaussian-torus (speed/curvature-aware width, look-ahead attenuation); (2) VRU field with forward-biased anisotropic kernel; (3) road penalty field using HD-map topology for off-road and lane-aware risk. Linear composition on bird's-eye-view grid.

Result: Best overall risk localization and earliest hazard indication on RiskBench's collision subset, with first standardized quantitative evaluation of a risk-field formulation.

Conclusion: MC-Risk enables plug-and-play risk-aware trajectory generation via MPC cost density without additional training, demonstrating practical planning interface utility.

Abstract: We present MC-Risk, a planner-aligned, multi-component risk field on a bird's-eye-view grid that yields early, calibrated, and class-aware risk localization. MC-Risk linearly composes three interpretable modules: (i) a motorized-agent field that fuses a black-box multimodal trajectory predictor with an analytic Gaussian-torus construction whose lateral width grows with speed/curvature and whose height attenuates with look-ahead; (ii) a VRU risk field that replaces isotropic pedestrian blobs with a forward-biased anisotropic kernel aligned to heading and speed; and (iii) a road penalty field that exploits full HD-map topology, imposing an off-road penalty and lane-aware risk exposure for same/opposite directions. We conduct, to our knowledge, the first standardized quantitative evaluation of a risk-field formulation on RiskBench's collision subset. MC-Risk attains the best overall risk localization and the earliest hazard indication. Finally, we demonstrate a plug-and-play planning interface by using the field as an MPC cost density, enabling risk-aware trajectory generation without additional training.

</details>


### [435] [PointACT: Vision-Language-Action Models with Multi-Scale Point-Action Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21414)
*Shizhe Chen,Paul Pacaud,Cordelia Schmid*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PointACT integrates 3D point cloud representations into VLA models via a dual-system architecture with multi-scale point-action interaction, achieving 10%+ success rate improvements on RLBench/LIBERO benchmarks over 2D-based VLAs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models rely on 2D visual representations, which limit fine-grained geometry and spatial grounding essential for precise 3D manipulation.

Method: Proposes PointACT, a dual-system 3D-aware VLA policy with multi-scale point-action interaction using bottleneck window self-attention, integrating hierarchical 3D point cloud representations into action decoding.

Result: 10% improvement on RLBench-10Tasks over state-of-the-art pretrained VLAs; consistent gains on LIBERO; larger gains when backbone is frozen and action expert trained from scratch.

Conclusion: Tightly coupling hierarchical 3D geometry with pretrained 2D semantic representations is critical for robust, spatially grounded robot control; pretrained 3D representations are promising for 3D-aware VLA policies.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for general-purpose robotic manipulation by leveraging large pretrained vision-language backbones. However, most existing VLAs rely primarily on 2D visual representations, which limit their ability to reason about fine-grained geometry and spatial grounding - capabilities that are essential for precise and robust manipulation in 3D environments. In this paper, we propose PointACT, a dual-system 3D-aware VLA policy that integrates hierarchical 3D point cloud representations directly into the action decoding process. PointACT employs a multi-scale point-action interaction mechanism with efficient bottleneck window self-attention, enabling evolving action tokens to densely attend to both local geometric detail and global scene structure. We evaluate PointACT on the LIBERO and RLBench benchmarks and systematically compare it against monolithic and dual-system VLA baselines, including variants augmented with point cloud inputs. PointACT achieves consistent improvements across both benchmarks, increasing success rates by 10% on the challenging RLBench-10Tasks suite over state-of-the-art pretrained VLAs, with even larger gains when the vision-language backbone is frozen and the action expert is trained from scratch. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate that tightly coupling hierarchical 3D geometry with pretrained 2D semantic representations is critical for robust and spatially grounded robot control. Our results also highlight the promise of pretrained 3D representations for 3D-aware VLA policies.

</details>


### [436] [roto 2.0: The Robot Tactile Olympiad](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21429)
*Elle Miller,Jayaram Reddy,Ayush Deshmukh,Trevor McInroe,David Abel,Oisin Mac Aodha,Sethu Vijayakumar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: roto 2.0 is a GPU-parallelised benchmark standardising tactile-based RL across four robot morphologies, achieving 13 Baoding ball rotations in 10 seconds (10x faster than previous SOTA) using blind end-to-end manipulation with only proprioception and tactile sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tactile-based RL is fragmented and over-focused on simple orientation tasks; the field lacks standardised benchmarks for complex manipulation.

Method: GPU-parallelised benchmark (roto 2.0) with four morphologies (16-24 DOF), enabling end-to-end "blind" manipulation using only proprioception + tactile sensing without state information or distillation.

Result: Blind agents achieve 13 Baoding ball rotations in 10 seconds, an order of magnitude faster than current state-of-the-art speeds.

Conclusion: Open-sourcing environments and tuned baselines lowers barriers, allowing researchers to focus on algorithmic challenges rather than RL tuning.

Abstract: Tactile-based reinforcement learning (RL) is currently hindered by fragmented research and a focus on over-saturated orientation tasks. We introduce v2 of the Robot Tactile Olympiad (\texttt{roto 2.0}), a GPU-parallelised benchmark designed to standardise tactile-based RL across four distinct robotic morphologies (16-DOF to 24-DOF). Unlike prior benchmarks, roto focuses on end-to-end "blind" manipulation, utilising only proprioception and tactile sensing without state information or distillation. We demonstrate a significant performance leap, with our blind agents achieving 13 Baoding ball rotations in 10 seconds, an order of magnitude faster than current state-of-the-art speeds. By open-sourcing our environments and robustly tuned baselines, we reduce the barrier to entry and enable researchers to prioritise fundamental algorithmic challenges over tedious RL tuning. Website: https://elle-miller.github.io/roto/

</details>


### [437] [Lost in Fog: Sensor Perturbations Expose Reasoning Fragility in Driving VLAs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21446)
*Abhinaw Priyadershi,Jelena Frtunikj*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper studies robustness of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) autonomous driving planners under sensor perturbations, finding that Chain-of-Causation explanation consistency strongly correlates with trajectory reliability, and that enabling CoC generation improves trajectory accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure interpretable autonomous driving planners remain reliable under real-world sensor degradation, not just generate explanations.

Method: Controlled perturbation study of Alpamayo R1 (10B) across 1,996 scenarios under 8 sensor perturbations (~18,000 trials), analyzing CoC reasoning consistency vs. trajectory deviation.

Result: Reasoning consistency is a high-fidelity trajectory reliability indicator: CoC change → 5.3× trajectory deviation spike, r=0.99 across attacks, r_pb=0.53 per-sample (Cohen's d=1.12). Enabling CoC improves trajectory accuracy by 11.8% (p<0.0001). Degradation is linear (R²=0.957) under noise; preprocessing defenses provide marginal relief.

Conclusion: CoC consistency serves as a quantitative proxy for planning safety, motivating reasoning-based runtime monitoring for safer VLA deployment.

Abstract: Interpretable autonomous driving planners depend not only on generating explanations, but also on those explanations remaining reliable under real-world sensor degradation. In this paper we present a controlled perturbation study of Vision-Language-Action (VLA) robustness in autonomous driving, evaluating Alpamayo R1 (10B parameters) across 1,996 scenarios under eight sensor perturbations (Gaussian noise at four intensities, two lighting extremes, and two fog levels; ${\sim}18{,}000$ inference trials). We find that reasoning consistency is a high-fidelity indicator of trajectory reliability: when Chain-of-Causation (CoC) explanations change after perturbation, trajectory deviation spikes $5.3{\times}$ (21.8m vs 4.1m), with $r\!=\!0.99$ across attack types and $r_{pb}\!=\!0.53$ per-sample (Cohen's $d\!=\!1.12$). A controlled ablation provides evidence that enabling CoC generation is associated with improved trajectory accuracy (11.8% on average across conditions; $p < 0.0001$) under matched inference settings. Over the tested noise range ($σ\in \{10, 30, 50, 70\}$), degradation is approximately linear ($R^2\!=\!0.957$), while standard input preprocessing defenses provide only marginal relief. Together, these results establish CoC consistency as a quantitative proxy for planning safety and motivate reasoning-based runtime monitoring for safer VLA deployment.

</details>


### [438] [HITL-D: Human In The Loop Diffusion Assisted Shared Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21460)
*Riley Zilka,Sergey Khlynovskiy,Allie Wang,Martin Jagersand*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HITL-D is a shared control framework combining diffusion-based policies with human input for robotic manipulation, reducing task completion time by 40% and workload by 37% versus traditional teleoperation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integration of human expertise with diffusion-based policies in shared control remains relatively unexplored for autonomous manipulation systems.

Method: Proposes Human-In-The-Loop Diffusion (HITL-D) framework that uses diffusion-based policies combined with human control to provide autonomous end effector orientation updates conditioned on scene point clouds and Cartesian position, reducing required joystick control axes.

Result: In a 12-participant multi-task user study, HITL-D reduced task completion times by 40%, perceived workload by 37%, and improved Likert-scale ratings for independence, intuitiveness, and confidence.

Conclusion: HITL-D effectively integrates human expertise with autonomous assistance, improving both objective and subjective aspects of teleoperation.

Abstract: Autonomous manipulation systems have achieved remarkable capabilities, yet the integration of human expertise with diffusion-based policies in shared control remains relatively unexplored. In this paper, we propose Human-In-The-Loop Diffusion (HITL-D), a shared control framework that enhances user performance in multi-step, insertion, and fine manipulation tasks. HITL-D leverages a novel combination of diffusion-based policies and human control to provide autonomous end effector orientation updates conditioned on a scene point cloud and the Cartesian position of the end effector. This approach reduces the number of joystick control axes required, thereby lowering mental workload. In a multi-task user study with 12 participants, HITL-D reduced average task completion times by 40%, decreased perceived workload by 37%, and improved Likert-scale ratings for independence, intuitiveness, and confidence compared to traditional teleoperation methods. These results demonstrate that HITL-D effectively integrates human expertise with autonomous assistance, improving both objective and subjective aspects of teleoperation.

</details>
